experiments.title: How does cause-specific messaging on the donation form affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on different themes for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2025 - 07/28/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of focusing the donation form messaging around specific thematic areas of work. We hypothesized that more focused messaging would create a deeper connection with users and motivate them to give more often.  This experiment was based on market research the creative team conducted on different themes they thought might motivate users.
Control: Donate now to give every child a strong start in life.
Variant 1: Make an impact on a child’s life today–focus on rural poverty.
Variant 2: Make an impact on a child’s life today–focus on conflict.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether focusing the donation form messaging around specific thematic areas would create a stronger connection with donors and drive higher giving. The hypothesis: more targeted messaging would feel more relevant and motivate more people to give.

What the Results Suggest:
The drop in recurring giving for both treatments shows that thematic, time-specific messaging may encourage one-time gifts but discourage ongoing support. Donors may need to perceive an ongoing, unresolved need to commit to monthly giving, while focused themes can make the problem seem solvable with a single gift.
Desktop RPV was a bright spot for both treatments, but conversion dropped across the board on that device, suggesting higher-value donors may respond to focused messaging while casual browsers are put off by it.
Next Steps
Given the significant drop in recurring conversion for Treatment A, implementing either variant as-is is risky. Future tests should investigate integrating thematic messaging into the form, alongside a clear, recurring giving prompt—for example, begin with the theme and also emphasize the ongoing need before presenting gift frequency options.

experiments.title: How pre-selecting a monthly gift and amount for a Meta donation form impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting a gift amount for Meta donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2025 - 06/26/2025
Based on previous experiments, we had seen that a combination of pre-selecting monthly and $35 on the gift array had helped increase recurring conversions. We wanted to test whether this was the case across different channels; in this experiment, we focused on Meta specifically. The traffic was split 85% to control and 15% to treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether increasing the donation amounts shown on a Meta form would lead donors to give more. The hypothesis: higher displayed amounts would shift donor choices upward, raising the average gift.
Key Results:

What the Results Suggest
Two behavioral patterns help explain the lift:
The middle effect: Donors tend to gravitate toward middle options. Raising the array raised that middle choice.
 	Perceived appropriateness: The uptick in conversion suggests higher amounts may actually feel more fitting for the cause — a lower ask, like $25, may signal that the need isn't serious.
Next Steps:
Given the consistent directional lift across conversion, RPV, and average gift, and acknowledging that limited sample sizes reduce confidence in the findings, this test warrants further validation with a larger sample — particularly for desktop and recurring giving, where data was too limited to draw robust conclusions.

experiments.title: How increasing the one-time gift array on a Meta donation form impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing gift arrays for Meta donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2025 - 12/10/2025
This test evaluated the effect of increasing the one-time ask array on Meta forms. Our hypothesis was that keeping the end anchor amount of $150 but increasing the amounts between would increase average gift without negatively impacting conversion. We had previously tested the one-time pre-selected gift array for Meta; the test results can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine if increasing the middle amounts within a one-time ask array on Meta forms, while keeping the $150 end anchor, would increase the average gift value without decreasing conversion rates.
What Drove the Lift
Most donors default to the pre-selected amount, so the gains were concentrated among active choosers — donors who navigated away from the default. Two dynamics appear to have influenced their behavior:
The compromise effect: Donors tend to avoid extremes and gravitate toward a middle option. Raising the middle amounts nudged that "safe" choice upward.
 	Reduced sticker shock: Tighter gaps between amounts made the transition from lower to higher options feel more natural.
Next Steps
We'll be rolling out the variant and continuing to test — both around the specific amounts and which value is pre-selected as the default.

experiments.title: How incorporating claims of value on a matching donation page impacted transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating Bible League Canada’s claims of value on their campaign donation page will increase transactions because the added-value prop language will make the ask more appealing and better aligned with what potential donors care about.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 04/23/2024
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization that provides Bibles and training for local champions and believers around the world. They ran a Value Proposition Workshop to identify their key claims of value that are appealing and unique to BLC, which were then shared with their donors and subscribers in a survey.
The survey revealed that supporters were most motivated by the claims identified in the Value Proposition Workshop.
Bible League Canada follows Jesus’ model for reaching people by equipping local believers to share the Word of God with their neighbors and build healthy, sustainable churches.
Bible League Canada intentionally provides Bibles, one at a time, to people who want to learn more about Jesus through discipleship programs in their local community.
We set out to test whether emphasizing these key claims of value on a high-urgency campaign donation page would increase the number of visitors who donate. Our hypothesis was that if the donation page highlights the claims supporters care about, visitors will be more likely to feel aligned with the campaign and choose to make a gift.
We ran an A/B test in VWO: half of the visitors saw the original landing page, while the other half saw a version with the value claims.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating Bible League Canada's claims of value on their campaign donation page led to a significant increase in transactions. The treatment group, which saw the page with the value-added claims, experienced a 44.6% increase in transactions compared to the control group. This indicates that aligning the donation ask with what visitors care about can positively influence their decision to donate.
However, the statistical confidence level for these results is 87.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to fully confirm that emphasizing the claims of value is indeed the cause of the increase in transactions.
The treatment group also saw a 64.3% increase in their average gift amount ($38.08 vs $33.51 for the control), which supports the hypothesis that the added claims of value make the donation ask more appealing to visitors. This increase had a 86% level of confidence, so further testing is needed to fully validate this lift.
Future experiments should continue testing the impact of highlighting Bible League Canada's value claims on donation pages, such as through new A/B tests or by varying their presentation across different donation pages.

experiments.title: Does a shortened landing page with added credibility language improve sign-up rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that swapping in credibility language for new names on the “Taboo Questions” landing page will increase sign-up rates by prioritizing copy that addresses the hypothesis (users aren’t sure who Time of Grace or Pastor Mike Novotny is) without simply adding to the page’s overall length.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2026 - 03/18/2026
Time of Grace landing page, “Taboo Questions With Pastor Mike” has struggled with a low sign-up rate of 10% – 14%. With ad targeting suppressing known audience and only being directed to new names, we hypothesized that credibility may be a point of friction. “Who is Time of Grace?” “Why should I care what Pastor Mike thinks about these topics”? An initial test that simply added this credibility language resulted in an even greater decrease. However, we hypothesized that it was not the credibility language itself. Rather, this latest iteration added in credibility language while also shortening the length of the page. This way, the treatment wasn’t simply giving the user a longer page to read, but was swapping existing copy for copy that addressed our hypothesis—keeping the overall length similar, or if anything a little bit shorter.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Adding credibility language about Time of Grace and Pastor Mike Novotny led to a significant increase in email signups for all traffic on the "Taboo Questions" landing page. In contrast, this same language "failed" in a previous experiment, providing critical insight into test execution and Time of Grace's audience. This iteration shows the language addresses a friction point that users encountered on the landing page. Its success depended on being swapped in, not just added to and lengthened. Simply increasing its length previously had a negative impact. This demonstrates that an asset cannot "have it all" or use every tool at once. Instead, it must prioritize what donors need most.

experiments.title: How Does a Monthly Ask Influence Recurring Donor Conversion?

rows.name: Control: Recurring Donations w/ One-Time Gift Default
Treatment: Recurring Donations w/ Recurring Gift Default

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a clear monthly giving ask to donors arriving from an urgency-driven Facebook ad will increase recurring conversion and long-term value because framing the gift as ongoing support encourages deeper commitment than a one-time ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2026 - 03/20/2026
Human Coalition running a Facebook fundraising campaign built around a clear and compelling $26 one-time donation ask. This ad is one of our strongest performers, driven by a message that invites donors to step into a moment of urgent need and provide immediate, compassionate support.
While this approach is effective at generating initial gifts, we want to better understand how to increase long-term donor value from this traffic. Specifically, we are exploring whether donors who respond to this type of urgent, emotionally resonant appeal are open to making an ongoing commitment (monthly gift) rather than a one-time gift.
To test this, we ran a split URL experiment on the donation experience following the ad click. All users see the same Facebook ad and $26 framing, but are randomly directed to one of two landing page experiences:
Control: A standard donation page focused on a one-time gift
Treatment: A modified donation page that clearly and prominently asks for a monthly recurring gift, with language intentionally adapted to remove ambiguity and reinforce the ongoing nature of the commitment
This test is designed to isolate whether reframing the post-click experience, from a single act of giving to a sustained monthly impact, changes how donors choose to give.
At its core, this experiment explores a key motivational question: When someone is moved to act in a moment of urgency, are they more likely to give once—or to commit to ongoing support when that path is made clear?
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not reach statistical significance (93.5% confidence); however, the directional results provide meaningful insight into donor behavior.
The monthly-first experience increased recurring gift conversion from 0.86% to 1.7% (+100.5% relative lift). On its own, this suggests that when donors are explicitly and clearly asked to give monthly, more of them will choose to do so. The treatment successfully surfaced and captured latent willingness among a subset of donors to commit to ongoing support.
However, this increase came from a relatively small base and did not translate into enough total recurring volume to outweigh declines in overall conversion. This indicates that while the ask itself is effective at shifting behavior for some donors, the broader audience arriving from this ad is not primarily motivated by a recurring commitment.
Secondary Metrics
1. Overall Conversion Rate (Transactions)
Control: 10.87%Treatment: 7.01%
The treatment saw a significant drop in overall conversion rate. This indicates that introducing a monthly-first ask created friction at the point of decision, reducing the likelihood that donors would complete any gift.
2. One-Time Donations
Control: 9.66%Treatment: 5.28%
One-time giving declined sharply in the treatment. This is a critical driver of overall performance loss and suggests that the monthly framing may have interrupted donor momentum rather than redirecting it.
3. Revenue & Value per Visitor
Control: $5,220.50 total | $4.50 per visitorTreatment: $3,385.50 total | $2.93 per visitor
Revenue followed the same pattern as conversion rate. The lower value per visitor confirms that the monthly ask did not generate enough higher-value gifts to compensate for reduced participation.
Reviewing all metrics together:
The monthly ask works for a subset of donors (as seen in the lift in recurring conversion), but reduces participation across the broader audienceThere is a clear tradeoff between commitment and conversion: increasing upfront commitment decreases the number of people willing to actThe ad creates a moment of urgency and simplicity (“give $26 now”), and the monthly ask introduces cognitive and emotional friction that disrupts that momentumDirectional consistency across metrics increases confidence in the insight, despite not reaching 95% statistical significance
Next Steps
Test aligning the ask earlier in the journey (Test ad variants that frame the gift as monthly from the start to better match donor expectations)Test variations of a post-donation monthly upsell

experiments.title: Does the placement of the payment module impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe moving the module to the end of the form for donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2025 - 04/21/2025
Due to a PCI update from our donation form vendor, the payment module now loads more slowly. We aimed to test where to place this module on the form to avoid adding friction. Our hypothesis was that placing the slow-loading module near the top would increase user friction and reduce conversions. To address this, we moved the module to the last step, giving it time to load.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of repositioning the PCI payment module within the donation form. We hypothesized that placing the module near the top of the form was causing friction due to its slow load time, leading users to encounter an unresponsive payment field before it fully rendered. By moving the module to the final step, we aimed to give it sufficient time to load before the user needed to interact with it.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate: +18% lift (80% confidence, meaning there is an 80% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
 	Overall Revenue Per Visitor: +8% lift (64% confidence, meaning there is a 64% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
Insights:
The improvement in conversion rate reflects a meaningfully smoother customer journey. Repositioning the payment module to the last step ensures it is fully loaded by the time users reach it, eliminating a key point of friction. Mobile results showed significant gains, while desktop RPV declined and warrants further investigation.
Recommendation & Next Steps
Based on these results, we have moved the module to the bottom of all top-trafficked donation forms and will adopt this placement as the default for new forms going forward.

experiments.title: Does pre-selecting higher default gift amounts within donation tiers increase overall revenue while maintaining average gift value?”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and relies on reader-supported giving to fund its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover sought to optimize its donation form experience to increase revenue per visitor while maintaining stable average gift values—particularly for prospect-heavy acquisition traffic.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how different gift arrays and preselection strategies affect conversion and gift value. For this test, we combined two variables: gift array size ($25/50/100 vs. $50/100/250) and preselecting the second gift amount on both arrays, resulting in four total variants. The test was run across multiple content offers to build sample size and understand how performance varied by offer type.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
directional but not statistically valid
Performance metrics
Total samples: 364
 	Total donations: 25
 	Overall average gift: $33.40
 	Control array ($50/100/250) was the lowest-performing variant on both transactions and revenue per visitor
 	Lower array variants ($25/50/100) showed higher conversion rates and higher revenue per visitor than control in some treatments
Audience and offer context
Baseline AOV for this offer is ~$40
 	~70% of traffic is from prospecting audiences
 	Preselection did not materially change performance for this offer or audience mix

experiments.title: How adding an option to donate through ACH impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ACH option for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2025 - 08/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the donation form’s payment section. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option, but would improve the user experience for those who would interact with it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the payment section on the donation form. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option but would create a better user experience for the users who would interact with it.

Key Results:

-       +10% non-statistically significant lift (81%) in overall conversion rate.

-       No change in overall RPV.

This test showed that by offering another familiar, often trusted option, we removed a potential barrier for donors who prefer or are accustomed to direct debit. Also, direct debit is often perceived as more secure and reliable, so the mere presence of an additional trusted option reduced friction for all users and made more donors comfortable completing the gift.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have implemented the direct debit option across all donation forms.

experiments.title: General vs Specific: Does a Tangible Ask Increase Sticky Button Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sticky button ask from “Donate” to “Give Shoes” for website visitors will achieve more gifts and revenue because a specific, tangible ask makes the impact clearer and more motivating than a generic donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2026 - 03/11/2026
Buckner is a humanitarian ministry organization with many varying initiatives and campaigns. We’ve recently been evaluating how to convert visitors to their websites into donors, and decided on a sticky donate button design. However, we wanted to test if a specific campaign call-to-action would outperform a general donation ask in driving donation conversions through the sticky button.
We tested two versions of a persistent sticky button:
Control: A heart icon with the text: “Donate”, leading to a general donation page supporting children and families
Treatment: A shoe icon with the text: “Give Shoes”, leading to the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page (one of Buckner’s most recognizable giving opportunities)
Shoes for Orphan Souls is an evergreen campaign that is also currently promoted with Meta Advertising. It has a strong correlation to Buckner’s brand and a clear cost-per-impact, at $15 for a pair of shoes for one child.
Our hypothesis centered on whether specificity, especially tied to this well-known and emotionally resonant program, would make the giving opportunity feel more compelling.
This test ran for 30 days with a 50/50 traffic split on their general homepage and blog pages, excluding any sensitive blog topics or resources that usually avoid donation asks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 1 month, but the site traffic and sticky button conversion rates were too low for us to acquire enough samples for a valid result. The data we do have shows the specific treatment ask to Give Shoes was likely preferred over the control ask to Donate, but further testing is needed to see if this result is truly valid.
Key Learnings:
1.) Tangible framing may also influence gift amount - The treatment saw a higher average gift ($70 vs $50), suggesting that the specific impact framing anchored donors to give more generously, not just convert at higher rates. This suggests that reducing ambiguity at the first touchpoint  may increase both conversion and gift size.
2.) Familiar, campaign based asks can outperform generic ones - Shoes for Orphan Souls is a well-known and emotionally resonant program. This test suggests that leading with a recognizable and mission-specific initiative can outperform general appeals, especially on high traffic entry points like a homepage, where they may be motivated to give to that campaign in the first place.
3.) The Sample size limits confidence, but the direction is strong enough to warrant future testing - With only 8 donations, this test lacks the volume needed for a firm conclusion. However, the consistency of the lift indicates that this is a promising direction worth retesting or scaling in a new way.
In future testing, we would need to increase the sample size by running the element on more pages, and also track clicks on each CTA button to see if the landing pages had any effect on the conversion rate.
We should also test alternate elements such as a slide-up element to see if that would increase visitor engagement and get more samples for a test like this.

experiments.title: How updating the layout of the homepage to focus on content impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clearer navigation for website visitors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2025 - 10/06/2025
Save the Children’s homepage serves multiple purposes and often appears cluttered. In this experiment, we introduced larger image blocks with concise text to guide users to relevant content. The test evaluated the effect of placing navigation-oriented content blocks at the top of the homepage. We hypothesized that this change would streamline access, helping users reach their desired content more efficiently and, in turn, boosting engagement and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine the impact of adding navigation focused content blocks to the top of the homepage. We hypothesized that this would reduce friction by providing users with an easier way to reach the content they want most. This would then increase engagement and conversion rate.
Key Results:
12% non-statistically significant decrease (94%) in overall conversion rate.
 	18% statistically significant decrease (95%) in overall revenue per visitor.
This test showed that changing the homepage content blocks from news-related to navigational blocks reduced emotional engagement and urgency cues, leading to lower donor conversion. News content activates empathy and a sense of immediacy, prompting spontaneous giving, while navigational blocks encourage exploration rather than action.

experiments.title: Does including an author image improve email performance for non-donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we add an image of a credible Hoover fellow to the email, it will increase engagement and gifts. Recognizable and authoritative figures can increase trust and attention, which may make readers more likely to engage with the message and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2026
This experiment aimed to determine whether including an image in an email promoting a free ebook would increase engagement and donations. Conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization focused on individual, economic, and political freedom, the test compared two versions of the email. The only variable was the addition of an image featuring a well-known Hoover fellow, while all other elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Image)
CTR: 1.27%

experiments.title: How adding match math language to a donation form impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the impact of a match gift for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/22/2025 - 10/28/2025
Save the Children runs multiple match campaigns throughout the year; for their fall match campaign, we wanted to test whether adding in ‘match math’ to a donation form would help increase conversions. Our hypothesis was that by showing the impact of a donor’ gift through ‘math’– Your gift of $25 becomes $50 in impact— we will help users recognize the increased impact of their gift, which will increase conversion. This experiment had 3 variants, with a control and 2 treatments that had the match math either above or below the gift array selections.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
-       -14% non-statistically significant decrease (90%) in overall conversion rate for variant B and –17% non-statistically significant decrease (94%) for variant C.
-       26% non-statistically significant increase (88%) in desktop conversion rate for variant C.
-       68% statistically significant increase (98%) in desktop RPV for variant C.
This test shows that our hypothesis proved true for desktop users but added friction for mobile users. The additional copy on mobile led to a shift in layout each time they selected a new ask amount, which has been shown to correlate with higher abandonment.

experiments.title: Does Displaying Multiple Ministry Brands Strengthen Donor Confidence?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that displaying the logos of Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands below the donation button for Facebook-driven content audiences will achieve a higher conversion rate and increased revenue, as visible scale and brand familiarity strengthen trust and signal broader mission impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2026
Luther Seminary operates multiple well-known ministry brands, including Enter the Bible, Working Preacher, and Faith+Lead. Each brand serves a distinct audience while advancing Luther’s broader mission of providing free, high-quality biblical resources and leadership development.
Many donors first encounter Luther through a single resource, such as an Enter the Bible article or a Faith+Lead course, and may not immediately recognize that these offerings are part of a larger network. This raises an important motivational question: Does emphasizing the breadth of Luther’s ministries increase donor confidence and willingness to give?
To explore this, we ran an A/B test on Facebook-driven traffic to two content-based donation pages:
Enter the Bible: Matthew
Faith+Lead: Genesis to Revelation Course
Visitors were randomly split between:
Control: A standard donation page featuring only the originating brand.
Treatment: The same donation page, with the addition of logos representing Luther Seminary’s affiliated ministry brands displayed prominently below the “Give” button.
The donation form defaulted to one-time giving in both versions to isolate the impact of brand presentation.
This experiment aims to answer a central donor motivation question: Does highlighting organizational scale and a family of trusted brands increase giving by reinforcing credibility and perceived impact?
The primary success metric is transactions (both one-time and monthly gifts), with revenue per visitor, one-time, and recurring gifts as secondary measures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The overall transaction result achieved 89.3% statistical confidence, which does not meet the conventional 95% threshold for declaring a statistically significant winner. Because of this, we cannot definitively claim that the treatment caused the decrease in conversion rate. However, the results are directionally consistent across nearly every metric:
Overall conversion rate declined (-15.14%)
 	One-time giving declined (-15.51%)
 	Recurring giving declined (-34.53%, low volume)
 	Revenue per visitor declined (-7.18%)
When multiple related metrics move in the same direction, it strengthens the directional signal — even if the test does not reach full statistical significance. Directionally, this suggests that displaying multiple affiliated brand logos below the donation button likely introduced distraction or diluted the primary brand connection at the moment of giving. For Facebook-driven, content-based traffic, donors may be primarily motivated by the specific resource or brand they engaged with, rather than by the broader institutional ecosystem. At the point of conversion, clarity and singular focus may be more powerful than signals of scale.

experiments.title: How a past year-end donation page performed compared to the current year-end donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running last year’s donation page copy versus this year’s donation page copy for CYE donation page visitors will yield no meaningful difference in overall donor conversion, because both pages communicate a sufficiently similar value proposition, urgency, and call to action, motivating giving at comparable levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2025 - 12/19/2025
During IMB’s CYE Campaign, we observed unexpected softness in overall performance. While several potential contributing factors were identified, we prioritized validating whether the donation page itself was contributing to the issue.
To isolate that risk, we ran a direct A/B test comparing last year’s donation page copy to this year’s version. Although the test ran for only a few days, the results were directional and clear enough to warrant decisive action during IMB’s largest fundraising season.
This year’s donation page copy converted donors at a higher rate, allowing us to confidently remove last year’s version to protect conversions and revenue at a critical moment in the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results indicate that the treatment donation page (last year’s copy) drove a 32.6% decrease in overall transactions (81% LOC). While the test did not reach full statistical validation, the directional signal was clear and unfavorable, reflecting a negative audience response to the treatment.

Given the strength of this directional learning—and the risk of suppressing conversion during IMB’s largest fundraising season—we ended the test.

These findings confirm that this year’s donation page copy is converting donors at a higher rate, indicating that the donation page itself is not the primary driver of the campaign’s softness. As a result, additional testing was required to better diagnose the underlying factors impacting performance.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a year-end donation page impacts overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the condensed copy, countdown clock, and goal thermometer on the CYE Donation page will increase overall donor conversion by reducing friction in the giving experience and allowing donors—particularly mobile users—to complete the donation process more seamlessly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/25/2025
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we launched a three-way test designed to introduce meaningful variation and identify elements that could improve donor conversion.
Control: Standard CYE donation page
Treatment A: CYE donation page with countdown clock and goal thermometer
Treatment B: Hyper-condensed copy with countdown clock and goal thermometer
We anticipated Treatment B would outperform the other variations, as it significantly reduced page length—an advantage for mobile donors—while preserving urgency and social proof through the countdown clock and goal thermometer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results show that Treatment A led to a modest 3.4% decrease in overall transactions (15.2% LOC), while Treatment B drove a substantial 27.8% decrease in transactions with a high level of directional confidence (90.1% LOC). Although neither variation reached full statistical validation, the magnitude and confidence of the decline associated with Treatment B clearly indicated a negative impact on donor conversion. Given the critical nature of the CYE fundraising window and the risk of continued conversion suppression, we have decided to remove Treatment B from rotation. This allowed Treatment A to continue running against the control, enabling additional learning without materially jeopardizing overall conversion or revenue during a high-stakes fundraising period.

experiments.title: How a dynamic goal thermometer on a high urgency donation page affects revenue and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a single, static $500,000 campaign thermometer with a dynamic thermometer that resets in $5,000 increments will achieve an increase in revenue and conversion rates because smaller, frequently achievable goals make progress feel more attainable, reinforce donor impact, and create a stronger sense of momentum and urgency during the year-end giving period.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2025
This experiment took place during The Heritage Foundation’s annual calendar year-end fundraising campaign, one of their most important giving periods of the year. The campaign had a total goal of $500,000, and the primary donation experience included a progress thermometer meant to signal momentum and urgency to donors as they considered giving.
Because year-end traffic is high and donors are especially motivated by deadlines and progress cues, this was an ideal moment to test whether an alternative framing of the goal and progress indicator could increase motivation and perceived impact.
The test was run directly on the campaign donation page.
Control: The standard donation page displayed a traditional progress thermometer showing the total amount raised toward the fixed $500,000 campaign goal.
Treatment: The donation page used a dynamic thermometer that “reset” once a smaller goal was reached, increasing by $5,000 increments each time the goal was met. This created the perception of frequent wins and ongoing momentum rather than a single, distant finish line.
Both versions were otherwise consistent, ensuring the thermometer behavior was the primary variable being tested.
The motivation for this experiment came from a behavioral insight: large, distant goals can feel abstract and discouraging, especially late in a campaign when progress may slow. In contrast, smaller, rapidly achievable goals can increase donor confidence, urgency, and the sense that their gift meaningfully contributes to immediate success.
We wanted to test whether breaking a large year-end goal into visible, recurring milestones would:
Make progress feel more attainable
Increase perceived donor impact
Sustain momentum throughout the campaign rather than only near the final deadline
This experiment was designed to evaluate whether a dynamic, milestone-based progress indicator could outperform a traditional static thermometer during a high-stakes year-end fundraising push.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The most significant takeaway from this experiment was that the dynamic, milestone-based thermometer meaningfully increased revenue, even without a corresponding increase in transactions. The treatment generated a 30% lift in revenue, with an 89.7% level of confidence, indicating a strong and practically meaningful impact on total dollars raised.
At the same time, transactions decreased by 10.8%, though this result came with only 48% confidence, suggesting the decline is not statistically reliable and may be due to natural variance rather than a true negative effect. Taken together, these results indicate that the revenue lift was driven primarily by higher average gift amounts, not by more donors completing gifts.
This suggests that the dynamic thermometer helped donors feel more confident making larger contributions by reinforcing momentum and the sense that their gift could meaningfully “complete” a near-term goal.
Moving forward, this experiment reinforces the value of breaking large fundraising goals into visible, attainable milestones, especially during high-pressure moments like year-end campaigns. Rather than relying solely on a single, long-term progress bar, future donation experiences can leverage smaller, frequently achieved goals to sustain momentum and encourage generosity.
Specifically, we will:
Continue testing dynamic or segmented goal framing in high-volume fundraising momentsPair milestone-based thermometers with strategic ask array testing to better understand how goal framing influences gift sizeBe cautious about interpreting transaction volume changes when confidence is low, focusing instead on revenue-per-visitor and average gift as primary success metrics
Overall, this test demonstrates that optimizing how progress is framed can materially impact fundraising outcomes—even when surface-level conversion metrics appear flat or mixed.

experiments.title: Can democracy-focused messaging outperform urgency-based copy on a donation page during EOY?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using EOY-reframed, democracy- and community-focused messaging for WUNC donors would increase donor motivation and conversion compared to crisis-focused copy because values-based framing can strengthen intrinsic motivation and reinforce donors’ identity as supporters of public media.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2025 - 11/25/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving North Carolina with a mission to inform, engage, and strengthen civic life, supported largely by listener contributions. As the organization entered the 2025 end-of-year (EOY) fundraising period, sustaining revenue growth and donor motivation remained key program goals.
This experiment tested donation page copy during a high-traffic EOY moment to understand whether reframing the ask around democracy and community impact could outperform WUNC’s existing urgency-based messaging tied to federal funding cuts. The test was prioritized as part of a broader effort to evolve messaging beyond crisis framing and assess when values-based appeals resonate most with donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
Big takeaway: The urgency-based control outperformed the democracy-focused treatment on the primary KPI (donations). Secondary metrics show a similar directional pattern, though only one reaches near-significant confidence.
📊 Performance metrics (with confidence comparisons)
Total donations (primary KPI)Control: 25 / 126 (19.8%)Treatment: 13 / 111 (11.7%)Difference: +8.1 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~92%❌ Not statistically valid at 95%, but directionally meaningfulOne-time donationsControl: 19 / 126 (15.1%)Treatment: 8 / 111 (7.2%)Difference: +7.9 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~95%⚠️ Borderline statistical validity; strongest secondary signal in favor of urgency messagingSustaining donationsControl: 6 / 126 (4.8%)Treatment: 5 / 111 (4.5%)Difference: +0.3 ptsConfidence level: <10%❌ No meaningful or directional differenceRevenueControl: $4,100Treatment: $880Revenue strongly favored the control, driven by higher conversion volume.⚠️ No statistical confidence calculated, as revenue is not suitable for a two-proportion z-test in this context.
✂️ Segment interpretation
The performance gap was driven primarily by one-time donor conversion, not sustaining behavior.Sustainer response was effectively neutral between variants.
Next Steps
Continue running with the current urgency-based messaging for EOY. Additional messaging tests are planned to determine when donor responsiveness begins shifting toward values-based framing. Timing is a key variable under consideration, as this test ran immediately before Giving Tuesday, when urgency messaging historically performs better.

experiments.title: Content Offer 2: Can adjusting gift arrays and removing preselection improve donor acquisition for paid social content offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and depends on donor support to sustain its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that balance donor acquisition with gift value, particularly for newer content offers distributed through paid media.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection affect conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($25/50/100 and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This specific read focuses on the “Nine Myths about Capitalism” content offer, which was newly launched and distributed primarily through Meta during the test period.
Note: this is a drilldown of a larger experiment (link).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
For the “Nine Myths about Capitalism” offer, results were mixed and not statistically valid, but directional patterns were consistent across variants.
📊 Performance metrics
Total samples: 1,445Total conversions: 52Overall conversion rate: 3.6%Overall average gift: $38.64Control ($50/100/250, no select):Conversion rate: 3.4%Average gift: $52.50$25/50/100, no select:Conversion rate: 5.2% (+56.6% vs. control)Average gift: $34.97Preselecting $100 on the higher array:Conversion rate: 1.3% (–60.6% vs. control)Average gift: $57.00
✂️ Offer and audience context
This was a new content offerTraffic skewed heavily toward Meta-paid acquisitionPreselecting a gift amount reduced conversion rates on both gift arraysThe $25/50/100 array without preselection leaned toward acquiring more donors, while the higher array without preselection maintained a strong average gift
The data suggests that preselection may introduce friction for this audience and offer type. While the control performed well on average gift, removing preselection—particularly on the lower array—appears more favorable for donor acquisition. Given the newness of the offer and paid social context, these signals likely reflect early-funnel donor sensitivity rather than price resistance alone.
✅ Statistical validity
Results did not reach statistical significanceConfidence levels were below the 95% threshold required for validation
Next Steps
Consider breaking out audiences for the higher gift array without preselection to evaluate whether it performs better for higher-intent segments. Alternatively, launch new offers initially with a higher array and no preselect to capture early high-value donors, then shift to a lower array configuration to optimize cost per conversion as the offer matures.

experiments.title: How adding tangible benefits about monthly giving impacts monthly donor conversion on video series acquisition donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clear, tangible benefits for becoming a monthly donor on acquisition donation pages will increase monthly donor conversion, as it makes the offer to become a monthly donor clearer and appealing, thereby increasing motivation to commit.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2025 - 09/23/2025
A nonprofit organization committed to supporting families through biblically based resources and guidance. As part of ongoing efforts to increase recurring donor conversion, we designed a test to evaluate whether highlighting exclusive monthly donor benefits would encourage more visitors to become monthly givers on high-traffic acquisition donation pages.
We ran this test across three acquisition funnels tied to popular video series: Proof You Need, Contagious Joy, and Becoming Unoffendable. Their donation pages are shown to users after they sign up for the video series. The pages request a gift of $30 or more, and the gift form defaults to a monthly option. The pages also show a yellow callout above the form to further promote monthly giving.
To build on that foundation, we tested the addition of a new message block near the bottom of the page that highlighted two concrete benefits for monthly donors:
A 40% discount at the  store, plus free shipping on all online orders
A free, hand-picked faith-building resource delivered monthly
We hypothesized that highlighting these tangible, recurring benefits would increase perceived value and commitment, ultimately leading to more recurring donor conversions.
This A/B test was structured as a 50/50 traffic split across all three donation pages, with the control maintaining the current design and messaging, and the treatment introducing the new benefits copy above the donation form. This write-up covers the aggregated results from the three campaigns.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to expectations, adding benefit language to promote monthly giving led to a decrease in both recurring and overall donor conversions. While not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level, the treatment showed a 24.4% drop in monthly donor conversions (48 gifts in the control group vs. 37 in the treatment group) at 80% confidence, and a 14.4% drop in total transactions (220 in the control group vs. 192 in the treatment group) at 89.4% confidence. These results fall below the standard threshold for statistical significance but indicate a consistent downward trend worth noting. This outcome was surprising, as we hypothesized that surfacing tangible donor perks would increase perceived value and encourage more visitors to become monthly givers. However, our interpretation is that introducing additional value proposition elements — even if well-intentioned — may have overwhelmed users on pages already rich in content. These acquisition donation pages serve multiple purposes: confirming video series sign-ups, offering a free book premium, explaining the mission, and prompting donations. By layering on monthly donor benefits, we may have inadvertently created cognitive overload, making the decision-making process harder rather than easier. This result suggests that in highly content-dense environments, more messaging is not always better — even when it adds value. In these contexts, simplicity and a focused message may be more effective than trying to incorporate every persuasive angle. It also reinforces the importance of considering where and how value propositions are introduced on a page. Next Steps:
Test placement and simplicity: Test simpler, more integrated monthly giving prompts (e.g., in the callout or post–one-time gift popup) to avoid competing with other key messages.
 	Explore benefit framing: Future tests could frame benefits around impact or mission alignment, rather than just transactional perks.

experiments.title: Does Calling Out the Giving Tuesday Donor Goal in the Sticky Bar Increase Engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the Giving Tuesday donor goal for IMB homepage visitors will increase engagement with the web asset, as making the goal visible taps into goal-oriented behavior. Visitors are more likely to click, explore, and give when they understand exactly what their participation contributes to.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2025
During IMB’s 2025 Giving Tuesday campaign, we tested the addition of a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar. Our hypothesis was that clearly displaying the goal would increase engagement with the sticky bar—measured by higher click-through rates—and drive more motivated traffic further down the giving path.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a notable increase in engagement, yielding a 29.9% rise in clicks across all traffic. This suggests that prominently displaying a clear Giving Tuesday goal can attract more visitors to the giving pathway. However, because the confidence level fell below the standard 95% threshold, these engagement results should be interpreted as directional rather than definitive for future decision-making. When examining downstream giving behavior, both the control and treatment produced 9 total gifts.
Transactions per session: Treatment showed a 1.3% lift (2.1% LOC)
 	Transactions per click: Treatment showed a 22% decrease (43.5% LOC)
In other words, while the treatment succeeded in motivating more users to click into the donation experience, it did not increase their likelihood of completing a gift once they arrived at the donation page. The sticky bar drove more traffic to the page, but not more donors. Revenue: To prevent outliers from skewing insights, gifts over $500 were removed. Following this adjustment, the treatment resulted in a 93.7% decrease in revenue (94.1% LOC) compared to the control. This suggests that although engagement was higher, the additional traffic did not translate into higher-value giving behavior. In future experiments, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the donor goal messaging to see if different phrasing or formats may have a more significant impact on user engagement. It is also important to continue monitoring and analyzing results from experiments to continually improve strategies for engaging with website visitors and driving them further down the giving path.

experiments.title: How adding an image of the premium in an appeal email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email for the housefile will increase the click-through rate because the cover image is unique and would encourage users to click through.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2025
Time of Grace offers a new premium each month. They send out appeal emails for this premium. The cover for their October premium was particularly distinctive and stood out to the audience.
Therefore, we wanted to test including the image in the email. We wanted to see how that would affect click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding the image of the premium to the appeal email did not achieve the desired increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 25.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a 91.2% statistical level of confidence. While these results are not considered valid, they are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the effect.
In terms of donations, however, we did see an increase in the treatment, but not with a 95% level of confidence.
Control - 11 Donations
Treatment - 17 Donations
LOC = 74.9%
Future studies should investigate how images in emails influence user behavior. Further testing can reveal whether certain images or situations are more effective. Keep analyzing results to improve email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: Does the fact that 100% of a donors gift goes to mission affect conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
For Giving Tuesday, IMB wanted to test how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals.
We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
They believed it would increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by showing how their gift directly supports missionary work.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about where the donor's gift is allocated can have a significant impact on the conversion rate for donations. In this case, the treatment group saw a 43.9% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that donors may be more inclined to give when they have a clear understanding of how their gift will be used.

Additionally, we calculated the impact of click-through rates in the emails and only saw a 1.1% increase with a 28.6% LoC. This means the addition of the transparency note did not have an effect on click-through rate, but helped increase giving once people got to the donation page.

To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of transparency on donation conversion rates. By conducting additional experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different variations of allocation transparency, we can further validate the initial findings and determine the most effective strategies for communicating allocation to donors.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that transparency in donation appeals can build trust and confidence in the organization, ultimately leading to increased support for the mission.

experiments.title: How a one-step donation form performs against a 2-step control for a content offer instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rates because of the reduction in friction on the form generated by the extra steps.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2025 - 10/15/2025
The Hoover Institution is a think thank that is affiliated with Stanford University. For the last couple of years, their donation forms had a 2-step process; the first step was fill out your information and the second step (you had to click on payment information) was add in your billing info. This was a format we had tested into many years ago, but given the data we have seen with other organizations, we wanted to test whether a one-step form would make an impact.
We ran this test on one of Hoover’s more popular content offers, and ran it for 3 months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the steps needed to make a donation for content offer downloaders can lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment group, which used a one-step form instead of a two-step form, experienced a 12.7% increase in transactions for all traffic, with a directional level of confidence of 85%. There were no recurring transactions as a result of this test, so the results are for one-time donations.
Due to some platform limitations, the one-step process was still not as 'clean' as we would have liked (you still needed to click on 'show total' on the treatment form to complete the gift), but the level of confidence was high enough were we felt comfortable declaring a winner on this test.
We will continue to test this set-up on different offers to try and pinpoint if indeed it was just the simplification of the form that made an impact on conversion rates, or if it was tied to the offer itself.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a main donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: A monthly first ask on the main donation page will increase recurring giving with minimal impact on overall transactions, as it highlights the ease and effect of becoming a monthly supporter. Meanwhile, the preselected option subtly reinforces that most donors choose to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 10/07/2025
For the American Cornerstone Institute, our primary focus has been on growing their recurring donor base. To build on that momentum, we tested adding a recurring gift ask directly on the main donation page, inspired by strong results from earlier acquisition offer tests using a similar approach.
This A/B test ran for just over a month, with equal traffic distributed between the control and treatment versions.
The control utilized the existing donation page without any modifications. The treatment mirrored the control’s copy but added details about the benefits of becoming a monthly partner and preselected the monthly giving option on the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Adding a monthly first ask on the main donation page led to a significant increase in recurring donations, with a 223.0% increase in recurring donations for All Traffic. This indicates that highlighting the benefits of becoming a monthly supporter and preselecting the monthly giving option can effectively encourage more donors to choose recurring donations.
2. The treatment also resulted in a substantial increase in overall Transactions, with a 543% increase. This shows that while the focus was on increasing recurring donations, the strategy also had a positive impact on overall donation transactions.
3. The statistical confidence level of 93.6% for the increase in recurring donations and 95% for the rise in transactions suggests that the results are approaching validity, but further testing would be needed for confirmation.
In future experimentation, we should:
1. Continue to explore the impact of emphasizing recurring donations on the main donation page. Further testing can help us validate the results and assess the long-term impact on donor retention and revenue.
2. Consider refining the messaging and design of the monthly giving ask to optimize its effectiveness. This could involve A/B testing different languages, visuals, or incentives to increase recurring donations further.
3. Monitor the overall impact on donation transactions to ensure that the focus on recurring donations does not negatively affect one-time contributions. Finding the right balance between the two types of giving is essential for maximizing fundraising outcomes.
Overall, these findings provide valuable insights into the potential of strategic messaging and design changes to increase recurring donations. By analyzing the results and refining our approach, we can continue to enhance our fundraising strategies and foster sustainable support for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (ICE Survey)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment tested whether clarifying the gift type on the ICE Survey donation form’s submit button would increase completed transactions. The submit button previously did not specify whether the donor was making a one-time or recurring gift, which could introduce uncertainty at the point of decision. To address this, we ran an A/B test for just under 30 days, introducing a variation that clearly stated the gift type on the button (for example, “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift”) to reduce friction and give donors more confidence in their commitment.
The goal was to see if clearer messaging on the submit button would lead to more completed transactions. This aligns with behavioral research showing that reducing uncertainty at critical decision points can increase conversion rates. The test measured the impact on completed transactions as the primary success metric, with button click-throughs as a secondary metric to understand engagement before completion.
If the treatment variation outperformed the control, it would demonstrate the value of transparent communication at the point of conversion. Insights from this test could inform updates to other donation forms and campaigns, ultimately helping drive overall donation growth. By clarifying donor expectations, we aim to create a smoother giving experience and encourage more supporters to complete their gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment variation that added clarity about the gift type to the submit button resulted in a -63.7% relative difference in transactions compared to the control, indicating a substantial decrease in performance. The experiment reached a 93.1% confidence level, providing strong statistical evidence that the treatment negatively impacted conversions.

This suggests that adding gift type language to the submit button created unintended friction or confusion for donors, reducing their likelihood of completing the transaction. 

Future tests should focus on maintaining simplicity and momentum at the conversion point, while addressing commitment clarity at earlier, lower-friction stages of the donor journey.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (Membercard)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2025 - 09/04/2025
The Heritage Foundation is urging its donors to refresh their membership cards as part of an effort to improve transaction completion.This experiment tested the hypothesis that adding clarity about the selected gift type (one-time or monthly) to the submit button on a donation page would increase completed transactions. The goal was to reduce any hesitation or uncertainty at the final step of the giving process by confirming to donors exactly what type of gift they were about to make.
We conducted an A/B test on the donation page from August 20, 2025 to September 4, 2025. The control version displayed the existing generic submit button “Make My Gift”, while the treatment version dynamically updated the button text to reflect the donor’s selected gift type (e.g. “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift”). All other elements of the page remained the same. The primary success metric was completed transactions, with secondary metrics including form starts and abandonment rate.
This two-week A/B test provided insight into how small, high-visibility text changes at the point of conversion can influence donor behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment were that adding gift type clarity to the submit button did not increase transactions as hypothesized; instead, it resulted in a -30.3% relative decrease in conversion rate with an 81.1% confidence level, indicating a directional but not statistically significant negative effect. 

Donors may have perceived the added detail as complexity or an unexpected change in their intended gift, creating last-minute doubt or second-guessing. Instead of offering reassurance, the extra wording may have disrupted their momentum toward completing the donation.

Future testing could explore lighter, more subtle ways to reinforce gift type clarity earlier in the flow rather than at the final submit button. This could preserve donor confidence without disrupting the decision moment at the point of conversion.

experiments.title: How removing checkboxes from a declaration affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the checkboxes for declaration signers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because we are removing friction from the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment tested the hypothesis that removing the “vote of confidence” checkboxes from the Vote of Confidence form would reduce friction and increase email acquisition. The checkboxes were originally included to allow signers to show public support, but they added an extra action to the form. We believed that eliminating these checkboxes while keeping their descriptions as a bulleted list, would create a smoother experience that encourages more people to complete the form and provide their email addresses.
We conducted a split URL test, sending half of the traffic to the original form (with checkboxes) and half to a new version of the form (with the checklist shown as a bulleted list). This setup ensured completely isolated experiences, minimizing cross-contamination between variants. Aside from the removal of the checkboxes, all other design, copy, and flow elements remained consistent. We tracked email acquisition as the primary success metric, while also monitoring revenue, and transaction rates as secondary indicators.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The removal of the checkboxes resulted in a -3.7% relative decrease in email acquisition, with an 88.6% level of confidence, indicating a directional but not statistically significant effect.

Contrary to our hypothesis, the 30-day split URL landing page test showed a small decrease in email acquisition when the checkboxes were removed. While the simplified form reduced friction, it may have also reduced the sense of commitment or engagement that the checkboxes provided, slightly lowering sign-up intent. This suggests that even seemingly minor elements can play a meaningful psychological role, and that removing them does not always result in higher conversions.

This suggests that while the checkboxes introduced a small amount of friction, they may also have served as a commitment or credibility cue, signaling seriousness or social proof to users. Removing them may have subtly reduced users’ sense of engagement or trust in completing the form, which could explain the dip in sign-ups.

For future tests, it may be valuable to explore alternative low-friction commitment indicators to preserve the psychological benefit of commitment signals while reducing perceived effort. Testing placement or wording changes rather than complete removal could also help identify the balance between friction and motivation.

experiments.title: How preselecting the gift frequency and gift amount on a giving form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting and expanding the gift array for Today in the Word acquisition giving page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help reduce decision friction for donors by clearly presenting the preferred giving frequency and gift size within the acquisition funnel .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/24/2025
Moody Bible’s giving forms have historically been designed without pre-selected gift frequencies or amounts. While this has always been their standard setup, it differs from many other donation platforms where defaults are commonly used.
To test whether defaults could reduce decision friction and encourage giving, we introduced two pre-selections:
Gift frequency: defaulted to one-time
Gift amount: defaulted to the lowest option in the gift array ($25)
Our goal was to streamline the donor experience—minimizing effort and uncertainty—while also testing whether this change could increase overall conversion and potentially lift the average gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that pre-selecting gift frequency and amount—combined with expanding the gift array options—produced a significant lift in donor conversion metrics.
The treatment group outperformed the control with a 454.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic (94.4% LOC) and a 1,015% increase in Revenue (100% LOC).
 	Notably, while the sample size was small, the treatment showed no negative impact on transactions or revenue, indicating a clear positive response from Today in the Word’s audience.
 	The average gift amount also increased by 116.7%, confirming our hypothesis that the treatment would encourage larger donations.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array for members on a campaign donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array for non-members will achieve an increase in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2025
The Heritage Foundation ran their annual President’s Match campaign in July. In three of their emails to members, they wanted to test increasing the one-time gift amounts on the donation page to $50, $100, $250 to see if revenue would increase as well.
They changed the gift array for 50% of the traffic.
***The monthly tab’s gift array was also adjusted from $15, $25, $35 to $14, $24, $42, but we do not have the data to split apart recurring from one-time gifts, so this write up includes all transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the gift array for members did not achieve the intended increase in revenue. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in revenue of 22.8% compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at an 86.4% level of confidence.

Additionally, the treatment segment saw a 25.9% decrease in transactions with a 94.3% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of changes to donation strategies before fully implementing them. It may be beneficial to conduct smaller-scale tests or pilot studies before rolling out changes to a larger audience. Additionally, considering factors such as donor behavior, demographics, and giving preferences can help tailor fundraising strategies to better meet the needs and expectations of supporters.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and evaluating different approaches to fundraising to ensure that the intended outcomes are achieved and to optimize revenue generation for the organization.

experiments.title: Do Visual Elements on a Donation Page Help or Hurt Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified donation page design for users visiting a fundraising campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because removing competing visual elements will reduce mental friction for the donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2025 - 07/28/2025
Bible League Canada typically uses a highly visual donation page style as the default format for all of their fundraising campaigns. These pages often include banners, images, and video elements, along with a FundraiseUp donation form embedded at the bottom. While these pages are designed to create an engaging donor experience, we didn’t know whether these visual elements were helping donor conversion, or adding unnecessary distraction and mental friction.
To explore this question, Bible League Canada tested whether removing visual components from the donation page would reduce friction and lead to more completed gifts.
The Control was their standard visual campaign page, and the Treatment was a simplified FundraiseUp campaign page containing only one image and the donation form. The test was structured as a 50/50 split URL experiment during an active fundraising campaign to determine whether visual richness improves performance or adds unnecessary friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we hypothesized that a simplified donation page would reduce friction and increase conversions, the original designed page outperformed the simplified version. The Treatment page saw a 24.4% decrease in donor conversion compared to the Control, with an 89.1% level of confidence—just shy of full statistical validation but directionally meaningful.
In addition to stronger conversion, the Control also generated a higher revenue-per-visitor. However, this may have been influenced by one or more larger gifts, which could skew the average and may not reflect a consistent trend.
This result suggests that in fundraising campaigns, donors may benefit from richer context and emotional reinforcement provided by visual elements like images, banners, and video. Rather than creating distraction, these elements may help reinforce the campaign’s importance and build trust. It’s possible that removing them made the experience feel less compelling or authoritative.
Future testing could explore which specific elements (e.g., video, banners, testimonial quotes) contribute most to conversion. Alternatively, testing a hybrid layout—retaining key persuasive visuals while simplifying navigation and layout—might reveal a better-performing balance.

experiments.title: How removing the footer on a donation page affects the conversation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Removing the footer on a donation page will achieve an increase in the conversion rate because people won’t get distracted by the many page options to visit and allow them to focus on the purpose of the page–to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2025 - 03/18/2025
Alliance Defending Freedom is one of the leading Christian law firms committed to protecting religious freedom, free speech, marriage and family, parental rights, and the sanctity of life.
We ran this test on the donation page for one of their grant programs. Traffic came to this page through house file email.
We want to see if removing the footer would stop people from bouncing off the donation page and increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the footer on the donation page led to a significant increase in transactions for all traffic. The 70.9% increase in conversions shows that the hypothesis that removing the footer would increase the conversion rate was supported by the data.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered fully valid. Further testing would be necessary to confirm that removing the footer consistently leads to higher conversion rates.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of removing the footer on different pages or across different campaigns to see if this trend holds true. Additionally, it would be important to consider other elements on the page that may impact conversion rates and test those as well to optimize donation pages further.

experiments.title: How shortening a survey affects email conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of survey questions to the top 10 most compelling issues will cause an increase in survey completion rate because a shorter, more focused survey reduces respondent fatigue and keeps engagement high by highlighting only the most resonant and emotionally charged topics.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/30/2025
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a survey centered on President Trump’s agenda. The existing version of the survey featured 15 questions. While this provided comprehensive insight, it also posed a potential barrier to conversion due to its length. To optimize for better engagement and increased email conversion rates, we hypothesized that a shorter, more focused survey could outperform the original by reducing participant fatigue and enhancing relevance. This led to the formulation of a controlled A/B test where the only variation was the length and content of the survey.
This experiment was executed through a 50/50 split test across all incoming paid media traffic. For one full month, half of the audience received the original 15-question survey (control), while the other half was shown a trimmed version containing just the top 10 most compelling questions (treatment).
The idea for this test stemmed from a strategic insight: longer surveys often experience higher drop-off rates. We believed that by prioritizing the most emotionally resonant and broadly relevant issues, we could not only streamline the experience but also deepen engagement. The decision to cut five questions was guided by three criteria:
Redundancy – Questions that overlapped thematically with stronger, more comprehensive ones.
Narrowness – Topics that were too specific or niche to drive widespread action.
Relevance – Issues that, while important, were not seen as high-priority by the core Trump-supporting audience.
Questions removed included topics such as electric vehicle mandates, women’s sports, campus radicalism, entitlements, and military modernization—all of which, though valid, were deemed less galvanizing compared to issues like immigration, inflation, and election integrity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reducing the number of survey questions to the top 10 most compelling issues did not lead to an increase in survey completion rates. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of survey length on respondent engagement. While the hypothesis that a shorter, more focused survey would lead to higher completion rates seemed logical, the results of this experiment suggest otherwise.
