It may be beneficial to conduct further research to understand why the treatment group performed lower than the control group and to explore other potential factors that may influence survey completion rates.

When designing future experiments, it is essential to ensure that the hypotheses are based on solid reasoning and supported by data. It is also important to consider the potential impact of changes on the desired outcome and to set clear criteria for assessing the validity of the results. Additionally, incorporating feedback from participants and analyzing user behavior can provide valuable insights for optimizing future surveys and experiments.

experiments.title: Can Changing the Way We Frame a Newsletter Impact Sign-Ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe framing the newsletter as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world will increase conversions by tapping into uncertainty avoidance and information overload.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2025 - 07/19/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners throughout North Carolina. The test focused on the landing page for WUNC’s “What’s Trending” newsletter. We wanted to determine whether reframing the newsletter copy—as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world—would lead to an increase in email sign-ups. All other elements of the landing page remained unchanged.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment
Framing Boosted Sign-Ups
Reframing the newsletter copy to position it as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world led to a 41.1% increase in email sign-ups.
Statistical Confidence
The result had a 90.1% confidence level, suggesting a strong signal, though additional testing is recommended to confirm validity.
Gift Size Observation
Revenue performance was observed as a 1-to-1 gift comparison, with the treatment receiving a substantially larger gift. However, this is anecdotal and not sufficient to conclude a higher average gift resulted from the treatment.
Hypothesis Validation
The experiment supported the hypothesis that appealing to uncertainty avoidance and information overload can effectively drive newsletter sign-ups.
Recommendations for Future Testing
Test Messaging Themes That Address Emotional or Cognitive Needs
Building on the success of the “trusted filter” framing, explore other emotionally resonant themes—such as reliability, belonging, or empowerment—to identify which underlying motivations most effectively drive sign-ups.
Experiment with Messaging Hierarchy and Presentation
Evaluate how the placement, tone, and format of key messaging (e.g., headlines vs. supporting copy, minimalist vs. detailed explanations) influence conversion rates on acquisition pages.
Extend Testing Across Related Channels and Entry Points
Apply and test winning messaging strategies on other digital touchpoints—such as social media ads, on-site pop-ups, or podcast mentions—to assess consistency and scalability in email acquisition performance.

experiments.title: How donor-focused language on the Tribute Donation Page impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donor-focused language for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in Tribute giving because the shift in language prioritizes the donor and their gift as the primary actors, rather than CaringBridge as the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2025 - 07/11/2025
CaringBridge’s Tribute donation page is a primary driver of the organization’s revenue, acting as the destination when users click on one of the many Tribute widgets displayed throughout the site. While the language on the Tribute widgets is very focused on the donor and their impact, the subsequent donation page is primarily focused on the organization: “CaringBridges helps keep [Name] connected…” “You make CaringBridge…” “When you give to CaringBridge…”
We hypothesized that by updating the headline to be more donor-focused, eliminating the many uses of the org’s name, and offering more educational copy around their tribute gift and its impact, donor conversion rate would be improved.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved donor-focused language on the Tribute donation page, did not perform as well as the control, with a 7.8% decrease in Tribute Donations.

These results indicate that the donor-focused language may not have resonated as effectively with users as hoped. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further explore the impact of different messaging strategies on donor behavior. There are two points for further consideration as we intepret these initial results and consider future testing opportunities:
While the goal of the experiment was to be donor-focused, there is the potential that the headline's specificty of "Your Tribute Gift" ended up feeling more impersonal and distant. Their gift is the primary subject, rather than they themselves.
 	Additionally, users often have confusion around Tribute giving because it is a donation to CaringBridge, which then by extension ensures the site can remain active and keep users connected. Potentially, this treatment language removed the "How" to the donor's giving when it removed the use of CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How adding a quote in email copy impacts email acquisition and donor conversion with a unengaged nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a quote from VDH to the body of the email for Hoover’s unengaged nondonor audience will achieve an increase in email conversions and donations because the quote may act as a stimulating prompt, especially for readers predisposed to Hoover’s values or VDH’s worldview.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their unengaged non donor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s (VDH) latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age with a goal of reactivating them through compelling thought leadership content. The goal was to determine whether adding a VDH quote to the body of the email would increase email acquisitions and donations by leveraging name recognition and the intellectual appeal of Hanson’s thought leadership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Impact on donations:

Key learnings:
The Quote May Spark Reengagement - Among previously unengaged users, the VDH quote appears to have increased interest and action, leading to a 16.8% increase in email acquisitions and a notable lift in donations. This suggests that high-authority intellectual content can serve as a re-engagement tool when placed in the right context.
 	Curiosity and Gravitas Help Reposition Hoover’s Value - The quote likely acted as a thought-provoking hook that reframed the email’s tone—from promotional to contemplative. This could appeal to intellectually dormant segments who still identify with Hoover’s worldview but need a deeper reason to act.
 	Small Sample = Cautious Interpretation - While results are promising, especially in contrast to the flat or negative performance in donor and non-donor audiences, the sample size for donations is too low to draw firm conclusions. This underscores the need to retest at scale or over a longer window.
• Test VDH Quotes as Reengagement Tools
• Try Quote as a Subject Line or Lead -
• Segmentation Refinement

experiments.title: How a tabbed form on the main donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tabbed form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the tabbed form provides more clarity on what type of gift the user is giving, therefore reducing potential friction while giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 07/13/2025
For Time of Grace’s main donation page, we wanted to add a tabbed gift array. We previously tested this, but we made some changes to this version. The first version said “Single Gift” for one-time gifts, and also had a dark green color for the selected tabs which was a bit confusing. So for this version, we changed the copy to say “One-Time Gift” and changed the selected tab color to be the gradient green they have on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The addition of a tabbed gift array on the main donation page resulted in a 46.3% increase in transactions for all traffic, showing potential clarity on the type of gift reducing potential friction in the donation process.
 	The treatment group also saw a significant 659% increase in One-Time Donation transactions, indicating that the tabbed form may have been particularly effective for this type of donation.
 	Despite the positive results, the statistical confidence level of 92.3% for All Traffic is slightly below the 95% threshold for validity. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the tabbed gift array.
 	Future experimentation should focus on refining the tabbed gift array design based on these results, such as adjusting colors, copy, or layout to further optimize the donation experience. Additionally, conducting additional tests with a larger sample size could help validate the initial findings and provide more conclusive results.

experiments.title: How creating clarity with bullet points on the landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reinforcing the article featured in the Facebook ad on the corresponding landing page for the Hoover audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition rates because clear and consistent messaging will give users greater confidence in what they’re signing up for, creating a smoother cognitive experience and reducing uncertainty.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2025 - 07/08/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. The goal was to increase email acquisition for their Spring Digest 2025, a curated digital collection of thought-provoking articles and commentary.
The experiment focused on the landing page experience, specifically how well it reinforced the messaging from the Facebook ads driving traffic to the offer.
The control version of the landing page featured a set of general article highlights from the digest.
In contrast, the treatment version aligned the landing page content with the specific article referenced in the Facebook ad, including a featured political cartoon and title.
The premise for this test was rooted in message match—the idea that maintaining visual and verbal continuity from ad to landing page could increase trust, reduce friction, and improve conversions. This approach leverages psychological principles such as the Consistency Bias (people prefer information that aligns with what they’ve already seen) and Fluency Heuristics (easier-to-process content tends to be more persuasive).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Message Match Isn’t Always the Winner - While the treatment offered tighter alignment between the ad and landing page content, the control outperformed it in both email acquisition rate and donation volume. This suggests that too narrow a focus on a single article may inadvertently limit perceived value, especially if the highlighted article doesn’t resonate universally. Broad Appeal May Drive Higher Engagement - The control’s use of diverse article highlights may have created a stronger sense of breadth and value within the Spring Digest, increasing the appeal to a wider audience. Users may have felt they were signing up for a more comprehensive and enriching offer.High Average Gift in Treatment Signals Donor Intent - Though the treatment underperformed in email acquisition, the average gift amount was nearly 3x higher. This could suggest that users who did convert in the treatment were more primed or aligned with the specific content, possibly indicating higher intent or alignment with Hoover’s donor profile. However, since there was only 1 gift to treatment and 3 gifts to control this is not statistically significant. 4. Clarity vs. Curiosity - This experiment raises an interesting tension: should a landing page lead with clarity (specificity) or curiosity (variety)? The data implies that curiosity-based framing (control) can boost signups, while clarity-based framing (treatment) may filter for higher-value leads.
Future Testing
•	Segmentation Testing: Consider targeting high-intent users with treatment-style pages and broader audiences with control-style pages.
•	Message Framing Tests: Explore emotional vs. intellectual framing of article titles—does the tone of the content preview affect conversion?

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
The addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, there was an 8.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in terms of transactions.
 	Despite the decrease in transactions, there was a significant increase in revenue by 489% in the treatment group with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that although fewer people may have donated in the treatment group, those who did contribute gave more on average.
 	The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in transactions is 82.7%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered reliable or used for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with donor centric language affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the stickybar for website visitors will achieve an increase in clicks because having language where the donor is first and focusing on what their donation does would encourage users to click more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/27/2025
For CaringBridge, during their 2 day match in June, we wanted to test changing the language on the stickybar. This was an appeal stickybar. The language change was to put the donor “first”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the language on the stickybar to put the donor first and focus on what their donation does resulted in a 12.2% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that the revised language resonated with website visitors and motivated them to engage with the call to action.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the language to further optimize for donor engagement. Additionally, conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence would help ensure the validity of the results and the effectiveness of the changes implemented. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies based on data-driven insights to maximize impact and drive successful outcomes for Caringbridge.

experiments.title: Can Introducing a Slide-Up Prompt Donations from Prospective Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a slide-up donation prompt to the high-traffic “Ways to Listen” page will increase conversions by reaching engaged users at a moment of intent without disrupting their experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 06/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners across North Carolina. The goal was to explore whether introducing a slide-up donation prompt on the station’s “Ways to Listen” page could increase conversions.
To do this, the team developed a new web asset specifically for this high-traffic page. The slide-up was designed to engage users with a subtle, timely prompt that encourages giving—without interrupting their browsing experience. By targeting a page that typically serves an informational (rather than fundraising) purpose, the test aims to uncover a new opportunity to engage potential donors in a low-friction, high-visibility context.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Conclusion & Next Steps

The slide-up treatment increased conversions from 0.07% to 0.27%, a 297% relative lift. While the confidence level of 81.9% does not meet the threshold for statistical significance, the notable increase suggests that the slide-up is an effective way to engage visitors on the “Ways to Listen” page—a section typically focused on information rather than fundraising.

Key Learnings:
The “Ways to Listen” page presents an untapped opportunity to introduce donation asks in a non-intrusive way.
 	Visitors on this page are open to engaging with donation prompts when presented subtly through slide-ups.
 	Even a simple slide-up design can significantly improve conversion rates over no prompt.
Recommendations for Next Steps:
Design Test: Introduce a more humanized slide-up featuring a photo of a popular WUNC host to build trust and emotional connection.
 	Messaging Test: Experiment with different copy that emphasizes the listener’s impact or the value of WUNC’s programming to increase motivation.
 	CTA Test: Try varying calls to action (e.g., “Support WUNC,” “Join Our Community,” “Help Keep Us On Air”) to find the most compelling phrasing.

experiments.title: How changing the call-to-action on the homepage affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the CTA on the homepage banner for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because having the CTA be donation focused would give clarity and encourage users to give a donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2025 - 05/02/2025
Every month, Time of Grace has a banner on their homepage of the premium running that month, with the CTA “Get the Book”. For this experiment, we wanted to change the CTA to be more focused on donating rather than it being focused on the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA on the homepage banner to focus on donations instead of a book led to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment showed a 102.3% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with an 89.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the change was effective in encouraging users to donate.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the impact of donation-focused CTAs on user behavior and conversion rates. It would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size and over a longer period to validate the results and ensure consistency. Additionally, we can consider testing different variations of donation-focused CTAs to optimize and further enhance the impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will Defaulting to $50 monthly ask improve conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting the $50 gift amount in the gift array for the monthly donation page will achieve more conversions because by having it preselected there will be one less step for the user to do in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2025
In the past, we that having this organization break out the episodic and monthly giving pages perform better by having a ‘donate’ and ‘donate monthly’ button in the navigation bar.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could improve conversion and increase average gift on the monthly donation page by preselecting $50 in the gift array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that preselecting the $50 gift amount in the gift array for the monthly donation page did not result in more conversions. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 44.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. While this difference is not statistically significant at a 93.7% confidence level, it suggests that further testing is necessary to determine the true impact of preselecting the gift amount.

When digging into the results, we noticed that the average gift amount is $35 for this page. Coming in at $50 could have added more friction to this test because the amount was too high. In our next test, we are going to see if preselecting $25 will increase conversion.

experiments.title: How updating the messaging on a donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by updating the donation copy to focus on the value of research and impact of the donor’s support for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in donations because it better aligns with the motivations of an intellectually curious, policy-oriented audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 04/07/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of donation page copy for one of their content offers, “Trickle Down” Theory and “Tax Cuts for the Rich” eBook. We wanted to see if we present a donation appeal that uses a rational, research-focused tone (Treatment) rather than an emotionally driven, values-focused tone (Control), then we will see an increase in donation conversion rate because it better aligns with the motivations of an intellectually curious, policy-oriented audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Takeaways:
Control outperformed Treatment in both conversion rate and average gift size.The emotional, values-based message appears to resonate more with this audience than the rational, research-focused appeal.While the decrease in conversion rate (-19.5%) is directionally meaningful, the confidence level (83.6%) does not meet the 95% threshold required for statistical significance.The average gift size also declined significantly in the treatment, suggesting not only fewer donors, but potentially less engaged or less motivated ones.
Future Testing Ideas:
Combine the data-driven credibility of the Treatment with the emotional resonance of the Control.Test a hybrid version that emphasizes the research but still uses inspiring language and appeals to personal legacy, patriotism, or civic duty.Break down the test further:Test just the headline change.Test impact of bullet points vs. paragraph style.Try swapping just the CTA phrasing (e.g. “Support research” vs. “Leave your mark”).

experiments.title: Does a pop-up with future framing copy generate more donations than a contrast effect copy?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believed that emphasizing urgency and the challenges ahead would better motivate donations than the paywall-focused message, as it could create a stronger emotional incentive and sense of immediacy for users.
Psychological Framing
This test compared two psychological tactics:
Future Framing (Test): Highlights what lies ahead—upcoming threats, opportunities, or needs. This approach encourages users to act now to influence future outcomes.
Contrast Effect (Control): Draws attention to what users currently have (free access) and implies a risk of losing it. This technique relies on juxtaposing the current benefit with the potential consequences of inaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2025 - 04/01/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of homepage pop-up messaging on donor acquisition. The goal was to determine whether leaning into urgency and future challenges would outperform messaging focused on WUNC’s lack of a paywall.

experiments.title: How an impact pop-up Dear Reader message affects donor conversion on article pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that displaying the “Dear Reader” message as an impact pop-up rather than at the end of the article will increase donor conversion rates by capturing attention earlier and making the donation ask more immediate and visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/26/2025
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life and fostering lasting, healthy relationships.​
In previous campaigns, FamilyLife has utilized “Dear Reader” messages at the end of article pages—a tactic that has proven successful in increasing donation rates. To build upon this success, we are testing an alternative approach: implementing an impact pop-up “Dear Reader” message that appears shortly after the page loads, eliminating the need for users to scroll to the end of the article. Our hypothesis is that the immediacy and visibility of the pop-up will capture users’ attention more effectively, thereby increasing the likelihood of securing donations.​
For this test, we are promoting the devotional “You’re Going to Make It” by Lysa TerKeurst, a resource that has demonstrated strong performance in FamilyLife’s Facebook advertising campaigns. The experiment is structured as a 50/50 split: half of the site traffic encounters the traditional end-of-article “Dear Reader” message (Control), while the other half experiences the impact pop-up “Dear Reader” message (Treatment).
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 30 days, from February 26 to March 26. While the results did not reach statistical validity, the impact pop-up "Dear Reader" message (Treatment) showed a positive trend, leading to a 49.5% increase in donor conversion rate with an 85.9% level of confidence compared to the end-of-article "Dear Reader" message (Control).

Although the overall conversion rate remained low, the pop-up format demonstrated a higher engagement potential, suggesting that making the donation ask more immediate and visible may help drive conversions.

Next Steps: Since the results were not statistically valid, retesting this approach in different contexts—such as other article pages, adjusting pop-up timing, or refining the message—could help validate the learning. Further optimizations to the design or placement of the pop-up may also enhance its effectiveness in future tests.

experiments.title: Does changing the headline language on a donation form impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headings to a question for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/15/2024
This test explored the psychological impact of changing the headings for the steps on the donation form. The goal was to increase conversion and average gift by creating a sense of empowerment for the visitor through phrasing the headings as questions.
Our hypothesis was that changing the headings on the donation form will increase donor conversion. This is because when users are presented with a softer, more personalized heading, they are more likely to engage, and ultimately convert. Changing the headings to a question provides the user with more sense of control during their interaction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the headings on the donation form to questions did not result in an increase in donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the data and consider the statistical significance of the results before making any conclusions or changes based on an experiment. It may also be beneficial to test different variations of the headings or other elements of the donation form to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, considering factors such as the wording, tone, and placement of elements on the form could also impact donor conversion rates.

Overall, it is essential to approach experimentation with a mindset of continuous learning and improvement, using data-driven insights to inform decision-making and optimize the donation process for the best results.

experiments.title: How removing the message field from the tribute widget for single page posts affects Tribute donations

rows.name: Remove message field from tribute widget

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the message field from Tribute widget for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donations because removing the field would reduce confusion and friction and improve clarity of the widget.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2025 - 03/25/2025
For this experiment, we wanted to remove the message field from the tribute widget to see if it would lead to an increase in tribute donations. The message field allowed a user to send a tribute message along with their donation. We wanted to remove the field to see if that would reduce some confusion among users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing the message field from the tribute widget led to a decrease in donations. Despite the decrease, the results do not have a high level of statistical confidence, which indicates that further testing would be needed to confirm these findings.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of removing certain features from a widget. While the hypothesis suggested that removing the message field would reduce confusion and friction, it appears that this feature may have actually encouraged donations from users.

When designing future experiments, it is important to thoroughly analyze the potential impact of changes and to ensure that the results are statistically significant before drawing conclusions. Additionally, considering the preferences and behaviors of users is crucial in determining the effectiveness of different features in donation widgets.

experiments.title: Should asks for the content pages be premium focused?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having the ask on the content pages be similar to the homepage for the Spring Share campaign will achieve more transactions because there will be continuity between the asks on the site, which can lead to more trust and credibility.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2025
Moody Radio is running a Spring Share campaign that drives a ton of traffic to their website from on-air, paid traffic, and social.
Earlier in the campaign, we tested how an ask on the content pages should look with a premium.
For this test, we wanted to see if presenting the ask with the premium being the focus is better at converting donors, or if presenting the ask similar to how we are on the homepage would be better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that presenting the ask on content pages differently from the homepage for the Spring Share campaign resulted in a 20.6% decrease in donations. Although the results did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95%, the trend indicates that the control (current homepage format) performed better than the treatment (focus on premium).

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the potential impact of changing the format of the ask on different parts of the website. It is clear that continuity and consistency in messaging across different pages can play a crucial role in building trust and credibility with donors. Therefore, it may be beneficial to maintain a similar approach to the ask throughout the website to ensure a unified donor experience.

Further testing is recommended to validate these findings and to explore other factors that may influence donation conversions on content pages. Adjusting the format of the ask, testing different premiums, or even considering the timing of the ask in the donor journey could all be valuable areas for future experimentation to optimize donation conversions for the Spring Share campaign on Moody Radio's website.

experiments.title: Will adding value proposition to the donation button increase conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for the homepage donate button will achieve more transactions because the added value proposition will reinforce the ‘why’.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could optimize the ‘Donate Now’ button that is on their homepage. To do so, we added some value prop language inside the button itself that will help reinforce the the ‘why’ to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition to the homepage donate button actually led to a decrease in donations, contrary to the initial hypothesis. However, the results are not statistically significant with a confidence level of 80.3%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

Moving forward, it is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the value proposition language before drawing any conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that future experiments reach a statistically significant level of confidence (at least 95%) in order to make informed decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Does adding a video slide-out to a landing page increase instant donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that by adding a small slide-out video to the landing page—mirroring the ad experience that likely brought users there—we could increase donor conversion rates by creating a more consistent, engaging, and compelling user journey.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/24/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The goal was to assess whether a video slide-out on the landing page of a popular content offer would improve donor conversion rates. The feature mimicked the ad experience and was only shown to desktop users.

experiments.title: Will adding the value proposition to the mobile number field harm conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition under the mobile number field for the main episodic donation page form will achieve more transactions because the added value proposition will lead into trust, transparency and credibility with the organization.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of phone numbers that donors are giving us on the episodic donation page by adding some value proposition copy under it. In doing so, we also wanted to see if by adding the copy, it could end up increasing trust and transparency within the organization and result in more transactions.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition under the mobile number field for the main episodic donation page did not yield the intended results. In fact, there was a significant decrease in donations for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 85.6%. This indicates that the control version without the added value proposition performed better in terms of transactions.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of any changes made to donation pages before implementing them broadly. It is also crucial to conduct thorough testing and analysis to ensure that any modifications do not have a negative impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different value propositions or messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with donors.

Further testing is recommended to validate the results of this experiment and to gain a better understanding of the factors that influence donor behavior on the donation page. By conducting additional experiments and gathering more data, Care Net can make informed decisions about how to optimize their donation pages for increased donations and donor trust in the future.

experiments.title: How a shorter and more conversational housefile email impacted donations and email acquisitions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a shorter and more conversational emaill for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in donations because it will improve engagement and enhance clarity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out a NEW offer, “Federal Entitlement Programs”. This specific email test was to their donor audience.
These were some key differences between the copy:
Conciseness – The treatment is shorter and more direct, removing the detailed breakdown of course modules.
Engagement – The treatment uses a more conversational tone with rhetorical questions (“Why does this matter?”) to draw the reader in.
Clarity & Readability – The treatment simplifies complex ideas, avoiding dense phrasing like “glacially slow economic growth”.
Urgency & Impact – The treatment highlights the real-world consequences of entitlement spending earlier and more clearly.
Call to Action (CTA) – The treatment places the course link in a more prominent, action-driven sentence.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Shorter Emails Didn’t Increase Donations
The treatment email, while more concise and conversational, resulted in fewer donations (27 vs. 42) and fewer acquired emails (138 vs. 209) than the control.Next Step: Analyze why donations dropped—did the shorter format remove persuasive elements that resonated with donors?
Audience May Prefer More Detail
The control email provided a structured breakdown of the course, which may have been more compelling for Hoover’s donor audience.Next Step: Test a hybrid email that keeps the engaging tone of the treatment while reintroducing key persuasive elements from the control.
CTA Placement and Wording Could Be Optimized
The placement and clarity of the donation ask might have impacted conversions.Next Step: Experiment with stronger, more direct CTA wording and placement to drive more action.
Consider Multi-Touch Campaigns
A single email might not be enough to drive engagement and donations.Next Step: Explore follow-up emails, retargeting, or landing pages to reinforce the message and increase conversions.

experiments.title: Will adding a donor testimonal to a donation page help conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial to the donation pages for the spring fundraiser campaign will achieve more conversions on the page because the added donor testimonial will include social proof on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
Right now, KCBI is in the middle of its annual Spring Fundraiser. This campaign drives traffic to the campaign donation pages more than the donation pages would receive outside of an on-air campaign.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase conversions on the donation page by adding a testimonial to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor testimonial to the donation pages for the spring fundraiser campaign resulted in a 35.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. Although the results were not statistically significant at a 95% confidence level, the 94.5% confidence level suggests that the trend towards decreased conversions is strong.

In future experimentation, it is important to further test the impact of adding donor testimonials to donation pages before fully implementing this change. It might be beneficial to explore different types of testimonials, placement on the page, or the content of the testimonial itself to see if there is a way to present social proof more effectively to increase conversions. Additionally, considering other factors that could have influenced the results, such as the timing of the campaign or the overall messaging, could provide more insights into how to optimize donation pages for better results.

experiments.title: How including a gift array in a high urgency campaign overlay affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array on the pop up for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it allows the user to begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/12/2025
The Navigators are running a pop-up on their website as a part of their February Sustainer campaign. They wanted to test how including a gift array on the pop up would affect donor conversion rates.
They believed that more people would choose to give once getting to the donation page because the user has already begun the process of giving by selecting how much to give.
This test ran for the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array on the pop-up for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group saw a 65.9% higher donation rate compared to the control group. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 81.9% is below the standard threshold of 95%, indicating that the results may not be entirely reliable.

Furthermore, the treatment saw a 104.1% lift in revenue at a 67.7% LOC. While neither of these metrics validated, they do suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to this addition.

It is also important to note that the gift array buttons preselected the donation page with the amount selected on the overlay. Therefore, taking one step out of the process once the user landed on the donation page which may have been perceived as less work and friction.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the gift array on donation conversion rates, ensuring a robust and reliable methodology to draw conclusive insights. It would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and different variations of the gift array to validate the initial findings. Additionally, considering factors such as donor preferences and user experience could help optimize the gift array strategy for better results in future campaigns.

experiments.title: Will adding a button on mobile donation pages impact gifts?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing the donation page for mobile users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the button will allow users to jump straight to the donation form instead of having to scroll.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2025
Moody is running their Spring Share campaign which generates a ton of traffic to their donation pages from mobile.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could make the donation page be mobile first by adding a ‘start my gift button’ at the top of the page. When clicked the button will anchor a user down to the form.
Below is what we found
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that optimizing the donation page for mobile users with a 'start my gift button' did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing donor conversions. In fact, the treatment resulted in a decrease of 18.1% in donations for all traffic compared to the control.

Based on these results, it is clear that the 'start my gift button' did not have the intended effect on donor behavior. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different strategies to optimize the donation page for mobile users. This could include exploring other design changes, such as simplifying the donation process, improving mobile responsiveness, or testing different call-to-action buttons. Additionally, conducting more rigorous testing with a larger sample size and longer testing period can help ensure more accurate and reliable results.

experiments.title: Does a specific ask perform better than a general for an offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a suggested gift amoutn on the button for the premium slide in will achieve a higher conversion rate because the donor will have less to think about if we already present them with a donation amount.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2025
Moody Radio runs a Spring Share each year that drives a ton of traffic to their website.
With the campaign launch, we promoted one of their premiums “Killing Sin”. We put the offer on a slide in for the content pages on the website. We’ve tested into this element before and we know that it works really well for the Moody audiences. For this test, we really wanted to see what would happen if we added a suggested gift amount on the button to go to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a suggested gift amount on the button for the premium slide in resulted in a 30.1% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that providing a specific donation amount can help simplify the decision-making process for donors and prompt them to take action.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the impact of suggesting gift amounts on donations. It would be beneficial to conduct further tests to validate the initial findings and determine if this strategy consistently leads to increased conversion rates. Additionally, we can experiment with different suggested gift amounts to see if certain amounts are more effective than others in driving donations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that providing a suggested gift amount can be a successful tactic for increasing donations on the website. This insight can inform future strategies for optimizing donation processes and enhancing the overall fundraising efforts of Moody Radio.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout on the general donation page impacted transactions and recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an Avila Army callout for Avila’s audience on the general donation page will achieve an increase in recurring gifts and increase transactions because highlighting the program’s mission and impact will inspire donors to see themselves as part of a dedicated community making a tangible difference.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2025 - 03/04/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether adding a banner about their recurring program, the Avila Army, impacted recurring donors and transactions. The Avila Foundation encourages people to join their recurring program to be apart of the battalion of friends and followers actively deployed in the war for souls through the monthly support of the mission of the Avila Foundation.
We wanted to see if putting information and a testimonial about this program would encourage others to sign up as well as donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Takeaways:
Recurring Callout Reduced One-Time Gifts – The Avila Army banner led to a 58.1% decrease in one-time donations, likely by distracting or overwhelming donors.
 	Minimal Impact on Recurring Giving – Only one new recurring donor was added, with low statistical confidence (68.4%), indicating the callout wasn’t compelling enough.
 	User Experience Issues – The callout might have disrupted the natural donation flow, making giving less intuitive.
Next Steps & Ideas for Promoting Recurring Gifts:
Balance One-Time & Recurring Giving – Test a softer recurring ask (e.g., an optional checkbox) rather than making it the focal point.
 	Refine Messaging – Emphasize impact, ease, and community benefits rather than duty-driven language.
 	Segment Audiences – Show recurring gift appeals only to engaged donors or past givers, rather than all visitors.
 	Test Avila Army's multiple giving levels – Offer tiered giving levels with exclusive perks (e.g., special updates, donor recognition, or impact reports) for recurring donors.
 	Use Social Proof – Feature multiple testimonials and highlight how recurring donors create lasting change.
 	Retarget One-Time Donors – Send personalized follow-ups thanking them and inviting them to upgrade to recurring.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and relevancy on the email copy on a recirculation email for donors impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and relevancy for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in donations because a direct, credible, and more streamlined approach will cause people to be more engaged.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “Leaving Socialism”. This specific email test was to their donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment conducted by the Hoover Institution for their donor audience revolved around the importance of clarity and relevancy in communication. The treatment, which aimed to provide a more direct, credible, and streamlined approach, resulted in a significant 68.2% increase in donations for all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue focusing on clarity and relevancy in communication with the donor audience. This approach seems to have a positive impact on engagement and donation rates. Additionally, conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes could help in achieving a higher level of statistical confidence and providing more reliable results.

Overall, the key takeaway is that communicating directly and clearly with donors, providing relevant information, and streamlining the approach can lead to increased engagement and support. These learnings should be applied in future experiments to optimize communication strategies and drive donor participation.

experiments.title: How should we lay out a landing page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that applying a radical redesign to the landing page for ebook offers will achieve more email conversations because the radical redesign will remove friction and increase clarity of the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/24/2025
For all moody ministries, we’ve been wondering if we’re presenting the landing page in the best way to optimize for email captures. After brainstorming, we decided that we wanted to try a radical redesign test to get rid of the footer, move the logo to the middle of the page, shorten the copy, and add an image of the ebook. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the radical redesign of the landing page for ebook offers resulted in a 12.8% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. This indicates that the changes made in the treatment version may have had a positive impact on attracting more email conversions.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to further validate these findings. By continuing to test different variations of the landing page, we can gather more data to determine if the radical redesign consistently leads to higher email conversions. Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand why the treatment version performed better and make informed decisions for future optimizations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that simplifying the landing page and focusing on key elements such as the ebook image can potentially enhance the user experience and encourage more email sign-ups. This information can guide future strategies for improving email capture rates on landing pages for Moody Radio ministries.

experiments.title: How a change in layout impacts engagement on a donation landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic will achieve a higher engagement with the site because the items are easier to view with less scrolling.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/25/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go when they click the main donation button the PTV website.
We decided to test a grid layout with the hypothesis that the grid layout presents multiple viewing opportunities with less scrolling
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic resulted in a 19.7% decrease in donations for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence was at 92.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the grid layout on engagement with the site.

In addition, we saw:
11% decrease in time spent on the site for treatment
 	an increase from 1 to 6 Pathway Partner (recurring gifts) between control and treatment

experiments.title: How shortening the length of landing page copy impacted email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a shorter landing page, with less content and a more prominent form placement, will lead to an increase in email sign-ups by reducing friction and making it easier for visitors to find and complete the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary created an ebook on the book of Romans to engage their audience and drive sign-ups. The team wanted to test whether a shorter content offer landing page would result in more sign-ups by reducing friction for visitors, particularly by eliminating the need for them to scroll down to find the sign-up form.
The Control landing page featured a longer length, with detailed content above the fold and additional sections below the form. The Treatment landing page was shortened, focusing more succinctly on the value proposition and the sign-up form without as much content above and below.
Our hypothesis was that reducing the length of the page would reduce friction and increase sign-up conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test, with 50% of traffic seeing the longer Control page, and the other 50% seeing the shorter Treatment page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test demonstrated that a shorter landing page led to a 37.1% increase in email sign-ups, though the statistical confidence of 92.2% means the result is promising but not fully validated. Several factors may explain this outcome:
Reduced Cognitive Load & Friction: By simplifying the landing page and minimizing content above and below the form, we reduced the need for visitors to process excessive information, leading them to more easily take the desired action.
 	Clearer Path to Action: With less content to distract from the call to action, the sign-up form became the clear focus of the page, potentially prompting more visitors to complete the form.
 	User Behavior: Shorter pages can align with the behavior of web visitors who may be less patient or more likely to convert when they feel they are not overwhelmed with excessive information.
further testing with other variations of page length and content may lead to even higher conversion rates

experiments.title: Will a personalized dear reader perform better than a non personalized?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a personalized ask for the dear reader on the content page will achieve a higher donor conversion because the ask is more humanized and they can associate it with the editor that they see writing them everyday.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
In previous campaigns we’ve seen dear readers work remarkable well for TW. For this test we wanted to build off of our previous tests to find the best way to present a dear reader to their audience.
In this case, we wanted to see if having a personalized ask for the editor would improve conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that having a personalized ask for the editor in the content page did not result in an increase in donor conversion rates as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze why the personalized ask did not resonate with the audience as expected. It may be beneficial to conduct further research to better understand the preferences and motivations of the audience before implementing personalized asks. Additionally, it is essential to always run experiments to statistically significant levels of confidence (95% or higher) to ensure the results are reliable.

Moving forward, it may be valuable to test different types of personalization or messaging to see what resonates best with donors. By learning from this experiment and making adjustments based on the findings, we can continue to optimize our strategies for increasing donor conversions in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How sending “View Full Post” traffic to the single-post page impacts tribute giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending “View Full Post” traffic to a single-post page will help improve the user-experience while also increasing traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask—increasing tribute giving and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 02/17/2025
In the current experience, longer posts from an author are shortened with a hyperlink “Show More” which then expands the full post. From a UI perspective, there are limitations to this experience. In mobile especially (where a majority of CB’s traffic originates from) the full post expanding in feed can overwhelm and disorient—requiring the user to scroll all the way back up or down to resume their browsing of the feed.
We hypothesize that by changing this hyperlinked copy to “View Full Post” and instead sending the traffic to the designated Single-Post Page will eliminate this friction, while also increasing Tribute giving. The single-post page contains a tribute widget (16003) that typically converts at a high volume. We believe this could accomplish both goals at once—making the experience more user-friendly while also sending more traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To understand the key learnings from this experiment we must first differentiate two tracked metrics, that appear very similar at first glance. The first is Tribute Donations/Revenue (From Widget). This metric tracks tribute gifts only on the single-post page—with the 16003 code. This was important to analyze because ultimately it was a large part of our hypothesis: sending more traffic to single-post page will increase gifts to the widget on this page.

However, there is a more encompassing Tribute Gift metric that was measured in this test as well. Because users can technically make a tribute gift on an author's page to various different widgets as well as the donate button in navigation—tracking this metric was equally important. It would answer a key question: are we simply directing traffic to give on a different widget, or does this lead to an overall lift in gifts and revenue as well.

With this in mind, here are some key callouts from this experiment.
The click rate was about the same between "Show More" and "View Full Post." The changed copy did not necessarily mean users were more likely to expand the post.
 	There was a significant 1376.9% increase in donations to Tribute (Widget/16003) in the treatment with a 100% level of confidence. Confirming part of our hypothesis that by increasing traffic to the single-post page, we would increase donations to the 16003 Tribute Widget.
 	However, pulling back to analyze all Tribute Gifts and Revenue shows that the treatment seems to also result in a decrease overall. This is not statistically valid at an 80% level of confidence, however, the trend did remain consistent throughout the entirety of the test run. This could indicate something very important.
overall

experiments.title: How a redesigned “Post” modal impacts journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction in the modal itself versus directing more traffic to the modal for authors will achieve an increase in journal posts because it helps address the point in the funnel where authors are abandoning the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2025 - 02/07/2025
In a series of tests to improve Author journal posts, we had largely focused our efforts at the top of the funnel. We presented tests that primarily removed friction at the start: a prompt card in their feed, ghost text options underscoring that posts could be simple updates, etc. In this test, we hypothesized that re-working the modal itself would remove friction at the point of decision and increase journal posts. The changes in this experience were to soften the headline “How are you?,” include an encouraging subhead, ghost text options in the modal itself instead of outside the experience, and update the CTA to “Share My Post.” It was large overhaul, but one where each of the elements were cohesively under the umbrella of making the modal itself more user-friendly and encouraging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the changes made to the journal entry modal did not have the intended effect of increasing journal posts. In fact the treatment resulted in a slight decrease, however, the statistical level of confidence is below the acceptable threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further analyze the user behavior and gather feedback to understand why the changes did not resonate with authors. This can help in crafting more effective interventions in the future. It's also important to continue testing different elements at various stages of the funnel to find the most impactful ways to increase journal posts.

Because users can technically make a tribute gift on an author's page to various different widgets as well as the donate button in navigation—tracking this metric was equally important. It would answer a key question: are we simply directing traffic to give on a different widget, or does this lead to an overall lift in gifts and revenue as well.

With this in mind, here are some key callouts from this experiment.

experiments.title: Will adding a heart to the monthly tab increase monthly transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form will achieve a higher number of recurring transactions because the heart will the users eyes to the monthly tab and lead them to giving a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/18/2025
In the past we have tested adding different hearts to elements to catch the users eye and get them to take the action that we are wanting.
In this case, we wanted to see if we could add a heart on the monthly tab on the donation form would increase the number of monthly transactions.
We ran this test on the main sustainer donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a heart on the monthly tab for the sustainer donation page giving form resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 69.4% decrease in recurring transactions with a 92.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better in this case.

While the results are not statistically significant at the usual 95% confidence level, they are approaching validity, suggesting that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the heart icon on user behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of visual elements like hearts on donation forms, as they may not always lead to the desired outcome. It would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with different visual cues or design elements to see which ones are most effective in increasing recurring transactions. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand the reasons behind the results and make informed decisions for future optimization efforts.

experiments.title: Is a graphic CTA better than a dear reader for non campaign season asks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing the graphic cta with a dear reader for the content pages cta will achieve more transactions because the dear reader is clearer to understand what the cta is and offers additional value proposition that can not be replaced by a graphic.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2025 - 02/18/2025
For campaign seasons we’ve found that including a dear reader on the content pages significantly increases transactions and revenue. We wondered if we could use this same tactic during noncampaign seasons.
For this test, we replaced a content offer graphic with a dear reader to see if we could increase overall transactions and revenue. Below is what we found
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that replacing a graphic call-to-action (CTA) with a dear reader on content pages resulted in a 21.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with an 88.3% level of statistical confidence. This indicates that the dear reader CTA was clearer and offered additional value proposition that resonated with users.

Moreover, there was a significant 319% increase in Transactions - One-Time, with a high level of confidence at 96%. This suggests that the dear reader CTA was particularly effective in driving one-time transactions.

Moving forward, it is important to note that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to validate the findings. The learnings from this experiment can be used in future experimentation by continuing to test the effectiveness of different CTAs, including dear reader messages, to optimize conversion rates and revenue. By iterating on these tests and analyzing the results, we can continue to refine our strategies and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience on the donation page increase donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy to have a guided reading experience for the main episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the copy will be laid out in an easy to understand manner that puts the donor as the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/29/2025
In the past, we have tested adding a guided reading experience to donation pages during a campaign.. We have found that it can be helpful to help guide readers down the page in an easy-to-understand way.
We wondered if we could find this same result during a non campaign season. For this test we adjusted the donation page copy to lay out the problem, solution, and how the individual donor can make a difference. We ran this test on the main episodic donation page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the guided reading experience on the main episodic donation page did not lead to an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment actually saw a decrease in transactions by 14.0% compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 81.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making. In the future, it may be beneficial to continue testing different approaches to optimize the donation page, keeping in mind the importance of clear and easy-to-understand messaging for donors. It is also advisable to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How updating the value prop on a donation page with urgency and relevancy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and relevancy to the value proposition for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in the instant donation coversion because there will be more urgency and relevancy making the donor feel more connected to Hoover’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society.
Our hypothesis was that if we update the instant donation form copy to be more related to an immediate need, then the urgency will cause an increase in the instant donation conversion. If we update the value proposition with urgency and relevancy to the times it will encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the instant donation form copy to include urgency and relevancy to a current need resulted in a significant increase in instant donation conversions for the Hoover Institution. The treatment group experienced a 42.9% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 87.7%.

Why the Test May Have Won:
Stronger Immediate Relevance & Urgency: 
 	The test opens with, "Before you go, we’d like to ask you one more thing," which feels more immediate and action-oriented than the control’s "While you wait for it to arrive."
 	It emphasizes the present need for scholars like Thomas Sowell to continue their work, connecting the ask to an ongoing, timely cause.
 	Clearer Value Proposition:
 	The test provides concrete examples of what donations will support: "Examine the causes of current crises," "Enable our team of expert scholars," and "Advance a strategic communications program."
 	The control remains more abstract, focusing broadly on "data-driven research" and "an educated citizenry."
 	More Compelling Call to Action:
 	The test includes a clear call to action: "Will you consider making a tax-deductible gift to the Hoover Institution today?" followed by bullet points that break down the impact of a gift.
 	The control’s call to action feels less direct and more passive with phrases like, "Would you partner with us?"
 	Focus on Impact Over Philosophy:
 	The test copy shifts the focus toward what Hoover does and the tangible outcomes of its work.
 	The control talks more about philosophical beliefs, like "the importance of an educated citizenry" and "the American way of life."

experiments.title: How does sending voicemails during a high-urgency campaign affect donor response?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding voicemails to a multichannel high-urgency campaign for active donors will achieve a higher average gift because of the added personal touch points, relationship cultivation, and sense of ownership that a donor will feel.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2024 - 12/31/2024
Human Coalition is interested in expanding their multichannel approach to fundraising, and decided to test the effectiveness of sending voicemails to a portion of their active donor list during a high urgency campaign.
The control group received the standard campaign emails and Facebook ads.
The treatment group received the same communication as the control group along with a series of 4 voicemails that were delivered through Slybroadcast over a period of 4 weeks. The content of these voicemails mirrored the content of emails in the campaign and were most often sent from the same person.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding voicemails to a multichannel high-urgency campaign for active donors can lead to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment group, which received voicemails in addition to the standard campaign emails and Facebook ads, saw a 60.9% increase in revenue compared to the control group. Further analysis of the data showed that the treatment group also increased the number of donations by 37% at a 98% level of confidence.
This result suggests that the personal touch points, relationship cultivation, and sense of ownership that voicemails provide can have a positive impact on donor engagement and giving behavior. While the statistical level of confidence is below 95% for increasing revenue, the strong results warrant further testing of this strategy.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of adding voicemails to multichannel fundraising campaigns, perhaps with variations in the timing, frequency, or content of the voicemails. Additionally, collecting qualitative feedback from donors who received the voicemails could provide insights into why they may have been more effective in driving donations. Overall, this experiment highlights the potential of using voicemails as a valuable tool in donor communication and fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: Does adding recency and social proof on a landing page impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the amount of downloads from this month for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it suggests recent ongoing popularity and drives urgency so people will feel like they don’t want to miss out. It also might feel more believable as a smaller number rather than a marketing exaggeration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/05/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on the landing page for the offer, Dueling Populisms, focusing specifically on its effect on email acquisition. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer overall VS the number of downloads this month to determine which messaging could encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment:

1. Social proof language that highlights recent activity can have a positive impact on email acquisition. The treatment version, which featured the number of downloads for the current month, resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisition compared to the control version.

2. The treatment version created a sense of urgency and exclusivity, making the offer more compelling to potential donors.

3. While the results show promise, further testing is recommended to validate the effectiveness of using recent download numbers as social proof.

How to use what was learned in future experimentation:

1. Continue to test different types of social proof language to see what resonates best with potential donors. This could include not just recent activity, but also other forms of social proof such as testimonials or reviews.

2. Consider incorporating elements of urgency and exclusivity in future messaging to increase engagement and drive action.

3. Use this experiment as a foundation for further optimization of landing pages and donation offers, keeping in mind the potential impact of social proof on donor behavior.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor testimonial work on an evergreen donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonal for the goaa/soaa donation page during CYE will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”. because  having the donor testimonal right next to the point of decision will reinforce the ‘need’ and the ‘why”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/05/2025
For CYE this year, we wanted to see if adding a right rail donor testimonial on the donation pages would make an impact on transactions. We wanted to test this on all three major donation pages for Focus, the main campaign episodic giving page, the main campaign recurring giving page, and the GOAA/SOAA broadcast pages that generate a ton of traffic each day.
For this test, we wanted to see how adding the testimonal on the the GOAA/SOAA donation pages would impact conversion. We thought this would be neat to see more of an ‘evergreen’ type of donation page even during a high urgency campaign
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the GOAA/SOAA donation page during CYE did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 15.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.8%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to test different types of donor testimonials or placement of the testimonials on the page to see if there are any variations that could lead to increased conversions. It is also important to take into consideration the timing of the experiment and the specific campaign or context in which it is being conducted to ensure that the results are relevant and applicable.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal sticky bar to the donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar to the sohl donation page for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar that contains a donor goal would increase the appeal to give as well as convey the message that we’re wanting to send to the users. By adding a sticky bar on the family donation page we found that it will increase conversions. Below is what we found for the SOHL audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar containing a donor goal to the sohl donation page for the CYE campaign resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic.
