However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests or experiments to confirm the effectiveness of adding a sticky bar to the donation page. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other strategies or elements that could potentially increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday” donation page impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned “holiday appeal” donation page will increase donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce the 2-day match with additional value prop, while also tying the email into the larger December year-end goals.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, traffic from the email appeal is typically sent to a simplified form that is similar to the length of a general donation page – with match language added. We hypothesized that a longer, “holiday appeal” donation page that aligns with the overall campaign goals communicated in email and reinforces the 2-day match alongside the larger year-end goals will increase conversion rate
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading email appeal traffic to an aligned "holiday appeal" donation page can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 42.5% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the additional value proposition and alignment with the 2-day match and larger year-end goals had a positive impact on donor behavior.

However, the statistical level of confidence was 91.6%, which is just below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for validity. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is needed for validity.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of leading email traffic to specialized donation pages tailored to specific campaigns or appeals. This experiment suggests that aligning the donation page with the messaging and goals of the email appeal can lead to increased donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How $100 Prefill vs a $100 Pre-Select in the Array Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $100 prefill for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because whereas a 3-option array seemed to introduce friction, an open $100 prefill accomplished the desired impact on average gift without introducing more options for the user to choose between..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2024 - 01/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page, $100 is the default pre-selected amount. However, how that number has been presented to the user has varied. The standard practice has been to have no gift array, but simply an open field with $100 prefilled. Alternatively, some pages utilized an array of three options with $100 as the preselected amount. It is worth noting that currently, neither of these options were present on the CaringBridge main donation page due to a migration glitch. We hypothesized that when testing between the two options, an open field with $100 prefilled (the way it was originally intended) would improve donor conversion over a 3-option array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment of using an open field with a $100 prefill on the main donation page resulted in a 35.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 92.0%, indicating a high likelihood that the increase in revenue was due to the treatment and not random chance. Furthermore, the hypothesis that a $100 prefill would improve donor conversion rates compared to a 3-option array was supported by the results of the experiment.

Ultimately, these results help enforce two next steps for action items. 1. That CaringBridge should consider testing their other donation pages which have a gift array by default, to see whether an open field pre-fill can also improve conversion rate. 2. CaringBridge should prioritize correcting the current main donation page so that the $100 prefill is represented, rather than the current blank open field (0 is the prefill)

experiments.title: How donor autonomy language on active donor impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor autonomy language in our fundraising emails targeted at the active donor segment will lead to an increase in donations. By emphasizing the donor’s sense of choice and control, we expect to empower recipients, making them feel more confident and motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted in partnership with WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The primary goal of this study was to explore the impact of donor autonomy language on behavior and engagement.
The experiment focused on a series of five emails sent to an active segment of WUNC’s donor base. Each email aimed to evaluate whether emphasizing donor autonomy—language that reinforces a donor’s sense of choice and control over their giving—would influence donation rates and overall responsiveness.
To measure the effect, we created two variations of the emails. The control group received messages without any autonomy-focused language, while the test group received emails that intentionally incorporated phrases designed to highlight donor choice and empowerment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment demonstrated that incorporating donor autonomy language did not lead to improved conversion rates among active donors. Despite both the control and treatment emails generating the same number of clicks (42 each), the control group consistently outperformed the treatment group in donation conversions, achieving a 0.44% conversion rate compared to the treatment’s 0.26% conversion rate—a 40.4% decrease.

The control group saw higher performance in all email sends except for the third email (as seen above), where the treatment briefly outperformed the control with 6 donations compared to 3. However, this isolated success was not enough to offset the overall underperformance of the treatment.

These findings suggest that donor autonomy language, as tested in this campaign, may not resonate as strongly with active donors and could potentially detract from conversion effectiveness in this context. Further testing may be required to refine the messaging approach or explore other engagement strategies.

experiments.title: How Donor Autonomy Language on Lapsed Donor Impacts Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor autonomy language in fundraising emails targeted at a lapsed donor segment will re-engage recipients by emphasizing their sense of choice and control. This approach is expected to make lapsed donors feel more empowered and valued, ultimately increasing donation conversions and encouraging them to renew their support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted in partnership with WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The primary goal of this study was to explore the impact of donor autonomy language on behavior and engagement.
The experiment focused on a series of five emails sent to a lapsed segment of WUNC’s donor base. Each email aimed to evaluate whether emphasizing donor autonomy—language that reinforces a donor’s sense of choice and control over their giving—would influence donation rates and overall responsiveness.
To measure the effect, we created two variations of the emails. The control group received messages without any autonomy-focused language, while the test group received emails that intentionally incorporated phrases designed to highlight donor choice and empowerment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the lapsed donor segment, incorporating donor autonomy language in the treatment emails resulted in a significant improvement in conversion rates compared to the control. While the control group had a conversion rate of 0.01%, the treatment group achieved 0.07%—a 398.4% relative increase with 89.7% confidence.

Although the control group generated slightly more clicks (44 vs. 39), the treatment group consistently outperformed the control in donations across all five emails. This suggests that donor autonomy language may be more effective at re-engaging lapsed donors, potentially resonating with their desire for choice and control as they consider renewing their support.

These results highlight an opportunity to further explore and optimize autonomy-driven messaging for reactivating lapsed donors.

experiments.title: How a multiple choice mission-focused question on an email affects the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Using a support directed, mission-focused multiple choice question during a calendar year-end campaign will increase the conversion rate because they will read about the many ways the organization works towards their mission and the resources needed to accomplish them all.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible.
We ran this test during their calendar year-end campaign. Traffic came to this page through email. We sent the email to everyone on their house file.
We wanted to know if more people would donate after presented with four mission-focused and specific answers. The control email had a yes or no question, focusing only on one aspect of the DTS mission.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
1. The use of a support-directed, mission-focused multiple choice question during a calendar year-end campaign resulted in a significant increase in donations for Dallas Theological Seminary.
2. The treatment group, which was presented with four mission-focused and specific answers, experienced a 137.9% increase in donations compared to the control group.
3. The statistical level of confidence was 92.8%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the treatment.
Future Implications:
1. Incorporating multiple mission-focused options in donation appeals can potentially improve the effectiveness of fundraising campaigns.
2. Future experimentation should continue to explore different ways of presenting the organization's mission and impact to donors to maximize donation outcomes.
3. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied messaging strategies can help validate the initial findings and provide more robust insights for future campaign optimization.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to the top of a donation page reinforce the need for users to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a sticky bar that has specific donor goal language for the monthly family campaign giving page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar to the campaign donation page that contains donor goal language would increase the donor conversion rate on the page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a sticky bar with specific donor goal language to the monthly family campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic by 397.1% with a 91.0% level of confidence.

2. There was also a 48% increase in Transactions - One-Time with a 96% level of confidence.

3. While the results were positive, the statistical confidence level of 91.0% for Revenue for All Traffic falls slightly below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

4. Further testing is recommended to confirm the effectiveness of the sticky bar in increasing donor conversion rates.

5. Future experimentation should focus on refining the donor goal language in the sticky bar, testing different designs or placements, and running the experiment for a longer duration to ensure reliable results.

6. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies to optimize donor conversion rates and maximize fundraising efforts for the nonprofit "Focus on the Family".

experiments.title: What message resonates more “give hope” or “deliver hope”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy from “giving hope” to “deliver hope” for the homepage wiki header will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because while the language is slightly different the tangibility between deliver and give is significant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/21/2024
Each high-urgency campaign FOTF pushes a wiki header on their homepage. When the campaign first started we wanted to make sure that the language that we used in it was the most optimized that it could be going into our biggest testing season. For this we wanted to see if “give hope” or “deliver hope” resonates more with their audience. While the actual message itself is very similar, we were able to see a massive difference when it comes to making a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy from "giving hope" to "deliver hope" for the homepage wiki header resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic, specifically a 134.6% increase. However, the statistical confidence level of 87.8% falls slightly below the typical threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different language variations to validate the initial positive outcome. Additionally, considering the notable increase in revenue seen with the change from "giving hope" to "deliver hope," it might be beneficial to explore further tests that focus on emphasizing the tangible impact and action-oriented language to drive donor conversion rates. Ultimately, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and refining messaging to maximize effectiveness in engaging with the audience and achieving campaign goals.

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience improve donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by arranging our giving page copy in a problem/solution format for the calendar year end campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donation page will be easier to read and understand the impact that the donor makes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2024 - 12/27/2024
For our year-end campaign, we wanted to explore whether presenting the donation message in a problem/solution format would help donors feel a stronger connection to the impact of their calendar year-end gift. Specifically, we aimed to see if framing the need for support in terms of how donations directly provide a solution to more readers with the valuable materials and guidance offered by Today in the Word would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a guided reading experience to the donation page resulted in a 28.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was at 83.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should be tested further during another campaign or during a season without a campaign.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions. It might be worth revisiting the guided reading experience concept in future tests, possibly with adjustments or improvements, to see if it consistently leads to increased donor conversions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to continue experimenting with different elements on the donation page to optimize for the highest conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the revenue goal thermometer  for The Navigators CYE Donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page. because  it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To improve donor conversion rates during The Navigators’ year-end campaign, we conducted a test by adding a goal thermometer to the donation page.
The experiment ran from December 27 to December 31, 2025, with 50% of site traffic exposed to the treatment.
Our hypothesis was that the thermometer would positively influence donor conversion by serving as social proof, signaling that others are contributing and encouraging more people to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a goal thermometer to the donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates for The Navigators' year-end campaign. In fact, there was a 5.1% decrease in donations for all traffic during the experiment period.
Despite these results, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for making valid conclusions. As a result, these results should not be considered conclusive or used for future decision making but they do suggest that The Navigator's audience had a neutral reaction to the goal thermometer.
Moving forward, it is important to understand that not all experiments will yield the expected results, and it is essential to carefully evaluate the data and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations or factors to determine what truly influences donor conversion rates for The Navigators. Additionally, considering factors such as timing, audience segments, and messaging may also provide valuable insights for optimizing donation campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Lower Third Ad vs a Sticky Bar Perform Better on the Today in the Word Homepage During Calendar Year End?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a lower third ad for calendar year end on the homepage will achieve greater donor conversion because we’re introducing a novel web element that contains more gift options to interact with during critical giving days of the year.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2024 - 01/01/2025
During campaigns for Today in the Word, we often use a sticky bar message on the homepage to iterate a need and a campaign goal. After several campaigns, the sticky bar can get a little stale after several uses. We want to know what would happen if we introduced a lower third gift banner that scrolls up the page (vs sitting at the top) is helpful as a NEW element to interact with during a season of highly motivated giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a lower third gift banner on the homepage during critical giving days can lead to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 21.6% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. This indicates that introducing a new web element, such as a lower third ad, can capture the attention of donors and provide more gift options for interaction, ultimately boosting conversions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider the success of this experiment and use it as a basis for future experimentation. The results suggest that innovative web elements can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates, especially during crucial giving periods. Therefore, incorporating new and interactive features on the website, such as lower third banners, could potentially drive more donations in the future.

However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, further testing is recommended to confirm the validity of these results. Conducting additional experiments with different variations of web elements and analyzing their impact on donor behavior could provide more insight and help fine-tune strategies for maximizing donations during campaigns.

experiments.title: How including specific goals and stats in an email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including stats and goals for 2025 will increase click-through rate because specific and measurable outcomes demonstrate the tangible impact of a donor’s gift, making the appeal more compelling and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
This experiment involved testing email copy for The Navigators’ donor appeals in their calendar year-end campaign. The test was conducted within an email campaign sent to existing and prospective donors, comparing two variations: the Control copy, which used general language about the impact of donations, and the Treatment copy, which included specific statistics and measurable goals for 2025.
This experiment was prompted by the desire to understand whether more detailed and concrete information about The Navigators’ progress and goals would resonate more effectively with donors. The idea stemmed from the assumption that presenting measurable outcomes and future plans would create a stronger emotional and logical connection, leading to higher engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, recent donor feedback highlighted a preference for knowing the direct impact of their contributions, which further inspired the inclusion of these stats and goals in the Treatment copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including specific statistics and measurable goals for the future in donor appeals can significantly increase the click-through rate compared to using general language about the impact of donations. The Treatment copy, which contained detailed information about the organization's progress and goals for 2025, outperformed the Control copy with a 30.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with an 87% level of confidence.

Additionally, we saw the treatment group increase donor conversion rate by 27% but with only a 48% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of including specific and measurable outcomes in donor appeals to further validate these findings. It would also be valuable to explore how different types of statistics and goals resonate with donors, as well as the optimal way to present this information for maximum effectiveness. Additionally, considering donor feedback and preferences when crafting email copy and appeals can help tailor messages to better meet their expectations and motivations for giving.

Overall, these results suggest that donors respond positively to concrete data and future plans, indicating that this strategy may enhance donor engagement and support for The Navigators. Further experimentation and refinement of this approach can help optimize future fundraising efforts and drive continued success in connecting with donors.

experiments.title: How including relevant testimonials on the offer landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including participant testimonials for Jan 1 Scripture Memory Sign-up landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because sharing a positive experience from others will build more trust and confidence in the value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2024 - 12/30/2024
Each month The Navigators run a Scripture Memory Challenge via text. Before each campaign, we promote the next months challenge via Facebook ads, housefile emails invites, and text invites. This challenges have been very popular among their audience and their staff has had a lot of positive feedback about them. Therefore, we tested adding participant testimonials to the landing page for our December/January 2025 Challenge.
These testimonials were presented like a carousel that rotated through 7 different quotes every couple of seconds.
Our hypothesis was that adding social proof to the landing page in the form of testimonials would lead to more people signing up for this challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including participant testimonials on the landing page for The Navigators' Jan 1 Scripture Memory Challenge did not result in the expected increase in email acquisition. In fact, the treatment group saw a 4.7% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. While the results were statistically significant at a 92.3% confidence level, they did not meet the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

This indicates that the addition of participant testimonials may not have had the intended effect of building more trust and confidence in the offer. It is important to note that further testing would be necessary to confirm these results and understand the true impact of participant testimonials on email acquisition.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of participant testimonials in different formats or with different messaging. Additionally, considering other factors that may influence email acquisition, such as the landing page design, call-to-action, or audience targeting, would also be valuable. By continuously testing and analyzing different variables, The Navigators can gain deeper insights into what strategies are most effective in driving email acquisition for their Scripture Memory Challenges.

experiments.title: How changing the FB creative for an eBook offer impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the creative for avila’s audience will achieve an increase in CTR and engagement because it will increase intrigue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/19/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of Avila’s newest offers is “What is Purgatory?”. This is an eBook for the viewer to learn more about Purgatory. We wanted to test using creative that was not the ebook. The original ebook had a more traditional, artistic cover, we wanted to see if making the creative a bit more vague would increase intrigue and make people click on the ad. And even further if it would increase engagement and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the creative for Avila's audience led to an increase in clicks for the "What is Purgatory?" eBook offer, indicating that the more vague creative increased intrigue and interest among viewers. While the results were not statistically significant at the 95% level, the 8.8% increase in clicks with an 89.5% statistical confidence suggests that the treatment was effective in capturing the audience's attention.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing different creatives to further validate the initial findings and explore other creative approaches that may resonate with the audience. It is important to analyze the data from each test comprehensively to understand the impact of various creative elements on engagement and ultimately donations. Additionally, considering incorporating qualitative feedback from the audience to gain insights into their preferences and interests when developing future creatives.

experiments.title: How does adding the number of downloads as an incentive for a content offer impacts emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the number of downloads of the content offer for paid media audiences will achieve an increase in emails acquired because the number would reinforce credibility and incentives since it is so highly requested.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV offers numerous digital download content offers for free as a method to acquire new email addresses that can be cultivated into potential donors.
We decided to test PTV’s best-performing content offer, The Jesus Map, to share the number of downloads for the offer. We hypothesize that this number would help to add credibility and incentive as over 19,000 people had downloaded it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding the number of downloads of the content offer for paid media audiences led to a 17.0% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. This indicates that including social proof, such as the number of downloads, can potentially enhance credibility and incentivize users to engage.

2. Despite the positive impact on email acquisition, the statistical level of confidence was only 83.0%, falling below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered truly reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

3. On the other hand, the experiment also revealed a significant 763% decrease in transactions with a high level of confidence at 96%. This unexpected outcome suggests that while the social proof may have driven more email acquisitions, it could have potentially deterred conversions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider the following implications for future experimentation:

1. While social proof can be a powerful tool in driving user engagement, the impact on other metrics such as transactions should be carefully monitored. It is crucial to balance the benefits of increased credibility with potential drawbacks on conversion rates.

2. Future experiments should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence to ensure the reliability of the results. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or refining the data collection process.

3. It is essential to analyze the overall impact of any changes on multiple metrics to gain a comprehensive understanding of the experiment's effects. This will help in making informed decisions and avoiding unintended consequences.

In conclusion, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of carefully evaluating the trade-offs between different metrics when implementing changes. By considering these learnings and conducting future experiments with a more rigorous approach, we can better understand the impact of our interventions and make data-driven decisions to optimize our strategies.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase donor conversion rate by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2024
IMB is running a direct ask offer where people can give to fund Bibles, but we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. We hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamically changing slider for donors to select the level of their impact did not increase donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 35.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

While the results are approaching validity, with a statistical level of confidence of 93.8%, further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to the donation page. It may be beneficial to conduct more qualitative research or user testing before implementing a new feature to ensure it resonates with donors and effectively communicates the impact of their contribution. Additionally, testing smaller changes or variations of the dynamically changing slider could help pinpoint what specifically led to the decrease in donations in this experiment.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects monthly donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday resulted in a significant increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.

Although the results showed a 145.6% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%. This indicates that while the treatment showed promise, further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing this approach with a larger sample size to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it could be useful to explore different variations of the treatment to optimize the ask for monthly donations and potentially further increase donation rates.

Overall, the experiment highlights the potential benefits of tailoring donation asks to members based on their giving preferences and the importance of ongoing testing and optimization to drive success in fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: Does adding a sample of a daily devotional in the body of an email impact donations for a non-donor audience?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a sample of a devotional for nondonors will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2024 - 12/13/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
One of their most popular offers every year is a 265-page daily devotional that is sent to the donor as a thank you for their donation of any amount.
For this experiment, which was run on their housefile and targeted their non-donor audience, we wanted to see whether adding a sample passage from the devotional made an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing a sample of a devotional for non-donors potentially has a positive impact on donation conversions, as evidenced by the 57.3% increase in donations for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence of 81.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, indicating that these results may not be reliable and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical power to confidently determine the effectiveness of such strategies. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. This will help in making more informed decisions based on the outcomes of the experiments.

experiments.title: How making the problem relatable to the donor affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the problem more relatable to the donor for survey takers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it creates an emotional connection, helping donors see how their contribution directly impacts real people and situations they care about, making them more likely to feel personally responsible and motivated to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2024 - 10/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey on voter fraud through paid media and email prospecting efforts. They wanted to try and increase in the instant conversion rate on the donation page. To do so, we decided to test making the problem of election fraud more relatable to the donor by adding a paragraph that says “imagine casting your vote only to find out the fraudulent activities affected the impact of your vote.”
This experiment ran for 1 month to 50% of the traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a paragraph to make the issue of election fraud more relatable to donors did not lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates most with donors and leads to a higher conversion rate. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or external events that could have impacted donor behavior.

experiments.title: How including the benefits a person will receive to a landing page’s call-to-action affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe including the benefits a person receives to the call to action on an eBook landing page will increase the conversion rate because people will understand the value they receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running an eBook offer using Facebook ads and prospecting emails to send traffic to a landing page where people could request their free copy of their 2024 Index of U.S. Military Strength eBook. In an attempt to increase email conversion rate on this page, they previously tested adding a curiosity-creating headline, as well as a preview of the content available in the eBook. Both of these treatments, however, resulted in a decrease in email acquisition rate.
As a follow up, they wanted to test if adding the benefits a person receives in the page’s call-to-action (CTA) would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength by filling out the form below!
The treatment’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength to discover the state of the U.S. military and what it means for your future.
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic, and the experiment ran for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the benefits a person receives in the call-to-action on an eBook landing page actually led to a decrease in email conversion rate. The treatment group, which included the benefits in the CTA, saw a 10.4% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 87.3%, indicating that the results are not yet fully valid but are approaching validity.

Moving forward, it is important to acknowledge that in this specific case, highlighting the benefits did not have the intended effect of increasing conversion rates. This information should be used to guide future experimentation by considering alternative strategies for optimizing the landing page, such as testing different messaging, design elements, or incentives to encourage email sign-ups. It is also crucial to continue testing and iterating on different approaches to find the most effective way to drive conversions for the eBook offer.

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results should not be used as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How exclusivity language on a high-urgency pop-up impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including exclusivity language for users on the RTB website will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it highlights the donor’s identification with a group, beyond just being a donor..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s first-ever Giving Day, we hypothesized that the inclusion of exclusivity language — highlighting that the donor is joining a unique or exclusive group of donors to the first RTB Giving Day — will improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including exclusivity language on the RTB website did lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 68.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results.

While the initial findings are promising and suggest that exclusivity language may indeed impact donor conversion rate positively, we should not make any definitive conclusions or implement permanent changes based on these results alone. Further testing with a larger sample size and longer duration is needed to validate the impact of exclusivity language on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: Does the fact that 100% of a donors gift goes to mission affect conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about where the donor’s gift is allocated will increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by clearly showing how their generosity directly supports missionaries and Kingdom work.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/03/2024
For Giving Tuesday, IMB wanted to test how adding transparency about the allocation of the donor’s gift would affect the donation conversion rate on both of their Giving Tuesday appeals.
We kept the control and treatment audiences consistent throughout both emails. The note of the allocation was added to the treatment emails as well as the donation page for those who received the treatment email.
They believed it would increase the conversion rate because it builds trust and confidence in the organization by showing how their gift directly supports missionary work.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about where the donor's gift is allocated can have a significant impact on the conversion rate for donations. In this case, the treatment group saw a 43.9% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that donors may be more inclined to give when they have a clear understanding of how their gift will be used.

Additionally, we calculated the impact of click-through rates in the emails and only saw a 1.1% increase with a 28.6% LoC. This means the addition of the transparency note did not have an effect on click-through rate, but helped increase giving once people got to the donation page.

To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of transparency on donation conversion rates. By conducting additional experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different variations of allocation transparency, we can further validate the initial findings and determine the most effective strategies for communicating allocation to donors.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that transparency in donation appeals can build trust and confidence in the organization, ultimately leading to increased support for the mission.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options on a membership donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who are activating their membership will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2024 - 08/07/2024
An organization has a donation page where people can make a donation of $25 or above and activate their membership. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that decreasing the gift array amounts did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate: Contrary to the hypothesis, the treatment group, which had lower gift options of $25, $50, and $100, experienced a 23.7% decrease in total transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, which had gift options of $50, $100, and $250. This decrease was at 83.8% which, while not valid, is directional.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to analyze the impact of the language used in the gift array on donor behavior. It is possible that the specific wording and formatting of the gift array may have influenced donor decision-making in ways that were not captured by this experiment.

Overall, it is essential to carefully design and execute experiments, take into account all relevant factors, and analyze results with a critical eye before making any decisions based on the findings.

experiments.title: How guided reading subheaders impact donor conversion on e-book donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that guided reading subheadings for users on an instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because increases readability of the copy while also clearly imparting the problem, solution, and how the donor’s gift makes a difference. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2024 - 11/19/2024
We believe that guided reading subheadings (that outline a problem, solution, and what you can do) on instant donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although it would appear that guided reading subheadings on instant donation page can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The statistical level of confidence was only 80.%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. In addition, the sample size is very small with a total of 5 transactions in the test.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence, ideally above 85%. This will help to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions about the effectiveness of different strategies.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts lapsed donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing value prop for lapsed donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
- The experiment aimed to test the impact of reinforcing the value proposition for lapsed donors in an email during WUNC's Fall Fund Drive.
- The treatment group, which received the additional call-out reinforcing WUNC's value proposition, showed a significant increase in clicks for all traffic, with an 81.8% improvement.
- The statistical level of confidence was 89.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, but further testing is recommended for certainty.
-Moreover, while there were only 3 gifts total, the addition of the value prop did increase conversion rates by 100%.

experiments.title: Will aligning the value proposition from the donate button and the episodic giving page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the value propostion from the donate button to the doantion page for main episodic donation page will achieve a higher number of transactions because the congruency between the button and the donation page will decrease friction and reinforce motivation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 11/01/2024
In this series of testing we have found that using Biblical Resources on the main donate button increases the number of clicks to the donation page.
We saw that aligning the value proposition with the main donation button on the monthly page increased transactions by 154%.
We wondered if we could duplicate those results for the episodic donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the value proposition from the donate button to the donation page for the main episodic donation page led to a 13.7% increase in transactions for all traffic. This indicates that having congruency between the button and the donation page can potentially decrease friction and reinforce motivation for users to make donations.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of aligning value propositions on donation pages to see if this trend holds true across different scenarios. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied approaches to alignment could help validate these findings further and provide more conclusive evidence. It is important to note that while the results of this experiment are encouraging, they do not meet the standard threshold of 95% statistical confidence, so further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.

experiments.title: How a more conversational tone on an email offering an ebook impacts click through rates for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more conversational tone for non-donors will achieve an increase in click throughs and email sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/08/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of Income Inequality, we wanted to see if a more conversational approach to email copy made an impact on click throughs and email sign ups for a non-donor audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a more conversational tone in email copy for non-donors resulted in a 6.1% increase in click-through rates. While this indicates a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence of 83.8% is below the threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following points:

1. Ensure a higher level of statistical confidence: To draw reliable conclusions, it is crucial to have a statistical level of confidence of at least 85%. This will help in making informed decisions based on the experiment results.

2. Continue testing conversational tone: The positive trend observed in this experiment suggests that a conversational tone may resonate well with non-donor audiences. It could be beneficial to further test this approach with a larger sample size to validate the impact.

3. Explore other variables: Apart from tone, it may be worthwhile to test other variables such as subject lines, call-to-action language, or design elements to optimize email performance for non-donor audiences.

4. Collect qualitative feedback: In addition to quantitative data, gathering qualitative feedback from the audience can provide valuable insights into their preferences and behaviors. This could help in refining future experiments and strategies.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend, it is essential to prioritize statistical significance and continue testing different approaches to effectively engage non-donor audiences and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How using more focused copy on a donor email offering up a content offer impacted offer performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing copy for donors will achieve a change in email performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and NonDonor audiences. For this specific recirculation of one of Hoover’s most popular offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to see whether copy that focused more on the offer, and not use pop culture references, impact email and content offer performance. This experiment was for an active donor audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing the copy on the offer itself can have a positive impact on email performance for donors. The treatment group, which received the email with copy focused on the offer without using pop culture references, saw an 11.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group.
Moreover, the treatment version saw a 55% increase in email sign-ups, with a 100% level of confidence. And it also saw an 11% increase in donations after someone signed up to get the offer, but only with a 35% level of confidence.
We can then say that for an active donor audience, for this specific content offfer, using more focused copy led to an increase in all KPIs. Future experiments could look at making sure there is congruency at the landing and donation page level.

experiments.title: How defaulting to recurring gift with a partner’s benefits call-out on a donation page impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner will increase the number of recurring donations because it provides added value to donors, strengthening their connection to the organization and making the recurring gift feel more rewarding and meaningful.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2024
FamilyLife is a Christian organization dedicated to strengthening marriages and families through biblically-based resources, events, and guidance, supporting individuals as they build enduring, healthy relationships. Previously, we tested a recurring gift default on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages, which led to a 0.53% increase in recurring donations with a 99.9% level of confidence. Based on this success, the recurring default has been implemented as a permanent feature across the donation microsite. In this experiment, we aim to test a new approach to the recurring gift call-out, focusing on language that highlights the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner, to assess if this approach will further encourage monthly giving.
If we emphasize the exclusive benefits of becoming a FamilyLife Partner (e.g., access to insider ministry updates, private events, and a curated library), then users will be more likely to commit to a recurring gift, as the added value and connection to the FamilyLife community will increase their perceived return on investment and loyalty.
The test involved two variations on the donation page. The Control version used a recurring gift call-out that stated, “Equip 140 families with practical insights to strengthen the relationships that matter most.” The Treatment version introduced a new call-out focused on membership benefits: “Receive exclusive benefits: insider ministry updates, invitation-only events, access to our curated library, and membership to our private Partners-only network.” This A/B test was split 50/50 and conducted on their best performing premium donation pages: Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and the Ferret Flush Couples Pack.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to determine if emphasizing exclusive benefits for recurring donors would increase the number of monthly donations on FamilyLife’s premium donation pages. However, partway through the test, two of the three offers (Seeing Beautiful Again, 52 Weeks in the Word, and Ferret Flush Couples Pack) were turned off due to budget limitations. As a result, traffic to the test was reduced, which impacted our ability to achieve a conclusive result.
Recurring Donations Results: The Control outperformed the Treatment in terms of recurring donations. The Control had a 0.59% conversion rate (13 recurring gifts from 2,210 samples), while the Treatment had a 0.30% conversion rate (7 recurring gifts from 2,366 samples), representing a relative decrease of 49.7% at an 86.1% level of confidence. This difference did not reach statistical significance, so the results are inconclusive.
Overall Transactions Results: When examining all transactions, the Control had a 4.8% conversion rate (107 total gifts from 2,210 samples) compared to a 4.9% conversion rate in the Treatment (115 total gifts from 2,366 samples). This represents a slight relative increase of 0.4% in the Treatment, with a very low level of confidence (2.4%). This result also did not validate, indicating that the new messaging had no meaningful effect on overall transactions.
Theories on Donor Response:
Mismatch with Motivation: FamilyLife donors may be motivated more by the immediate impact of their giving rather than exclusive, long-term benefits. The Control message, which directly highlighted the impact of equipping families, may have resonated more with donors’ desire to see their contribution create tangible change. The Treatment, which shifted focus to membership perks, may have unintentionally distracted from this primary motivation.Perceived Complexity: The mention of exclusive benefits and a private network may have introduced an element of complexity or commitment that was unappealing. Donors may respond best to messages that feel straightforward and immediately impactful. The “Partners-only network” or “curated library” might not have been clearly valuable in this context and could have made the ask feel less accessible.Different Expectations for Monthly Giving: Some donors may view recurring giving as a simple way to support the organization consistently, without needing additional incentives. This audience might value the ease and simplicity of monthly giving over exclusive access, making the Partner benefits message less motivating.

experiments.title: How a cleaner weekly email design impacts the click rate of readers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning Winston’s Weekly for subscribers will achieve a higher email click rate because there will be more information and resources available to them. because there will be more information and resources available to them.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2024
We’ve been running Winston’s Weekly for a little more than a year now. The weekly email shares a case study from our experiment library and breaks it down to provide fundraisers with a deeper look at fundraising tests to inspire their own testing and optimization.
Looking at the design, it felt partially designed and partially plain text. We thought that using more design elements might create a cleaner, more readable way to present the information.
Pulling some inspiration from the newsletter Case Studied, we broke each header section into its own blocks in the design. We added a resources section at the bottom that links to the week’s experiment and other relevant resources. We also added a section about our services with a button to learn more.
Unlike the control version, this treatment version provided more opportunities and more reason to click out of the email to our website.
The treatment resulted in an email that was almost twice the length of our typical email but presented the content in a much cleaner way.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The redesigned version of Winston's Weekly did not perform as well as the original version, with a 27.5% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was 92.4%, meaning the results are approaching validity but cannot be considered completely reliable. The additional information and resources added to the treatment version did not lead to a higher click rate as hypothesized.

I'm interested in doing another email redesign in the control format with just the addition of the resources section and the section about NextAfter's services to see how that would impact clicks.

It could also be interesting to do another email redesign in a plain text format, but removing the gray background and using clearer headings like in this treatment to see if that gets more people to stick around till the end and click.

Future Experimentation:

1. Further testing is necessary to determine the impact of the redesigned layout on subscriber engagement.

2. Consider conducting additional A/B tests with variations in design elements to identify which specific changes lead to improved performance.

3. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future iterations of Winston's Weekly, taking into account subscriber preferences and engagement metrics to guide design decisions.

experiments.title: How adding relevancy to email copy impacted clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding relevancy to an offer for non donors will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/03/2024 - 10/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover recirculates a new content offer every Thursday by sending out an email to their Donor and Non Donor audiences. This specific test was on their non donor audience. For this test on one of their most popular offers, we wanted to see whether email copy focused on relevancy outperformed the control copy, which has been used multiple times.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding relevancy to an offer for non donors led to a 5.6% increase in clicks compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 80.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Donations had a 64% increase on the treatment with a 80.7% level of confidence. The control had 11 gifts, while the treatment with the relevancy copy had 18 gifts. It would be interesting to further test relevancy copy to see if this continues to impacts donations and makes it feel more personal to the donor.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of adding relevancy to offers for non donors to see if it consistently leads to increased engagement. By conducting experiments with a higher level of confidence and testing various approaches to relevancy, the Hoover Institution can gather more reliable insights to inform their email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: Does a content offer work better as a first text?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending a content offer for poeple to opt into text will achieve a higher engagement rate and a lower unsubscribe because offering something of value is an easier sell to get people involved .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2024
KCBI recently incorporated texting into their program and we wondered what the best route to activate people in this channel would be.
So, we ran a test. Would offering a content offer, or something of value, be a better route to get somebody to opt in to text vs. a generic welcome message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the offer did receive overall less clicks.. the most important part of the test is knowing how many people actually stayed on the file instead of unsubscribing.

By offering something of value in the first text we actually saw a decrease in unsubscribed contacts.

 

Test: United We Kneel eBook

1,461 sent messages, 42 unsubscribes: 2.87%

 

Control: General Message

1,463 sent messages, 88 unsubscribes: 6.02%

The test underscores the importance of offering valuable, targeted content to retain subscribers. While the eBook offer may not have driven as many clicks initially, its value proposition likely made recipients more inclined to stay subscribed, rather than seeing the email as just another message in their inbox. This outcome suggests that even with fewer clicks, offering relevant, engaging content can effectively reduce unsubscribe rates.

experiments.title: Does a missional or initiative ask convert donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a inititive ask for a high urgency campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a tangible ask for a short period of time can be easier for a potential donor to convert on.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2024
KCBI runs a Fall Fundraiser each year that brings in traffic to their website.
For this campaign, we wanted to see what type of messessaging converts donors during a high urgency campaign better.. Missional or Initiative.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that having an initiative ask for a high urgency campaign may potentially lead to a higher donor conversion rate compared to a missional ask. While the treatment did show a 52.6% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

It's also important to note that donors gave more generously when they saw the treatment of "share 25,000 Bibles". The average gift for the treatment was $205 and the average gift for the control was $123.

experiments.title: Will using shorter copy on an content offer landing page increase email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers will achieve greater rates of email acquisition because there is less friction on the page to distract from completing a form..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary has a number of ebook content offers that typically perform well for their audience. We wanted to see if we could increase the email acquisition rate on these landing pages by using more focused, concise copy compared to the standard longer control copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
The use of shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers resulted in a 37.1% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. The statistical level of confidence was 92.2%, which indicates a high likelihood that the increase in email acquisition was due to the change in copy length. While the results are encouraging, further testing would be necessary to ensure the validity and reliability of the findings.
 	Further analysis of the data shows a concerning drop in the average gift and overall revenue for this offer. While these metrics did not reach a statistical level of confidence, further testing should be mindful of the potential trade-off of acquiring more subscribers who are potentially less valuable to the organization.

experiments.title: Changing the default payment option for recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the default option to ACH/direct debit for donors considering a recurring gift will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the percieved greater ease for managing their ongoing gifts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2024 - 08/23/2024
Like most nonprofit organizations, Luther Seminary places a high value on acquiring new recurring donors. Their current donation form for recurring donations defaults to asking for a credit card payment. We wanted to measure the impact of asking for a direct debit or ACH gift. The donation page copy, format, form fields, and gift array all remained identical between the control and treatment. The only variable in this experiment was the default payment option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the default payment option to ACH/direct debit for donors considering a recurring gift resulted in a significant increase in donor conversions. The treatment group saw a 458.8% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was only 92.6%, slightly below the 95% threshold for standard significance. This means that while the results are promising and indicate that the treatment was successful, further testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.
While this was not the primary metric we measured, the treatment version also had a slight decrease in one-time donation conversions through this form. Since the donation page copy was focused on recurring donations, and the result was well below the level of confidence for validation, this result should not discourage future testing and optimizations for default ACH payments for recurring giving pages.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the default payment options for recurring donations to see if the initial results hold true - would offering additional methods of payment increase the conversion rate? Additionally, considering other variables such as the messaging around the payment options, the design of the donation form, and value proposition language throughout the page could affect the conversion rate. It is essential to continue experimenting and iterating to ensure that the organization is maximizing donor conversions and ultimately increasing support for Luther Seminary.

experiments.title: How adding visual clarity to a marriage video series landing page image impacted email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more visual clarity to the imagery on a video series landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisition because visitors will understand what’s being offered and why they should want it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/16/2024
This organization uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. Their video series landing pages include a “screenshot” image from one of the series videos to showcase the guest speaker for the series.
We have a theory that some of the visitors coming to the landing page may not be familiar with what a video series is or how they would access it, so we decided to test whether we could use the imagery on the page to add some visual clarity.
We designed this as a 3-way test with the following variants:
Control – No change, uses the plain image of the speaker
“Devices” Treatment – Shows the video series on a phone and laptop to show how it can be watched
“Devices & Value Flag” Treatment – Shows the video series on a phone and laptop, and also adds a value impact statement above it
Our hypothesis was that the treatment images would drive higher email acquisition rates because visitors would understand what the video series offer is and why they should sign up for it.
We split the campaign traffic using Facebook’s AB testing feature and conducted the test over 1 month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included adding more visual clarity to the imagery on the video series landing page, resulted in a 2.3% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 93.9%. This indicates that the control, which featured the plain image of the speaker, performed better.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that while the treatment did not perform as well in this instance, there may still be value in exploring ways to improve visual clarity on landing pages. Further testing with different variations of imagery or messaging could help to identify what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other elements of the landing page, such as the copy or call-to-action, to optimize for email acquisition.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used to inform future testing strategies and to continue to refine the approach to optimizing video series landing pages for email acquisition.

experiments.title: How reordering a headline’s CTAs impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rewording the headline to make the primary focus the campaign for October Scripture Memory landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign ups  because making the campaign the focus relieves the privacy concerns and concerns of spam that are often associated with texts from an organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/11/2024
Every month, The Navigators have been running a Scripture Memory Text Campaign to help their audience memorize more scriptures.
However, there has still been some confusion that this is a text campaign – a series of 7-8 texts, 1 per day. Therefore, their team decided to change the headline to read: “Sign up for Text Messages and join us for a Scripture Memory Challenge!” in an effort to emphasize participants would receive daily tests.
We hypothesized that this change would result in a lower number of sign ups due to the perceived level of commitment, privacy concerns, and spam all associated with texts.
So, we decided to test it against a new headline that focused on the Scripture Memory campaign first, rather than just signing up for texts.
Our goal was to get people bought into the campaign first then agree to receiving text messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the headline to focus on the campaign itself rather than the text messages resulted in a slight decrease in sign-ups, indicating that visitors were not impacted, neither positively or negatively, by the headline asking them to sign up for text messages first. While these results did not validate, it is a helpful learning that The Navigators audience did not have a heightened or lessened motivation to sign up by the commitment to texts.
In the future, it may be beneficial to strike a balance between promoting the campaign and addressing potential privacy concerns. A revised headline that emphasizes the benefits of the Scripture Memory Challenge while also addressing privacy and spam concerns could potentially yield better results. Additionally, it's important to ensure that any changes made are tested with a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing them on a larger scale.

experiments.title: Does aligning the copy on the donation page to the mission of the organization affect donor conversion?
