experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that editing the copy on the donation page to be in alignment with the mission of the organization for the recurring donor drive campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the ask to be a sustainer would match exactly what the mission of the organization is.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
TITW ran a recurring donor drive this past summer. Since this is a very specific ask, to become a monthly donor, we wondered if aligning the copy on the donation page to the mission of TITW would increase donor conversion
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that editing the copy on the donation page to be in alignment with the mission of the organization for the recurring donor drive campaign did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 24.7% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

This suggests that the initial hypothesis was not supported by the data and that the messaging alignment with the organization's mission may not have resonated with donors as expected. It is important to note that the results are not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level but are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the findings.

experiments.title: How adding a logo at the top of an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a logo at the top of emails for subscribers will achieve higher email engagement because the logo creates confidence that the email is legit.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/25/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
3 years ago we tested out of images within emails, however we decided to retest a logo on email to see if a little branding adds validity to the email and if it can impact clicks and downstream donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a logo at the top of emails for subscribers resulted in a 23.0% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. Although the results suggest potential benefits of including a logo for branding and legitimacy purposes, the statistical level of confidence of 85.0% falls below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

In regard to donations we saw a 28% decrease in gifts at a 81% level of confidence

So, while the email saw more engagement, it did not translate to more gifts. 

Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making. It is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the impact of adding a logo to emails. Additionally, considering other factors such as email content, layout, and targeting can provide more insights into improving email engagement and donations. Ultimately, the key takeaway is to prioritize rigorous testing and analysis to make informed decisions for optimizing email marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How changing copy on the donation page for prospecting audience impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy on the donation page for Hoover’s prospecting email audience will achieve an increase donations because the prospecting audience who is unfamiliar with the Hoover Institution will feel more connected with their mission since it’s more clearly stated.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 09/03/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
Hoover sends prospecting emails to any potential audiences or leads that have not yet engaged with the organization. The goal of a prospecting email is to introduce the organization (usually through a content offer), spark interest, and encourage the recipient to take the next step, such as signing up for a content offer.
For this test, we tested consolidating the copy and driving the value prop of The Hoover Institution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the copy on the donation page for Hoover's prospecting email audience did not lead to an increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions, indicating that the longer copy in the control group may have been more effective in this case.

Since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid. Therefore, it is important not to make any decisions based on these results alone.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of copy length and content to see what resonates best with the prospecting audience. It is also crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a long enough duration and with a large enough sample size to obtain statistically significant results. By continuously testing and learning from different variations, Hoover Institution can better understand what drives donations from their prospecting audience and optimize their email campaigns accordingly.

experiments.title: How the addition of impact bullets on a direct ask donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding impact bullets to the donation page for Bible Direct Ask donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the impact bullets give a more tangible connection for the donor’s impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 09/10/2024
We are currently running a direct ask offer for IMB called God’s Word for the Nations which is a Bible Direct Ask. This offer asks individuals to donate so that IMB can fund the translation and distribution of Bibles in native languages across the world.
While this funnel was performing relatively well, we wanted to further optimize the process and lower our gross cost per donor.
Therefore, we tested the addition of impact bullets. These bullets notated how much impact a $5, $25, $50 gift would have in terms of number of Bibles.
$5 will provide 1 Bible
$25 will provide 5 Bibles
$50 will provide 10 Bibles
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding impact bullets to the donation page for the Bible Direct Ask offer resulted in a 15.1% increase in donations for all traffic. While this increase is substantial, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Furthermore, we saw a 1.3% lift in revenue at a 52.2% LOC for the treatment. While neither of these metrics validated, they do suggest that IMB's audience resonated more with the impact bullets.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing the impact bullets or similar strategies in future experiments to determine if they consistently lead to increased donor conversions. Additionally, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically reliable results before making any conclusions. By conducting further experiments with a larger sample size, we can validate the effectiveness of adding impact bullets and make more informed decisions about optimizing the donation process for the God's Word for the Nations offer.

experiments.title: How changing ad creative impacted registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative to look like a book cover for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in registrations/sign ups for the content offer because the ad creative feels more like a book cover rather than a person’s face.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 09/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
The Hoover Institution is running one of its long-time content offers, Dueling Populisms. This offer has run for a while, so refreshing creative is a great way for this to seem new to the audience. After seeing a low CTR, we decided we wanted to test the beginning of the funnel with new creative to see if we could increase registrations. For this specific test, we tried testing an image that looks like a book cover and more historical rather than Victor Davis Hanson; however, users have often shown an increase in registrations when we use his face on an ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved changing the ad creative to look like a book cover, resulted in a 25.1% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic. This indicates that using an image resembling a book cover instead of featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face did not resonate as well with the audience in terms of driving registrations.

2. Clicks experienced a significant 34.5% decrease, Cost per Click increased by 66.6%, and Cost per Registration increased by 45.7% in the treatment group. These metrics suggest that the new creative may not have been as engaging or appealing to users, leading to a decrease in engagement and an increase in the cost of acquiring registrations.

3. Specific audience segments, such as visitors aged 65+ and Tablet visitors, also showed significant decreases in complete registrations. This indicates that the new creative may have particularly missed the mark with these demographics.

4. While the results show a clear preference for the control ad featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face, the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%. Further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. Audience preference: The audience may have a stronger affinity for ads featuring a recognizable personality like Victor Davis Hanson. It would be beneficial to continue testing different creatives to understand what resonates best with the target audience.

2. Iterative testing: Testing different ad creatives, messaging, and visuals is crucial for optimizing campaign performance. It is essential to analyze the results of each test carefully and iterate based on the learnings to continuously improve outcomes.

3. Audience segmentation: Understanding how different audience segments respond to various creatives can provide valuable insights for targeted campaigns. Segmenting the audience based on demographics, interests, or behaviors can help tailor the creative to specific groups for better engagement.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of testing and iterating on ad creatives to find the most effective approach for driving registrations. By leveraging these learnings and continuing to experiment, the Hoover Institution can optimize its marketing efforts and achieve better results in the future.

experiments.title: How a skip the donate page button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “skip to my course” option for instant donation page viewers will achieve the same amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS offers free online courses as content offers. When somebody signs up for a free course, they then go to a instant donation page. This has causes some course enrollees to get confused and email DTS asking why they need to pay for a free course. We decided to do an A/B/C test and test putting “You can access your course immediately by clicking here.” in the copy and adding a button to the bottom of the page that says “No thanks, take me to my course now.” We wanted to see if adding clarity would impact donations and impact the amount of people emailing DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "skip to my course" option on the instant donation page did not lead to the same amount of donations as the control group. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. While the results are not statistically significant at the 95% level, they are approaching validity at 86.9%. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact on donations.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and design of the instant donation page. While clarity is crucial in guiding users to their desired action, such as accessing their course immediately, it is also important to balance this with the donation call-to-action. It may be beneficial to test different variations of the messaging and design to find the optimal combination that drives both course access and donations.
Overall, the results from this experiment provide valuable insights for future testing and optimization of the instant donation page to improve user experience and donation conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a background to an eBook creative impacts ICR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a background to ad creative for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because it will help make a long-time running offer seem “fresher” and therefor want to click the ad and eventually sign up for the offer..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/31/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. For one of their long-term eBook offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to test whether updating the creative to have an image in the background of the eBook would impact ICR. We hypothesized that updating this creative would increase engagement and the person would be more interested in clicking on the ad and signing up for the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a background to ad creative for Hoover's Facebook audience resulted in a 19.9% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic. While this improvement is significant, the statistical level of confidence was 84.6%, which is below the 95% threshold for validity. Therefore, the results should not be considered conclusive for future decision-making.

2. The treatment also led to a 55.6% increase in Clicks, a 41.9% increase in Cost per Click, and an 84.2% increase in Cost per Registration, all with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the treatment had a strong impact on user engagement and cost-effectiveness.

3. Specifically targeting the 65+ age group resulted in a 45.0% increase in Complete Registrations with a 98% level of confidence. This suggests that tailoring ad content to specific audience segments can yield significant improvements in conversion rates.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider running the experiment for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level of the results.

2. Test different variations of the ad creative, such as using different background images or colors, to understand what resonates best with the target audience.

3. Continue to segment the audience and tailor ad content accordingly to maximize conversion rates among different demographic groups.

4. Use the insights gained from this experiment to inform future ad campaigns and optimizations, focusing on strategies that drive higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How localizing the language on a donation page impacts donor conversion rates – part 2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that localizing the impact of a donation for potential donors will achieve a change in donation conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to test new copy on their primary donation page. Our goal was to assess whether incorporating language that emphasizes North Carolina and focuses on the donor’s personal connection would impact donation levels.
This experiment is the 2nd iteration of an original experiment we ran here, https://app.winstonknows.com/e/jm3z. In this version, we increased the level of localization by adding additional value proposition statements and impact on North Carolina.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which emphasized the local impact of donations in North Carolina and focused on the donor's personal connection, resulted in a 31.6% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control.

2. While the results show a positive impact of the localized copy on donation conversions, the lack of a higher confidence level means that these results should not be used for future decision-making.

3 . In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing variations of copy that emphasize the local impact and personal connection to donors, while also ensuring that the sample size is large enough to reach a higher level of statistical confidence. This will help in determining the true effectiveness of such messaging strategies in increasing donation conversions for WUNC.

experiments.title: How offering a premium version of the content offer impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium offer for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 06/13/2024
Hoover Institution has a quarterly research digest, featuring 30 of their scholar’s work, which is promoted as a free PDF download. We have run experiments in the past where adding in a premium offer–in this case asking for $125 to get all 4 quarterly editions mailed to you–has an impact on conversion rates and average gift.
In this experiment, due to increased mailing and production costs, we want to see whether increasing that ask to $150 would make an impact on response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which asked for a higher donation amount of $150, resulted in a 23.7% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.
2. The statistical confidence level of 81.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. In this specific case, the higher donation ask did not lead to an increase in response rates and could have potentially deterred donors from completing transactions.
4. It is important to carefully analyze the impact of donation amounts on response rates and not assume that a higher request will always result in higher revenues.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
1. Conduct further experiments with different donation amounts to see the impact on response rates and revenue.
2. Ensure that experiments have a statistically significant confidence level of at least 85% to ensure the validity of the results.
3. Consider other factors such as messaging, timing, and audience segmentation in future experiments to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior.
4. Continuously analyze and iterate on experiments to optimize donation strategies and maximize revenue for Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How using a pop-up impacts donations for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a pop-up for a specific offer for general website visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2022 - 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that primarily generated revenue through monthly premium offers. Typically premium offers will have multiple giving levels that include bundles of various or multiple premiums.
For the month of October 2022, the premium was a brand-new book written by Dr. Jeffress titled, “18 Minutes with Jesus.” Dr. Jeffress traveled the country and did multiple interviews for this premium so the extra traffic provided an opportunity for us to run an A/B/C test.
One of our homepage experiments focused on whether using a pop-up promoting the new book would make an impact on donor conversion rates. Our hypothesis was that with all the traffic being driven to the website by TV and radio promos, having the offer be front and center would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While using a pop-up helped increase donation conversion rates across the entire site, when drilling down into donations for the specific 18 Minutes with Jesus offer, the results were different. Overall, using the pop-up resulted in a 12% decrease in donor conversion rates with a 100% level of confidence, but it also had a 8.9% increase in revenue with a 83% level of confidence.

Since the number of sessions was not equally weighted, this experiment requires further refinement in the next iteration.

experiments.title: Will asking a question in the header of a landing page create a better hook?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting a landing page off with a question for an acquistion offer will achieve a higher conversion rate because the question on the page will lead the user to stay on the page longer and continue to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2024 - 07/09/2024
For this test we wanted to see if by changing the headline of a landing page to be a question if we could increase conversion rate on the page.
We decided to test this approach on an older offer that gets a lot of traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that starting a landing page with a question for an acquisition offer may not always lead to a higher conversion rate. In this particular case, the treatment with the question actually resulted in a decrease in transactions for all traffic.
It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level. This means that further testing is needed to determine if starting a landing page with a question is truly effective in increasing conversion rates.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different approaches to see what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, it is important to consider factors such as messaging, design, and user experience when creating and testing landing pages to maximize conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a banner style Facebook ad impacted clicks to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running a yes/no ad during a high urgency campaign for a direct mail audience will achieve increased clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2021
During a high urgency campaign, we wanted to target direct mail recipients with digital ads to prime and reinforce the campaign messaging in order to drive additional highly motivated and qualified traffic to the donation page. We wanted to test our control copy which included the deadline and the Challenge Gift against a treatment that made a specific ask to give a monthly gift and become a new Cornerstone Partner. We also developed a second treatment that asked a question to frame and align the donor’s values and beliefs with the organization and used more sliding door copy that presents what happens if we do not equip Christian leaders with Scripture. We wanted to activate the power of the “micro-yes” through this ad and bring those visitors to the donation page while they are already nodding their heads in agreement with the mission of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: While neither treatment had enough run-time to see valid results since it was a short, time-bound campaign, the ad that had "Learn more about becoming a Cornerstone Partner" as the call to action increased traffic by 65.6% with a 91.8% level of confidence. The second treatment with the banner graphic also drove clicks up 21.8% but only had a 62.7% level of confidence. We will look for additional opportunities to test the banner graphic in acquisition ads to see how the belief alignment impacts traffic and conversion rate for both online courses and instant donation pages.

experiments.title: How pinning a widget above a post on a landing page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pinning the tribute widget above a new author post for JEN traffic will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it increases visibility of the widget, but this reordering also better highlights the importance of the widget as a goal action for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2024 - 08/13/2024
When CaringBridge user makes a new post, their followers receive an email alert that takes them to directly the new journal entry on their profile. These users receive a Tribute Widget with an ask for a donation, however, this is pinned to be at the very bottom of the posts and any comments on the post in the control. If a particular post has many comments, this results in the Tribute Widget not being seen at all. We hypothesized that by pinning this tribute widget to be above the new post will resolve this potential point of friction and increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pinning the tribute widget above a new author post for JEN traffic resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 24.3% increase in Donations - Tribute (from Widget) for All Traffic with a 91.2% statistical level of confidence.

The treatment also resulted in a directional increase in revenue as well, with an 18% lift and an 85% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the placement of the tribute widget and other elements on the platform to optimize donor conversion rates. It may also be valuable to explore additional factors, such as the timing of the donation ask in relation to the user's engagement with the content, to further improve the effectiveness of fundraising efforts on the platform. Additionally, considering the potential impact on user experience and engagement is crucial when making changes to the platform layout. By continuing to test and iterate based on data-driven insights, Caringbridge can better understand user behavior and preferences, ultimately leading to more successful fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: Does Adding Leading Copy on the Donation Page Affect Generosity?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the body copy for the acquistion donation page will achieve a higher average gift because the copy tell people exactly what their money is going to and how they (the donor) is making a difference.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2024 - 08/12/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if changing the copy on the donation page would affect how generous donors give.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the body copy on the donation page led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 690.9% and a 362% increase in Email Acquisition. The treatment group that received the new copy that clearly explained how the donation would make a difference saw higher average gifts from donors.

These results suggest that providing specific and impactful information about where the donor's money is going and how it will make a difference can significantly influence their decision to give more generously. Utilizing this approach in future experimentation could result in higher donation amounts and increased revenue for KCBI.

It is important to note that while the results were promising, further testing is recommended to ensure the validity of the findings. By continuing to iterate and test different messaging strategies, KCBI can continue to optimize its donation pages and ultimately drive more impactful giving from donors.

experiments.title: How using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it makes it more personalized to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/01/2023
This organization wanted to test how using donor-centric language on their donation page for Giving Tuesday affected conversion rates overall. Traffic came to this page through paid Facebook ads, housefile emails and website traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page will likely lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the donor-centric language, saw a 23.3% increase in transactions compared to the control group with an 88% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version saw an 9% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing the use of donor-centric language and other personalization strategies to optimize the donation page. It would be beneficial to run longer experiments to gather more data and improve the statistical validity of the results. Additionally, testing different variations of donor-centric language and comparing them against each other can help identify the most effective messaging.

experiments.title: How using a credible figure to talk in first person on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a donation page like its a letter from an important figure for potential donors will achieve a increase conversion rate because it adds credibility and trust to the process. because  it adds credibility and trust to the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2023 - 12/14/2023
As apart of ACI’s calendar year-end campaign, they sent a survey to their housefile asking for feedback on how the organization is doing. After you complete the survey, you go to an instant donation page that includes value proposition on making a donation before the end of the year. ACI’s founder, Dr. Ben Carson, is a huge voice in the organization and to its followers, so we decided to test writing the donation page as if it were a letter from him.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language that presents the donation page as a letter from an important figure, such as Dr. Ben Carson in this case, can cause an increase the conversion rate. The results of the experiment showed a 29.6% increase in transactions for the treatment version with a statistical level of confidence of 83.4%.
Because we saw a directional lift in conversion rate, we believe that Dr. Carson writing the donation page as a letter does increase credibility and trust with the organization and the user.

experiments.title: How positive value-focused headlines affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing positive value-focused headlines for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2021
The Navigators had been trying to increase email acquisition rate on a offer they had that was doing very well in acquisition. The headline on the page offered the value proposition “Get the help you need to memorize scripture”. They hypothesized that this might imply that the visitor needed help, which might be off-putting to them.
So they decided to try to new approaches. First, they wanted to ask a question that implied that the visitor wanted to gain more knowledge: Do you want to memorize more scripture?
Then, they decided to test a headline that teased the future “superpower” of the visitor after they had absorbed the content: “Be equipped with the right scripture at the right time.”
They tested all three with the torrent of Facebook traffic that the page was receiving.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither headline was able to validate, with more than 21,000 conversions. This was partially because the motivation from the Facebook ad was very high and the email conversion on the page was more than 35%. However, it also showed that the changes might not have been radical enough to warrant a large difference. In addition to testing upstream and downstream of this page, they wanted to next test a more radical approach.

experiments.title: How Adding “Yes/No” language to Facebook Ads Impacts Performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding “yes/no” buttons to creative for questions posed in ad will achieve more clicks because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions. because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2023 - 10/31/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
Using “Yes/No” language buttons on a static creative piece has improved performance for some other clients, and the NextAfter team believes Hoover’s audience will react positively and improve CTR.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from utilizing "Yes/No" buttons on static ads within Facebook are as follows:

1. Adding "yes/no" buttons to ad creative for questions posed in the ad resulted in an 11.6% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that using close-ended questions with binary options in ad creative can effectively drive engagement.

2. The statistical confidence level is below 95%, but the results approach validity. This suggests that further testing is required to confirm the effectiveness of the "yes/no" buttons.

3. The experiment also revealed some negative impacts. Complete registrations experienced a 442% decrease, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons might have discouraged users from completing the registration process.

4. Cost per click and cost per registration saw significant increases of 115% and 1228%, respectively. This suggests that "yes/no" buttons may have attracted more clicks, but these clicks may not have resulted in a higher number of registrations.

5. Specific audience segments, such as 65+ visitors, tablet visitors, and male visitors, saw significant increases in clicks, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons may appeal more to these demographics.

Based on the insights gained, it is highly recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the effectiveness of employing "yes/no" buttons in ad creative. To understand if there are any usability issues or how they can be mitigated, it is essential to investigate the negative impact on complete registrations. Additionally, analyzing the metrics of cost per click and cost per registration will help determine the overall effectiveness and ROI of implementing "yes/no" buttons in future campaigns. Through careful and controlled experiments, the Hoover Institution can obtain more confidence and better understand the use of "yes/no" buttons to enhance ad creativity and prompt desired user actions.

experiments.title: How focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because users will be reminded of the deadline and the importance of giving before then.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2023 - 12/31/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition which told the story of Lottie Moon against a page that increased urgency to give before the deadline of December 31st. This test ran on the final four emails of the campaign which led up to the deadline.
They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign may not necessarily lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group, which received the urgency-focused messaging, experienced a decrease of 19% in transactions compared to the control group.

Based on these results, it is important to consider that urgency itself may not be a significant motivating factor for potential donors. Other factors such as the emotional appeal of the story or the credibility of the nonprofit may have a stronger influence on donor behavior.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of urgency messaging on donor conversion rates. This could involve testing different variations of urgency messaging, such as emphasizing specific deadlines or highlighting the impact of giving before a certain date. Additionally, it could be valuable to investigate the potential interaction between urgency and other elements of the value proposition, such as emotional storytelling.

By conducting further testing and refining the messaging strategy, the nonprofit can gain a clearer understanding of what truly drives donor conversion and optimize their campaigns accordingly.

experiments.title: How Does adding Consistent Value Language on the CTA Button Affect Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the call-to-action value claims for the main navigation donate buttons will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is less friction for the donor. They will no longer have to choose which initiative they would want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/30/2024
Our first test in this series was seeing if aligning the CTA on the duel donation buttons would make an impact. You can read that test writeup here: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/8277.
For this test, we wanted to see if aligning the value proposition on the buttons could increase transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted on the FOTF homepage showed that aligning the call-to-action value claims for the main navigation donate buttons resulted in a 61.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic. While this result is statistically significant, the confidence level of 84.0% falls slightly below the threshold of 95% for validity.
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the call-to-action value claims can potentially reduce friction for donors and lead to an increase in conversions and revenue. With the directional lift in transactions and revenue by using Biblical resources this variant will become the control for the next iteration of testing.

experiments.title: Will adding urgency to an in-line banner increase donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer on the Wikipedia banner will increase donor conversion because the countdown to the deadline would increase urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/02/2024
As the campaign deadline draws near, we believe adding a countdown to the Wikipedia banner may insight more donor participation by visually showing time is running out and the time to act — time to save America’s families and make an impact — was coming to a close. Would adding a countdown timer increase donor perception that they have a deadline to make a donation and make a difference?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the Wikipedia banner may increase donor conversion by creating a sense of urgency. The treatment group saw a 23.9% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the countdown timer potentially motivated more donors to take action before the deadline. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, falling short of the typically accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of introducing a countdown timer or similar elements that create a sense of urgency. While this experiment provided promising results, the validity is not strong enough to definitively conclude that the countdown timer was the sole reason for the increase in donations. Therefore, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations of the countdown timer may be necessary to draw more reliable conclusions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies to increase donor conversion and engagement to complement the potential effectiveness of a countdown timer.

experiments.title: How Adding More Than One Link In Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a second link for the FYE email will achieve a higher click rate because there will be more opportunity to click and go to the survey page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campagin every year. For this campaign the organization will publish ads, web elements, and email in an effort to support the campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase traffic to a survey page from their email channel. To do this we tested having one link vs. two link in the email body. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a second link in the FYE email resulted in a 5.2% increase in clicks for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on click rates, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

It is also important to note that once a user completes the survey, the user gets redirected to a donation page. While we don't have the data to see if increasing the number of links increased the completion rate of the survey, we can see that people who did receive the treatment had a 11% in giving at a 41% LoC (40 gifts vs 45 gifts).

experiments.title: How Adding An Image To an Email Effects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image for the edu FYE email appeal will achieve a higher click rate because the image will resonate with the user to continue reading the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that consists of a full email campaign to their Education housefile.
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding an image to the email copy led to more link clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we learned that adding an image to the email decreases the click rate if you look at the overall impact. To make this test even, we split the housefile into donors, nondonors, and lapsed donors. Each segment did produce a decrease in clicks, however, the treatment version with the image created a 47% lift in unsubscribes.

Looking at transactions from each email, the control also had a significant increase in transactions and revenue.

experiments.title: Does using a PDF icon on a content offer’s Facebook ad impact signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a PDF icon on Facebook creative for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is clarity in the type of offer they are receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
PTV offers multiple digital content offers in an effort to acquire new names, new donors, and to keep known contacts and donors engaged with content.
One of PTV’s most successful offers is about Billy Graham. In an effort to increase the success of this offer, we decided to test adding a PDF icon on the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment, which involved adding a PDF icon to the Facebook creative, resulted in a 10.0% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 83.8% statistical level of confidence. However, since the confidence level is below the 85% threshold, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making

experiments.title: Does Changing the Position of an Image Affect Clicks in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image at the bottom of an email for the FYE EDU appeals will achieve a higher click rate because the image will be reinforcing the copy that was just read.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Earlier in Moody’s FYE campaign we tested adding an image towards the top of an email to see if it could catch peoples attention and make them want to read more of them email. The results showed that it did the opposite and made people click out or unsubscribe.
For this test we wanted to see if placing an image at the bottom of an email copy could reinforce the message and create a higher click rate in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image at the bottom of an email for FYE EDU appeals did not achieve a higher click rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, if you look at revenue you can see a slight increase in the generosity of the donors when the image is present in the email. Both the control and treatment received the same number of donations, however the gift the average gift is up slightly when compared to the control. It is important to note that the donation size is very small, and the control did receive large outlying gifts that had to be taken out to not skew the average gift.

It would be interesting to test this tactic again to get a larger pool of donors to see if this is something that can be validated.

experiments.title: titw: Does Adding Moody Bible Institute in Emails Affect Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that tying the message back to MBI for a FYE Appeal Email will achieve a higher donor conversion rate  because donors will give more if the ask is associated with the parent ministry.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For this appeal email, we wanted to see if a drawing a line back to Moody Bible Institute instead of Today in the Word could aspire more giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, which tied the message back to Moody Bible Institute instead of Today in the Word, resulted in a 24.5% decrease in donations for all traffic. Backing up the top of the funnel, there was also a 1% decrease in clicks in the treatment

experiments.title: How relocating the Tribute widget to the right for all users impacts tribute giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that relocating the tribute widget to the right side for visitors on a user’s profile will achieve an increase in tribute donations because it will spotlight the ask and keep the CTA’s distinct..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2024 - 06/11/2024
For CaringBridge visitors on a user profile, they are met with a few different action possibilities. They can read a User’s posts, leave a comment of their own, as well as a make a gift a through the Tribute Widget. Historically, this widget has been one of CaringBridge’s highest sources for donations on the site. With the migration to CB2 however, they received complaints from users who found the widget intrusive or misleading — implying that they must make a gift in order to leave a comment. This was not the case. To help address this confusion and remove potential friction for users who do not want to make a gift, we’ve hypothesized that pinning the Tribute Widget to the right would help better spotlight the ask and keep these two CTA’s (whether to comment or to make a gift) as separate for the visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that relocating the Tribute Widget to the right side for visitors on a user's profile did not achieve the intended increase in tribute donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

Before abandoning this experiment, we hypothesized a contributor that could be contributing to the decrease in Tribute Gifts:

Have we unintentionally reduced the visibility of the Tribute? There is potential to re-test with a widget that has higher visibility by outlining with a color or making the widget a solid color.

In future experimentation, it would be important to consider the potential impact of changing the placement of key elements on a website, particularly when it comes to donation prompts. It is crucial to test such changes thoroughly before implementing them site-wide. Additionally, further testing should be conducted to determine the actual impact of relocating the Tribute Widget and to understand why it may have led to a decrease in donations in this particular experiment.

experiments.title: How repositioning the tribute widget to be inside the comment section impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that unpinning the tribute widget from the top and placing it within the comment section for visitors on a user’s profile will achieve an increase in tribute gifts because it will address the perceived friction that the widget is a paywall.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
With the migration to  2.0, CaringBridge received complaints from users who found the tribute widget intrusive or misleading — implying that they must make a gift in order to leave a comment. This was not the case. To help address this confusion and remove potential friction for users who do not want to make a gift, we’ve hypothesized that pinning the Tribute Widget into the comment section rather than having it at the top would better spotlight the ask while also clearing up the misconception that there was a paywall to commeting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that moving the tribute widget from the top of the page to within the comment section did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing tribute gifts. In fact, there was a 21.2% decrease in donations for all traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group. While the statistical level of confidence was only 93.5%, indicating that the results are not yet fully valid, they are approaching validity.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the placement and design of features such as the tribute widget to ensure they do not create confusion or friction for users. It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different variations or to gather qualitative feedback from users to better understand their perceptions and preferences. Additionally, it is essential to monitor user behavior and donation patterns closely to accurately assess the impact of any changes made.

experiments.title: How using the concept of autonomy and donor volition impacts giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because we focused less on urgency/pressure and more on value .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted shift the messaging of a campaign email to be less urgency/pressure focused and more autonomy driven – giving the donor the choice to give/not give. This is based on autonomous (volitional) motivation – donors give because they personally understand and value the cause; it’s their way to enact and share their values. This test was a complete copy overhaul and was tested across 2 of the last emails of a high urgency campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients led to a significant decrease in donations compared to the urgency/pressure focused messaging; a directional 46% decrease in donor conversion rate with a LoC of 93%. This indicates that the shift in messaging strategy did not resonate as effectively with donors as intended.

Additionally we experienced a valid 16% decrease in clicks, indicating that the messaging did not resonate enough with recipients to even click through the donation page.

Given that these are the last 2 emails of a campaign, where urgency is what typically drives action, it may be that RTB's audience is primed and expectant of this type of messaging as they approach the deadline.

experiments.title: How an autonomy-supportive treatment impacted clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing more autonomy  for the housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there will be an enhanced level of motivation that follows the increase in the copy’s autonomy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2024 - 07/09/2024
When running a fiscal year end campaign, we were curious if giving the email readers more autonomy in the email would lead to higher motivation to give and increase clicks in the email.
First and foremost, autonomy support begins with an orientation. As much as we might wish to reduce autonomy support to an algorithmic set of tactics (e.g., offering choice, avoiding pressure or guilt), true autonomy support starts with an attitude: we are not persuading donors, but rather inviting them to support a mission we believe they would value, and informing them about the ways they can help.
Having said that, when we start with “make this match-back or premium more autonomy supportive,” we are already a few steps behind. Within the “autonomy paradigm,” one would not even offer a premium or match-back to entice or seduce donors.
Nonetheless, we believe our treatment copy has made the appeals less pressuring or controlling and, in some instances, supportive of autonomy. This is demonstrated by the removal of controlling language such as “I still need your help” and “We need you”, simplifying sentences to enhance readability and clarity, using “your support” and “your contribution” to emphasize the reader’s autonomy, maintaining urgency without using pressuring language, reducing redundant phrases to streamline the appeal, preserving the core message and call to action, replacing the sense of urgency with a focus on making the strongest effort as the campaign comes to a close, and emphasizing the reader’s autonomy and the impact of their contribution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to test the impact of introducing more autonomy in emails for a fiscal year end campaign at Care Net showed that the treatment version resulted in a 57.5% decrease in clicks. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% so the results are not valid.
One key learning from this experiment is that while the hypothesis that increased autonomy in email copy would lead to higher motivation and increased clicks seemed plausible, the actual results did not support this. It is essential to recognize that autonomy support is more than just the language used in the emails; it is about the overall attitude of inviting donors to participate in a mission they believe in.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to consider the holistic approach to autonomy support rather than just focusing on the language used in the emails. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a long enough duration to gather sufficient data for meaningful analysis and to have a higher statistical level of confidence before drawing any conclusions.

experiments.title: Does a countdown clock increase urgency and donor conversion for a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a timer on the sticky bar for the fye campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a visible timeline of when the campaign ends draws more urgency than a donor countdown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/18/2024
For Moody’s FYE campaign, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion rate by replacing the donor countdown sticky bar with a different tactic to see if we could find out what their audience responds best to.
For this test we wanted to see if a timer would increase urgency more than a donor countdown
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which involved replacing the donor countdown sticky bar with a timer for the FYE campaign, resulted in a significant increase in donations for all traffic. The 38.3% increase in donations is a promising result, indicating that the timer may have indeed created a higher sense of urgency among donors.

However, the statistical level of confidence of 94.1% falls slightly short of the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This suggests that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to confirm that the timer is indeed more effective at increasing donor conversion rates compared to the donor countdown.

It's also important to note that while this did not validate with transactions. However, there was a $11,285 increase in revenue when a countdown timer was present.

experiments.title: How a simplified campaign Challenge Grant goal impacts overall revenue and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the campaign challenge grant goal for Human Coalition housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversion and revenue because it eliminates potential confusion and instead focuses on highlighting the impact of the challenge grant.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
For Human Coalition’s Fiscal Year End campaign, readers were presented with two goals: the overall goal of $703,875 as well as the challenge grant goal of $352,000. In reality, these two goals represent the same number. $703,875 is the total of what would be raised if the challenge grant goal was met. We hypothesized that presenting the user with both could cause unnecessary confusion and friction. We believe that by simplifying this goal in the treatment, to only highlight the challenge grant as the main priority will help improve donor conversion and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the campaign challenge grant goal for Human Coalition by focusing solely on the $352,000 goal led to a significant increase in donor conversion and revenue. By eliminating potential confusion caused by presenting two goals, the treatment version saw a 124.8% increase in revenue for all traffic.
Our hypothesis is that by simplifying the ask, and focusing the reader's attention on the doubled impact of the challenge grant, users were more likely to make a larger gift knowing.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing this simplified approach to determine its consistent impact on donor behavior. Further testing with larger sample sizes and variations in messaging could help strengthen the validity of these findings. Additionally, applying this learning to other campaigns within Human Coalition and potentially extending it to other similar nonprofit organizations could help optimize fundraising efforts and improve overall outcomes.

experiments.title: How autonomy in an email impacts revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing an autonomy technique for email subscribers will achieve higher revenue due to an increase in gifts and average gift because the donor is making the decision themselves.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/08/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts matching campaigns to increase incentive for donors and prospective donors.
For the final email in the June campaign, we decided to test autonomy to see if that approach would encourage more gifts and higher gifts from donors
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on utilizing autonomy in email campaigns for Pathway to Victory's matching campaigns are:

The treatment group, which received emails with an autonomy technique, experienced a 34.0% increase in revenue for all traffic compared to the control group. This includes a 16% in average gift and 15% increase in donor conversion.

Two gifts were removed from this experiment as they were viewed as outliers. A $1,200 gift from the control and $5,000 from the treatment.

experiments.title: How zooming in on ad creative impacted cost per registration

rows.name: Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Control
Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover  for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in the CTR because it engages the viewer with a highly colorful and visual image and provides consistency throughout the offer funnel with the eBook cover.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 06/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. Currently, the Hoover Institution is running one of its seasonal offers, the Spring Digest. It showed a low CTR. We wanted to test the beginning of the funnel to see how this would impact clicks and, ultimately, registrations. Since the covers for the Digest are visually appealing, we decided to zoom in on the eBook and delete some of the negative space around the eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover resulted in a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 81.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. Therefore, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 18.3% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment had a positive impact on this metric.
4. The Cost per Registration also experienced a significant decrease of 16.2% with a 99.9% level of confidence, revealing that there was an increase in conversions with a lower cost.
In future experimentation, it is important to:
1. Ensure that experiments are run for a longer period to gather more data and increase the statistical validity of the results.
2. Consider other factors that may have influenced the outcome, such as seasonal trends or changes in the target audience.
3. Continue to test different variations of ad creatives to optimize performance, but always prioritize statistical significance in order to make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How aligning a premium offer with seasonality during CYE impacts clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a seasonally-aligned premium offer in the Dear Listener for visitors on FamilyLife’s podcast pages will achieve an increase in clicks to the premium donation page because the seasonally-aligned premium offer will feel more relevant and appealing to the viewer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/01/2023
During their Calendar Year-End (CYE) fundraising season, FamilyLife has used “Dear Listener” elements on their podcast pages to convert their web visitors into donors. Before CYE began, we had running a Dear Listener that promoted a card game premium for married couples. But we wondered if aligning the premium offer with the time of the year would make it more relevant and acquire more donors.
The premium offer that FamilyLife was promoting during CYE was a 52-week devotional book. This book was included as an incentive in their CYE campaign emails and on the campaign’s donation page. We decided to run this offer as our Treatment to see if it would get more users to engage and make a gift to FamilyLife.
Our hypothesis was that the devotional premium would achieve an increase in clicks to the premium donation page because it would feel more relevant to the web visitors; this is because December is a popular season for people to get devotionals that they can begin in the new year.
We ran this as an A/B test in VWO for ~1.5 weeks. The Control Dear Listener promoted the game and pointed to the game’s donation page, and the Treatment Dear Listener promoted the devotional book and pointed to its donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for a week and a half, we found that the seasonally-aligned devotional premium actually to a -31.4% decrease in clicks to the donation page, with an 87% level of confidence. This test didn't meet the 95% level of confidence that's required to consider it a fully valid result, but we turned the test off early due to the poor trending results and the importance of the Calendar Year-End fundraising season.
There are a few different angles that we can test with this idea in the future:
Would we see a different result if we tested a seasonally-aligned offer during a different time of year (ex: Valentine's Day, Easter, etc.)?Would we see a different result if the control and treatment are the same type of premium offer (ex: both are books, or both are games, etc.)?

experiments.title: How humanizing Facebook Creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on optimizing click-through rates and conversions for an eBook promotion are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an ad with targeted imagery of a person engaged in outdoor activities, resulted in a 35.0% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control ad with the eBook's cover image.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 92.6%, indicating that the effectiveness of the treatment is approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm its impact.

3. The hypothesis that targeted imagery helps audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook, fostering a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates, was supported by the results of the experiment.

Moving forward, future experimentation should consider the following points:

1. Continue to test and refine the use of targeted imagery in advertisements to enhance engagement and conversions. Further experiments can help strengthen the validity of the findings and optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

2. Explore different types of imagery and messaging to determine which combinations resonate best with the target audience. This can inform future advertising strategies and improve campaign performance.

3. Implement A/B testing on a larger scale or over an extended period to gather more data and increase the confidence level of results. This will provide a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of different ad elements on audience behavior.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that targeted imagery can have a significant effect on engagement and conversions. By leveraging this insight and continuing to test and refine strategies, WUNC can enhance the effectiveness of its promotional campaigns and drive better outcomes for future eBook promotions and other marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a social proof popup to a membership renewal page affected donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a social proof popup for donors on a membership renewal page will achieve an increase in donation rate because people will be motivated by the fear of missing out.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2024 - 06/04/2024
In an effort to increase donation conversion rate on their membership renewal donation page, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding a popup that showed who had recently made a donation would lead to this increase in donations due to a fear of missing out (FOMO) on what actions others are taking.
They launched an A/B test where the control was the donation page without any popup, and the treatment had a small unintrusive popup in the corner of the screen that read:
“(Donor’s Name) from (State) just renewed their Heritage Foundation membership”
The treatment FOMO popup, which showed a new donor every 15 seconds, also had an American flag icon to align with the same flag icon in the monthly tab of the donation form and appeal to the donor’s patriotism.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This A/B test ran for 13 days. The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The addition of a social proof popup on the membership renewal donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, it resulted in a decrease of 43.5% in transactions for all traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence was 84.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making, but are indicative that further testing is needed to solidify the learning.
Reasons for this decrease may include potential donors preferring total privacy, not wanting their personal information (name and state) shared once they made a donation. Visitors to the treatment could have also been too distracted by the popup's animation to read and fully take in the donation page's copy.
In future, we should test this social proof popup on another donation page related to a content offer or a non-membership direct appeal to test whether they lead to the same results.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form gift array button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s gift array and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
The Heritage team wondered whether this flag image would appeal to potential donors on another direct appeal donation page. They believed testing this image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, due to both the image’s appeal to the donors’ patriotism and the suggestion of $100 as the “preselected” array option.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon only in the $100 gift array button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's gift array button may not always lead to an increase in conversion rate as expected. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 42.6% with an 88.3% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control performed better.
This result may indicate that the flag image was a distraction from the task at hand for this direct appeal. The image on the gift array button specifically may have confused or dissuaded some potential donors who planned on giving an amount other than the preselected amount.
In future experimentation, it would be important to further test the impact of adding the flag image in different elements of the donation form, such as the monthly tab as well as the submit button, or even testing different patriotic symbols or messaging to see if that resonates better with potential donors.

experiments.title: How reducing the length of copy and increasing urgency impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening copy and increasing urgency for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate and revenue because the ask is stronger and less copy to digest.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/05/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts matching challenges in order to increase donor conversion.
For the next to last email during their June match campaign we decided to test shortening the email and increasing urgency to see if we could get more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the email copy and increasing urgency can lead to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment group saw a 38.6% increase in revenue for all traffic, which is a promising result.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of shorter copy and increased urgency in driving conversions. Further testing with larger sample sizes and different variations of the email content could help confirm the initial findings and determine the best approach for maximizing donations and revenue during matching challenges. Additionally, we should strive to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence to ensure the validity of the results before making any significant changes to our email strategy.

experiments.title: Will a missional ask or a premium offer perform better as a text message
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a mission ask for text message receipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they are focused on impacting and supporting the mission of PTV rather than being motivated to receive an item.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/02/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each June and December, PTV does a match campaign to encourage donors to give as their impact is doubled.
Typically PTV will lead with their premium book with the match to encourage giving.
In email we have seen success in testing the premium book vs a missional ask, and seeing the missional ask prevail as winner.
Therefore we decided to test and see if the same would be true through a text message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a missional ask in a text message resulted in a 42.2% increase in revenue and 40% increase in donations for Pathway to Victory. This indicates that donors who received a message focused on impacting and supporting the mission of PTV were more likely to donate compared to those who were motivated by receiving a premium book.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of missional asks in different communication channels to see if the trend of higher donor conversion rates holds true across various platforms. Additionally, conducting more experiments with a larger sample size may help to increase the level of statistical confidence and provide more conclusive results.

Overall, the results suggest that emphasizing the mission and impact of Pathway to Victory in donation appeals may be more compelling to donors than offering incentives or premium items. It would be valuable to incorporate this learning into future fundraising campaigns to potentially increase donor engagement and support for the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up effects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 06/24/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to assess the impact of implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the homepage for new website visitors. The primary goal was to determine if this approach could effectively boost donation rates by immediately engaging visitors upon entry and potentially fostering greater support for WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the implementation of an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the WUNC homepage did not lead to an increase in donation rates. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a 21.3% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is 84.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to continue experimenting with different strategies and approaches to engage website visitors and encourage donations. Future experiments should focus on testing a variety of tactics, such as different messaging, timing of pop-ups, and targeting specific segments of website visitors, to determine what truly resonates and drives donation conversions. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure a high level of statistical confidence in the results before drawing conclusions and making decisions based on the findings of any experiment.

experiments.title: How including questions to increase relevance impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using conversational “you-focused” language for appeal email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them. because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them. because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/02/2024
When sending an appeal email to the email file, we wanted to find out if using a conversational approach and including two-way communication would improve overall engagement. Rather than just telling the reader what to do and what to read, we wondered if adding relevance, credibility and opening a loop of curiosity will improve the conversion rate by using conversational language. We developed a treatment version of the email pointing to the same donation link, but introduced a series of questions to the reader throughout the message to attempt to pull the reader in and asking them questions to get them thinking and interested in the rest of the email content. We split the message audience 50/50 and measured results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment using conversational language and two-way communication in the email did not lead to the desired increase in donations. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 43.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 84.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.
