In future experimentation, it is important to consider the balance between personalization and effectiveness of the messaging. While using conversational language and asking questions may increase engagement in some cases, it is crucial to carefully test and analyze the impact of these strategies before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence is essential for making informed decisions based on the results of an experiment. This experiment highlights the importance of thorough testing and cautious interpretation of results before making changes to email communication strategies.

experiments.title: How using organic direct appeal text ads on Facebook impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that using an organic-style text direct appeal ads for users on Facebook and Instagram will achieve an increased in clicks and donations because   the organic ads will feel like organic content on the platform instead of a paid advertisement.
because    the organic ads will feel like organic content on the platform instead of a paid advertisement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/29/2024
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. Their top donor acquisition campaign has been their Shoes for Orphan Souls (SOS) campaign.
For this experiment, we wanted to test an organic-style text ad against their current dynamic ads with photos of children receiving shoes. Dynamic ads use a catalog of photos in which Facebook’s algorithm will select what image visitors will see.
Our hypothesis is that using an organic-style text direct appeal ads for users on Facebook and Instagram will achieve an increased in clicks and donations because the organic ads will feel like organic content instead of paid advertisement.
In addition, with the organic ads’ streamlined design, it minimizes visual friction and users will be able to clearly see the call-to-action without the potential confusion of plain imagery.
We set this test up in Facebook as an A/B test so that 50% of our audience would see the control ads and the other 50% would see the treatment ads. The control campaign had the image-only ads and the treatment campaign had the organic text ads. Both campaigns had the same budget and audience targeting, and the test ran for 26 days on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for about a month, we found that the organic-style text ads had a 111.5% increase in conversion rates at a 90.1% level of confidence. Since the LoC is below our benchmark for validity of 95%, this experiment is considered invalid. This tells us that organic-style text ads can potentially lead to a significant increase in purchases, however furthering testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of the ads.
Additionally, the experiment revealed that the Cost per Click experienced a significant 441% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This implies that while the treatment ads may be driving more purchases, they are also more expensive in terms of cost per click.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing organic-style text ads against other ad formats to determine the most effective approach for driving donations. It may also be beneficial to conduct additional experiments with variations in messaging, visuals, and audience targeting to optimize ad performance while keeping the cost per click in check. Lastly, exploring ways to increase the statistical confidence level of the results would provide more reliable insights for decision-making.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout above the gift array impacted average gift and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact callout to the gift array for visitors on the confirmation donation pages will achieve an increase in average gift because the callout will incentivizing and highlighting a larger gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/23/2024
Focus on the Family is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. After someone signs up for a free video series, they’re brought to a confirmation page that asks if they will make a gift to share the series with others. These confirmation pages also promote a physical book premium offer as a thank-you for donating.
In reviewing these confirmation pages, we realized that the body copy on the page was asking for a specific gift amount — usually $30 or $50 — so that the reader can get the premium book offer. However, the gift array did not have this specific amount highlighted in any way.
We decided to run an experiment to test whether adding a callout that highlights a desired gift amount and reminds the reader of their gift’s impact would increase average gift amounts and overall donor conversion.
Our theory was that the impact callout would increase average gift by highlighting a higher default gift amount in the array. We also theorized that the impact statement would increase donor conversion by reminding the reader of the impact their gift would have on other families looking for resources like the video series they just signed up for.
We built this test in VWO and ran it as a strict A/B test across four video series confirmation pages. The Control did not have any changes to its design. The Treatment version highlighted the second value in the gift array and also added the impact callout.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an impact callout to the gift array on confirmation donation pages could potentially increase the average gift amount and revenue. The Control had an average gift of $38.26, while the Treatment had an average gift of $41.70. This increase in average gift translates to a 9.0% lift, but it only achieved a 90.0% level of confidence (LOC).

However, while the average gift saw a positive trend, the impact callout Treatment led to significant decreases in donor conversion. We saw that the Treatment led to a -17.7% decrease in overall donor conversion with a 98.6% LOC. This trend was primarily driven by new visitors on mobile devices; returning visitors and desktop users did not see any changes in donor conversion, but they made up a minority of the test traffic.

Based on these results, we can't consider the impact callout as a validated strategy for increasing average gift. But the 90.0% LOC on lifting the average gift is promising and can be tested into further. It would be worth testing the impact callout separately from the highlighted amount in the gift array to see which element of the Treatment design led to the increase in average gift and decrease in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How mentioning a last match donation impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by mentioning a donors participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors will achieve a high donor conversion rate because the donor is reminded of the impact they have already made.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts match campaigns that double the impact of a donors gift.
For the June 2024 campaign, we decided to test with the segment who made a gift during the December 2023 campaign to see if by mentioning their gift in December would encourage them to donate again in June.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning a donor's participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this strategy may not be effective in encouraging repeat donations.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of mentioning previous donations on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test this strategy with different messaging or timing to see if it can yield positive results. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically valid before drawing conclusions. Further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy and determine the best approach to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How mentioning a last match donation impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by mentioning a donors participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors will achieve a high donor conversion rate because the donor is reminded of the impact they have already made.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts match campaigns that double the impact of a donors gift.
For the June 2024 campaign, we decided to test with the segment who made a gift during the December 2023 campaign to see if by mentioning their gift in December would encourage them to donate again in June.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that mentioning a donor's participation in a previous match campaign for December match donors did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this strategy may not be effective in encouraging repeat donations.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of mentioning previous donations on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test this strategy with different messaging or timing to see if it can yield positive results. Perhaps sharing more of the impact that their first gift has and encouraging them to keep it going with a second gift.

experiments.title: How using donor-centric language impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing organizational language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is more “you” focused language that aligns the donor’s beliefs with the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2024 - 06/14/2024
When sending visitors from a free course registration page to a donation form, we noticed there were multiple references to the organization and fewer references to the page visitor. We wondered if treating this donation page like a conversation with a potential donor would improve conversion on the page. We developed a treatment based on a past variant that had out-performed the control and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing organizational language for donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion. The treatment, which involved using more "you" focused language to align the donor's beliefs with the donation ask, resulted in a 20.6% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.7%, so the results are not statistically valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of using donor-centric language on donation page performance. By refining the treatment and running further tests with a larger sample size, we may be able to validate the initial findings and gain more insights into what drives donor conversion on the page.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for ebook downloaders will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/13/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Guide to the Constitution eBook offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that changing the tab titles on a donation form to add clarity and donor autonomy led to a 32.6% increase in overall donor conversion rate at a 91.2% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%, the results were approaching validity. The treatment version, which used the language "Make a One-Time Gift" and "Make a Monthly Gift", was rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations of tab titles and messaging on donation forms to further evaluate their impact on donor conversion rates. It would be beneficial to explore additional iterations of language that provide clear guidance and empower donors to make informed decisions. Additionally, considering other elements of the donation form, such as design, adding icons that resonate with the donor's motivation, and call-to-action buttons, in conjunction with the tab titles, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of how to optimize the donation process for increased conversions.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small amount of people donate after downloading an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”. because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2024 - 03/04/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering a free ebook called The Founder’s Almanac.
They had previously tested the idea of highlighting the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take the survey are willing to take the next step and donate.” This was tested on a survey offer, and it produced a 105% lift in conversion rate with a 98% level of confidence.
This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They wanted to retest this concept on an ebook funnel to see if they would get a similar lift in conversion rate.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Highlighting the fact that only a small minority of people donate resulted in a significant increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a 64.0% lift and a statistical level of confidence of 92.8%. This indicates that the language in the treatment version resonated with the ideal donor who is motivated by being part of a select group willing to do the "right thing".

2. The treatment also saw a substantial increase in Transactions - One-Time, experiencing a 770% lift with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the messaging specifically appealed to individuals looking to make a one-time contribution.

This is an application of social identity theory. This theory suggests that people tend to categorize themselves and others into various social groups. Supporting the underdog can be related to group identity and solidarity with a particular group.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue using the language that highlights the small minority of people who donate in future experimentation. This messaging appears to resonate with the target audience and create a sense of exclusivity and righteousness in supporting the cause. However, it would also be beneficial to continuously test and optimize this messaging to ensure its continued effectiveness and relevance.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy in a CTA impacted clicks on a survey
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving survey takers s a choice rather than a command for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is an improved sense of autonomy in the process, giving the potential donors ownership.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 05/20/2024
The concept behind donor autonomy is that people like to make choices for themselves, rather than being told what to do. When applying this concept to online behavior, we wondered if using language in a CTA that gave the email recipients the chance to indicate their willingness to participate, rather than instructing them with a command would have any influence on the click rate to complete a survey. We split traffic 50/50 between each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that giving survey takers a choice, rather than instructing them with a command, resulted in a 5.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This suggests that there is a positive impact on engagement when potential donors feel a sense of autonomy and ownership in the process. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of language that offer choices to recipients rather than giving commands. In our next test, we will build on the concept of donor autonomy by including the option to say "no" rather than only including one place to click and say "yes" to the request, which may lead to an even greater sense of agency for the email recipient and potentially increased engagement.

experiments.title: How humanizing a dear reader impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a human invitation for website visitors will achieve an increase in newsletter signups because we know that people tend to respond to things that are “alive” in some way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2024 - 05/07/2024
When launching a newsletter and advertising it to site visitors below blogs/articles on the webpage, we wondered if we could replicate the success we had seen through other orgnization’s testing of effectively increasing email acquisition by humanizing our communications. We developed a treatment that leaned into the humanity of the newsletter offer and that emphasized that you’re going to hear directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happening inside the Canadian government if you sign up for weekly newsletter updates. We ran this experiment for 4 weeks to determine just how adding a clear value proposition and highlighting a familiar face from your organization can improve response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a human invitation for website visitors can significantly increase email acquisition for the Canadian Taxpayers Federation's newsletter. The treatment, which emphasized hearing directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happenings inside the Canadian government, led to a 57.6% increase in email signups compared to the control group.

While the results of this experiment are promising, the statistical level of confidence of 90.9% falls slightly below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. This suggests that further testing may be necessary to confirm that the treatment consistently has the intended effect.

In future experimentation, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation should continue to explore the impact of humanizing communications and highlighting familiar faces within the organization to engage website visitors. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations of the human invitation, such as featuring other insiders or providing specific examples of the type of content subscribers can expect to receive. By iteratively testing and optimizing these approaches, the organization can continue to improve email acquisition and overall engagement with its audience.

experiments.title: How adding a headline impacts conversions pt1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a headling for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because there is additional clarity in the offer. because there is additional clarity in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month, PTV promotes a different book with a gift of a generous amount.
For April, we decided to test adding a headline to the donation page. We have seen with other organizations that a headline can drive clarity and, therefore, drive results as it presents clear, brief, easy-to-read information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment is that adding a headline to the donation page for Pathway to Victory did not lead to the expected increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations for all traffic. While the results did not reach a statistically valid level of confidence, they are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of adding a headline on web traffic.

As this saw a significant decrease, we did run the test for the entire month of April. We still believe our hypothesis is true but question the copy utilized. In the next iteration we will adjust the copy.

experiments.title: Does Humanizing Facebook Creative Improve Click-Through-Rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/30/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that utilizing targeted imagery, specifically featuring a person engaged in outdoor activities, can significantly increase engagement and conversions for promotional campaigns such as eBooks. In this case, the treatment ad featuring a person saw a 12.7% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control ad featuring the eBook cover image, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.2%.
These results suggest that incorporating images of people enjoying the outdoor activities showcased in the eBook can help the audience visualize themselves benefiting from the content, thus fostering a stronger connection and driving action. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using targeted imagery that resonates with the target audience to enhance engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it could be beneficial to further test different types of imagery and messaging to continuously optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve more revenue because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake can significantly increase revenue. The treatment group saw a 40.2% increase in revenue for all traffic, which indicates that providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used can motivate donors to give more.
However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 82.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact, they are not completely reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of gift handles and impact descriptions in donation appeals to see if the results can be replicated and validated. Additionally, it may be helpful to investigate other factors that could influence donor behavior and revenue generation to further optimize fundraising efforts for Eagle Lake.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to petition summaries led to increase in emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding descriptions to the petition summary cards for non-paid website traffic will lead to an increase in petition signatures because it adds more clarity and strengthens the motivation of the user clicking through.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/17/2024
In this experiment, Canadian Taxpayers Federation was exploring how they might optimize the top of their petition funnel. Historically they have spent a lot of time and effort on optimizing paid ad campaigns driving traffic to specific petition offers. But they wondered if they could drive more motivated visitors to petition pages from their existing website traffic.
Their original petitions landing page had lots of summary cards showing the title of the petition, a representative image, and a “Sign the Petition” call-to-action button.
They wondered if adding brief descriptions to the petition summary cards would give more context to the user and ultimately lead to more petition signers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the brief descriptions to the petition summaries led to a 17% increase in petition signatures.

 

While historically this page has not been a significant driver of petition signatures, this experiment shows that the potential is there to drive more motivated users into a key acquisition funnel. It just requires some optimization to understand what will drive motivated click-throughs from existing website traffic.

 

Future experimentation should focus on template-level ideas that can increase the clarity of these petition offers as well as drive more motivated traffic through to the next step.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options on a gift array impacted non-donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of options on a gift array for nondonors will achieve an increase in conversion because there are fewer options to consider thereby reducing decision friction in the donation process. because  there are fewer options to consider thereby reducing decision friction in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/09/2024
We wondered if having a $200 option on the gift array for nondonors was causing friction in the decision making process. We set up a test removing the highest amount from the gift array to help the donation ask feel more accessible to someone who has never made a gift to CTF before and reduce the decision friction by having too many options to consider.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by reducing the number of options on a gift array for non-donors on a post-survey donation page, there was a 14.4% directional increase in donations for all traffic at 91% level of confidence. This suggests that having fewer options to consider can reduce decision friction in the donation process and potentially increase conversion rates.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the impact of simplifying the donation process by offering fewer options on the gift array. Further testing is necessary to confirm the results and determine if this approach consistently leads to higher donation rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations in donation amounts to see which ones resonate best with potential donors and drive the highest conversion rates.

experiments.title: What happens when you shorten copy on the landing page of a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for the People of Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in conversions because there is less friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 03/31/2024
Moody Bible Institute is a nonprofit that has multiple sectors that roll up underneath their main MBI umbrella. One of their ministries is Today in the Word, this ministries only focus is to provide daily devotionals to their audiences through magazines, email, or on the app.
We wanted to see if we could increase their email file by marketing some of the bible studies for acquisition in Facebook. The original signup rate was lower than expected. We wanted to the impact that the change in copy length would have on the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Shortening the copy for the People of Prayer landing page did not result in the intended increase in conversions; instead, there was a 14.5% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic. Inadvertently when shortening the copy, we could have reduced the value proposition on the page. Further testing will need to be done on the area to determine a good baseline.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: Homepage “Regular” Wiki vs Autonomy Wiki v2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a decision point to make an impact for people who land on the homepage during a HUC will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2024 - 03/21/2024
For our first test in this series we wondered if adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact increase donor conversion on the homepage campaign wiki banner? By adding a level of autonomy to the donor experience and giving them “control” to say “Yes, I’d like to make an impact” or “No, I’m not interested today,” would we invite more participation in the campaign and insight additional donor conversions over our “regular” campaign messaging with a gift array? You can see the results of the first test here.
For this test, the second in the series, we wanted to see if we created the autonomy treatment that’s more closely aligned with the control, will more donors say “yes” to help give families the resources they need?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment, which involved a decision point for donors to make an impact, resulted in a 64.6% increase in Revenue for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 84.5%, which is below the desired threshold of 95% for validity.
3. While the results suggest that the treatment was more effective in driving donor conversions, the lack of statistical significance means that these results should not be used for future decision making

experiments.title: How reminding lapsed members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can significantly increase the click-through rate for lapsed members. The treatment in this experiment saw a 41.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, indicating that reminding members of the benefits they receive can be a compelling reason for them to donate again.

Note: While the treatment email had a higher click-through rate, it also had a slightly lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test and refine the messaging around the value proposition of a membership card. This could include highlighting specific benefits, such as exclusive content, special discounts, or access to events. By effectively communicating the value that members receive in exchange for their donation, we can increase the likelihood of them re-engaging with the organization.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to further test different variations of the value proposition messaging to optimize the impact on member reactivation. By continuing to experiment and analyze the results, we can uncover the most effective ways to encourage lapsed members to donate and renew their support for The Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How changing the color of form donate buttons impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the color of the donate button for donation forms will achieve a decrease in donor conversion because the color is darker than the previous and is more difficult to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 02/21/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary is celebrating their centennial this year and for it, they had an update to their brand.
For this test, we wanted to see if the updated colors would impact overall donor conversion due to the new yellow/gold being darker than the previous.
We ran this test across all donation pages, including acquisition pages and the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The updated color of the donate button for donation forms resulted in a 9.9% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

2. Although the increase in One-Time Donations was statistically significant at 81.4% confidence level, it did not meet the standard threshold of 95% confidence for valid results.

3. While the darker color of the donate button may have initially been thought to be more difficult to read and potentially decrease donor conversion, the results showed a positive impact on conversions and revenue.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations in colors, designs, and elements on donation forms to understand their impact on donor conversion and revenue. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and reach a higher level of statistical confidence before making conclusive decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How a high contrast donate button in the main navigation of a website affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors will achieve a lift in transactions and revenue because it helps website visitors visual interpret the webpages hiearchy of purpose and breaks down their decision process to decide to give a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2024 - 03/07/2024
After successfully testing the addition of a red donate button in the main navigation on the homepage of heritage.org we decided to run the test side wide to Heritage.org visitors in a 50/50 split. The test excluded the homepage from results. This experiment validated several times and ran for over a month, but for logistical reasons it was kept running. While there was lift among all website visitor types, (Email, Social, Search, Returning, and NEw Visitors, etc), the largest lift came from organic traffic to the home page, which created a 55% lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic, specifically a 24.9% increase. This indicates that the addition of the donate button had a positive impact on user engagement and conversion rates.

2. The treatment also led to a 55% lift in transactions from organic traffic to the home page, showing that the donate button was particularly effective in capturing the attention of visitors coming through this channel.

3. Although the results showed a high level of statistical confidence (85.2%), further testing is recommended to ensure the validity of the findings. The statistical confidence level of 95% was not reached in this experiment.

4. It is important to note that the treatment resulted in a 4% increase in bounce rate. While this may be a concern, it could also indicate that the donate button led to more engaged visitors who explored the website further before exiting.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to validate the impact of the donate button on revenue and transaction rates. It may also be worth exploring different variations of the donate button, such as varying the placement or color, to determine the most effective design for encouraging donations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that the addition of a prominent donate button to the main navigation can potentially improve user engagement and increase revenue for Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that urgent language instead of a premium may be effective in motivating The Navigators audience to give in the final hours of an appeal. The treatment email saw a 47.2% lift in transactions at an 80.8% level of confidence. While this test did not validate, the results are compelling in that, urgent language resonated well with their audience.
While the clicks did not validate for the treatment in this test, it does suggest that those who were compelled to click the link were more motivated to give once they landed on the donation page.
One thing that is important to note is the presence of the premium throughout previous emails. The lack of the premium in the treatment may have been a deterrent to give with the email because they could have received the premium in previous emails.
In future experimentation, it may be worth including the premium and have the addition of the urgent language in the treatment rather than removing the premium all together from the treatment email.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a content offer email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including social proof in a content offer email for the housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it reinforces the perceived value of the offer based on what others are already saying about it..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2024 - 02/14/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s resend of the Genesis 1 e-book offer to their housefile, we hypothesized that since this offer has already run previously including a line of social proof in the email to generate interest would impact sign-up rate and donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inclusion of social proof in the content offer email for the housefile led to a significant increase in donor conversions. The statistical level of confidence was at 88.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing is recommended for certainty.

What's notable is that although this experiment only took place at the email level, however the primary metrics impacted were downstream in the funnel. The line of social proof's impact on click rate was negligible, with a 1.2% lift at a 16.1% LOC. However, those who clicked from the social proof treatment were far more likely to sign up and far more likely to give with a 14.3% increase in sign-ups on the landing page and a 59.6% in donor conversions.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential effectiveness of social proof in driving donor conversions. By incorporating this strategy into future email campaigns and conducting further testing, Reasons to Believe can continue to optimize their efforts to attract and convert donors effectively.

experiments.title: Does a book image or video convert more donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image on donation page to a video for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because the video provides additional details of the book.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2024 - 03/08/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For PTV’s newest book “Are We Living on the End Times” we decided to test swapping the book image for a video promoting the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the image on the donation page to a video for web traffic led to a 40.9% increase in revenue for all traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 83.4%, below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.

Therefore, we cannot consider these results as valid and should not make decisions based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing and refining different elements to optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 55.5% lift in recurring donations at an 86.3% level of confidence. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test because we did have some one-time donations also coming through.
We did see a drop in revenue at 38.6% with a 62.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.
Although these results did not validate, the results, in terms of number of recurring donors, were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.
While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How a direct appeal in a dear reader affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a direct donation appeal on Dear Reader Ads for daily signal readers will achieve a decrease in traffic and revenue to the content offer page and subsequent donation page because traffic to Daily Signal is motivated more for information and research and less towards making an impact through giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 02/22/2024
We tested a Founders’ Day donation request vs. an eBook Guide to the Constitution offer. Previous testing had shown that the Guide offer outperformed a direct ask during a special campaign, so we wanted to lay to rest whether we should run content offers or direct solicitations on TDS. We utilized “less than 1%” language that had been boosting results in other acquisition funnels. The campaign quickly validated in favor of the control 73% more clicks and 60 more transactions. We will use these results to inform future testing on TDS with Dear Reader Ads and no longer include direct appeals in campaign assets.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that adding a direct donation appeal on Dear Reader Ads for daily signal readers resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control (eBook Guide to the Constitution offer) outperformed the treatment (direct donation appeal). The statistical level of confidence was at 88.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity, but it is approaching validity.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing the impact of content offers versus direct donation appeals on Dear Reader Ads. Since the initial results indicate that the content offer performed better in this case, it might be beneficial to focus on providing valuable content to Daily Signal readers rather than making direct donation appeals. Further testing could help to confirm these findings and provide insights for optimizing donation strategies on the platform.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency in a sticky bar impacted conversion rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visible countdown deadline for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion rate by adding a sense of urgency. To do this we added a countdown timer to a sticky bar to give a deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a visible countdown deadline to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 21.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the urgent language in the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign did not result in an increase in clicks from potential donors. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a drop of 13.3% in clicks at a 81.0% level of confidence. While these results did not validate, they do suggest that the urgent language was not a motivation factor for The Navigators audience to click the link, and that they potentially respond better to the presence of an incentive or premium.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of urgent language and deadlines on donor behavior. It is possible that in this instance, potential donors may have already been motivated by the premium incentive in previous emails and adding urgent language only served to create confusion or negatively impact their decision-making process.
Moving forward, it may be beneficial to continue testing different incentives and messaging strategies to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, ensuring that experiments are properly powered and have a high level of statistical confidence will help to draw more reliable conclusions for future decision making.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a recurring ask pop-up impacts recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rate because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we hypothesized that “yes/no” autonomy language would provide the user with a micro-decision therefore increasing the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interest option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donation conversions. The treatment group saw a 126.1% increase in donations for all traffic at an 87.8% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that The Navigators audience appreciates the autonomy provided by both a "yes" and "no" option, and in turn, they are more likely to give a gift.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of autonomy language to see if the trend of increased conversion rates holds true. Additionally, exploring other ways to empower visitors and make them feel in control of their actions could further enhance donation outcomes for The Navigator's audience.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 02/28/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 35.6% lift in recurring donations at a 73.3% level of confidence, as well as a 29.3% lift at a 93.1% level of confidence for overall transactions. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test, hence one-time donations also coming through.

We did see a slight drop in revenue at a 10.8% drop at a 32.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.

Although these results did not validate, they were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.

While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons. 50% of home page traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.

Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.

Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How removing images and html styling impacted click through rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that stripping html from an email for the Today in the Word Audience will achieve a higher CTR because it will appeal more personal and less spam like.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could improve the CTR of an email by changing the layout to make it feel like it is a personal email. To do this we stripped all of the HTML, besides the header logo at the top.
In doing this it makes the email seem less like a marketing email and more like a 1 on 1 email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that stripping HTML from an email for the Today in the Word Audience can significantly increase click-through rates (CTR). The treatment, which made the email feel more personal by removing all HTML except for the header logo, resulted in a 10.9% increase in clicks for all traffic.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for acquisition funnel donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/01/2024
As a follow up to previous experiments, we wanted to see if using a tabbed donation form for visitors to an acquisition donation page would improve donor conversion rate and recurring donor acquisition. In previous experiments, we found no difference in one-time giving, but had seen 99% lift in recurring giving. However this was tested on the main donation page where motivation to give is very different than giving through an acquisition funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a tabbed donation form, resulted in a 29.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

2. While the statistical level of confidence (90.3%) is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, the results are approaching validity. This means that there is a strong indication that the treatment is effective, but further testing is needed to confirm its impact.

3. The previous experiment on the main donation page showed a 99% lift in recurring giving with a tabbed donation form. However, the current experiment on the acquisition donation page did not see a significant difference in recurring giving. This suggests that the motivation to give may differ between the two situations.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate its effectiveness. Consider running additional experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to improve statistical confidence.

Additionally, it may be valuable to explore the impact of different language and messaging on the donation form. Testing variations in messaging can help determine the most effective language that resonates with potential donors and increases conversion rates.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment suggest that a tabbed donation form has the potential to improve donor conversion rates on acquisition donation pages.

experiments.title: How increasing an ask from $30 to $50 impacts giving for onsite tribute widgets
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the ask amount  for site visitors will achieve an increase in revenue  because there is a larger initial ask (without doubling the previous amount).
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/12/2024
CaringBridge has a long-standing ask of $30 on their on-site tribute widgets. This was the approximate cost to power a site for a month several years ago but has since needed updating. In order to get closer to the actual cost to-date, we tested using a $50 ask instead – knowing that we could potential lose donors but perhaps make up the difference in revenue by seeing a higher average gift. This was tested among multiple places on-site where a tribute widget appeared.
The first test ran (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/j6kp) was a $60 ask and that proved to be too high for site visitors – resulting in a valid 16% decrease in conversion rate and a decrease in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Increasing the ask amount for site visitors from $30 to $50 resulted in a 10.3% decrease (LoC 90%) in donations for all traffic. When looking at revenue we saw a 1.7% decrease (LoC 40%) - which is virtually no difference in revenue. While the larger ask decreased conversion rate, it did not decrease conversion rate as much as the $60 ask and the higher average gift made up for the difference in revenue.
It's suggested that in the next iteration of the test, we pose a lower ask for a shorter time period (i.e. $25 powers a site for 2 weeks) in order lower the barrier to entry and increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with lapsed members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/22/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign.  The email was a personal communication informing lapsed members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask lapsed members to “reactivate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage lapsed members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed donors resulted in a 28.0% increase in the click-through rate. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
Note: While the click-through rate increased the conversion rate decreased by 50%. The sample size was not large enough to attain the required level of confidence of 95% or above.
In the future, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing different variations of the call to action to determine the most effective approach for encouraging lapsed donors to re-engage. By using what was learned from this experiment, we can refine our strategies and optimize our communication to better connect with our audience and achieve our goals.

experiments.title: Does running brand ads impact CYE donations on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Daily Devotional 2024 CYE 2023 - treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by running brand ads for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there will be brand awareness and knowledge of who PTV is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
December is always a key month for nonprofit fundraising. PTV is no different. Every December, PTV offers a Daily Devotional for the next year as well as a matching challenge.
For this year, on Facebook, we decided to run brand ads to see if this would increase the number of donations.
For the set up, we did 2 campaigns in Facebook.
The control ran Dec 1-31 with ads promoting the Daily Devotional and matching challenge.
The treatment ran Dec 1-15 with brand ads (no ask) just ads about PTV optimized for reach. Then from Dec 16-31 this campaign ran the same Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Brand awareness can positively impact conversion rates:** The treatment group, which focused on brand ads rather than direct ask ads, saw a 16.1% increase in Purchases for All Traffic. This indicates that building brand awareness through ads can lead to higher conversion rates.

2. **Variations in different metrics:** Clicks experienced a significant decrease, while Complete Registrations saw a substantial increase during the treatment phase. Cost per Click, Cost per Registration, and Cost per Purchase also showed notable changes. Additionally, there were decreases in purchases across different visitor types (65+, Mobile, Tablet, Desktop, Female, and Male visitors).

3. **Statistical confidence levels:** While the changes in metrics were significant, the statistical confidence levels were not high enough (85% or above) to consider the results valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Consider longer test durations:** Running experiments for a longer duration may help in achieving higher statistical confidence levels, providing more reliable results for decision-making.

2. **Diversify test approaches:** While brand awareness seems to have a positive impact on conversion rates, it is essential to continue testing different strategies to find the most effective approach for fundraising campaigns.

3. **Segmentation analysis:** Understanding the impact of different visitor types on conversion rates can help tailor campaigns to specific audiences, potentially increasing overall effectiveness.

Overall, while the experiment showed promising results in terms of the impact of brand ads on conversion rates, it is crucial to conduct further analysis and experiments with higher statistical confidence levels before making significant decisions based on these findings.

experiments.title: Does running brand ads impact CYE donations on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by running brand ads for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there will be brand awareness and knowledge of who PTV is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2023 - 12/31/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
December is always a key month for nonprofit fundraising. PTV is no different. Every December, PTV offers a Daily Devotional for the next year as well as a matching challenge.
For this year, on Facebook, we decided to run brand ads to see if this would increase the number of donations.
For the set up, we did 2 campaigns in Facebook.
The control ran Dec 1-31 with ads promoting the Daily Devotional and matching challenge.
The treatment ran Dec 1-15 with brand ads (no ask) just ads about PTV optimized for reach. Then from Dec 16-31 this campaign ran the same Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads.
Results are based on December 16-31 as this is the timeframe that would show the fruits of the brand ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that running brand ads on Facebook can potentially lead to a higher conversion rate in terms of donations for a nonprofit like Pathway to Victory. The treatment group, which was exposed to brand ads before the Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads, saw a 16.1% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be relied upon for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we will test running brand ads alongside offer ads rather than only brand at the beginning.

experiments.title: How linking straight to a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by going directly to the donation page for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate in gifts because it is a single step process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV shared a book of the month. The process to receive the book is for the email subscriber to make a donation in order to receive the book.
We observed that a majority of email subscribers were making donations in the “generous gift” level therefore we decided to test by skipping the landing page where they choose the level to going straight to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that skipping the landing page and going directly to the donation page for email subscribers resulted in a higher conversion rate in donations. The treatment group saw a 23.2% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, it should be noted that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 81.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered definitive or used for future decision-making.
The average gift for the treatment did decrease by 25% due to the lack of large-level gifts, as the option is not presented on the donation page, only on the landing page.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a significant increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

Based on this experiment, it can be concluded that personalization and a less marketing-like approach can have a positive impact on the conversion rate. This suggests that future experimentation should focus on creating more personalized and less promotional messages for lapsed donors.

To ensure more valid results, future experiments should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence. It might be beneficial to increase the sample size or consider other factors that could influence the conversion rate, such as the timing of the messages or the wording of the texts.

Overall, the experiment highlights the potential effectiveness of personalization in communication with lapsed donors, and this insight should be taken into account when planning future campaigns and experiments.

experiments.title: How adding an anchor button on an experience page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an anchor button at the top of the page for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because it makes the offer more easily accessible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2023 - 11/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For November 2023, PTV released a brand-new children’s book by Julia Jeffress Sadler. To promote this page, PTV built an experience page with tons of graphics, testimonials from readers and images of the interior of the book. At the bottom of the page is where the web visitor can select the offer bundle they would like to receive with their generous donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment was a decrease of 11.6% with a 92% level of confidence in transactions.
We believe that the version with the button actually caused web visitors to miss all value proposition for the offer and made it more transactional in nature.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing. because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
experiments.keyLearnings:

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 84.0%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue exploring the impact of removing images in MMS messages for lapsed donors. However, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient and that the statistical level of confidence reaches the desired threshold. This will help to ensure the validity of the results and allow for more confident decision making. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the reasons behind the increase in donations when images are removed, as this may provide insights for future campaigns and messaging strategies.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for lapsed members affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership.
