For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to reactivate their membership. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and reactivate their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading an email with the problem did not increase click-through rates for lapsed members to reactivate their membership with The Heritage Foundation. The treatment actually resulted in a 19.3% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 46.9% decrease in donor conversion with an 85.3% level of confidence.

Based on these findings, it is important to reconsider the hypothesis and approach for future experimentation. It may be necessary to use a different strategy to engage lapsed members and entice them to reactivate their membership. This could include focusing on the benefits and value of being a member, highlighting success stories or achievements of the organization, or providing exclusive content or offers. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that future experiments have a sufficient statistical level of confidence to ensure the validity of the results.

experiments.title: How does calling out the loss of exclusive membership benefits for lapsed members affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership for lapsed members will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fear of losing something can be a more powerful motivator than the prospect of gaining something of equal value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was sent to lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership in 2024.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that calling out what lapsed members will lose by not reactivating their membership can be a powerful motivator for increasing donor conversion rates. The treatment in which the loss aversion approach was used saw a 105.3% increase in donations compared to the control with an 89% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to refine the messaging and presentation of the loss aversion approach to optimize its impact. Testing different variations, such as emphasizing specific benefits or highlighting the potential negative consequences of not reactivating, could help identify the most compelling approach.

Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the characteristics and preferences of lapsed members who responded positively to the loss aversion strategy. Understanding their motivations and demographics can inform targeted marketing efforts to similar segments in the future.

Overall, this experiment suggests that leveraging loss aversion can be a successful strategy to reactivate lapsed members. Continued testing and refinement will provide more conclusive evidence and insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for existing members affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which employed a more personalized and gratitude-centered approach, did not result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email. In fact, the treatment email had a 13.0% decrease in donations for all traffic.

While the results did not reach a 95% level of confidence, they are approaching validity, indicating that there might be some truth to the findings. Future experimentation should be conducted to further validate these results.

Additionally, the treatment group saw a 11.6% decrease in click-through rate in the email with an 87.9% level of confidence.

Based on this experiment, it is clear that a personalized and gratitude-centered approach may not be effective in encouraging existing members to renew their membership.

When conducting future experiments, it is important to keep in mind the potential impact of a more personalized and gratitude-centered approach on donor behavior. This could be further explored by testing different variations of personalized content or by targeting specific segments of the existing member base.

Overall, this experiment provides valuable insights into the impact of a personalized and gratitude-centered approach on membership renewal rates, and the learnings should be used to inform future experimentation and optimization of renewal campaigns.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher click-through rate because users will be able to see what they’re requesting before clicking out of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the calendar to the email did not have a positive impact on the click-through rate to the landing page. In fact, the treatment group showed a 3.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group with an 80.3% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence to determine the true impact of adding an image to the email. Additionally, other factors such as the content of the email, the subject line, and the timing of the email should be taken into consideration in future experiments. It is also important to continue testing different variables to optimize the email and landing page conversion rates for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How having short direct copy impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having shorter copy for all users will achieve increase donations because they could skip the copy more quickly. because they could skip the copy more quickly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2022 - 07/15/2022
Wrong images
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The hypothesis that shorter copy would lead to increased donations was proven wrong. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

2. The statistical level of confidence for the overall results is below the threshold of 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

3. However, it is worth noting that there was a significant decrease in donations from visitors aged 25-34, with a 99% level of confidence. This suggests that the shorter copy may have had a negative impact on this specific demographic.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the hypothesis before conducting the experiment. The result of this experiment indicates that shorter copy may not always lead to increased donations, so it would be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior.

Additionally, it may be worth conducting further research or experiments specifically targeting visitors aged 25-34 to better understand their behavior and preferences. This could help in creating more effective strategies and messaging for this particular demographic.

experiments.title: Dear Reader You vs. Organization Copy
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the value proposition more you focused for people who land on the dear readers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we will have created appeal in saying you can help people instead of KCBI can help people.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the copy of a dear reader to be more “you” focused instead of organizational focused if we could convert more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: For the KCBI audience, it seems that they would like to give to a ministry that is making a difference. This experiment caused a 51% decrease in donor conversion by changing the copy to be 'you' focused.

experiments.title: How posing a lower, $10 ask for non donors impacts email engagement and giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a specific, lower ask amount for non donors will achieve an increase in click-through rates and ultimately gifts because having a specific, low amount can reduce decision friction and potential anxiety.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2023 - 12/22/2023
During calendar year end, we wanted to activate more nondonors on RTB’s file. In order to lower the threshold for those who have never given, we posed a $10 ask as a test. The control received no specific amount while the treatment received a $10 ask in their email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a specific, lower ask amount of $10 to non-donors can result in an increase in click-through rates compared to not mentioning a specific amount. The treatment version of the email showed a 20.5% increase in clicks for all traffic with a level of confidence of 87%.

Additionally, only two gifts were generated from this email both of which came from the treatment and  both at an amount of $25 which is higher than the initial proposed amount.

It's suggested that this test is ran again with the $10 ask as the starting point of the gift array that the treatment group receives. That way, there's further congruency in the ask and can potentially activate more donors.

experiments.title: How tying claims of value to a match impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by expounding the claim of value and tie to the match for email subscriebrs will achieve an increase in donation conversions because the value prop and impact of the match is more clearly explained.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/29/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During December, PTV has a $500k match available to increase motivation for CYE gifts.
In an effort to increase conversions further, we decided to test listing out claims of value for PTV and tie them directly to the match as a method to increase impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Expounding the claim of value and tie to the match for email subscribers can potentially lead to an increase in donation conversions.
2. The treatment group showed a 22.2% increase in donations, which is promising, but not statistically significant at the traditional 95% confidence level.
3. The statistical level of confidence reached 91.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity and further testing is recommended to confirm the effect.
4. It is important to continue testing and refining the messaging and value proposition to maximize the impact on donation conversions.
5. Future experiments should consider adjusting variables such as the match amount, the format of the message, and the target audience to further optimize the results.
6. The insights gained from this experiment can serve as a starting point for future experimentation and allow for more informed decision-making in improving donation conversions.
Note: we did not run this experiment based on revenue due to additional large gifts that significantly skewed results due to calendar year end and additional motivation of the match.

experiments.title: How adding a Security Callout to a Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the security of an online gift with a callout line for prospective tribute donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because eliminate a potential point of friction to giving — mainly the suspicion that sharing payment info or making an online gift isn’t secure.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 12/26/2023
CaringBridge’s Tribute Donation Page already has elements to reinforce security around the giving experience: a shaded box around the payment info and a lock icon. However, we hypothesized that an added line of copy in the payment section, “Your Information is Secure” would help tie together these elements, call out the security of an online gift, and increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a line of copy to reinforce the security of an online gift in the payment section of the Tribute Donation Page resulted in an 8.2% increase in donations. Although the results are not statistically valid, they are approaching validity with a 87.5% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a follow-up experiment with a larger sample size or longer duration to increase the level of confidence and validate the results.

Overall, the findings of this experiment suggest that calling out the security of the giving experience can help reduce any suspicions or concerns potential donors may have about sharing payment information. By addressing this potential point of friction, organizations like CaringBridge can potentially increase conversion rates and encourage more online donations.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page. because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: How does addressing someone directly and asking them a personal question affect click-through rate for a high urgency email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors will achieve a higher click-through rate because it keeps the user engaged and helps them feel more relatable to the situation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators launched their calendar year campaign with an email that started with a direct statement about how Christians face problems more now than ever before. We decided to test how opening an email with a personalized question to draw them in would affect click-through rate to the donation page.
In previous testing, we’ve seen a more personalized tone perform better because it allows the user to relate to the problem at hand and causes them to engage with the email longer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors can have a positive impact on click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the personalized question at the beginning of the email, saw a 20.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group with an 87.2% level of confidence.

Because of these results, we believe that opening an email with a personalized question rather than a direct statement will keep users engaged with the email causing them to be more likely to click the link to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of personalized tones and messaging to optimize click-through rates. Additionally, it would be important to analyze other metrics such as conversions and donations to understand the full impact of the personalized approach. By continuously testing and iterating, we can refine our email strategies to effectively engage potential donors and drive meaningful action.

experiments.title: How using priming language on a landing page affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the treatment page using “priming” will increase the email sign-up rate for the video series because it primes visitors to associate positive emotions and a sense of warmth with ‘The Inklings’ and the video series, making them more inclined to take action by signing up for access.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 01/26/2024
Hillsdale College is offering a free video series on “The Inklings” through paid Facebook advertising. They wanted to test how using the psychological concept “priming” would affect email conversion on the page to sign up to get the video series. On the treatment page, the priming language helps associate the video series with positive emotions and a sense of warmth like the Inklings experienced when they were discussing literature and theology.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment page using "priming" actually resulted in a decrease in email sign-up rate for the video series. This means that the hypothesis that priming visitors with positive emotions and a sense of warmth would increase their likelihood of signing up for access was incorrect.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of priming on the desired outcome. While priming can be a powerful psychological tool, it is not always effective in achieving the desired results. This experiment suggests that in this particular case, priming may have had a negative effect on the decision to sign up for the video series.
Therefore, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies and messaging techniques that could potentially increase email acquisition. This could involve testing different types of language, imagery, or incentives to better appeal to the target audience. Additionally, conducting further research to gain a deeper understanding of the target audience's motivations and preferences could also inform future experimentation and optimize the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How softening the opening of a Facebook ad impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the opening fear line for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate because fear is not always a successful tactic. Does curiosity perform better?.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/17/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV launched a brand-new book “Are We Living in the End Times?” as a response to the war in Israel. In an effort to increase Facebook conversions we decided to test removing the hard opening sentence “With all the chaos in the world recently, you would not be wrong to start worrying that the End Times are right around the corner.”
Our thought was that this opening line was too much of a discouragement to people especially with so many negative things happening in the world. By removing that line, the ad opens with a more curious approach and less fear.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing a fear-based opening line in a Facebook ad resulted in a significant increase in donations. This suggests that curiosity may be a more effective tactic for driving conversions compared to fear. However, the results are not considered fully valid as the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
To further validate these findings and ensure the intended effect, it is recommended to conduct additional testing. This could involve running similar experiments with different variations of ad copy, such as using a different curiosity-based opening line or testing other emotional appeals. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different ad formats, targeting strategies, or visuals on conversion rates.
By continuing to experiment and iterate on ad messaging and strategies, the nonprofit can optimize their Facebook campaigns to achieve higher conversions and ultimately increase fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a more generalized ask in a sticky bar for Giving Tuesday impacted click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the message in terms of a broader impact will resonate with more people and lead to a higher click-through rate because it is more emotionally engaging.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
On Giving Tuesday, Dallas Theological Seminary made a concerted effort to acquire more monthly donors, called Cornerstone Partners. They’ve typically taken an approach that outlines the number of new Cornerstone Partners needed before the midnight deadline.
This year, they wanted to see if a more general ask would lead to higher click-through rates because the goal number could be perceived as overwhelming for someone reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version of the message, which framed the ask in terms of a broader impact, did not lead to a higher click-through rate compared to the control version. In fact, the treatment version saw a significant decrease in clicks by 61.1% with a 93.1% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the hypothesis was incorrect and that framing the message in terms of a broader impact may not resonate with more people or be more emotionally engaging. Therefore, it may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates and acquiring more monthly donors.
In future experimentation, it would be necessary to further test different messaging strategies to determine what resonates most with the target audience. It is important to take into account the specific characteristics and motivations of the donors to understand what messaging would be most compelling to them.
Additionally, it would be advisable to conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and run the experiment for a longer period to increase the statistical validity of the results. This would help ensure that the findings are more reliable and generalizable.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different messaging approaches and continuously refining strategies to optimize donor engagement and acquisition.

experiments.title: How aligning call-to-action verbs in a tribute widget impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning all the call-to-action words in the triibue widget for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because there will be additional clarity and affirmation in the conversion process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
CaringBridge’s users heavily rely on the tribute widget in order to make a gift in honor of their loved one. In order to increase conversion rate, we sought to add clarity to the pre-existing copy by using consistent CTA’s and driving the desired action: give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning all the call-to-action words in the tribute widget did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.3% in donations for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw the results vacillating between flat and a slight decrease throughout the experiment. This suggests that creating congruency in the call-to-action words for the tribute widget is not a significant motivating factor for journal page viewers. They likely already have enough clarity from the existing copy about what the tribute widget is asking of them

It's suggested we test other concepts in the copy such as an emotional appeal to see if that will drive an increase in giving.

experiments.title: How aligning call-to-action verbs in a tribute widget impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning all the call-to-action words in the triibue widget for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because there will be additional clarity and affirmation in the conversion process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
CaringBridge’s users heavily rely on the tribute widget in order to make a gift in honor of their loved one. In order to increase conversion rate, we sought to add clarity to the pre-existing copy by using consistent CTA’s and driving the desired action: give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning all the call-to-action words in the tribute widget did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.3% in donations for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw the results vacillating between flat and a slight decrease throughout the experiment. This suggests that creating congruency in the call-to-action words for the tribute widget is not a significant motivating factor for journal page viewers. They likely already have enough clarity from the existing copy about what the tribute widget is asking of them

It's suggested we test other concepts in the copy such as an emotional appeal to see if that will drive an increase in giving.

experiments.title: How an image of the premium impacted revenue for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for existing donors will achieve a higher revenue because their is a visual appeal of the new book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book in the promotion resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence is not quite at the desired 95%, the results are approaching validity, indicating that the image likely had a positive impact on donor response.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of images in promotions to further validate the results. This could involve testing different types of images, such as a picture of the author or a graphic related to the book's content, to see if they have a similar effect on donor response.

Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze other metrics, such as click-through rates and conversion rates, to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of including images in promotions. This will further inform decision-making and provide a more accurate assessment of the effectiveness of different promotional strategies.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that including an image of the book in promotions can have a positive impact on donor response. By continuing to test and refine this strategy, the nonprofit can potentially increase revenue and attract more donors.

experiments.title: How a premium offer dear reader CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader premium offer on podcast pages will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2023 - 08/14/2023
FamilyLife has a number of popular podcasts with thousands of visitors a month coming to their site to tune in. In reviewing these podcast pages, we hypothesized that we could provide further value for visitors and increase donor conversion by presenting a premium offer in a “dear listener” message on the page.
In our prior testing, we found that an end-of-article dear reader would increase donor conversion on article pages by reducing the friction of making a gift and increasing the appeal of the donation CTA through more space for value-driven copy. We believed that a dear listener message would achieve the same result by providing a way for listeners to engage in a deeper way with what they were hearing and receive a valuable resource in exchange for their gift.
We selected their most popular premium offer, Seeing Beautiful Again and created a dear-listener message on the podcast pages to promote it. We split traffic evenly and ran the experiment for a full month.
experiments.keyLearnings: The dear listener message improved donor conversion significantly. While we weren't able to validate this increase to a 95% or greater level of confidence, the lack of a negative impact from the treatment makes us confident that featuring a dear-listener premium offer is more likely than not to improve donor conversion. For our next experiment we will test this premium offer against a different one to see which sort of offer best aligns with the visitors to these podcasts. We will also continue to test the placement of the message on the page to see how that impacts donor conversion and user friction.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout Impacts Sign-Up Rate on Offer Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the How to Memorize Scripture landing page will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because it removes anxiety for those who might wonder if their information is secure or why The Navigators needs their email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2023 - 06/10/2023
The Navigators’ landing page for How to Memorize Scripture was experiencing a slowing in sign-up rate. We hypothesized that adding a security callout at the bottom of the form will increase sign-up rate. This test was conducted on another offer, and was expanded to How to Memorize Scripture to see the result of more testing.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment led to a 12% decrease in sign-up rate with a 94% level of confidence. Although, this is technically just under the accepted threshold for validity (95%) this in conjunction with similar results from the same test on a different offer does  indicate that perhaps this learning should be observed for Navigators' audience.
Why would a callout for security cause less people to sign-up? Perhaps in an attempt to limit anxiety around giving up an email, the callout actually introduced it. If their security hadn't been a consideration before, and the test then bring attention to it — it may have led users to overthink about "Do I really want to give my email? Is this safe?"
The takeaway here is that perhaps security wasn't a big concern for Navigators' audience. An effort to improve the sign-up rate on the page, should move on to testing other factors that might be inhibiting conversion.

experiments.title: How Turning Campaign Graphics into a Plain-Text Design Affects Conversion on Donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain-text donation page rather than stylized campaign graphics for RTB’s FYE campaign  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it positions the copy/value as being from a human..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 07/03/2023
For Reason to Believe’s Fiscal Year End campaign, they had created an array of graphics illustrating their goals and the impact of their Scholar Community. We hypothesized that removing these graphics on the donation page in favor of a plain text design would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. Note that any copy in the images was translated into bullet points for the plain-text treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Plain-text donation pages are more effective in converting donors compared to stylized campaign graphics. The treatment group experienced a 332.0% increase in donations, indicating that the plain-text design resonated better with potential donors. The plain-text design may have created a more personal and relatable experience for visitors, leading to higher engagement and donations.
The statistical level of confidence for the increase in donations is 93.2%, which is approaching the 95% threshold for validity. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to ensure the results are consistent and reliable — the test should also have a greater sample size than the 7 donations for validity.
Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing and refining the plain-text donation page design. Further experimentation can be conducted to validate the results and explore additional factors that may influence donor conversion rates.
Overall, this experiment provides valuable insights for improving donor conversion rates through the use of plain-text design and a more human-centric approach. By continuing to iterate and test, the organization can optimize its donation page for better fundraising outcomes.

experiments.title: How a Lower Suggested Gift and Array Impacts Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array and reversing it to start low to high for users on the Designed to the Core premium will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lowered “entry level” will appeal to a wider variety of donors versus excluding those who may not be ready to give a $500 gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s donation pages go from highest to lowest, starting at a high of $500 and then decreasing to the suggested gift of $50. We hypothesized that reversing this array to go from low to high as well as lowering the four amounts will improve the donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which involved lowering the gift array and reversing it to start low to high, showed a significant increase in donor conversion rate compared to the control version.
2. The treatment resulted in a 163.2% increase in donations for all traffic.
3. The statistical level of confidence reached 88.1%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but are not yet considered fully valid.
4. Further testing would be necessary to confirm if the treatment has the intended effect with a higher level of confidence.
Based on these learnings, it is clear that the changes made in the treatment version resonated with donors. The lower entry level and the reversed order of the gift array likely appealed to a wider variety of potential donors, attracting those who may not have been ready to give a $500 gift.
To continue experimenting in the future, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to further validate the effectiveness of the treatment. This could involve increasing the sample size, extending the duration of the experiment, or implementing additional variations to the gift array. By doing so, the organization can gain more confidence in the impact of these changes and make informed decisions about optimizing its donation pages to maximize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of a donation with visual cues affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2023 - 03/18/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at or near the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a security “lock” icon to the button affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a security "lock" icon to the donation button resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the treatment showed a 122.8% increase in revenue, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.0%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid but can be considered directional with potential for future iterations.
However, the positive impact observed in this experiment suggests that including more prevalent security-themed enhancements may be effective in addressing donor hesitancy and increasing donation rates. Therefore, it would be advisable to continue testing and experimenting with different security enhancements in future campaigns to further validate their impact on donor conversion rates.
It is important to note that future experiments should consider a longer testing period to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. This will ensure that the results are more reliable and can be used for making informed decisions.
Overall, the key learning is that security-themed enhancements have the potential positively increase average gifts and overall revenue. Further experimentation is needed to verify these findings and determine the most effective security enhancements to implement in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of donation information with an impact statement beneath the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more prevalent security-themed statement below the donate button will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 04/01/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitant or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the security of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The inclusion of a security-themed statement below the donate button had a negative impact on donation rates, with a significant decrease in donations for the treatment.

2. Although the results of this experiment are not statistically valid due to a level of confidence below 85%, the consistently negative trends observed across multiple metrics and segments indicate that caution should be taken when considering the implementation of a security-themed statement in future experimentation.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to further investigate and address the underlying drivers of hesitancy or concerns among potential donors. Future experimentation should focus on optimizing the donation process, simplifying the user experience, and building trust through other means such as social proof, testimonials, or transparency in how donations are used.

Additionally, it may be valuable to conduct user research or surveys to better understand the reasons for hesitancy and gather feedback from potential donors. This qualitative data can provide insights into the specific concerns and help inform future experimentation and optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How does using specificity and more active language that more closely relates to the mission in a donation page headline affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more active/specific language to the headline on Care Net’s main donation page will increase donation rates by better matching donor motivation and adding value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/07/2023
While Care Net’s homepage (which drives ~40% of traffic to the donation page over past 12 months), their mission, and even the gift array are focused largely on choice/options/alternatives for parents as a means to prevent abortion, this aspect is absent from the donation page headline, which is currently focused on saving lives, post Roe.
This could be causing confusion for potential donors as to “why to give” and a simple headline change may create a stronger “message match” and more incentive for potential donors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved adding more active/specific language to the headline on Care Net's main donation page, did not have the intended effect of increasing donation rates. Instead, it resulted in a 28.3% decrease in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in donations for all traffic was 88.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for validity. However, the results are approaching validity, meaning that this is a directional indicator that this result could happen again.

3. When looking at specific segments, the treatment had a significant negative impact on returning visitors, with a 583% decrease in donations and a 98% level of confidence. It also had a significant negative impact on visitors aged 45-54, with an 829% decrease in donations and a 99% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the treatment did not resonate with potential donors and had a negative impact on donation rates. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in the donation page headline to ensure it aligns with donor motivations and adds value. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the specific segments that were significantly affected, such as returning visitors and visitors aged 45-54, to better understand their preferences and tailor the messaging to their needs.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2023 - 04/28/2023
Historically, Care Net has typically run a sticky bar that dynamically populates the number of donors needed across their website during high urgency campaigns. This year, we wanted to test and see how adding a gift array to this would impact donor conversion to have the amount selected happen at this level instead of when they are redirected to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner led to a significant decrease in donor conversion for all traffic, with a 47.1% decrease.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this decrease is 91.1%, which means that while it is not yet considered valid, it is approaching validity.

3. Returning visitors also saw a significant decrease in donations by 862% with a 98% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the addition of a gift array to the home page sticky banner did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion. It actually had a negative impact on donations, particularly for female visitors and returning visitors.

In future experimentation, it would be important to further investigate why the gift array had this negative impact and to explore alternative approaches that may be more effective in increasing donor conversion. This could involve conducting user research to better understand why certain segments of visitors were deterred from donating and using these insights to inform the design of future experiments.

It is also important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This indicates that the results may not be statistically significant and further testing is necessary to draw more conclusive findings. Therefore, we'd like to test this again during their fiscal year-end campaign in June.

experiments.title: How the structure and design of the email acquisition page impacts email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more designed landing page that presents the reader with a different experience showing components of the course will lead to a higher email sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 07/02/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a study guide about the Book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We want to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page therefore we decided to test the design of the landing page.
Historically, we’ve tested into a more simple donation page, so with this treatment, we wanted to see if a more designed approach that presents the characteristics of the study guide in a new way would lead to more people signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which involved a more designed landing page, resulted in a 9.2% decrease in email acquisitions for all traffic compared to the control page with a 90.4% level of confidence.

2. Desktop visitors had a significant decrease of 216% in email acquisitions compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 99%.

Based on these findings, it can be concluded that the more designed landing page did not lead to a higher email sign-up rate. In fact, it had a negative impact, particularly among desktop visitors.

experiments.title: How a humanized donation page impacted gifts from survey takers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a donation page for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the message is aligned from a relevant staff member and positioned as a personal request.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/05/2023
On an instant donation page after visitors completed a survey, we wondered if the value proposition message would resonate more strongly with potential donors if it was presented in the first person. Since the survey was relevant to investigative journalism, we tested writing the donation page language as a message from an investigative journalist “Ryan” and signing the donation page with his name as if it were a personal message to the reader. We positioned this against the winning control from our previous test – using language around the principle of scarcity and referencing that only 1% of survey takers would take an invested next step toward change by making a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personal message from an investigative journalist did not resonate more strongly with potential donors. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 12% decrease in donations compared to the control with 83% level of confidence.

This experiment suggests that potential donors may not respond favorably to a personal message from a staff member. It is important to carefully consider the messaging and tone used in donation pages to ensure that they are effective in motivating donors to take action.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other messaging strategies that may be more effective in driving donations. It may also be valuable to further analyze the specific group of new visitors who saw a significant decrease in donations and determine if there are any underlying factors that contributed to this outcome.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and iterating on donation page messaging to continually optimize and improve conversion rates. Since this was a radical redesign from the existing copy, we could also pair the 1% language as a humanized message from Ryan going forward and see if pairing the winning message with an isolated variable makes a positive impact on conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and specificity to an email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate (Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity for site visitors  will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate  because it reduces anxiety and increase the perceived value of what they are receiving by subscribing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2023 - 07/01/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section. The treatment copy let’s people know why they would want to be part of their email list and what specific communications they would be receiving. Our hypothesis is that this added clarity, specificity and value language would increase email acquisition rate. We ran this test once and saw a lift in acquisition rate among returning visitors but a decrease among new visitors. The initial test had header language that encouraged people to commit to disciplemaking. Our hypothesis was that it was too large of a commitment for new site visitors to make. So we kept the spirit of the test (adding clarity) and changed the headline to be more accessible for people new to the Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a directional 30% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 89%. While not a statistically valid result, it affirms our hypothesis that the initial header may have been too much of a commitment for newer site visitors. New visitors experienced a 33.2% increase in name acquisition with a level of confidence of 87.7%. Using the new header and continuing to add clarity in the CTA copy resulted in an increase in names acquired. In fact, new visitors experienced the larger impact in name acquisition.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt.3)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety. In the second experiment, we saw a decrease in conversion rate when using “page.”
In this particular experiment we tested this concept on a separate offer that used slightly different verbiage in the button and started with the language “page” under the first assumption that people responded more to the use of “page”. Therefore, the control is “page,” treatment 1 is “site,” and treatment 2 is “journal.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment affirms what was uncovered in experiment #2 - "page" (the control in this experiment) is not universally beneficial. In fact the usage of "site" resulted in a 93% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 80%. While the use of "journal" resulted in a 138% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 91%.
Based on this, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of "journal" as a potential alternative to "site" or "page".
Overall, these findings suggest that there is value in exploring alternative language options to reduce perceived commitment or anxiety when prompting users to create a site on CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How unique value propositions impacted clicks for a direct ask sticky bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the giving motivation of equipping the next generation of Christian leaders for website visitors will achieve an increase in click conversions because that value will resonate the strongest with our audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/30/2023
DTS is amid their fiscal year end fundraising campaign. With 15 days remaining, we want to add a website sticky bar as an additional revenue opportunity. We’ve built two different sticky bars unique in value proposition. The control uses the value proposition of “reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible” and the control used the value proposition of “equipping the next generation of Christian leaders.” We want to see which value resonates the strongest with our website audience and generates the most clicks to the FYE donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment sticky bar resulted in a 33.3% decrease in clicks with a nearly valid 90.6% level of confidence. The treatment produced 22 clicks compared to 52 to the control.
We noticed the treatment heading in the wrong direction so we ended the experiment after 11 days in order to send 100% of traffic to the control variant.
It seems the control value proposition of "reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible" resonates the most with our online audience. There are other factors to consider with the experiment, however.
The treatment led with "SPECIAL OPPORTUNITY" while the control led with "DEADLINE APPROACHING". Perhaps the control was more effective in this case as it notified users of an important deadline immediately. The treatment lead-in is much more broad which could lead users to overlook it or move casually past it. Also worth noting is the treatment mentioned the Challenge Gift amount of $600,000 while the control only referenced the Challenge Gift without stating the amount.
It's possible that including the $600k amount turned visitors off as they may have felt as if their modest gift would not make a significant difference in the overall goal.
These are all isolated elements that would be worth testing into to get more refined results. Though, in this high urgency scenario, we wanted to act fast and get our best two sticky bars copy versions out to the public.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted monthly giving for membership renewals
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign with customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals. because  the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 07/02/2023
For visitors looking to renew their membership from the homepage, we wondered if the existing donation page language for renewals was too focused on the organization. We developed a radical redesign language with customized value proposition for existing members who are already familiar with the organization and had shown historical motivation to make a gift. We hypothesized that increasing the value prop elements of appeal and exclusivity would frame a significantly more attractive way of describing what the user desires. Even if the internal goal is to get a user to renew their membership, the user wants to help protect our nation’s founding principles and keep their membership active. We wanted to communicate this clearly on the page to see if it would increase conversion. We updated the language to recognize that we were talking to visitors who had previously supported the organization and had arrived on this page to make an additional gift to support the organization. We also tested the presentation of the monthly ask, changing it from a checkbox after the gift amount had already been selected to a tabbed form with “Join the Leaders Club” as the text for monthly gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two months, the radical redesign treatment of the donation page with a customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew resulted in a 97% increase in Leaders Club Member conversion with 92% level of confidence. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results are directionally approaching validity. This suggests that the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focused on what the user wants to do rather than the organization's internal goals successfully attracted more donors. The treatment version also removed an additional step that asked the visitor to select which area their donation should go towards, so this reduced friction could have also played a role in the increased conversion.
We also saw flat results for one-time donations on this page, so there is opportunity to isolate the value proposition copy and do further testing to increase one-time gifts as well as monthly. We believe future iterations could include additional clarity around the benefits of being a Leaders Club member in addition to the tabbed form design and title to help more visitors choose this option.
Overall, the key learning is to focus on the user's desires and motivations, enhancing the value proposition in alignment with what we already know about the intended audience, in order to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a content offer instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because it will provide extra incentive for people to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s School Choice survey is producing a low instant donor conversion rate once users complete the survey and are redirected to an instant donation page. In an effort to increase donation conversion rate, we’ve built a treatment that leverages a premium offer when someone makes a donation of at least $10.
The premium is TPPF’s Pocket Constitution. We’ve seen past success when offering the Pocket Constitution as a free offer, so we want to see if using the content offer as a premium in this instance proves to be successful through an increase in donations.
The only difference between the control and treatment donation page is the closing bold paragraph in the treatment that informs page visitors of the premium opportunity and incudes an image of the premium to the right of the paragraph for visual appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, the treatment produced a 100% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 84.5% level of confidence. The treatment produced zero donations compared to two to the control.

Despite the nearly valid level of confidence, the outcome of this experiment should be considered lightly due to a total of only two transactions. The recommended number of transactions to confidently review outcomes is 50, so these results are well below that bar.

The traffic sources to this experiment was limited to only a Dear Reader, as we turned the School Choice offer off in paid social due to consistently low performance. Thus, this experiment did not benefit from the healthy amount of traffic it needed to generate more transactions.

The treatment would be worth testing again on another more popular/active offer to gain a better idea of the effectiveness of the premium concept and the specific premium offer.

experiments.title: How a pulsing heart sticky donate button affected donations on homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a side donate button for homepage visitors will achieve higher conversion in donors because the side button has a pulsing sacred heart and is eye catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donations from the Spiritual Directions Homepage we decided to test adding a side sticky donate button with a pulsing heart.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment version with the side donate button featuring a pulsing sacred heart significantly increased donations, with a 193.6% increase for all traffic.
2. The treatment version had a higher level of confidence (91.2%) compared to the control version, indicating that the results are approaching validity.
3. When looking at specific visitor segments, the treatment version showed a significant increase in donations for desktop visitors (5903% increase with 97% confidence level) and email visitors (9% increase with 96% confidence level).
4. The changes made in the treatment version, including the addition of the side donate button with a pulsing sacred heart, seem to have a positive impact on donor conversion.
To use these learnings in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests to validate the findings and increase the level of confidence. This could involve testing different versions of the side donate button, such as variations in size, color, or animation. Additionally, exploring other elements on the homepage that could be modified, such as placement or wording of the donate button, could provide further insights into increasing donations. It's also important to consider the specific characteristics of different visitor segments and tailor the donation strategy accordingly, as seen with the significant increase in donations for desktop and email visitors in this experiment.
When results of the blog page experiment is combined with the blog pages results of the same test (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6142) we find a 66% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence

experiments.title: How a mysterious subject line + conversational email copy impacted email clicks

rows.name: Version A - Subject Line: The taxes you pay

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a mysterious subject line coupled with conversational email copy for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because more people will open the email to find out the meaning of the mysterious subject line and the conversational appeal of the email will lead more people to click to find out more about the focus article of the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 06/09/2023
IPI is sending a monthly cultivation email to their housefile email list. In order to learn about our audience and what motivates them to open emails and click within emails, we’ve set up a subject line + body copy experiment.
1/4 of our list will receive
control subject line and control body copy
treatment subject line with control body copy
control subject line with treatment body copy
treatment subject line with treatment body copy
The treatment subject line plays into curiosity and intrigue, while the treatment body copy takes an especially conversational approach, sharing the perspective of the sender while also asking for the thoughts and feedback of the reader.
We want to see which combination generates the most clicks within the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment #2 (treatment subject line + control email copy) produced a nearly valid (89.9% level of confidence) 11.9% increase in email clicks. Treatments #1 (control SL and treatment email copy) and #3 (treatment SL and control email copy) produced negative results with invalid level of confidences.

Thus, treatment #2 was the big winner here. Our audience has responded well to mystery subject lines in the past, and we now have more evidence that this approach to subject line framing is effective. These type of subject lines creative intrigue and curiosity, prompting recipients to open the email to find out what's inside. The control subject line did not have the same curiosity, as it exposed the topic up front.

When we look specifically at open rates, treatment #2 produced a 49.3% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence; treatment #3 produced a 24.5% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence.

Ultimately, we should lead many more cultivation emails using mysterious subject lines.

As for the body copy element, perhaps the conversational approach consisted of too much fluff, as the control – less conversational – variant was a bit more to the point and brief, and resulted in higher click rates. We may want to be more considerate of our recipients' time and focus more on clarity and being direct when sending future cultivation emails.

experiments.title: How a Donation Interruptor Impacts Monthly Giving on an Offer’s Instant Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donation interruptor  for users who pre-select a one-time gift will achieve an increase monthly donation conversion because psychologically it lowers the amount of their original gift, while providing value prop copy about how that smaller amount each month has a far greater impact..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 07/02/2023
The Donation Interruptor is an experiment that we’ve tested on IMB’s general donation page in the past. For a greater collection of data, we wanted to test the experience on one of their offers, a Course on Prayer. The tabbed form on the donation page pre-selects One-Time, we hypothesized that a pop-up appearing directly after they submit their gift amount (but before they provide their payment info) will increase monthly gifts. The reason being that the popup will present the user value copy around how a lower amount (but each month) can make a greater impact even though a lower gift upfront.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing the Donation Interruptor on the Course on Prayer offer resulted in a significant increase in monthly donations for all traffic. Although the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, and the results are approaching validity.
This suggests that the interruptor pop-up, which highlights the impact of smaller monthly donations, has the potential to influence donors to choose the monthly giving option instead of a one-time gift. However, further testing is necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.
One observation is that although this increased monthly gifts, the treatment does appear to lower the total number of gifts. This however, did not validate as a statistical result. It is something to continue observing, while also keeping in mind that repeated monthly gifts can still be an exponential revenue gain even despite a small decrease off in total gifts

experiments.title: How donation page value proposition led by evidentials impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that angling donation page value proposition based on evidentials for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain a better grasp of the problem and become more motivated to contribute to the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 06/09/2023
IPI has launched a state fiscal responsibility survey that culminates with a donation opportunity once someone completes the survey. We want to test two donation pages each with a different approach.
The control will test into clarity: Painting a picture for potential donors of why we put together this survey for them, what the plan is behind it, and why their help is needed.
The treatment will test into evidentials, alerting potential donors to the extent of the problem by pointing to multiple facts and research. We want to see which approach resonates best with them and leads to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the control approach of painting a clear picture and explaining the "why" for potential donors is more effective in achieving donations compared to the treatment approach led by evidentials.

The treatment resulted in a 68.0% decrease in donations for All Traffic with a nearly valid 93.0% statistical level of confidence. Revenue also saw a 29% decrease through the treatment with a 66% level of confidence.

Donors appreciated the clarity of the control which gave them a clear understanding of why we're offering this survey, how it will help improve Illinois fiscal responsibility, and why we need their help. One possible reason that donors did not respond as well to the treatment is because they already have a firm understanding of the problem at hand and did not need the additional background or context.

We should keep these experiment results in mind as we build value proposition for future offer donation pages. It may be wise to test into these approaches again to gain more data. But in this instance, clarity seems to be the key!

experiments.title: How testing gift images in a dear reader helps influence gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding images of the thank you gifts for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates because people want to see the gifts they will be getting as a thank-you for their donation..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. During their spring pledge drive, we wanted to run a 3-way test to see whether promoting their premium thank-you gifts using a dear reader style placement would help influence more donations.
We’ve tested dear reader asks in the past, with little lift in direct donations. So this test was designed to see whether having the images, versus not (and no-dear reader at all) would help influence overall online donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a lot of direct donations that can be attributed to the dear readers, we did see a slight lift approaching a valid level of confidence by using the dear reader overall, with a higher lift in donor conversion rates through using the images of potential thank you gifts.

Further testing of this concept would test the placement of the dear reader, as well as strengthening the language and making the connection between those reading the news articles and the 'why' they need to donate to support the work of WUNC.

experiments.title: How the way we address page visitors once they arrive to the donation page impact donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing page visitors as “Friend” or by their first name will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will appreciate the personal touch of a salutation and feel like they’re being spoken to conversationally rather than just visiting a regular old web page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
ACI’s Cancel Culture donation page is producing a low instant donor conversion rate compared to their other offers. We wondered if the way we address donation page visitors once they arrive on the page would impact donations.
We separated this test into a, b, and c variant.
The “a” variant is a regular headline
The “b” variant implements “Friend” in the headline
And the “c” variant implements the user’s first name in the headline
We want to see if either the “Friend” or first name headlines move the needle in terms of increasing donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 15 days, each treatment did produce an increase in donor conversion rate compared to the control. Treatment 1 (Friend acknowledgement) produced a 28.9% increase with a 63.1% level of confidence, while treatment 2 (first name acknowledgement) produced a 42.4% increase with an 80% level of confidence.
Treatment 2 also produced a 20.6% increase in revenue with a 69% level of confidence.
It's clear that our donation page visitors prefer a personal acknowledgement over no acknowledgement at all.
Interestingly, new visitors led to the highest increase in donations when being addressed as "Friend". This segment resulted in a 64% increase with a nearly valid 83.1% level of confidence. Our existing visitors responded best to the first name acknowledgement.
We plan to run a new a/b test as our next iteration testing "Friend" vs First Name against each other, eliminating the current control, to see which generates the greater increase in donor conversion. This test should be able to run for the fully recommended 30 days to see if either variant reaches a valid result.
It will be interesting to see if we see the same trend in the results for the way new visitors and existing visitors are being addressed when we run the a/b test.

experiments.title: How personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality impacted donations during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality for pledge donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. Since a lot of web traffic during pledge drives is driven by offline sources (mainly radio) we wanted to see whether personalizing a sticky bar with a quote/image from a specific on-air personality, who would be on the air during a specific time and day, would make an impact on overall online donations.
We ran this as a 3-way test, with a control, sticky bar with quote but no image, and sticky bar with quote and image of on-air personality.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test whether personalizing a sticky bar with a specific on-air personality would impact overall online donations for WUNC, a nonprofit NPR station. The treatment showed a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic, with an 87.6% statistical level of confidence, indicating that personalizing the sticky bar had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the results are not entirely valid and require further testing to ensure the intended impact.
While results varied from day-to-day, the overall lift that adding a sticky bar to the homepage during the pledge drive was impactful, with an 87% level of confidence that adding one would help increase overall donations.
Interestingly enough, while the image treatment version had a slightly lower conversion rate vis-a-vis the impact on overall online donations, when digging into the directly-attributed results, the treatment version had 6 gifts compared to the no image version of 3 gifts.
Further testing is definitely warranted, as is trying to quantify the connection between offline (radio) and online traffic.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for Grace Gone Wild impacted conversion on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Grace Gone Wild traditional
PTV DA Grace Gone Wild radical

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by promoting the book with an exciting creative, that matches the cover for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion of donors because there is excitement around the topic and interest.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2023 - 06/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Grace Gone Wild is a book published by PTV. In the past, this book has not be very successful. Therefore in an effort to increase conversions we decided to test a radical approach to the Facebook creative to see if we could increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment was conducted to test whether promoting the book "Grace Gone Wild" with an exciting creative that matches the cover on Facebook would increase conversions.
