The results showed that the treatment had an 11.7% decrease in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the control version had a higher conversion rate. However, since the statistical level of confidence was just below 95%, further testing would be necessary to validate the results fully.
Another significant observation was that the cost per click increased by 67% with a 100% level of confidence. This information could be beneficial in future experimentation.
The key learning from this experiment is that an exciting creative alone may not necessarily increase conversions, as evidenced by the decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment. The language used in the promotional campaign may also be a factor worthy of testing in future experiments.
Based on these results, we should continue to conduct further experimentation to explore the most effective messaging and creative strategies for promoting the book, "Grace Gone Wild."

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Email Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a security callout at bottom of email acquisition form for users on the How to Save a World landing page will achieve an increase in acquisition rate because it relieves the stress and anxiety around how their email will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/03/2023
For Navigators’ How to Save the World landing page, we hypothesized that a simple security callout beneath the form would increase email acquisition rate. This short line of copy reinstates to the user that their information is secure and that they are only providing their email in order to receive the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a security callout at the bottom of an email acquisition form on a landing page may not have a positive impact on the email acquisition rate. The treatment group resulted in a 12.9% decrease in email acquired for all traffic with an 88.3% level of confidence, indicating that the control group was more effective. In addition, desktop visitors had a significant 730% decrease in email acquisition, suggesting that the callout may have had an adverse effect on this device.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct further testing to confirm whether the security callout is effective or not. If it does not work, other possible strategies could include using social proof or highlighting the benefits of subscribing to the newsletter. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the impact of the callout on different devices and user segments to ensure that it is not hurting the acquisition rate. Overall, ongoing experimentation and analysis are vital for improving the effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: How low-to-high gift array affected donor conversion (Adam and Eve)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a low-to-high gift array for users on the Adam and Eve instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will help increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 06/05/2023
Reasons to Believe orders their gift arrays from highest ask to lowest; the assumption being that starting with the larger gift will increase the average gift. However, we hypothesized that for instant donation pages on offers specifically—where perhaps a user isn’t already familiar with RTB— a $250 gift might be a daunting place to start. We believe that lowering the barrier to entry will increase the number of gifts. This will lead to an overall increase in revenue even if the average gift is lowered due to the increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that offering a low-to-high gift array for users on the Adam and Eve instant donation page did not increase donor conversion rate, as hypothesized. In fact, there was a 69.8% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 93.3% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the audience and context of the donation page. It may be that for certain causes or types of donors, a low-to-high gift array is not effective, but for others, it may be more successful. Therefore, further testing is needed, with a focus on identifying the specific factors that lead to successful donation pages. Additionally, it may be helpful to test multiple variations of gift arrays to determine the most effective approach for each audience and context.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition leading into a survey impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enhancing the value proposition communicated on the School Choice landing page will increase email acquisition rates because it will provide visitors with more clarity and appeal on the impact of their survey.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2023 - 06/18/2023
IPI’s School Choice Survey is producing a low 14.4% sign up rate. In an effort to acquire more emails and increase this metric, we’ve built a treatment that adds value proposition copy leading into the survey.
The current survey page includes just two short sentences leading into the survey. Our analysis dictates that these sentences feel transactional, lack personability, and due not do a good job of expressing the value to someone for taking and completing the survey.
That’s why we’ve built a treatment that we believe does those things. We added a call-to-action headline, focused the copy on the reader, and communicated the value and purpose of the survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing the value proposition communicated on the School Choice landing page has a significant impact on email acquisition rates. The treatment with a call-to-action headline, personalized copy, and clear communication of the value and purpose of the survey led to a 24.7% increase in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 82.5% statistical level of confidence.
People are much more likely to complete the action you want them to complete when you express value and the "why" behind the request. If they don't have good reasons to take that action, then they're less likely to take it. By expressing value and focusing on the reader through "you" language, we got more people to complete the survey!
It's also worth noting the the treatment variant led to two donations through the subsequent instant donation page compared to none from the control variant.
We will roll out the treatment variant to the page given the direction lift in email acquisition.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options in a gift array impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the gift array to match the copy callouts for Eagle Lake supporters will achieve an increase in gifts because you eliminate the friction of multiple-choice and better highlight the $150 amount called out in the page copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 05/12/2023
For Navigators’ Eagle Lake campaign, much of the language and copy focused on how a gift helps send kids to camp. The donation page called out the amount of $150 specifically, as what’s needed to send one child to camp this year. The Navigators hypothesized that by eliminating the number of choices in the gift array to either the $150 amount or $75 (while still keeping an option for other) would increase the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 29%. This means that the reduced number of options in the gift array had virtually no impact in people's motivation to give or not. The treatment version of the page, which only had options for $75 and $150, resulted in a 19.0% directional decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 86%. Our hypothesis is that because the control version that included $25 and $300, there were people who were inclined to give the larger amount when presented the option.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different copy callouts and messaging on the donation page or how the gift array is presented from a design standpoint (having a preselected amount).

experiments.title: How adding specificity to your micro-value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing specific Psalms Study Guide information for study guide enrollees will achieve a higher conversion rate because readers have a clearer idea of what they will be giving to and receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
For this theological seminary client, we tested if adding more detailed copy to fund the next biblical resource can increase donor conversion rate on the current Revelation Study Guide donation page.
The control donation page copy shares that the reader’s donation will help fund the following biblical resource. We wanted to run an experiment that goes deeper into this copy and provides the reader with more information on what the following biblical resource is, ie. the Psalms Study Guide, and why it will help them, ie. “deepen their understanding of the Book of Psalms”. We have seen success with previous experiments that go deeper into their copy about what they are offering, which is why we believe this treatment will lead to a greater donor conversion rate.
This treatment experiment has been shown to 50% of the viewers and was run as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding more detailed copy on the funding of the next resource, the Psalms Study Guide, to the donation page copy did not produce a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment page saw a 66.2% decrease in conversion rate with a 90.4% confidence level, so the experiment did not validate. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may help to include an ebook mockup image on the donation page so that viewers can physically see what their donation is going towards and be more motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How listing resources as a bullet list impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by listing resources with bullet points for web traffic will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is clarity and readability in what is being offered. because there is clarity and readability in what is being offered.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 06/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
For PTV’s book about Daniel, we decided to test listing out the resources for each giving level with bullet points. Historically PTV has listed the items as a paragraph.
experiments.keyLearnings: For this experiment, we saw a decrease in donor conversions by 6.8% with a level of confidence at 88.6%. While the results are not valid we were intrigued to see a decrease in performance.

experiments.title: How listing resources as a bullet list impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by listing resources with bullet points for web traffic will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there is clarity and readability in what is being offered..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/01/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
For PTV’s book about Daniel, we decided to test listing out the resources for each giving level with bullet points. Historically PTV has listed the items as a paragraph.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment tested the effectiveness of listing out resources for each giving level with bullet points instead of a paragraph for a book about Daniel by Pathway to Victory (PTV). The hypothesis was that bullet points would increase donor conversion rates due to clarity and readability. However, the treatment resulted in a 9.7% decrease in revenue for all traffic, with a statistical level of confidence of 84.4%.
From a donations perspective, we saw an 8.5% decrease in donor conversion with an 86.2% confidence level.
Both variations did see

experiments.title: How a Wiki-Style Dear Reader impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a Wiki-Style Dear Reader on Hour of Power‘s daily devotional pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is more visually arresting and transforms into a sticky bar if the user scrolls past it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 04/30/2023
Hour of Power is a Christian broadcast ministry that shares inspiring sermons, ebooks, and daily devotionals through their media presence and website. They have seen success in increasing email acquisition on their website’s daily devotional pages by adding Dear Readers below the devotion, and for their spring high-urgency campaign we decided to see how their traditional Dear Reader asking readers to become sustaining monthly donors would perform against a Wiki-Style Dear Reader.
The traditional Dear Reader appears on devotional pages underneath the devotion. The Wiki-Style Dear Reader appears closer to the top of the page and is above the devotional content. If a user scrolls down the page and goes past it, the Wiki-Style Dear Reader will disappear and a smaller version of it will appear at the bottom of the screen like a sticky bar.
The traditional Dear Reader only has one CTA button which brings the user directly to the campaign’s donation page, but the Wiki-Style version has two CTA buttons. The first button also leads the user directly to the donation page, but the second button that says “Give a Different Amount” opens up a small gift array within the Wiki-Style Dear Reader. The user can then select or enter the amount they would like to give and then they will be redirected to the donation page with their selection automatically chosen.
Our hypothesis was that new placement and style of the Wiki-Style Dear Reader would be more arresting and eye-catching for users on the page and would thus increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not fully validate, but we saw a decrease in donor conversion on the Wiki-Style Dear Reader with a 90% LOC. What this reveals about Hour of Power's audience is that they do not respond as well to arresting popups/sticky bars that use high-urgency language. Instead, it shows us that their audience is more responsive to CTAs that take a conversational approach.

In future testing we can try using the same kind of conversational language in the Wiki-Style that we use in the Dear Reader to clarify if their audience wasn't responding because of the language used in the Wiki-Style or if it was due the placement/presentation of the Wiki-Style.

experiments.title: How a slide-out with value prop affected Co-author sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prominently featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author will achieve an increase in Co-Author shares because the slide-out not only educates on the value, it makes it easier to share..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2023 - 05/21/2023
CaringBridge allows users to share their page access with a friend or family member—making them a Co-Author on their CaringBridge page. We hypothesized that making this a more prominent feature on the user’s homepage, would not only help inform them what a Co-Author is—but would also increase the sign-up/share rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main finding of the experiment is that featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author led to a statistically significant increase in the rate of sending invitations to co-authors.
However, the level of confidence suggests that further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of the slide-out feature in promoting co-author shares and to identify the conditions and segments where it works best. Future experiments could focus on refining the design of the slide-out or testing different formats or placements to assess their impact on co-author shares.

experiments.title: How removing a donation page’s header image on mobile affected donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a donation page’s background header image for mobile viewers will achieve an increase in instant donation conversion rate because they will be less distracted by imagery and likely to read more of the value proposition copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase instant donation conversion rates on a policy organization’s voter fraud survey offer, we created a donation page treatment that removed the background header image on the mobile experience.
We believed that by removing this header image and lessening the amount of negative space above the fold, the viewer would read more of the page’s content and be more likely to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
Removing the background header image on the mobile donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing the instant donation conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 54.1% decrease in donations compared to the control version.The treatment version also resulted in a 73.2% decrease in average gift compared to the control version.
The experiment only ran for two weeks, but the decrease in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence and decrease in average gift prompted us to stop the experiment before results could fully validate.
Reasons for the decrease in donations and average gift could include:
The lack of the header image may have led to visitors thinking there was too much copy to read.The header image of the organization's building may contribute to a feeling of legitimacy that helped prospective donors make the decision to contribute.

experiments.title: How increasing the ask amount on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by increasing the “ask amount” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue for this page because there will be a larger average gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
This theological seminary client offered a new biblical resource, the Revelation Online Course. The control donation page for the Revelation Online Course had a $10 donation ask in the copy. The treatment page had the original control donation page copy but also included a larger gift amount ask of a $50 or more donation. This treatment page experiment was set up to show to 50% of viewers as an A/B test. We wanted to test if asking for a larger gift amount in the treatment donation page could increase the average gift and revenue for this online course.
We have noticed in past experiments that including specific donation ask copy can produce a lift in conversion rate. We thought that this donation page copy for this new biblical resource would be a great place to ask specifically for a larger donation of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specific ask amount of $50 or more to the donation page copy for the Revelation Online Course, did not lead to an increase in average gift and revenue. In fact, the treatment page saw a 56.1% decrease in conversion rate. While the statistical sample size is approaching validity, the level of confidence was 88% which did not reach above 95% to validate this experiment. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to explore alternative ways of encouraging larger donations, such as providing more details on the course they will get if they donate $50 or more.

experiments.title: How positioning match oriented language impacted recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2023
During a recurring gift campaign, we wanted to test which message would be more motivating to donors to upgrade from a one time to a recurring gift. On the control page, we highlighted that a matching gift was in place which would double their donation. For the treatment donation page, we went into more detail about the match and focused the value proposition more around doubling the impact of their monthly donation. We split traffic to each page 50/50 for the duration of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors did not achieve the expected increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment page resulted in a 26.5% decrease in donations with a statistical level of confidence just below the acceptable threshold. Therefore, further testing is necessary before making any definitive conclusions about the effectiveness of messaging related to matching gift campaigns for recurring donors. We learned that the match language—on its own—was less effective in converting donors than keeping the values-based language and mentioning the match as one additional incentive to give. In future experimentation, it may be helpful to focus on more targeted messaging and testing smaller changes in order to better understand the impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it may be worthwhile to test other types of incentives beyond matching gifts to determine which strategies are most effective in motivating donors to convert to recurring gifts.

experiments.title: How asking for less money on a parenting premium offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the the donation amount to get the premium for people who have just signed up for a video series course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/05/2023
Focus on the Family is a prominent global ministry that provides video content resources and insights for parents and families on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
We previously tested a lower gift array ($30 instead of $50) with FamilyLife’s Cherish Your Spouse series which led to a valid increase in donor conversion of 37.6% and an increase in the average gift amount. You can find that test here.
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether the impact of the lower gift array would be consistent across all verticals of Focus on the Family’s acquisition offers by running the same test on their Recognizing Your Son’s Need for Respect donation page (a parenting offer) and on Winning your Inner Battles (a faith offer). The results of the Winning Your Inner Battles test can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In our other experiments testing this lower gift array for the Cherish Your Spouse and Winning Your Inner Battles donation pages we saw validated increases in donor conversion, but during this experiment we weren't able to get greater than 95% level of confidence for all traffic.
However, while this test did not validate any increase in donor conversion from the lower gift array, it did show in a directional lift of 68.8% with a 85% level of confidence. When looking at mobile traffic, the primary source for the offer, the LOC increased to 91%.  While this result does not meet the statistical level of confidence of 95% or higher, it does show a trend towards more users converting on the treatment version of the gift array. This result is in line with the results of the testing on Focus' other offers and reveals that, at least right now during high inflation, the lower gift array increases donor activation without harming revenue.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations- attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/01/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
This is our second try at this test. We extended the deadline of the campaign and will re-run this test.
The initial test resulted in a 7.8% increase with a 34.8% level of confidence to the treatment and 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
Let’s see how our second test performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the implementation of a goal thermometer for donation page visitors did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant 46.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group with a nearly valid 83.5% level of confidence.
The treatment went in the wrong direction compared to going in the right direction the first time we ran this experiment. That said, each experiment did produce an increase in revenue through the treatment.
1st test: 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
2nd  test: 16.7% increase in revenue with a 56.4% level of confidence.
Oddly, mobile donations through the treatment increased by 58.7% in the first test but saw a 77.5% decrease the second test. The second test did have a significantly smaller sample size, however.
It would be wise to test this again for a future high urgency campaign. An additional hypothesis would be that a more attainable goal would help produce an increase in donations. The goal was not even halfway reached for this campaign, which may have spoiled donor motivation because they felt their gift would not make a big enough dent towards accomplishing the giving goal. Revenue went up in both experiments, so it'd be interesting to see if it would go up the next time this experiment is run as well.

experiments.title: How prioritizing value proposition on an instant donation page’s header impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because we are reducing anxiety by focusing on “fund” language and giving people a clear call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2023 - 04/30/2023
This experiment serves as a follow up a previous experiment (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/8GVA) where we moved the transactional fund ID language from the header. We hypothesized that this transactional language would not inspire more people give. We wanted to give people the opportunity to read through the entire value proposition before making their gift. So, we tested moving the transactional language and Fund ID to the bottom of the experience, beneath the gift array.
When we initially ran the experiment we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. We hypothesized that there were several factors that contributed to the decrease in donations:
The change headline actually made the “thank you” line not seem like a an actual header. 
Though the fund ID sounds transactional – the first sentence says “Support the Navigators” and it may be that this sentence is giving the reader more clarity as to what they’re supposed to do on that page 
Seeing thank you at the very top with no other CTA may make the person think that this is the end of the interaction.
So we ran a followup experiment where we ran 2 treatments.
The first treatment enlarged the “thank you” line so that it looked like an actual header. The second treatment enlarged the copy and  added a transitional sentence to let people know that this was not just a confirmation page, nor is it the end of the interaction. This transitional copy served as a replacement for the “Support the Navigators” copy and had more value prop-based language.
This was tested across all the active instant donation pages for the Navigators’ offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors can achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. By providing a clear call to action in the header and removing the transactional language (fund ID), the treatment resulted in a 60% directional lift in donations for all traffic, with a 92.0% statistical level of confidence.
Results indicate that the font size of the header had no real impact on conversion rate but when paired with the transitional sentence we saw a significant impact. Our initial hypothesis was correct in that it's likely that ebook downloaders believed that this donation page was actually a confirmation page with no further action to be taken. Additionally, the use of more value based language ("do you want to make resources like this available to others who want to grow in their faith") inspired more people to take action and refocuses people on the "why" of their gift.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a landing page impacts site creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimony for prospective site creators will achieve an increase in site creation conversion rate because social proof reduces anxiety and adds credibility to the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/11/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added social proof in the form of a testimonial on a landing page.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment omitted any kind of testimonial whereas the treatment included a real-life quote from someone who has benefited from created a site. Our hypothesis that adding social proof will reduce any kind of anxiety someone may feel when creating a site with CaringBridge. This quote adds credibility to those who may be unfamiliar with CaringBridge.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a testimonial to a confirmation page may not have the intended effect of reducing anxiety and adding credibility to an organization. The treatment resulted in a 51.0% directional decrease in website creation compared to the control, but the statistical level of confidence was only 86.6%. This means that the results are not considered valid but are approaching validity.
These results are contrary to what was expected. It's probable that the selected testimonial may not be relevant enough to the subject matter of the ebook for this funnel. It's recommended that this is retested in a different ebook funnel to draw further conclusions. Additionally, CaringBridge could anchor the benefits of creating a site by using language like "Join over XXX,XXX families who have benefited from using CaringBridge". This reference point suggests that there are many others who have used CaringBridge successfully, which may add credibility and increase the perceived value of using the platform.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button on article pages with a slide-out donor appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button with a slide-out CTA featuring additional value proposition copy will increase donor conversion by strengthening the value proposition of the sticky donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 03/31/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They had previously tested a sticky donate button on article pages that floated on the right-hand side of the page with a pulsing heart and linked to the main donation page. This sticky button improved donor conversion. They wanted to test a new version of that button with an expanded value proposition that would appear in a slide-out when the button was clicked. This they posited would enable visitors to receive a reason to donate before visiting the donation page itself. Visitors could then choose to give or dismiss the message altogether.
For the experiment, they wanted to test two different value propositions. One would focus on FamilyLife’s standard appeal message about making a gift to support families in crisis. The other would focus on the perceived value the visitor received from FamilyLife this year and ask them to make a gift to keep FamilyLife here for them and their family.
Originally these two variations were going to be tested against the prior winning sticky donate button that lacked a slide-out CTA message. Due to a setup error, the control experience lacked a sticky donate button altogether, so it became an experiment between these two competing value proposition slide-outs and no sticky donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that inviting visitors to reflect on the value FamilyLife provided them and then asking them to donate to keep FamilyLife there for them and their family was far more motivational than asking them to make a gift to help other families. We hypothesize this is because it is harder for a potential donor to think about the needs of unidentified "families in critical need" than it is to remember their needs and how FamilyLife has been their for them.
We will continue testing this value proposition to further refine it and identify key motivational elements for donor conversion. Our next test will be this slide-out CTA sticky button against the former control experience of just a sticky donate button without a slide-out message.

experiments.title: How a lower ask amount affects revenue on a Wikipedia-style article appeal during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a lower recurring donation ask on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal banner will achieve greater revenue by offering a lower commitment, thus increasing donations enough to offset the lower ask amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 03/24/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on recurring donor acquisition, we wanted to know if lowering the ask amount on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal would increase recurring donor rates by offering a lower commitment given that donors would be giving on a monthly basis.
This test followed on the heels of a previous experiment (during the same campaign), where we learned that the Wikipedia-style appeal tactic outperformed both a banner ad and a slide-in appeal when presented on article pages being viewed by prospective donors.
We set this up as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $30 ask and roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $15 ask in the hopes that donor volume would outweigh the lower ask and provide a net increase in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 5 days, we were unable to validate our hypothesis that a lower ask amount would lift donations enough to overcome the lower ask and produce a net increase in revenue over an assumed 11-month period.
The treatment was only able to produce one additional recurring gift and the 48% decrease in revenue (81.4% level of confidence) did not justify continuing to run this experiment. Based on these results, we elected to run the $30 ask for the remainder of the campaign since it appeared that the decreased ask amount did little to increase the volume of donors while cutting overall revenue nearly in half.

experiments.title: How changing the language for existing donors impacts email conversion rates second iteration
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that recognizing a donor’s status for donors will achieve a change in conversion rate because we are showing them that they are more than a email address.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
One of their best-performing offers is a book titled Perfect Ending; this is an offer they run on multiple channels at least once a year. For this specific email appeal, we wanted to test whether recognizing the impact a donor has had on the ministry by changing the language in an email would impact their conversion rates. Our hypothesis was that by referencing that they were a donor, they would be more likely to make a donation. This test was run for the active donor segment only.
We previously tested adding one line of donor language but did not see much of an impact. For this iteration, we added donor language in 2 locations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that recognizing a donor's status in emails for donors is likely to increase the conversion rate. The treatment group showed a 50.2% increase in donations, but further testing is required to confirm the validity of the results. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing the impact of donor language in email appeals and measure the level of statistical confidence to determine the validity of the results. It is also important to continue testing different variations of language to optimize for the highest conversion rate. The findings from this experiment can guide future email appeal strategies for First Baptist Dallas (Pathway to Victory) to encourage donors to continue supporting the ministry.

 

In regards to revenue, we saw a 47.7% increase with a 87.1% level of confidence for the treatment version

Control average gift, $56.67

Treatment average gift, $61.42

experiments.title: Which value prop is more affective in a sponsorship ask for PFI–religion or justice?
experiments.researchQuestion: An appeal to someone’s sense of justice will work better at converting PFI’s audience into sponsors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2020 - 12/02/2020
On Giving Tuesday, PFI was going to send out a sponsorship ask to their general donors. Since they are a religious organization that also deals with the justice system, we wanted to test whether an appeal based on religious reasons or on the basis of justice was more motivating.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not have enough results to validate, and need to be careful in using such a small scaled response as proof that the religious element is more motivating. We will continue to test as their file grows.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a high urgency campaign website sticky bar impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to sticky bar copy promoting a key fundraising opportunity for website visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the value proposition will appeal more to potential donors and motivate them to learn more and make a gift through the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2023 - 03/31/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute was running a high urgency campaign through the end of March. To alert website visitors and get more people to the campaign donation page, we’re testing two sticky bars: One that is very brief and simply alerts users of an urgent matching gift and campaign deadline, and the other includes more value proposition copy, appealing to issues that the user likely cares about and telling them how they can learn more about a way to help – while also pointing out the urgency of a deadline.
Which angle will produce more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition to the sticky bar copy can lead to a significant increase in donations. The treatment version that included more value proposition copy saw a 304.6% increase in donations, with a statistical level of confidence of 94.4%.

All donations completed in this experiment were completed by desktop visitors and returning visitors. It is clear that the treatment sticky bar (with value prop) spoke more effectively to visitors through appeal and motivation. A "matching gift" does not mean as much to our audience as improving certain issues that they are passionate about.

Future sticky bars run for high urgency campaigns should include value proposition language, as that's what our audience wants to hear. It may also be worth testing variations of the treatment version to further optimize the messaging and achieve an even greater increase in donations.

experiments.title: How removing organization-centric language impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing organization-centric language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because we speak about the donor and what they believe and can accomplish rather than ourselves. because  we speak about the donor and what they believe and can accomplish rather than ourselves.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2023 - 03/26/2023
When reviewing performance on an instant donation page, we noticed there were several organization-centric references throughout the copy. We wondered if replacing the phrases where we talk about our organization and what we believe with language that instead spoke about the donor using “you” focused messaging could help us achieve a lift in donor response. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the experiment for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we observed a 39% lift on the treatment with 90% level of confidence so the experiment did not validate, but we were approaching statistical significance. We believe these results demonstrate that reducing organization-centric language for donation page visitors can lead to an increase in conversion rate and revenue. The 39.4% increase in donations for all traffic and a 556% increase in revenue further support this hypothesis. In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the effectiveness of donor-centric messaging and consider incorporating it in our donation pages.

experiments.title: How a direct ask tied to a wider impact affected clickthrough rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a gift with far-reaching impact for a prospecting audience will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 12/20/2022
As part of a calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, Illinois Policy Institute focused on raising funds for a new documentary film they are producing and releasing in 2023. Since this is a new project, we were curious to learn what would motivate donors to give their support. We wanted to test a narrow, specific ask – making a gift of $25 to cover the cost of tickets for people to view the documentary versus making a gift of $25 to help fund the cost of promoting the documentary on social media platforms. We split the audience 50/50 between the control and treatment emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found after sending the email was a 5.9% increase in click-through rates on the treatment email with 84.8% level of confidence so the results of the test are not valid. We believe the lift in click-through rate could be due to the fact that the control language suggests their gift will make an impact locally in their own state, but the treatment language expands the scope of the project to reach a national audience. This could be appealing to potential donors and could have motivated additional clicks. But since the confidence level is only 84.8%, we will have to continue testing the narrow ask vs. the wide ask to understand how the prospecting audience will respond to a tangible ask like a ticket for an audience member to view the documentary compared to a less tangible ask such as general promotion of the documentary.

When reviewing donor conversion rate results among the two variants, the treatment produced 14 donations to the control's 16 donations. The treatment saw a 12.9% decrease in donations at just a 29.4% level of confidence. Again, we will have to continue testing this approach (narrow ask vs. wide ask) to pick up on clicks and donations trends, and to reach a valid conclusion.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain clarity and motivation as to how serious the problem is that we’re asking them to help solve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/10/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis Survey instant donation page is getting an instant conversion rate below 1%. In brainstorming ways to potentially increase conversion rate, we wondered if the addition of evidentials would further clarify and intensify the problem that the donor’s gift would help solve. For a test treatment, we added three bullet points towards the beginning of the value proposition copy, each one stating a research fact that speaks to the problems at the border.
Will statistics and facts lead to more clarity and motivation, and ultimately more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy may not necessarily increase donations. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. The treatment resulted in a 52.3% decrease in conversions with a statistical level of confidence of 83.2%. These results suggest that potential donors may not be motivated by research facts and statistics. We can also surmise that donors already have a strong understanding of the problem and that they don't need evidentials to help with their understanding.

As the problem is clearly known and communicated throughout the offer funnel, future tests need to focus on other components of the instant donation page, such as the solutions or the ask. Potential donors see eye to eye with the organization on the problem, but maybe they are not on the same page with the solutions we are offering, or maybe the solutions are not clear enough.

experiments.title: How updating the call-to-action button on a Facebook ad impacts click-throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the call to action button for potential course takes will achieve a change in course sign up rates because the button relates to the action we are asking them to take. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2023 - 03/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For the launch of a new course led by Dan Burke, the founder of Avila, we wanted to test whether a change in the call-to-action on a Facebook ad would make an impact on click-throughs.
Our standard control for most of Avila’s ads has been to use a ‘learn more’ call to action; in this experiment, since we were asking people to sign up to take the course, we tested learn more versus a ‘sign up’ call to action button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the call-to-action button from 'Learn More' to 'Sign Up' did not lead to an increase in click-throughs for Avila Foundation's new course. The treatment resulted in a 6.7% decrease in clicks, which was statistically significant with a 94.9% level of confidence.
However, when viewing this experiment through the lens of how many people actually signed up for the course, the results are different. While there was a higher number of clicks on the 'learn more' control version, it actually had a 6.3% less sign-up rate once those people got to the landing page. Thus, we can see a correlation between asking people to sign up as the call-to-action, and them actually going through the process. While this only had a 61% level of confidence, it does show a directional lift in response.
Continued testing for this kind of experiment is needed. Future variations could include making the copy of the ad itself correlate more to the call to action, and/or testing within the ad headlines as well so they correlate to the call-to-action button.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar impacted donations after a course sign up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar encouraging support for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the personalized message from a prominent and relatable figure will inspire them to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 03/12/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary’s Galatians course reroutes users to a donation page after they sign up for the course. This donation page is experiencing a lower than expected donation conversion rate, so we want to test adding a humanized sticky bar to the top of the donation page in hopes that it will effectively nudge more people to make a donation.
The sticky bar messaging includes a thank you for signing up and kindly asks the page visitor to stick around to view a special message about how they can help more people get access to free bible study courses like the one they just signed up for. It also includes a picture and a signature of the organization president and course instructor implying that the message is being stated by that person.
We’ve seen encouraging results for the humanized sticky bar for various organizations so we want to see if it is successful in this instance in increasing donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 95.5% lift in donations at an 84.1% level of confidence. The results confirm our hypothesis that the personalized message from a prominent and relatable figure would inspire visitors to donate and increase donor conversion rate. It is also important to consider the person speaking through the humanized sticky bar. In this case, users likely appreciate that the message is coming not only from the president of the organization but also the course instructor - the person they will be engaging with as they learn more about Galatians.
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a personalized and humanized experience for potential donors can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The sticky bar separates the page from standard pages - the sticky bar stands out as a unique feature that catches the users attention right away and amplifies the donation value proposition. This treatment should be tested with other course donation pages.

experiments.title: How adding evidentials to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will gain clarity and motivation as to how serious the problem is that we’re asking them to help solve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/10/2023
TPPF’s Border Crisis Survey instant donation page is getting an instant conversion rate below 1%. In brainstorming ways to potentially increase conversion rate, we wondered if the addition of evidentials would further clarify and intensify the problem that the donor’s gift would help solve. For a test treatment, we added three bullet points towards the beginning of the value proposition copy, each one stating a research fact that speaks to the problems at the border.
Will statistics and facts lead to more clarity and motivation, and ultimately more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding evidentials to the instant donation page copy may not necessarily increase donations. In fact, the treatment saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. The treatment resulted in a 52.3% decrease in conversions with a statistical level of confidence of 83.2%. These results suggest that potential donors may not be motivated by research facts and statistics. We can also surmise that donors already have a strong understanding of the problem and that they don't need evidentials to help with their understanding.

As the problem is clearly known and communicated, future tests need to focus on other components of the instant donation page, such as the solutions or the ask. Potential donors see eye to eye with the organization on the problem, but maybe they are not on the same page with the solutions we are offering.

experiments.title: How humanizing an appeal by including a specific child’s name, age, and image on a slider affects conversion rates

rows.name: Slider with specific child details (name, age, photo)

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specific child details to the micro goal popup on the sponsorship list page for potential donors will achieve an increase in sponsorship conversion rates because we are mentioning an individual child by name instead of a certain number of children.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2022 - 05/16/2022
When a user enters the child list sponsorship page experience on all devices they will either see a slider with a certain number of kids in need of sponsorship or a specific child’s details that they could sponsor. We wanted to see if this personalized approach would increase potential donors desire to sponsor a child.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped after two weeks due to large declines in donor conversion.
• Donor conversion was lower for the variant (-19%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (86%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-25%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (89%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include specific child details as they may not match the particular child a donor would choose to sponsor.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including a dynamic donor countdown to a banner ad affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner for potential donors will achieve increased clicks to the donation page because people respond to an urgent message.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2021 - 05/08/2022
If we added a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner, donor conversion will increase because donors respond to urgent messaging. We used an emergency alert banner vs. an emergency banner with a countdown as our variable treatment.
Note – Dynamically counts down from 25. After a gift, the number will decrease to confirm with donors that the counter is valid.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant results.
• Unique banner click rate was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Donor conversion was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Recommendation is not to add the dynamic counter to the emergency banner.

experiments.title: How validating each form field impacted overall completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding form field validation for petition signers will achieve an increase in petition signatures because the checkmarks will reduce friction by adding cognitive momentum and clarity in the user process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2022 - 12/16/2022
When reviewing petition signature pages, we wondered what how we could create a treatment that would provide additional cognitive momentum to reward visitors for beginning the process and encourage them to complete the remaining steps. We developed a green checkmark that would appear when someone had completed a required field to let them know they had successfully completed that step and could move on to the next field. If a user missed a step on either the control or the treatment, they could clearly see an error message that would prompt them to return to that form field and correctly complete it, but the treatment also interjected a “Looks good!” message to confirm they could keep moving forward. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment were that adding form field validation did not result in an increase in petition signatures for all traffic. However, the treatment did lead to a significant increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Moreover, the treatment showed mixed results across different visitor segments. For instance, there was a decrease in signups for mobile and email visitors, a significant increase in tablet visitors, and a considerable decrease in signups for female and male visitors.

Given these results, it is important to keep in mind that implementing form field validation may not improve petition signatures for all segments of visitors. Therefore, future experimentation should focus on identifying which visitor segments may benefit from this treatment and which segments may not. Additionally, the revenue increase observed in the treatment group should be further investigated to understand the underlying factors contributing to this effect. Lastly, it is worthwhile to continue experimenting with different design treatments that can reduce friction and encourage visitors to complete the signup process.

experiments.title: How using images of politicians impacted petition signatures
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images of well-known politicians for petition signers will achieve an increase in signatures because the motivation to sign would be more heavily impacted by emotion by association.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2023 - 01/28/2023
On a petition to repeal alcohol tax, we wondered if instead of using a standard image of the issue itself, how petition signers would respond to images of the politicians in the decision-making seats who were the ones implementing the policies. Our hypothesis was that the treatments that used politician images would invoke more emotion from visitors which might fuel their momentum to complete the petition. We split traffic 3 ways and let the test run for 16 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using images of famous politicians for petition signers did not achieve the intended increase in signatures. The control version of the petition outperformed the treatment with a statistical level of confidence approaching validity. The use of politician images did not motivate visitors to sign the petition, and some categories of visitors, such as social, female, and 25-34 age group, had a significant decrease in signatures with a high level of confidence. Desktop and other visitors had an increase in signatures, but these results were not consistent across all traffic.

In future experimentation, it may be necessary to test the use of images of politicians in a more targeted way, such as using images of politicians who are in favor of repealing the alcohol tax. Also, it may be useful to test different types of emotional appeals, beyond the association with politicians, to motivate visitors to sign the petition. The language used in both versions of the petition was similar, so testing different wording may also be beneficial for future experiments. It is also important to consider the characteristics and preferences of different visitor segments when implementing new designs or strategies.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on a post-petition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more you-focused language for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the emphasis is less on the organization and more about what the donor wants to see happen.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 12/12/2022
When evaluating our standard donation page language presented to someone after they signed a petition, we noticed the language was very organization-centric and we wondered if the value proposition could be reconstructed. The control copy says “Would you consider donating so we can show this petition to people on social media?” and we wondered if this was what the donor actually wanted to do or if we could make a more compelling case for support using more donor-centric language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment revealed that using more donor-centric language for petition signers resulted in a 15.4% increase in donations with an 89.6% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence is below the 95% threshold, the results are approaching validity, implying that further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness. Additionally, the (Other) visitors had an 86% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

The key takeaway from this experiment is that a shift towards more you-focused language is likely to increase donor conversion rates. Future experimentation should focus on refining the language to gain further improvements in the results. Based on these insights, Canadian Taxpayers Federation can improve its donation conversion rates by creating more compelling donor-centric value propositions.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky “my cart” button when a user begins a donation affects conversion rates

rows.name: Cart button at top of page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky cart button for donors who add an item to their cart (scroll persistent), will achieve a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2021 - 09/27/2022
Added a sticky cart button that appears when users add an item to their cart (scroll persistent).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Mobile donor conversion was higher for the sticky cart (42%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).
• Mobile revenue per visitor was higher for the sticky cart (68%) at a statistically valid LoC (98%).
• Recommendation is sticky cart addition to all gift catalog-related pages.
• Projected yearly incremental lift of $200,000.

experiments.title: How Adding Privacy Language on an Acquisition Page Impacts Email Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement to the email series acquisition pages for visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate and donor conversion rate because there is a perceived sense of added security and communication guidelines.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They run several different acquisition offers to draw in donors. Several of these people that land on an acquisition page might not know who FOTF is, or if they do they might be worried that they will get their inboxes spammed with unwanted emails. They also could be concerned about data privacy.
We decided to run a series of tests to determine the impact of adding a statement promising to protect their privacy on the landing pages and donation pages.
We hypothesized that we could increase email acquisition and downstream donor conversion by committing to protect their privacy. We also wanted to test if we could further increase the value of the offer by also clarifying what types of emails they would receive.
We created two treatments. One emphasizing our commitment to privacy and the other incorporating a statement of the types of emails they would receive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of a privacy statement on landing pages for email series acquisition from visitors can have a positive impact on email acquisition. Specifically, our A/B experiment found there to be a 19.4% increase in email acquisition with a 90.4% statistical level of confidence, though this confidence level was not high enough to be considered valid. This is an encouraging result and indicates that this privacy statement can be helpful to visitors in making their decision to provide their information and sign up for the offer.
Most importantly, we increased donor conversion downstream by 63.3% with a 94.5% level of confidence in the treatment promising "relevant practical resources for your family."  This reveals that committing to provide ongoing value while protecting privacy increases the perceived value of the content offered and increases donor generosity.

experiments.title: How revamping email subscription button copy affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve a greater email conversion rate because it will create a more immediate response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/25/2022 - 03/04/2022
If we change the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency, email conversion will increase because users need to act immediately. We tested four different variants against our control language “join us” for this experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall email conversion was directionally higher for "Sign Up" and "Join Now."
• Mobile email conversion was directionally higher for "Join Now."
• Desktop email conversion was directionally higher for all variants.
• Of the five, "sign up" (13.7%) and "join now" (24.1%) had higher email capture rates.
• Mobile email conversion was higher for "join now" (19%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (64%).
• All four variants' desktop conversion was higher at a non-statistically valid confidence levels.
• Recommendation is not changing the HP email copy due to lack of statistical significance.
• We could potentially test "sign up" vs. "join now" button language in the future, to see if there is a more significant result with fewer variants since both had a more significant email conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding a micro-goal slider that stresses urgency to a sponsorship page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency for potential donors will achieve increase sponsorship conversion  because there is a clear CTA. because  there is a clear CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2022 - 03/28/2022
Adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency will increase sponsorship conversion because there is a clear CTA. When a user enters the Child List Sponsorship page in the variant, a popup will appear after 3 seconds on the page. It presents a variable number of lifeline children that need to be sponsored. The popup scrolls with the page and can be closed by the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Desktop donor conversion was higher for the variant (28%) at a statistically valid confidence level (99%).
• Recommendation is to add the slider to all sponsorship-related pages.
• Overall, the variant produced an increase (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (92%).
• RPV was higher for the variant (13%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (84%).
• Desktop RPV was higher for the variant (34%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (95%).
• Mobile donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (16%).
• Mobile RPV was slightly lower for the variant (-6%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (66%).
• Based on 2021 figures (assuming fixed traffic and average gift), we project an incremental lift of $76,000 annually. The net 60-month value of these donors is approximately $1.4M.
• Given the statistical significance in desktop donor conversion (28%) and limited directional downside on mobile, we recommend adding the slider to all sponsorship pages.
• Results may have had an even greater significance if not for the Ukraine emergency. The experiment was close to validating five days in, but highly motivated traffic related to Ukraine evened out the treatments.
• Additionally, we look to test different versions of copy and visual elements employed by the slider to optimize the experience further.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity with more descriptive menu language affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more descriptive language in a dropdown menu will achieve a greater click-through rate by providing a more personal motive to click.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2022 - 10/05/2022
If we change the language for “Join Team Tomorrow” in the navigation dropdown, there will be a greater click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Test stopped after several weeks due to a lack of signifiant results
• No significant difference in the click-through rate for any of the three treatments. Directionally, "Find a Cause" had a higher CTR (31% lift 85% confidence level - possible retest in the future).
• The overall test did not generate sufficient clicks. The next test iteration will individually list the four focus areas and have direct links to their corresponding donation forms.
• The treatment version using language like "Find a Cause" was more successful in motivating users to click, suggesting that works better than a generic request like "Join Team Tomorrow".
• The results of this experiment show that changing the language for "Join Team Tomorrow" on the website navigation dropdown resulted in a 30.9% increase in clicks for all visitors with 85.1% statistical confidence.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed donation form affect recurring conversion rates on a primary donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for donation page visitors will achieve higher recurring donation rates by making this giving option and its impact more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 02/10/2023
We wanted to know how a tabbed donation form (featuring single and monthly gifts) would affect recurring donations on the primary donation page for a public policy organization.
In this experiment, the control presented the option to make a recurring gift by featuring a checkbox below the gift array that asked the visitor if they would like to make their gift on a recurring basis. Beneath the checkbox, an impact statement communicated the impact of making a recurring gift.
The treatment instead presented the option to make a recurring gift in a tabbed form format, with “single gift” and “monthly gift” shown side-by-side above the gift array and the impact statement shown above the tabs.
For this experiment, ~50% of traffic was shown the control and ~50% of traffic was shown the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we observed that the tabbed donation form produced an 11.0% decrease in recurring gifts with a 25% level of confidence, a 25.4% decrease in overall donations with a 99.2% level of confidence, and a 32.1% decrease in traffic with a 94.4% level of confidence.
These decreases were most severe among returning visitors where total donations decreased by 29.5% with a 99.7% level of confidence and revenue decreased by 40.6% with a 97.6% level of confidence indicating.
For new visitors, we observed a large increase in revenue (177%) -- but because this increase was a result of just one additional donor, it is apparent that this is being skewed by a large gift and not indicative of overall performance.
These results disprove our hypothesis and indicate that in the context of visiting a primary donation page (high donor motivation), a tabbed donation form with the impact statement above the gift array may have distracted the donor or created friction by presenting multiple options before they made the decision to give.
It also suggests that it is more persuasive to ask someone to "upgrade" their donation after they have already made a micro-commitment by selecting their donation amount from the gift array.
Based on these results, we will look to test a monthly giving checkbox against the tabbed donation forms currently running on this organization's instant donation pages tied to acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page will increase donations by providing more “reasons to give” to prospective donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2022 - 01/21/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation by improving the “reason why” they should give.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed 12.5% decrease in donations from traffic that was redirected to the treatment donation page with more value proposition language. The low sample size of the experiment prevented us from validating these results, however, it does not appear that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page motivated more people to give and may even have demotivated visitors from giving.
And while we cannot draw statistically valid conclusions about these results, it is worth noting that this outcome may have been influenced by user motivation. Specifically, after reviewing results, we are led to hypothesize that because someone who clicks a "make a donation" button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to "stay out of their way" by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
This suspicion is supported by the fact that "new visitors" who we may assume would be the exact segment that would benefit most from increasing the "reason to give" gave 28% less (86% level of confidence) when visiting the treatment with longer copy than they did when they visited the control with minimal copy above the donation form.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V2 relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/11/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time. After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the second version of the experiment (V2 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V1 version. That write-up can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V2 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER the V1 version that we ended on 12/2 due to internal FOTF technical challenges. This version ran from 12/2 to 12/11. While this experiment was set up correctly, it was also running in parallel with a similar experiment that was targeted to only the SOHL audience. Unfortunately, challenges with the data capture for that experiment (linked here) were resulting in no returns. The design of the experiment was to run in parallel with the SOHL version, so that we could see the effect on both audiences seperately. We made the decision to end this experiment early on 12/11, so that we could determine what needed to be fixed on the SOHL version, so that we could relaunch both experiments at the same time, and accurately record data on both at the same time.

All of that said, we were seeing similar results (although not as drastic) to the V1 experiment before we ended this experiment. Revunue lift for V2 was around 55% with an 86% Level of Confidence. Even when we removed outlier donations over $2k from both the control and the treatment, we still saw a significant lift from the treatment.
