As with the V1 version of this experiment, we did not feel comfortable relying on these results to make any future recommendations other than to re-build the experiment as it was, and quickly re-launch (a second time) to see if the results continued to show a positive lift in revenue through the remainder of the year-end campaign. Results from V3 will be shown in a future write-up and linked here.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V3 second relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2022 - 12/31/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the third version of the experiment (V3 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V2 version. That write-up can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V3 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER the V2 version that we ended on 12/11 due to internal FOTF technical challenges. This version ran from 12/11 to 12/31. This experiment was running in parallel with a similar experiment that was targeted to only the SOHL audience. Unlike the previous 2 versions of this experiment, the results showed a decrease in the revenue by around 9% with an 82% Level of Confidence.
While previous versions of this experiment showed a positive lift (even validating in the V1 version), this one showed a decrease, but not until the final days of the experiment. As we experienced so many technical challenges with launching this experiment, we kept a watchful eye on the results on a daily basis. From 12/11 through 12/29, the treatment was actually leading in revenue….but on the last 2 days of the year, the opposite actually happened.
This led us to even more questions like...
Does the timing of the experiment change the results? Did donors in “non-peak” times get inspired to give more based on messaging?Were the donors in the last 2 days motivated to give regardless of messaging?Was the change in messaging more of a friction point in the last 2 days of the year?Would it be better to change messaging in the wiki based on the urgency and timing of the campaign? (Ex., show more impactful language earlier in the campaign, then make the most urgent times as simple and frictionless as possible)
There were several key take-aways that came from this series of experiments.
Timing/motivation may play a big role in the effectiveness of impact languageMake sure you are always monitoring your experiments for data anomalies, and don't be afraid to end an experiment early and re-launch if the data does not "look right"We cannot say definitively that the impact language led to great revenue during the majority of the high-urgency campaign...but we can say that there is enough data to show a positive trend and learnings that we will take into our next high-urgency campaign experiment

experiments.title: How testing YouTube ad text affected donor acquisition costs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing ad messaging for YouTube viewers will achieve a lower cost per donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2023
At Hoover, we’ve been expanding acquisition on YouTube as we’ve seen good results thus far, and a new video became available to launch.
Our main KPI is cost per donor.
So, we wanted to try this experiment in order to identify ad copy that was more effective at driving donations and reducing acquisition costs.
We believed that testing different versions of ad copy could be a major lever in improving the performance of our YouTube ads, and that it could also help us to better understand what types of messaging resonated with our target audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Cost per donor
C: $102.93T1: $63.07 T2: $64.79
Testing your ad messaging:
is easier to executespeed of implementation is usually fastercan be a major lever to reducing your acquisition costs
In this case, treatment 1 saw a 39% reduction in cost per donor for 20 minutes working on ad copy.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily goal in an email appeal affects donations during a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a daily goal in the body of an email appeal will increase donation rates by increasing the specificity of the ask and giving recipients a clear ‘micro’ goal that they can help achieve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/27/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to test how presenting a daily goal (“today I’m looking for X donors”) in the body copy of an email appeal would affect donation rates for a public policy organization during a year-end fundraising campaign.
For this experiment, both previous donors and non-donors were included in the sample universe with ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the control (containing an ask that did not include a daily goal) and ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the treatment (containing an ask centered around a daily goal. Nothing else about the appeal was changed.
Major donors were excluded from this test and our experiment spanned seven appeal emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment over the course of seven emails during the organization's year-end fundraising campaign, we observed that the treatment produced a 12.2% decrease in donations (84% level of confidence).
The sample size of this experiment did not allow us to validate these results and therefore prevents us from drawing statistically valid conclusions about how presenting a daily goal in the body of an appeal email affects donation rates.
However, the results of this experiment do suggest that including a daily goal was not a motivating factor for recipients of this appeal and may even have demotivated them from giving. We also observed that the daily goal did not serve as a significant motivating factor in terms of clicking through to the donation page from the email, producing only a 2.1% lift with a 53.6% level of statistical confidence.
This may be because a daily goal does not add tangible value or a reason to give to the appeal. It is also in essence an organizational-focused goal that lacks a specific outcome that the daily goal will help achieve.
Based on these results, this public policy client may want to pursue a different method for increasing engagement and donations on the next high-urgency campaign or consider including a specific outcome that the daily goal will achieve. In either case, the organization should not assume that including a daily goal, as structured in this experiment, will increase donation rates during a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How a video trailer vs the first video lesson as a YouTube ad affected instant donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a trailer for YouTube viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2023
While we were in our Google Ads account we discovered that we were running the 1st video lesson of the course instead of the course trailer as an ad. We had already seen good performance from the 1st lesson and saw this as an opportunity to test the course trailer against it and see which one performed better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We wanted to try this experiment in order to understand which type of content was more appealing to YouTube audiences and to better understand what types of videos work on YouTube.
Cost per Donor:
C: First Lesson $132.59 (0.48roi)
T: Trailer $164.41 (0.28roi)
The first lesson captures the viewers attention for a greater length of time which amplifies the intent to donate.

experiments.title: How a callout emphasizing a year-end matching challenges impacts donor conversion on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout emphasizing a year-end matching challenge to the acquisition offer donation page will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal of making a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2022 - 01/01/2023
Focus on the Family is an international ministry that equips families to grow closer to one another and to God. During their year-end campaign, they wanted to test the impact of emphasizing a year-end matching challenge on their acquisition offer donation pages to see if that would increase donor conversion on the page. They hypothesized that the opportunity to “double your impact” would increase the appeal of making a gift and with it the donor conversion on the page. The control page mentioned the matching challenge in the body copy. For the treatment page, they would add a callout box right under the donation button, the point of decision, to emphasize the matching opportunity there as well. The text in the box would read, “Don’t delay. The match expires on Dec 31. Give now to double your impact.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to our hypothesis, the callout box did not increase donor conversion. There was an increase in donor conversion on desktop traffic, however since the vast majority of traffic to the page is from mobile devices, there were too few donations in the segment to validate a learning. It seems that the box may have added friction by extending the length of the page on mobile devices which outweighed any of the benefit from reminding the donor of the available match.

Further testing is needed to see if the audience on acquisition offers is as motivated by "year-end" language on the donation page as their housefile audience would be. These visitors are less likely to already be invested in the ministry of Focus on the Family and thus less likely to be motivated to give a "year-end" gift.

experiments.title: How an exit intent impacted donor conversion during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an exit intent for web visitors will achieve higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth.
For previous high-urgency campaigns, we have tested exit intent popups but have not been able to see a valid response. We decided to test this again with the calendar year-end match campaign.
Previous tests, https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4829 and https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4417
For Giving Tuesday through December 31st, PTV offered a matching campaign of up to $525k for any donation made.
The exit intent ran on the entire website for anyone that visited a site to make a donation but did not complete the donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we were very close to achieving a valid level of confidence. We saw a 4.8% increase in donor conversions with a level of confidence of 92.9%.
Compared to the 2 other times we have run this experiment, this one achieved the highest level of validation for donor conversions.
Due to these results we will move forward with rolling out the exit intent popup during high urgency campaigns and begin testing into copy, CTA and strategy within the popup itsself.

experiments.title: How showing a reminder pop up to users that abandoned the donation process affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a pop up reminder to complete a donation for people that have closed out a donation page, without making a donation will achieve a higher amount of revenue than allowing users to remember on their own.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 09/30/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would cause users to complete their transaction…instead of abandoning their donation all-together. This experiment was based on abandoned cart functionality often seen in the e-commerce space, but seldom used in the NPO space. Their current donation platform does not natively allow for this functionality, so we built a test using the user’s last click on a donation amount (on an abandoned or closed-out donation page) to launch a pop up with that amount, asking the user to complete their transaction with a simple nudge.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for 41 days. While it did not reach statistical significance (only an 87% Level of Confidence), we did have some very interesting learnings. Since we were testing for revenue lift, we wanted to be sure the data was not skewed by abnormally large donation amounts from either the control or the treatment. FOTF often gets large donations that can skew a revenue experiment if we do not normalize the data. After we ran the experiment, we created a custom Google Analytics segment that only counted donations that were less than $1k in the sample size. After doing so, we noticed that the revenue for the treatment was significantly higher - an 85.2% lift in revenue. This took the average gift of this new sample group from $75.54 to $131.86. It is to be noted that prior to removing the gifts over $1k, the control had a sample size much closer to the treatment. Did this weigh into the final results? Are the motivations different for people that come to the main site vs coming from an article page or elsewhere? WHile this test provided some exciting potential lifts, it is still unclear if motivations based on entry point could skew this data. The next step in the process will be to run the same experiment, but split into two different ones, and test if the revenue lift is better or worse when directly visiting via the main page or visiting instead through a secondary page.

experiments.title: How removing organizational language in an email appeal impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing organizational language in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve more revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2022
For Giving Tuesday, the IMB sent out email appeals to their housefile. In one of the emails we wanted to test how removing organizational language would impact either donor conversion rate or overall revenue. We hypothesized by replacing “the IMB” with words like “us” or “our,” the prospective donor would feel like they’re giving to people instead of a organization or corporate entity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When pulling the results, the donor conversion rate was relatively the same. We saw a 16% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 52%. We also saw little no impact in click-thru rate for these emails. However when looking at revenue data, we saw a 51% directional lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 87.8%. While this is not a statistically valid result, it can be surmised that there are some donors who may have been motivated to give a larger gift when reading the treatment copy. The treatment copy speaks to the donor as if they're joining "us" to accomplish something as opposed to giving to someone–making this much more communal in nature.
It's recommended we retest this concept and also include the donation page copy in the experiment so that the donation page copy also has the organizational language removed in the treatment experience.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2022 - 11/25/2022
When launching a new content offer, we had similar donation page language in place from previous content offers and we knew that the housefile visitors would likely be returning visitors. We wanted to test a new approach that condensed some lengthy copy on the treatment into 3 specific items the reader should know. We also updated the headline from “Thank you” to “Congratulations” and mentioned the title of the specific offer they had requested. We split traffic equally between the two pages and ran the experiment for 2 weeks before evaluating results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After two weeks, we saw a 25.7% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence, so the results were indicating a directional decrease. We believe the treatment language in the headline "you're all set" was causing visitors to leave the page before reading the remaining message. We saw valid decreases for many visitor segments, so we decided to end the experiment early and reassess the best treatment to run for these visitors. On future testing for this page, we will reconstruct the headline language that asks people to read the message below before they exit the site. Then, we can better evaluate if the "3 things you should know" approach was the true cause for the decrease, or if there was another element from the radical redesign (in this case, the headline) that was inadvertently causing friction that interfered with the test and prevented people consuming the message.

experiments.title: How combining audiences in Facebook campaigns affects efficiency in donor acquisition
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Combining common ad sets into larger audiences in a Facebook campaign will increase donor conversion and decrease cost per donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/28/2021
The Facebook campaigns for the Missionary Oblates had multiple ad sets for similar audiences. For instance, they Catholic online, Knights of Columbus, Catholic and Proud, were all separate ad sets in Facebook, but are all Catholic audiences. In Facebook it’s more efficient to have ad sets with a larger reach because Facebook is more likely to spend money in a larger ad set. So we hypothesized that combining those smaller ad sets into one big Catholic ad set would increase efficiency and lower cost per donor. We tested a campaign with their broken out ad sets against a campaign with combined ad set of a common audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: As the results show, Facebook had to reach fewer people with combined ad sets. It did increase donor acquisition by 18%, but it did not reach validity, with only 84% level of confidence. However, this did not decrease the cost per donor. In fact, the cost per donor was 3% higher with the test campaign that combined audiences in the ad set. And it decreased the ROI by 9%. We will need to look at new ways to test and optimize Facebook audiences.

experiments.title: Does Adding Social Proof on a Donation Page Increase the Number of Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof to an instant donation page for people who have signed up for a free video course will result in a higher number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2022 - 11/14/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of donations on an Instant Donation Page by adding a statement of social proof.
We also wanted to see if there was an impact based on where the social proof was located on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test we found that adding social proof above the point of decision (on the donation form) led to a lift 27%, but did not reach validity with only a 52% level of confidence. Even though we were not testing for increases in revenue, we did find that while total donation amounts were similar between the control and variant 2, the average donation amount was around $3 higher for the variant with social proof. Did the social proof increase motivation to give more? Further testing would be needed to say with certainty.

We also discovered that placement did seem to matter...as adding the proof below the form near the submit button resulted in nearly a 50% decrease over the second variant. My hypothesis is that if a donor is to make it that far they do not need the extra motivation to donate, and in fact, could have led to more friction in the giving process.

There will need to be further testing to see if the variables can produce positive results. For our next experiment, we will test the social proof in variant 2 against different copy to see if the way the proof was written will affect the results. And, since we did not have a clear winner, we will start with the same control of no social proof against various statements of social proof above the donation form.

experiments.title: How adding first name personalization to the headline of an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding first name personalization to the headline of an instant donation page for page visitors will build trust and relations between the potential donor and the organization, and will ultimately achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2022 - 11/07/2022
For TPPF’s newest offer, the Top Issues in Texas survey, we were interested to see how users would take to first name personalization in the headline of the offer of instant donation page. Once a user submits the survey by providing their first name, last name, and email address, they would be redirected to the instant donation page where the headline would refer to them by their first name.
We wondered if this personalization method would make the user feel acknowledged, and build trust and relations with the visitor, leading them to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment validated with the treatment resulting in an 86.3% DECREASE in donor conversion rate (95.7% level of confidence). There was a significant decrease across all segments, revealing that users either lost motivation to give or did not gain motivation to give when they were acknowledged by their first name when reaching the instant donation page. This may be an audience that is suspicious of seeing their first name when they do not have a personal relationship with the organization. This experiment may be worth testing with another offer to determine if the TPPF audience overall is turned off by first name personalization, or if the response is specific to the audience for this offer.

experiments.title: How placement of a premium link impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by moving the placement of the link higher in the email copy for email subscribers will achieve an increase in conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that generates a majority of its revenue through monthly premium offers. Throughout the month, Pathway to Victory will email its file 4-5 times a month about the premium featured.
For the first email about the November 2022 premium, we decided to look at the placement of the link to get the premium. This particular premium is one that PTV has featured before and has seen great success. The success of this offer presented us with the opportunity to test having the link to the premium higher in the email copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did see an increase in email clicks, 16% with a 92.5% level of confidence, we did not see a significant increase in donations or revenue.

In fact we saw a very slight decrease of donation conversions by 2% with a 6% level of confidence.

 

So while we did see an increase in email engagement this did not translate to an increase in donations or revenue.

 

Further testing ideas for this offer via email include, specific dollar amount ask, other placement tests and images of the premium in the email copy.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition copy on a high urgency donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the value proposition for potential donors on a monthly appeal page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 10/01/2022
The Boy Scouts of America ran a monthly appeal campaign in September that consisted of one email and one follow-up to that email. The first email only received two donations therefore we needed to test changing the language on the donation page to see if this would increase donor conversion rate.
We felt like the donation page copy on the original page was too long to read therefore causing friction when someone landed on this page to potentially convert.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 43.7% decrease in donor conversion rate with a near-valid level of significance of 84.9%.
After sending the follow-up appeal that had more urgency within the copy and was shortened compared to the first email, we believed that the issue actually laid within the email content of the first email, not the original donation page. Therefore, by removing a lot of the value proposition from the treatment donation page, users coming from the second email did not have enough value given to them at this step in the funnel in order to convince them to make a donation.
Next, we would like to test keeping the email copy shorter that way we're more likely to get users to the donation page where they can be presented with more value proposition.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding first name personalization to a donation page affected donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by personalizing the headline with a first name for survey participants  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2022 - 07/30/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a survey offer that directed users to an instant donation page after completion. We wanted to see an increase in the instant donor conversion rate. We hypothesized that by personalizing the headline with a first name it would create a greater connection with the reader and communicate a deeper appreciation for their participation. Additionally, we suspected that the control headline was communicating to the user that there was no further action to be taken: “Thank you for your insights and for giving your opinion.” Without changing the primary message, we believed using a personalization would make for a headline that is less likely to be ignored.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for 50 days, and saw a 124% increase in donor conversion rate with a 83.5% confidence level.
The level of confidence and small low number of donations (preferably at least 50) still encourages room for testing. There are a few notable hints in the data regarding our initial concept. Our hypothesis was that a name personalization would show greater appreciation and stand out to the user. One interesting comparison is that the treatment caused little to no change in conversion rate for new visitors, but did cause a slight increase for returning visitors. But alternatively, Organic Search visitors had a 67% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence. Is there a learning to be had on when users perceive the most value in personalization? Is there thought to be more value in someone "remembering" our name, than an organization using it directly after it was provided?
Ultimately, more testing is still required in order to validate these findings.
Revenue experienced a 85.0% decrease with a 93% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 17.1% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 9.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 14.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 66.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making a headline more than just a thank you/confirmation on instant donation page affects results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by better aligning the headline copy with the body copy on the instant donation page for users completing the survey will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2022 - 10/27/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a survey offer that directed users to an instant donation page after completion. The body copy told a testimony of someone who was able to reconcile their doubts between faith and science through RTB’s resources. The ask was that their generosity is what makes this possible, and would continue to bring more skeptics back to the faith.
We wanted to see an increase in the instant donor conversion rate. We believed this number was low because users were not actually reading the testimony, but simply exiting because the headline didn’t communicate that there was more to do. The original read, “Thank you for giving your insights and for giving your opinion.” We replaced this with a headline that we believed would encourage the user to read on, “Thank you for your insights. We also want to see more skeptics come to Christ. Here’s why your response matters.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for 3 months, but could not reach an adequate sample size of at least 50 donations to determine validity. The result was that the treatment actually resulted in a 68.1% decrease in donations with an 85.7% confidence level. However, it should be noted that the entirety of this decrease seemed to result from mobile traffic. The desktop view showed a 1.3% increase in conversion rate (though with low confidence), meanwhile, the numbers on mobile view accounted for 84% of the 85.7% decrease at a nearly valid level of confidence (94.5%).
Aside from the change in messaging, the biggest difference between the treatment and the original is that the treatment is twice as long. If the problem was the actual message/value of the headline, one would think that it would cause a similar decrease whether read on desktop or on mobile. In other words, if the problem was an uncompelling sentence, it would stand to reason that it would be uncompelling whether you read it on small screen or on a big one.
This is not the case. Instead, the treatment didn't show any decrease in the desktop view. 84% of the 85% decrease came solely from mobile users. It's possible that by doubling the length of the headline, it increased friction for mobile users by eliminating the body copy from view unless they scrolled down. It appears that most of these mobile users were also new visitors. Meaning that if there appeared to only be a headline with no body copy beneath it,  a majority of these users had no prior experience to make them think otherwise.
The key learning is more of a reaffirmation: mobile traffic continues to be a huge factor in your results and the mobile experience should always be tested and considered.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 82.2% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 68.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had an 84.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.New visitors had an 81.1% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How offering a free ebook for email acquisition in the home page footer instead of a general email subscription form impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook as an email acquisition method in the home page footer for page vistiors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2022 - 10/13/2022
In their home page footer, TGC gives users the opportunity to subscribe to email updates. The section says “Email Updates” and users can enter their email below and sign up. We wondered if offering a free ebook to users when they sign up would acquire more emails than the existing “email updates” sign-up opportunity, so we tested the idea. In the footer, we replaced the email updates section with an offer to receive a popular ebook for free. We included value prop telling the user how the ebook could help them and below was a sign up form including first name, last name, and email, and a button that read “Get my free ebook »”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control saw a 0.03% conversion rate compared to a 0.02% conversion rate for the treatment. Ultimately, the treatment had a 26.6% decrease in emails acquired with an 89.1% level of confidence. Users were more likely to sign up for email updates than to receive a free ebook.

Given that TGC offers a ton of content, which users can see as they scroll through the home page and down to the footer of the page, a user's primary intent may be to receive email updates for new content and new articles. So the opportunity to receive only a single free ebook is limiting compared to receiving lots of updates with lots of new content.

We're going to run this same experiment on TGC's Commentaries page to see if/how the user motivation adjusts from the home page to the commentaries page.

It may be a good idea to test adding value prop language to the home page footer in line with receiving email updates. Right now, the sign up opportunity only says "Email Updates" offering no value prop as to why the user might want to sign up for email updates.

experiments.title: How adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a goal thermometer to a campaign donation page for Working Preacher users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 10/31/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign from October 1st – October 31st. We previously tested a countdown clock on the donation page for a majority of the campaign time, but we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion with the countdown clock.
We decided to test a goal thermometer for their $50,000 campaign goal on the last 4 days of the campaign. The thermometer started at $30,000 which is how much they had raised so far. The thermometer was set to grow every time someone made a donation on that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The goal thermometer saw a 19.5% decrease in donor conversion rate with an 89.2% level of confidence. Similar to the countdown clock, we can see from this experiment that the Working Preacher audience did not respond better to seeing the goal thermometer on the donation page.

We would like to test this concept further on another one of Luther Seminary's campaigns that's targeting a different audience so that we can try to understand if this type of "donor goal" experiment only performs negatively for the Working Preacher audience or for the Luther Seminary audience as a whole.

experiments.title: How adding Value Prop language to sign up form impacted emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding value prop for website visitors will achieve increased emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2022 - 09/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry that has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on emails acquired through the sign up form on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 25.0% decrease in email acquired with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 13.7% decrease in email acquired with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 22.1% decrease in email acquired with a 93% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 56.3% decrease in email acquired with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a “complete your gift” popup during a Fiscal Year End Campaign affects donor revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a “complete your gift” popup targeting visitors who choose a gift amount but abandon the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2022 - 09/04/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their august Fiscal Year-End campaign, they were seeing a decrease in donor revenue and we wanted to see if we could turn that around in the final days of the campaign. We decided to test a popup what would show if a visitor visited any donation page on their site and chose a gift amount but then abandoned the page before competing their donation. This popup would then fire when they went anywhere else on the site and offer them the prompt to complete their X gift or indicate they weren’t ready to do so. If they chose to, it would reload the donation page so they could complete the transaction.
We split the abandoned donation page traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for about 11 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were only able to record an extremely marginal impact on donor conversion for the popup experience, however the impact on revenue was found in all primary channels to the website. While we were just under a 95% LOC for all traffic, we found a similar increase on all other channels including a 41% increase for direct traffic at a 97% LOC.
The popup reduced friction for these donors, causing them to give a higher amount when they revisited the donation page. Since this ran during the Fiscal Year End campaign, we want to relaunch it in September to measure impact without a high urgency campaign skewing results one way or another.
Other revenue impact measured included:
Social visitors had a 77.0% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.7% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 13.8% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 16.6% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 17.9% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding an email acquisition form directly to a relevant blog page impacted offer sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an offer sign up opportunity directly below a related and important blog article for page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2022 - 08/04/2022
Reasons to Believe built an offer in which users could welcome the organization’s newly appointed CEO by submitting an “encouraging note” to him. The offer was set up in its traditional way: After clicking on an ad or email link, users would go to a landing page where they would read about this opportunity to submit a personal note. They could then write the note and submit it after providing their first name, last name, and email address.
RTB also posted a blog article on the news of naming the successor to their longstanding president and CEO. We wanted to test how adding the same acquisition form below the blog content would impact email submissions. So instead of reading about the opportunity to submit a personal note, users would read about the news through the blog article and be given the opportunity to submit the personal note afterwards.
For the treatment, we added a bolded call-to-action after the blog article and then added the same acquisition form as we used in the control. We wanted to test which presentation of the offer would lead to more email conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 60% lift in email conversions but only with a 72.5% level of confidence. The experiment did not validate. This offer could only run for a limited amount of time since it was news related. But it might be worth testing again in a similar capacity to see if there is in fact a trend in email conversion for a blog-level acquisition opportunity.
The experiment also revealed that returning visitors were twice as likely to convert than new visitors, which tells us that returning visitors are more familiar with RTB blogs and would perhaps get more out of reading a blog article about a new president taking over for the former president. If a similar offer is run again, it could be tested further for returning visitors.

experiments.title: How increasing narrative affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing narrative score for previous donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/02/2022
Luther Seminary was running their biannual Working Preacher campaign. They had read some research that increasing the “narrative score” using a tool called CopyOptimizer would increase conversion rate, and wanted to put it to the test. They began with a standard appeal written by their copywriter and looked for ways to “add narrative” to it.
They decided the best way would be to put the reader in the shoes of a pastor who lacked the resources to prepare their weekly sermon. Then, they connected the appeal to provide this imaginary preacher with the resources he or she would need to teach their congregation.
When they read the treatment copy, they realized that it no longer made sense to have the thermometer image at the top. They removed that, knowing that it would add a variable to the treatment, but hoping that it would help the narrative copy make more impact.
They split their email file and send one email to each half to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 47.7% increase in conversion rate due to one, or a combination of adjustments. First, this shows that the image of the thermometer may keep people from engaging. Second—the biggest change—was to change the perspective of the reader. Instead of a

experiments.title: How including branding and fund type in a appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials in a sidebar impacted email acquisitions on a free eBook offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility via book reviews for a free eBook offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 08/05/2022
We wanted to find out if increasing the appeal by adding social proof through credibility and evidentials would improve email acquisition rates on a free eBook offer. We took testimonial quotes from another author and from one of the organization’s trusted pastors and council members and featured them on the landing page to further reinforce to landing page visitors that this was a valuable book offer that was available for free. We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment and ran the test for a month.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed as a 2.2% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence. The results were pretty much flat but overall we saw a slight decrease in the percentage of total emails acquired across all traffic for those who experienced the treatment version of the landing page. Our hypothesis is that visitors could have been unfamiliar with those who were quoted in the testimonials. Because the recommendations were coming from another author and from a pastor rather than a layperson, it could have been assumed that this offer was only suitable for those in positions of ministry or Christian writers—rather than a person in the general workforce or average home. This could have led some people to think the offer was not intended for them and so they did not take the next step. Some changes in visitor segments in the experiment that were interesting to note were that while direct visitors had a 16.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence, email visitors had a 14.0% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. It's possible that email visitors could have been more receptive to the treatment since they are likely to be more familiar with the style and nature of TGC book reviews if they have been receiving regular communications via email from the organization.

experiments.title: How an exit intent popup on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing an exit intent modal for Lamplighters page visitors will achieve an increase in emails without a decrease in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2022 - 07/22/2022
Prison Fellowship International was running a lot of paid traffic to generate donations for their Lamplighters offer to share the gospel with prisoners. They knew that conversion rates were about 4%—and though they wanted to increase the conversion rate, they also wanted to capture the emails of people who were coming to the site but not giving so they could “waste fewer marketing dollars”.
However, they wanted to make sure that their exit intent popup didn’t reduce conversion, as it can sometimes be triggered when people are moving their cursor around without the intent to exit the page.
They built a popup in Unbounce and used Google Optimize to trigger it for half the visitors to determine the impact.
Note: Because the Unbounce popup had to be triggered for all visitors, the “treatment” removed the popup.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On desktop devices (where the exit intent popup fired), there was a 24.7% decrease in conversion with the exit intent popup. This showed that giving people an option not to give (even if it was on their way out) reduced conversion by adding friction.

It also suggests that this popup being triggered by cursor movement might trigger for more people than those who are leaving the page, and get in the way of donations.

The popup has since been turned off, as it wasn't a good exchange for the ~60 emails that were acquired through the popup.

experiments.title: How Moving the Global Navigation Donate Button Onto Its Own Menu Line impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the “give now” button onto its own global navigation menu line will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2022 - 07/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a prior test, they’d found that the addition of a “monthly” donate button to their global site navigation menu increased donor conversion. However, there were some issues with that test that made the results unclear. We had several factors that changed in the experiment (the placement of the “give now button,” the wording on the buttons, and the addition of a monthly donate button) that clouded the results. In order to understand WHICH factor led to the increase in donor conversion, we decided to test simply moving the “give now” button out of the menu line with the other options and onto its own line. We believed this would give it prominence as it would stand on its own in the navigation bar.
Since this would only impact Desktop traffic, that is the only source of donors we examined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn't wait to reach a 95% or greater level of confidence, there was no period during the test or segment of donors where we were seeing a positive impact and it was clear that moving the button was having a directional decrease on conversion, so we ended the test.
This showed us that donors were not more likely to click the "give now" button when it was in its own menu. Next, we will test the language of the donate button, "give now" vs "donate now" to see which is the most converting.

experiments.title: How does paginating a multi-section survey affect email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that paginating a multi-section survey for potential donors will achieve higher email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 07/10/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition rates on a multi-section survey offer, we wanted to test whether or not breaking a long 19-question survey into more manageable, less-intimidating chunks would decrease the perceived “cost” of completing the survey, in terms of time, and decrease perceived friction enough to improve sign-up rates.
We ran this experiment for 28 days, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 28 days, we observed a 31.5% lift in email acquisition rates, with a 97% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis that by reducing the perceived cost of completing the survey, in terms of time, visitors would experience less friction and would be more motivated to complete a multi-section, 19-question survey.

The results of this experiment suggest that presenting all 19 survey questions on one long page may have made completing the survey a long and laborious task, increasing the perceived cost (time) of taking the survey to a point where it outweighed the perceived value in the minds of prospects.

By breaking the survey into more manageable chunks, with each section on its own page, the perceived cost of completing the survey was reduced in the minds of prospects while the persuasion principle of 'behavioral consistency' likely helped to motivate prospects to complete the survey.

For mobile users, the experiment produced a 100.3% lift in acquisition rates, with a 100% level of confidence, likely due to the decreased scroll and potential "gamification" presented by the paginated version.

Based on these results, we recommend that organizations with longer survey offers consider paginating such surveys to reduce the perceived cost and leverage the behavioral principle of 'behavioral consistency' to lift acquisition rates.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout to a main donate page affects recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly call out to the main donate page for prospective donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 07/09/2022
The Navigators had an initiative to increase the acquisition of monthly donors. At the heel of their recurring donor campaign, we wanted to implement a simple way to acquire more recurring donors outside of campaign season. When looking at their main donation page we noticed that the monthly option blended with the other options presented for giving, among “Anonymous Gift” and “In memory of.” It was our hypothesis that the monthly option was not prominent enough for the prospective user to select this option. Furthermore, there was no value proposition language that positioned monthly giving as a better option for the prospective donor. We decided to implement a monthly callout with two variables. The first treatment simply stated what their monthly gift would do – the core value proposition: “Help believers become disciplemakers with your monthly gift today.” The second treatment gave the prospective donor a goal and identified the person as a believer who could help others mature in their faith: “We’re looking for 250 believers to give monthly to help others mature in their faith. Will you be one?” Traffic to this main donate page was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for nearly a month we saw the most impact on desktop users. For the first callout we saw a nearly valid 50% lift in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 94%. In the second callout we saw a 11% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 34%. When looking across all channels we did see a 40% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 91%. Given that the highest and most statistically valid lift was seen from the first callout, we had decided to rollout this variant to 100% of their traffic. These results indicate that the Navigators' audience are more motivated by their core value proposition to create more disciplemakers. They did not respond nearly as much to the second, goal-oriented variant. It could be that the goal of 250 seemed too large for the end-user and could've deterred them from making their monthly contribution. We are proposing to test this same concept on the Navigator's staff pages as well.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacted donor conversions on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve a change in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/01/2022
Luther Seminary was offering a free online course called Cosmic Adventure that explained the relationship between science and faith including the history behind this relationship. They offered this free course through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email marketing. This offer was running for almost a year and the conversion rate on the donation page was on a steep decline.
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding a humanized sticky bar, featuring a message and image of Andy Root, Professor at Luther Seminary and Chair of Youth and Family Ministry, would have an impact on donor conversion rates from prospective donors coming from this digital acquisition offer.
The message tried to focus the prospective donor’s attention on why they should make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 61%, but with only an 86.7% level of confidence. We hypothesize that this is due to the nature of who we included in the messaging, as well as the messaging itself. While Andy Root is well known in his community at Luther Seminary, he might not be that well known to those prospective donors who come onto the file through digital acquisition efforts--they are downloading the content because it appeals to them, and not necessarily because of who it's coming from.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 59.9% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding specifics to a subject line impacted open rates for Pathway to Victory

rows.name: Join me for the Bible Prophecy Tour of Israel
Join me for the experience of a lifetime

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being specific in the subject line for the email file will achieve a change in response rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 07/19/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of the offerings they have is a Tour of Israel, led by Senior Pastor Dr. Robert Jeffress and his wife.
As part of the ongoing promotion for this tour, we are testing different subject lines to see which one gets a better response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The difference in subject lines--one referencing the Tour of Israel and the other one being more vague but intriguing--had a negligible impact on open rates and click-through rates. With only a 0.79% increase in open rates, and a decrease of 4.9% in the experiment version in click through rate, this experiment indicates that further testing and finessing of the subject line is called for.

experiments.title: The impact on email acquisition of a content offer image placed above the fold for mobile visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the eBook image above-the-fold will increase email acquisition for mobile visitors to the landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 06/17/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life is one of Buckner’s most important online premium offers on Facebook. It has the highest number of emails acquired, yet its landing page has one of the lowest conversion rates.
When viewing the landing page on mobile devices, the image appeared all the way at the bottom of the page, below the form.
We’ve seen in testing with similar organizations that placing an image above-the-fold for mobile visitors can increase email acquisition. Since the majority of traffic to that page is mobile, we wanted to see if making the eBook image more prominent would likewise increase email for Buckner’s traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: During the experiment, we were not able to validate the test due to lower traffic to the page. However, since we came close to validation (with between a 75% and 84% level of confidence) and didn't see any negative impacts from the placement, we rolled out the top of fold image for all traffic to the page.

experiments.title: How previewing the survey affects click-through-rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing a survey for FB users will achieve higher click-through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2020 - 07/09/2022
In an effort to improve click-through-rates for a FB ad pointing people to a survey on the Border Crisis, we wanted to test if a radical redesign centered on previewing the survey, rather than highlighting the problem, would inspire more viewers to click. For this experiment, we mocked up a survey question on the ad image so that the viewer would be prompted to click to select their answer
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 25.6% lift in clicks with an 84% level of confidence. These results lead us to believe that by previewing the survey and thus focusing on the solution in the form of an action, rather than the problem, more viewers were inspired to act.

While these results are promising, because this experiment altered several elements of the ad: previewing the survey in the ad image + shortening the body text to focus on the desired action, we can not say with certainty which change had more of an impact on this lift.

Our hunch is that the image, being more visible when people scroll their feeds, played more of a role ... but to further validate these results, we will next want to test a new image using the same body copy as the winning ad above.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an ebook instant donation page impacts conversion rates and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for ebook downloaders will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 08/08/2022
Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive a ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding this offer to the instant donation page for the Spring Digest ebook would impact donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Providing a subscription for all 4 of the physical versions of the Hoover Digest as an enticement to increase ones' gift amount had a mixed effect. While the donor conversion rate not impacted at all (only a 0.9% difference), the average gift increased by 37%, with a 85% level of confidence. This shows that people are interested in giving more to receive the physical versions of the Digest.
Since this is a quarterly publication, we will continue testing this, as well as testing to see whether the same premium offer will work across other ebook offers for Hoover.

experiments.title: How will adding a value proposition to an email sign-up form affect acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a value proposition to an email sign-up form will increase email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2022 - 07/28/2022
In an effort to increase email acquisition rates for a public policy client, we wanted to test the impact of adding language that illustrates the value of signing up to receive email communications on email acquisition rates.
We tested this concept on a pop-up form that appears after website visitors click a “get updates’ button on the header of their site. The “control” version is a plain form with no headline or body copy above the form fields.
The treatment adds a headline and body copy to reinforce the value subscribers receive by signing up for email communications. We set this experiment up as an A/B test, splitting traffic evenly between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 34 days, we observed a 60.8% lift in email acquisition rates, with an 81.7% level of confidence. And encouragingly, new site visitors experienced a 284% lift in form submission, with a 94% level of confidence.

And while we were unable to collect enough samples to validate our hypothesis, the results of this experiment suggest that conveying the value of signing up for email communications may provide prospects with more motivation to share their email address. As data privacy becomes increasingly important to internet users ... and as inboxes become stuffed with more and more junk and graymail, it becomes increasingly important for organizations to place the value of signing up for your email communications front and center.

While reviewing the data we did realize that more than one concept was tested in the treatment version of the form. Not only was value proposition language added, but the form fields were also reduced in an effort to reduce friction. And while it always feels great to improve performance, testing these two concepts concurrently makes it impossible to know with certainty which of these changes produced the observed lift, and which to what degree. However, due to the encouraging results of this experiment, we decided to roll the treatment version out to 100% of traffic going forward.

experiments.title: How adding a personalized monthly call out to staff donation pages affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized monthly call out  for prospective staff donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Navigators recently ran experiment where they added a monthly call out to their main donation page. When they saw promising results through this experiment, we decided to run this same type of experiment on their staff pages. In an effort to increase monthly donations we drew attention to the monthly giving option on the donation page by adding a callout and adding a personalization. This personalization pulled in their first name in the call out so as to humanize the giving experience. Our hypothesis was that if we highlighted giving monthly as an option and we reminded the donor that they were in fact giving to a person, we would convert more monthly donors.
We tested this concept on a select group of staff members who were actively fundraising and were receiving a high amount of traffic on their pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at all recurring gifts acquired through this experiment we saw a 22% decrease in recurring gifts with a level of confidence of 66.5%. However, when examining a specific segment, new visitors, we saw a nearly valid decrease of 43.9% with a level of confidence of 94.6%. These were surprising results compared to what we experienced on the main donate page.
We concluded that:
The main donate page's callout was actually much more focused on the impact of the monthly gift "making disciplemakers" as opposed to just stating they should make a monthly gift like this callout. New site visitors may not be as swayed to see a specific person's name on the callout. Adding the personalization in third person, i.e. "Support Joe", may seem to organizationally-centric and could deter potential donors.
Our recommendation is to run this test again across all staff pages, change the statement so that it once again focuses on the value and impact of monthly giving, and change the personalization so that it reads "Support me/us."

experiments.title: How removing branding and fund type in an appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: How adding an ebook offer to a survey funnel impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ebook offer in a funnel  for survey takers will achieve a higher instant donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2022 - 06/20/2022
Reasons to believe was running an offer that encouraged people to take a science faith survey. After taking the science faith survey, they were directed to an instant donation page. Seeing that they had a low instant donor conversion rate, we wanted to add an additional step in the funnel that would provide additional value for the user. We selected one of their high performing ebook, Genesis 1, as an additional offer we can provide. The new funnel would lead people to the ebook landing page after they filled out the survey. Once they filled out the ebook form, they were led to an instant donation page. Our hypothesis that by giving the survey taker additional value in the form of an ebook, they would be more likely to give.
This was ran as a redirect experiment as 2 separate funnels. The traffic to this funnel was generated primarily through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the results of this experiment did not validate we did see a near directional 75% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 82%. While there weren't many donations in this experiment it ran for nearly 2 weeks and was not gaining much traction. It's possible that RTB's audience may have already received this ebook and did not take action in downloading it. As a result, they did not get to the instant donation page and had no opportunity to give. It may be worth testing this on some sort of prospecting channel where it is more unlikely that anyone has encountered this specific ebook. It's possible that the donation page itself needs further testing to motivate survey takers to donate.

experiments.title: How adding an ebook incentive in a survey impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ebook incentive to a survey for survey takers will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2022 - 06/10/2022
RTB launched a new offer: Science and Faith Survey. Surveys have historically produced a lower sign up rate than other offers like ebooks. In order to provide additional incentive to provide contact information as well as provide additional value for the site visitor, we included a checkbox at the end of the survey that, if checked, will send them a free ebook. Our hypothesis is that this extra added incentive and value, will increase email acquisition.
Traffic to this offer was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not validate, we did see a directional 13% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 93%. This is counter to what our initial hypothesis had stated. A possibility is that some users saw this as an extra step in their process instead of an added incentive and therefore did not perceive the ebook as an added value. However, looking at these users at the instant donation page step, the users who downloaded the ebook were more likely to take action on the instant donation page. In fact, we saw a directional 383% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 89%. This tells us that while many did not take advantage of the added incentive, those who understood the value of this ebook were more motivated to give to Reasons to Believe after they submitted their survey responses.
Potential next steps include:
Prechecking the box for the ebook; checking the box may be adding additional friction and more "work" for the end userAdding an image of the eBook to reinforce the added value Add another step after the survey to get the ebook in order to increase the donor conversion rate

experiments.title: How moving the donation page copy to “below the fold” impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the donation page copy to “below the fold” to streamline the giving experience for prospective donors in the text to give channel will achieve decrease revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 05/23/2022
As a part of a recent acquisition campaign in the “text to give” channel, the MMS partner noted that the donation page copy was “too long” and felt like it should be moved to “below the fold” (a nice newspaper term), or they wouldn’t agree to promote the offer because it was likely to not be as productive as what their “best practices” would dictate.
So, to appease them, the Leadership Institute agreed to run an A/B experiment to track the performance on the campaign and see how it impacted overall giving from their audiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 91.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in revenue of -57.6%, largely driven by a decrease in the average gift of more than 50% coming from visitors flowing through the treatment variant.
What's interesting is that there was the exactly same number of transactions between the treatment and the control experiences (0% difference), however, the generosity of the donors in the control experience was nearly twice as much as the treatment visitors.
The question is: Why?
In reviewing the transactions, we noticed that there were not any outlier gifts in the overall experiment, but there were 4 gifts at or above $100 that came through the control experience.
When you consider the channel used to drive visitors to this page (MMS) — it's clear what is behind this increase in generosity. The MMS channel messaging is designed to get a click, but after a visitor arrives on the donation page — the lack of value proposition and reason to give communicated on the page was enough to drive visitors in the treatment to give at lower amounts. Alternatively, when the value proposition to give in the control experience was placed where it normally has been, it showed that donors to the control decided not only to give at the same rate as those in the treatment experience, but they were more informed on WHY they should give and WHO they were giving to — thereby increasing the amount they decided to give.
When possible, it's highly recommended — especially in campaigns that are not driving donor-motivated clicks (like from your house email file) — to include copy that properly articulates the value proposition on the donation page, so that once they arrive on the page, they'll not only decide to give, but when they do — they'll give more generously.

experiments.title: How humanizing a sticky bar impacts donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2022 - 05/21/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for Eagle Lake camps. In order to drive more traffic to the campaign’s donation page, we wanted to implement a site-wide sticky bar. Their team member, Abby Anderson, would be the sender of these emails so we wanted to user her likeness (image and signature) to humanize the sticky bar message. People would be hearing from her from both emails and the site which would create consistency, familiarity, and would would make site visitors feel like they’re hearing from an actual person. Our hypothesis is that the humanized sticky bar would result in an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 50% directional decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 89%. Though this experiment did not validate, we have sufficient data to hypothesize that The Navigators' audience are not motivated to give by seeing a humanized sticky bar. It's also difficult to discern what percentage of site visitors were actually receiving the campaign emails. That means that particular group of people would not have been with familiar with Abby as an email sender. For future tests, it would be beneficial to retest this concept but use the picture of a student who needs to be sponsored for Eagle Lake Camps. Giving the prospective donor a visual of who they would actually be helping (or someone like this student), would humanize the content further without needing to know a specific Navigators staff member.

experiments.title: How a Personal Introduction at the Beginning of an Appeal to Donors Impacts Donor Conversion During a High Urgency Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal introduction at the beginning of an appeal will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2022 - 04/26/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas. For their spring fundraising campaign, they wanted to see if beginning an appeal with a personal story and introduction by the sender would increase donor conversion. When testing with another organization, we found that beginning an appeal like this increased engagement by humanizing the copy and building rapport. We know from research that donors give to people, not organizations, so we wanted to see if this would increase conversion during an urgent campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, we had a small group of people open the email, so we could not achieve statistical reliability. We will keep testing into more conversational and personalized intro copy to see if we can validate this approach with their audience.

experiments.title: Does adding value proposition language affect email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that broader copy and less imposing text for course prospects will achieve more course sign-ups.
