experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2021 - 10/02/2021
For RTB’s Listen and Learn course, we wanted to test broader language that may not have been as intimidating for the course prospect. We removed language like “22-part audio series” and “part 1” details. We also limited the amount of bolded text. We thought copy that appeared calmer and read more broadly might result in more email sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The course-specific language resonated better with users and resulted in more emails acquired than the broader language in the treatment.

Users like to know the specifics of a course and appreciate bolded details. It gives them greater clarity as they decide to sign up for a new course.

experiments.title: How using mystery in the subject line affects email open rate on a cohort invite
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating mystery around the learning opportunity for our general subscribers will achieve more email opens than explicitly saying what the opportunity is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2022 - 03/02/2022
It had been a while since we’d run a test using mystery vs. clarity in an email subject line. In this test, we wanted to see whether being clear that the email was an invitation to the next certification cohort or creating a mystery around a “new learning opportunity” would be more effective.
The control subject line read “An email fundraising cohort for you” and the treatment read “A new learning opportunity for you.”
Both emails were exactly the same otherwise.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate. There was hardly a change between the control and treatment results. Requires more testing.

experiments.title: How an ascending gift array affects revenue on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an ascending gift array for potential donors will achieve higher revenue per donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2021 - 08/27/2021
RTB has typically had a gift array on their donation pages with descending amounts, meaning the largest amount is the first option on their donation page. On RTB’s main donation page, we wanted to test reordering the gift array, updating it from decreasing amounts to increasing amounts. We wanted to see how it impacted revenue – would it lead people to give higher amounts? And how would it impact overall giving?
experiments.keyLearnings: Results indicated a 46.9% directional decrease in revenue (level of confidence 88%). While a typical gift array is set up in an ascending order, RTB's audiences seem to respond more to the descending order already put in place. Although the results are not statistically valid, we did see a directional  decrease after a month of testing. It is possible that RTB's audiences are already conditioned to respond to their gift array order. When observing donor conversion rate there is even less statistical significance (10% difference at 50% level of confidence). It is recommended that this is retested on the instant donation pages of offers as those funnels tend to have more newer users which may respond differently than RTB's current audience.

experiments.title: Does creating more clarity in the text of a landing page affect email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that broader copy and less imposing text for course prospects will achieve more course sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2021 - 10/02/2021
For RTB’s Listen and Learn course, we wanted to test broader language that may not have been as intimidating for the course prospect. We removed language like “22-part audio series” and “part 1” details. We also limited the amount of bolded text. We thought copy that appeared passive and read more broadly might result in more email sign-ups.
Traffic to this free online course primarily came through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a directional 16% decrease in email acquisition (level of confidence 93%). We had theorized that the extra detail regarding the course were a point of friction for landing page visitors but the data seems to indicate that RTB's audience is actually looking for these details to make their decision.
The course-specific language resonated better with users and resulted in more emails acquired than the broader language in the treatment.
Users like to know the specifics of a course and appreciate bolded details. It gives them greater clarity as they decide to sign up for a new course.

experiments.title: How a yes/no pop up with value proposition language impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a value prop based pop up  for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate during calendar year end.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2021 - 01/04/2022
During calendar year end, Avila wanted to try an exit intent pop ups in lieu of a homepage takeover for the last few days of the campaign. There was concern that constituents who come to their site for content, may not want to contribute to a campaign. So we decided to test the language in the yes/no exit intent pop up. The control asked the site visitor if they wanted to donate to Avila to support their on-going efforts. While the treatment would have value proposition based language, asking the site visitor if they want to bring spiritual guidance to those looking for truth.
Site to their page was organic and driven by the blog content and resources they continuously provide to their constituents.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results indicate that Avila's site visitors were more likely to give to their calendar year end campaign when presented with a yes/no pop up that identified Avila's key value proposition. Without having any ask attached to the copy, that audience segment produced a 3.1% conversion rate resulting in a 125% increase in donor conversion rate with level of confidence of 94.8%. This tells us that Avila's audience responds to their core mission and cause over just being asked for a donation. It is recommended that this concept is retested outside of calendar year end in order to determine if this is also true in a different context.

experiments.title: The Impact of a Higher Suggested Gift Array on a Premium Offer Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by slightly increasing the suggest gift amounts on the donation page’s giving array, we will increase revenue for FamilyLife’s premium offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2022 - 04/25/2022
FamilyLife uses premium offers as their primary acquisition source for new donors on Facebook. Their most popular offer is the Seeing Beautiful Again book that is given with a donation to the ministry. The vast majority of traffic to this donation page is from new visitors who saw an ad and clicked through to the page. We hypothesized that a slightly higher suggested gift amount on the giving array would increase donor revenue. We increased each option by $5.
NOTE: In the results below, the treatment was mislabeled as “lower” array when it was in fact higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to reach a greater than 95% level of confidence for any of the visitor segments during the test, however we were able to achieve the highest confidence on our new visitors audience, the largest audience for the premium. New Visitors gave 22% more revenue on average with the higher array. Returning visitors however, gave 33% les (with an 82% LOC). While this negative result for returning visitors is less reliable with its lower LOC, our hypothesis is that new visitors don't have a specific gift amount in mind when they visit. The new array serves as the MSRP for the premium and more choose the higher amount because they have no other frame of reference for how much the premium may be worth. Returning visitors, however, are likely used to "paying" less for the premium and are less likely to convert due to the "sticker shock" of the higher array.
More testing is needed to confirm whether this hypothesis is correct.  We also noticed after running the test that we preselected the $30 amount on the control but preselected the lower option ($20) on the treatment. This also could have colored the results and muddied the test. Before this treatment is used for new visitors to this or other offers, it should be tested further without a mismatch in which option is preselected.

experiments.title: The Impact of Aligning a Landing Page’s headline with the CTA and adding Social Proof to the page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by aligning a landing page’s headline with the CTA and adding social proof, we will improve email acquisition conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/22/2022
Focus on the Family uses free video series offers on Facebook to acquire new email addresses and donors. In a prior test, they found that adding social proof language to a landing page improved email signups by 4% with an LOC of 89%. They decided to test this approach on their Praying the Scripture Over Your Children video series offer to see if it would improve conversion. For the treatment, they added “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!” under the image of the offer.
While preparing for the test, they also decided to align the headline of the page with the CTA. So, they changed the headline from Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids to Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids With This Free Video Series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Even though the addition of social proof improved conversion on their other offer, for this video series, it did not. That said, we don't know if the result would have been different had this test not included the additional copy on the headline. Because we changed two elements of the page at the same time, it's impossible to know if both elements decreased conversion or if they worked to cancel each other out.
For future tests we should test one element at a time to ensure a clean result. Also, even in the prior test when the social proof increased conversion, it only had a 4.3% impact on conversion. We will prioritize future tests on Focus offers that are likely to result in more significant impacts.

experiments.title: How tightening copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink affected click rates on an auto-responder
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that tightening the copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink for email recipients will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/15/2022
The Gospel Coalition developed a content offer which provided a free e-book download when people signed up. To further cultivate this audience and provide even greater value to their experience, a discussion guide was sent the next day to anyone who downloaded the ebook. We wanted to know how different tones and link presentations might affect click rates and developed a treatment which would be run through an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a 9.2% directional lift in click rates with an 87.2% level of confidence indicating that the shorter, more direct copy of our treatment (compared to the more conversational, interrogative copy of the control) did have a measurable positive effect on click rates.
More subtly, the treatment copy was structured to provide the "why" (value proposition) of downloading the question guide at the beginning of the email, directly after the salutation. This is based on the theory of influence that states that providing a clear 'why' within a request is more likely to trigger a response of compliance—in this case by clicking to download the question guide "to help you get the most from this insightful resource."
This value proposition is later reinforced near the CTA (call to action) by illustrating that the question guide will a) help readers apply the text's lessons to their own life and b) provide a framework for discussing the text with others.
The treatment also hypothesized that a simple hyperlink may also help improve click rates over a button which, while very visible, may create additional friction at the CTA.
These results show that providing a "why" and streamlining the language within an email promotion can trigger a more favorable response from recipients by creating a slippery slide which carries them effortlessly from salutation to CTA while providing enough benefit for recipients to click through.
In the future, we will test these elements individually to help isolate the variable that produced the lift.

experiments.title: How adding a donate ‘button’ impacts donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button for web visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 04/16/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry that has a fundraising program that is built around premium offers. The main navigation on their homepage does not lend itself easily to a donor finding a way to make a donation; in this test we created a donate ‘button’ and tested it against the regular ‘donate’ link in the header navigation to see whether it would drive a higher conversion rate. Both of the donate links went to a landing page where the donor could choose which premium the wanted to receive as thanks for their donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a slight increase in conversion rates for users seeing the donate button, with a 3.6% lift in conversion rates and an 81% level of confidence. Interestingly, there was a larger increase in conversion rates when looking at returning visitor traffic, with an increase of 6.7% in conversion rates and an 87% level of confidence.
This is most likely due to the nature of web traffic to the Pathway to Victory website, and the path people take to get there. Generally, people get to the website by heading about Pathway to Victory on TV, the radio, or seeing a digital ad for a specific premium offer. They have no real context about what Pathway to Victory does, so they are less likely to make a donation and are more likely to look for the specific premium they wanted to purchase. However, returning visitors already have some context about the work Pathway does, and are more likely to make a donation to support the organization.

experiments.title: How the addition of a callout clarifying the impact of a specific donation amount impacted donor conversion on a campaign donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a callout clarifying the impact of a specific donation amount will increase donor conversion on the campaign donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2022 - 04/15/2022
For KCBI’s January Day of Bibles campaign, they were wanting donors to sponsor putting 5,000 Bibles into the hands of people in need. We hypothesized that adding a callout to the donation form on the campaign donation page that clarified the impact of a specific donation amount would increase donor conversion and reduce friction because visitors would have greater clarity about the impact of their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During the three days of the campaign, we did not observe any noticeable impact from the new callout. However, during the subsequent 10 weeks, as more visitors came to the page, we were able to validate the test for multiple visitor segments and achieve a directional lift for all traffic.
We believe that this greater lift was the result of the improved clarity of WHAT a donor's gift would do, in this case, provide a "bible a day" to those in need. Clarity is one of the key factors determining the strength of an appeal's value proposition.  The greater our clarity in describing the need for and impact from a donor's gift, the greater our conversion rate should be.
Since we did not achieve validity during the short duration of the campaign, we want to test this approach again on a donation page that will get more consistent traffic and have a larger sample size.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition impacted email signups for a newsletter – Part 1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal and clarity for a weekly newsletter offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2022 - 03/27/2022
In looking at ways to acquire more email addresses through a weekly newsletter offer, we looked at the current value prop offered on the landing page. With just a brief headline on the control, we saw an opportunity to develop a treatment that would provide additional language to increase specificity and exclusivity in order to get more people to subscribe. We developed a version with more appeal, outlining the benefits to the reader and giving them more details about the newsletter’s contents. We split email traffic to this page from the new subscriber welcome series.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 3 weeks, we observed a directional decrease of 19.9% in email acquisition with a 92% level of confidence. Specifically, we saw valid decreases for desktop visitors and email visitors. Because we had already "made the sale" in the body copy of the email where our visitors were arriving from for why someone should subscribe, we believe the additional value proposition was actually causing unnecessary friction on the page. We would like to run a similar test in the future to see how this would impact visitors from other channels or calls to action in other website placements that do not lead with clarity and value proposition like the email experience provides.

experiments.title: How adding tabs to an acquisition donation form affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding tabs to a donation form for the users who downloaded the offer will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2021 - 01/30/2022
For Luther Seminary’s acquisition offer, Reconnect: The Way Forward, we wanted to test the tabbed donation form on their instant donation page in order to increase the donor conversion rate. For this experiment, we kept all steps of the funnel identical in each test with the exception of changing the donation form to have a one-time donation tab and a monthly donation tab. In doing this, we removed the friction in the standard donation form that included asking the donor to check a box to make the gift recurring and asking if this donation was a change to the monthly gift they already provide.
This ebook was offered through paid Facebook advertising and the housefile email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment did not reach the 95% level of confidence standard to be valid, we saw a directional lift in conversion rate on the tabbed donation form. We believe this happened because we removed the friction within the form causing the overall donation experience to be shorter and more organized for the donor.

This concept can be tested on any acquisition donation page or main donation page, and we believe this will increase the donor conversion rate.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 58.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 220.2% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a premium on a donation page impacted donor conversion rates for Hoover Institution
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for a digital audience will achieve an increased average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2021 - 03/04/2022
Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive all ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding the premium offer to a donation page from an ebook offer impacted donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the Hoover Digest premium drove down conversion rates, but had a large increase in average gift (a lift of 78%). Since the Digest has many articles from different scholars, it might not appeal to as wide an audience as those coming through an ebook funnel. However, the increase in average gift did show that, in this experiment, adding the premium did increase the average gift.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to a donation page impacted donor conversion rates for Hoover Institution
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for a digital audience will achieve increase the average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2021 - 03/09/2022
Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive a ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding the premium offer to a donation page from an ebook offer impacted donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the Digest premium to this ebook instant donation page led to a decrease in donor conversion rates. This could be attributed to the general nature of the Digest--it is a publication with many scholarly articles, which could appeal only to a limited subset of the audience downloading an eBook.

experiments.title: How removing the organization from a sender name affected click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the organization from the sender name for email recipients will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2022
A new president was announced within the organization, meaning their email list would be receiving emails from a sender name they may not recognize. To mitigate confusion and prevent disengagement, unsubscribes, and SPAM complaints, the organization name was appended to the sender name on emails sent to their list. We wanted to know how this was affecting click rates and decided to test if we would see a lift in click rates by once again removing the organization from the sender name. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a 101.5% lift in click rates, with an 84.7% level of confidence. Despite being unable to validate this lift, the results provide a strong indication that there may be a link between sender name and click rates. This is especially interesting because the sender name is most often thought to affect open rates, but its impact on click rates is less commonly documented.
These results reinforce the knowledge that the more personal and human your outreach (especially via email), the better chance you have of connecting with donors and inspiring them to act. Whenever possible, organizations should seek to humanize their outreach and appeals—this includes ensuring that emails are sent from real people within an organization and should appear in an inbox as much like a personal email as possible. The results also show how even a small change made to one part of a campaign can have a significant affect on downstream performance.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a gift amount impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will communicating the impact each gift amount can have increase donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/27/2021
Care Net uses a traditional approach to their gift array buttons. This traditional approach simply has the gift amount on them. In an effort to increase donor conversion and, ultimately, overall revenue, we hypothesized that adding the impact of each specific gift amount might help motivate more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the way the gift array were presented and adding the impact of each specific amount on the button, we saw no difference in donor conversion. That said, we saw a directional lift in overall revenue by 22.8%! Additional here are some other observations from other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 77.0% increase in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 186.0% increase in revenue with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by presenting a micro goal on a child sponsorship funnel impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing urgency and a need for people consider child sponsorship  will achieve an increase in conversion (sponsorships/donations).
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2022 - 03/28/2022
Save the Children give people the opportunity to sponsor a child and provide for their needs. There is a lot of choices that a prospective donor has to make when they land on the child sponsorship page. From deciding if they want to sponsor a child, to deciding what child they need to sponsor, it can be overwhelming to a user. And while there is urgency in a need to sponsor a child, that message isn’t adequately communicated on the page and in the funnel.
To test into this and see if we could increase the urgency within the funnel, we created a slide in banner that would appear after 3 seconds on the page. This banner/pop-up would present a variable number of children (a micro-goal) that needed to be sponsored “by midnight tonight”. The popup scrolls with the page and has the ability to be closed by the user. We showed this variation to 50% of site users and ran the experiment for five weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we increase child sponsorships with the micro-goal urgency banner by 12% at a 90% level of confidence. Additionally, the greatest impact on conversion was for desktop devices. Desktop devices saw a 27.5% increase in conversion with a 97% level of confidence. There was no positive or negative impact on mobile devices.
The largest lift was related to Lifeline Sponsorship purchases, where the treatment saw a 151% lift over the control (46 vs 18). This was to be expected, as engaging with the slider added a Lifeline child to the cart, but was confirmation that if we guide donors to this product, they will respond positively.
There was no statistical significance between the treatments by channel, location, or new vs returning visitors.
What all of this tells us is that by presenting this micro-goal to site visitors (and telling them the deadline to meet this goal was midnight tonight), increase the urgency of the need which increased the motivation of the user. We know urgency drives conversion, but to see this urgency used during a season where urgency isn't naturally present is good to know that we can create urgency in our funnels and that donors are, in fact, highly driven by a need with a goal and deadline.
Next steps: While we hypothesize the increase in conversion was caused by the urgency that the micro-goal slide in banner created, the slide-in banner also was a totally different and new experience from the control. To isolate some of the variables used in this experiment and better understand and optimize this experience, continued testing of these different elements is the logical next step. Specifically, the copy being used, the functionality of the slide-in of placing a child "in the cart" vs taking users to a learn more page, the visual elements of the slide-in, etc.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results – Part II
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was decreasing results by 58% with 82% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 26% directional increase but only had 36% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing. Further analysis on individual transactions will be needed to analyze and uncover the cause for the decrease in average gift and revenue.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 82.4% decrease with a 98% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 70.4% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 54.7% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 4.9% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 35.7% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making an ask for a specific program impacted donation page results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a program-specific value proposition for free online course takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2022 - 03/06/2022
As we looked to increase donor conversion for free online courses, we wanted to find what messaging would most resonate with donors. The existing donation page standard copy was centered on the value proposition of getting the free course into the hands of more people. We wanted to test if transitioning the value proposition to a more specific outcome would motivate donation page visitors. We developed the first treatment to focus on using their gift to promote free online courses to pastors to supplement their training and meet this specific need/problem that exists. We developed a second treatment that highlighted current efforts to translate the free online courses into additional languages to expand their reach, which will require additional funding to build and promote. We wanted to find out if either of these treatments would increase donor conversion by addressing the needs at hand the organization is trying to meet and communicating a specific impact that the donor’s gift would be supporting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the split test across all 3 versions for one month on the donation page. What we found was the first treatment that emphasized pastoral training was mostly flat and saw a 1% decrease with 2.98% level of confidence. On the second treatment however, we learned that the translation of courses seemed to be lifting donor conversion and at the end of the experiment resulted in a 68% directional increase with 89% level of confidence. Because we saw similar results on two additional online course donation pages that were running concurrently, we made the decision to roll out the second treatment as the new control for this donation page experience. We will continue to find opportunities to optimize this copy in future testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Social visitors had a 106.9% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.New visitors had a 101.8% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 154.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 400.0% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing images from a premium-based offer impacts click-throughs and donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing copy for a digital audience will achieve higher click-throughs and donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2022 - 03/16/2022
Encouragement from a Place called Heaven was a premium-based offer that Pathway to Victory used for a very select audience in March. For the digital appeal, we tested removing the images of the premium offer, as well as humanizing the copy, all with the war in Ukraine raging in the background. We also included a quote from Dr. Jeffress’ Sunday sermon in the treatment version.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing the images and humanizing the copy, as well as referencing the upheaval caused by the war in Ukraine, the treatment version had a 38% increase in click-throughs. Donation conversion rates on both versions were the same, due to the smaller sample size for this appeal.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button increases donor conversion rates for Oblates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button for website visitors will achieve increased donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2022 - 03/04/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate had a website where the donate call to action from the homepage as part of the header navigation menu and not a button as most non-profit websites have. As they are in the process of getting a new redesign, we wanted to test whether creating a donation button would increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a donate button increased donor conversion rates by 15.5% overall, while the increase for new visitors was over 27%.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 31.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 27.2% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 105.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 168.4% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How moving the placement of the premium affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the placement of the premium for prospective donros will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2022 - 03/06/2022
RTB was running a direct appeal ad centered around a premium. In order to increase donor conversion rate we wanted to move the placement of the premium image. Having it above the fold can possibly inspire more conversions as people see what they’re getting in exchange for their donation
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we assumed that changing the placement of the book would increase donor conversion, it seems as if the control was the preferable placement. While the experiment did not validate, we did see a directional decrease in donor conversion rate. However, the traction on this offer was slow (only 3 donations to account for), so we recommend retesting this concept on a page with more traffic.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to an instant donation page impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency  for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2022 - 03/17/2022
We were seeing an overall decline in donor acquisition response and became curious if the “standard” page messaging we had been using for video series offers had been in rotation too long and needed a refresh. When we reviewed the copy, we realized that if returning donors had already signed up for a separate video series, they would have already seen this messaging and had already been told why they should give…however, the main thing that was lacking was the answer to the question: why should I give right now? We built a treatment keeping this component in mind that we wanted to create a sense of urgency and clearly outline the problem that this video series is designed to address. We incorporated language explaining why “Praying Scripture over your Children” is especially needed and important in today’s culture and emphasized we must not wait until it’s too late! We weaved in elements like Scripture and donor alignment and shortened the copy from 5 important things to 3 important things.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the treatment for a month and saw some valuable results. Overall there was a 29.9% lift in donations with 80% confidence so we did not reach statistical significance. If we drill down into DESKTOP traffic, we did validate a 226.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence for this page, but note that it is only based on 22 total transactions.  Similarly, returning visitors had a 482.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence. This is a huge learning for our other video series donation pages as this matches up with our theory that the old donation page copy may have been stale and glazed over by returning visitors who have read similar messaging copy on multiple video series over the past year.
--Moving forward, we will work this sense of urgency into our copy as it specifically relates to the video series topic at hand as it seems to be a huge motivator for our traffic that is already familiar with the ministry.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 38.9% increase with a 93% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 5.2% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 677.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 7.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 151.8% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a stronger value proposition in the website footer affects email acquisition

rows.name: Value Prop Angle – Tested and Proven Strateiges

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity to the website footer opt-in value proposition for website visitors will achieve greater email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2021 - 02/10/2022
In this experiment, we wondered if users were really understanding the value that they would get from signing up for NextAfter emails. We hypothesized that a clearer and more specific value proposition would lead to more people signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The clearer and more specific value proposition in the footer led to a 33% increase in email signups over a 90 day period - reaching a 94% level of confidence. And it appeared to have the most significant impact on new visitors who would be learning about NextAfter for the first time.
This is a great experiment to further confirm the need for clear articulation of a value proposition - especially when every other company, business, and organization has a "newsletter" as well. There is a lot of noise out there about best practices and training for nonprofit marketers and fundraiser. We must always articulate why our offerings are both different and better.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 28.5% increase in footer subscribe with an 88% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 42.3% increase in footer subscribe with a 94% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had a 194.4% increase in footer subscribe with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding social proof impacted email acquisition on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2021 - 02/10/2022
Focus on the Family reached a milestone of over 1 million sign-ups for their free video series, and we wanted to test our hypothesis that highlighting this as social proof on the landing page would increase email sign-ups on the landing page. We added a call out underneath the image to “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a directional lift of 4% on the treatment page with an 89.9% level of confidence. Highlighting the number of people who have accessed the free video series seemed to compel landing page visitors to move forward with completing the registration form. This treatment specifically worked well for mobile visitors and email visitors. Based on these promising results, we will look for additional opportunities to test adding social proof elements like this one on future offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.1% decrease in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 5.2% increase in emails acquired with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 10.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 98% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 19.0% increase in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 37.0% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 40.0% increase in emails acquired with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 91.7% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 54.5% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a matching gift announcement impacted donor conversion rate for non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that announcing a matching gift for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2022 - 02/06/2022
A generous donor made a matching gift available to the Canadian Taxpayer Federation so that they could specifically inspire non-donors to become first-time donors.
They wanted to know how a matching gift announcement for non-donors who just signed a petition would react and whether or not the matching gift would increase giving for this audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 83.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in donor conversion rate of -28.7% for non-donors on acquisition donation forms.
Other observations observed in this experiment include:
Tablet visitors had an 88.8% decrease in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 67.4% decrease in revenue with a 93% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that this message is perhaps too long and not targeted enough. Therefore, we will be re-building this experiment to try another treatment that meets these needs.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation form impacted gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an optimized donation form for Giving Tuesday donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2021 - 01/04/2022
Before launching a calendar year-end campaign, we want to test a radically redesigned treatment of Buckner’s donation form to see if we should roll out a new version during year-end. We wanted to first test this for their Giving Tuesday donation form. For the treatment, we made several changes to the form to optimize it. The credit card information was relocated to be immediately underneath the gift array (above donor information) We removed the .00 from the gift array dollar amounts and instead show $25 $50 and $100. We added a tabbed form with a monthly giving option with the header “Can you give monthly?” and added a value prop for giving monthly when the monthly tab is selected – “Show an orphan God’s love all year long with your monthly gift on Giving Tuesday”. We removed optional fields including Title and Phone Number. We revised the email sign-up disclaimer language to Our team will use your email address to share occasional updates with you and communicate the ways your gift is making a difference. And we changed the donate button text to Help a hurting child with my gift »
We split traffic to both versions of the form during their high urgency campaign for Giving Tuesday.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was not what we expected, which is why it is valuable to test and assess results before making changes. Since the campaign was run on a limited window for a 24 hour giving day, we did not receive enough traffic to validate the learnings. But we did notice a trend of a 24% decrease in gifts with an 83% level of confidence. This gave us reason to pause considering implementing the changes to the form for the year-end campaign. Moving forward, we will need to test these elements individually to get a better idea of which piece was responsible for the decrease in transactions.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 69.6% decrease with a 95% level of confidence.
Email visitors had a 28.8% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 50.9% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 309.1% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 48.4% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 92.1% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 300.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How promoting a subscription type instead of a content offer impacted email signup rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that promoting an eBook in the “dear reader” ads for visitors to the newsroom will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2022 - 01/14/2022
As a part of experimenting with article traffic conversion opportunities, we wanted to experiment with whether promoting a subscription would increase the email signup and donor conversion rates over promoting a downloadable PDF eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not shown here was that email signup rates decreased by 51.5% (LoC: 99.6%). It appears that the downloadable content isn't as motivating to subscribe (and subsequently to give) when it comes to this ad unit type.

Our recommendation is to test a different offer type (a petition) now that we have seen that this content offer type was defeated by the subscription promotion.

Other notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 77.5% decrease with a 90% level of confidence.
Returning visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to sticky bar messaging impacted donations for a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and seasonally relevant messaging for website visitors will achieve more calendar year-end donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2021 - 01/04/2022
During a calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test various messaging on a sticky bar to see what would most resonate with donors to get them to the donation page to make a gift. We had a general control message that prompted a gift to ‘share resources with believers’ and mentioned the upcoming deadline. The first treatment added clarity around what the donor’s gift would accomplish – highlighting that each dollar would impact 10 people. The second treatment posed a question to introduce donor autonomy – will you help us? The third treatment was the same message, but appeared at the bottom of the screen rather than the top of the page. We split traffic to all 4 versions for site visitors during the last week of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach statistical significance since the experiment was time-bound and only ran for a short period of time, what we did find is that the language that clearly outlined the impact of each dollar given seemed to be moving more donors to conversion. This will need to be tested again outside of a high urgency campaign window for more clear results, but we can find additional ways to incorporate this language into the value proposition for future donor communications.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 5.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 163.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 78.0% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 5.8% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communicating how many more donors are needed in a campaign to meet your goal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the number of target donors for year-end campaign recipients will achieve improve motivation to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/01/2022
As a part of the 2021 year end fundraising campaign, the Leadership Institute wanted to experiment with the final email of the campaign to see what would motivate donors to make their gift before the deadline.
Throughout the campaign, they had been communicating to donors that they needed a certain number of donors to make gifts at a particular gift level. But, they wondered if changing the number of donors on the final email would have any impact on driving gifts before the midnight deadline.
We theorized the following variations for the corresponding reasons:
Control: This variant simply repeats the goal number of donors from the email from earlier in the day.
Treatment 1:  This variant implies that we achieved the goal number of donors from the email earlier in the day, but we wanted to get X more (e.g. “we want to do more!” messaging).
Treatment 2: This variant tells the reader that they could be the last donor that is needed to achieve the goal.
Would any of these variants drive motivation to give to a higher rate and lift overall results?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although there are no validated results, we observed two outcomes that were of interest:
The "5 More" (treatment 1) variant actually directionally decreased results. Could this mean that communicating that we've hit and are now attempting to surpass the campaign goal is de-motivating for donors? Future experimentation with this messaging type may need to be paired with an additional value proposition as to what additional gifts secured would lead to in order to see if this motivation type can work in the future to lift results. And,The "One More" (treatment 2) variant did not achieve a reliable lift in donor conversion rate. Our team assumed that this would significantly lift response rates, but although there is a slight lift (+18.6%), the confidence score is so low (39.6%) that assuming this kind of lift will be reproduced in a future experiment should be considered unreliable.
Further experimentation is required with these variant types. It would be advisable to carry this motivation messaging sequence throughout multiple emails to ensure that the gift samples are high enough to get a reliable reading in the future.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted monthly and one-time gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign focused on recurring giving for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in monthly recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2021 - 01/07/2022
Ahead of their calendar year-end campaign, DTS sent out a digital Christmas Devotional as a cultivation piece to their housefile. The control donation page used their standard appeal language with a paragraph outlining the benefits of monthly giving. To increase recurring gifts, we wanted to test a donation page treatment that specifically asked for a monthly recurring gift emphasizing a microask goal of 10 new daily monthly donors. In addition to the microask goal, we made several other optimizations to the page. We swapped out the headline to reflect that their monthly gift would have an impact 365 days a year and introduced the concept of donor autonomy and value alignment throughout the copy by asking conversational questions such as “Would you consider becoming a Cornerstone Partner?” and “Will you make a monthly gift today to help share biblical truth with the world?” We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment for visitors after they signed up to receive their copy of the digital Christmas Devotional.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive enough monthly gifts to validate the experiment's impact on recurring giving, we did see other interesting trends in overall giving as a result of running the test. We did not reach statistical significance overall, but we identified that the treatment page was showing a 29.4% lift in donor conversion with 86% level of confidence. Furthermore, as you'll see below, when we analyzed traffic by channel, mobile visitors saw a valid increase of 116% in donations with a 98% level of confidence. The treatment copy is significantly shorter than the control, so we will need to do additional testing to see if reducing the volume of text and limiting the amount of user scrolling contributed to the lift for mobile visitors. Again, even though we did not see movement in monthly giving as the experiment was intended to produce, we identified learnings to assist in improving conversion rate and overall revenue moving forward for future content offers.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 62.2% increase with a 90% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 33.2% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 116.0% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 53.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 116.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.New visitors had a 64.4% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a tabbed donation form impacted recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a tabbed donation form for visitors to /renew and the main donation page will achieve an increase in recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2020 - 02/28/2020
As a follow up to a previous experiment, we wanted to see if using a tabbed recurring donation form for visitors to the two primary donation forms would improve recurring donor acquisition. A previous experiment found a significant books in recurring gifts but many of these ended up being mistakes that had to be refunded. We wanted to see if we could have the same impact if we started the form on one-time gift instead of recurring.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full month, we discovered there was no statistically significant impact to recurring gifts. While we did see a directional lift, it would require a significantly higher volume of gifts to validate. We decided to hold off on rolling out this treatment until it could be fully validated with a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve increase overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Care Net wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we nearly doubled the revenue from the campaign, we did not statistically validate the experiment due to an 88.6% level of confidence. With that said, this is certainly encouraging. When analyzing the individual gift, we did not see a decrease in overall donor average gift. Instead, we saw increases in the number of lower dollar donors and the higher dollar donors ($250+) maintained their previous level of giving.

It would be worth iterating on this concept but to validate this concept with the Care Net donor file, we will need to expand the length of this test for several email during a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve an increase in overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, Care Net wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday, and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we came close to validating with a 91.2% level of confidence, the number of actual gifts was too small to determine whether this strategy was successful. To validate this concept with the Care Net donor file, we will need to expand the length of this test for several email during a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: Will offering a free membership trial leads to more membership activations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free 1-month trial for landing page visitors will achieve more membership activations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/01/2021
As we reconfigured what membership looked like, we were trying to figure out a way to get more people to activate a membership to the NextAfter Institute. So, we thought if we offered folks a free 1-month trial, it would allow them the chance to learn what membership is about and be able to access our resources right away. We ran this experiment on our membership landing page with a 50/50 split in traffic between signing up for the typical $49/month membership vs. the 1-month free trial.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Something important to note is that metric tracking between all systems in this experiment was messy. We did our best to isolate variables, but they are not perfect.

Although the experiment did not validate, results point in the direction that the free trial is most effective in achieving more membership activations. However, something worth considering is if those folks will be retained as well as those who pay for the membership right away—more testing and analysis needs to be done to figure that out.

experiments.title: Impact of reducing mental anxiety by adding, “cancel anytime” to a monthly donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that Adding “cancel anytime” language to our monthly donation page for visitors will achieve a greater conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2021 - 11/14/2021
We know that decreasing mental anxiety leads to an increase in donor conversion. One potential point of anxiety when asking for a monthly donation is the concern that the donor is then “locked into” a recurring transaction that they can’t get out of. We decided to test adding “cancel anytime” language to the donation page for our Fall monthly donor campaign to see which converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Due to the limited duration of the test (one two-week campaign) we couldn't validate the results on all audiences, however we had enough mobile traffic to validate based on that segment. Here, we found a 38% increase in donor conversion. Most other segments also reported an increase in donor conversion by adding the "cancel anytime language." ( the overall average was a 12% increase). This means that by reducing this mental anxiety we can convert more visitors into monthly donors. We will roll this out to other pages.

experiments.title: How a premium impacted donor convertion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a book premium for survey takers from the email house file will achieve increase donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2021 - 11/18/2021
American Cornerstone Institute wanted to find new ways to incentivize their email house file to make a donation. As part of their education survey instant donation page, they decided to test out including a free copy of the Little Patriots first children’s book with a donation of $25 or more. Since the donor audience is a bit older, the book was framed as a great way for them to impart this same curriculum to their children or grandchildren.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After receiving over 150 donations, we found that the book premium did not improve statistically donor conversion--it actually seemed to have a negative impact (although that was not statistically significant.) Since this premium had incremental costs associated with it, we decided to end the experiment prior to it validating.
With an average instant donor conversion rate of 18.5%, the next logical step is to find ways to increase the value received from the donors rather than trying to getting more of them to give. The average instant donor conversion rate is normally between 1% to 4%. With this in mind, the next experiment will focus in increasing recurring donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How suggested gift amounts affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does suggesting gift amounts based upon previous giving increase the revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2015 - 06/22/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to find a way to lower the number of decisions donors had to make when giving their gift. The idea was the suggest a gift amount based upon their previous giving history with the hypothesis that this would lower the friction of the giving form.
To test this hypothesis, we set up an experiment to split traffic between their standard blank gift amount field and a form that would populate three different buttons based upon their last gift amount (this was passed in via the query string).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By suggesting a gift amount, the overall donation page conversion dropped by 54.9%. This was a surprising result given the success of this type of experiment with other research partners.
This outcome taught us something significant about DTS donors. The average gift for their online donors is significantly higher than the most other nonprofits.  As a result, the suggested gift amounts were far larger than would normally be seen on a donation page.  It is our hypothesis that these larger gift options scared away potential donors to the campaign by presuming too much.  We get better results when we let the donor determine their own level of generosity.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest gift for recent donors will achieve an increase in overall appeal revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2021 - 09/30/2021
For KCBI’s fall fundraising campaign, we gained access to a new tool (Pursuant’s GivingDNA) with the ability to predict donors who were likely to upgrade. To validate this modeled data, we decided to launch a two-prong experiment. We wanted to see if we could accelerate the upgrade process by asking for a gift above their highest previous gift. We also decided to see if we could increase conversion by asking for a donation less than their highest previous gift for the segment that was not identified as likely to upgrade.
We split the donor file in four ways to create control and treatment groups of the upgrade and non-upgrade identified segments for this experiment. We then created four distinct emails in an eight email campaign that would either include the standard ask or would ask them for the upgraded/downgraded amount. The upgrade/downgrade language would consist of logic that explained why we were asking for more or less. This same language was used in all four experiment emails.
The experiment information below is specific to the segment that was identified as not likely to upgrade.
In this campaign, we utilize a tactic known as the chart of standards. We segmented each segment by their highest previous contribution and then selected a limited number of donors for a specific ask amount we wanted for this campaign. You can see this breakdown here. This concept is based upon research done on a theory in social psychology known as construal level theory. Our previous research has found this to lift giving in high urgency campaign–this experiment was a continuation of this research effort.
At the end of the campaign, we analyzed the behavior of these distinct donors to understand how it both impacted their likelihood to upgrade and their likelihood to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a directional lift of 31% in revenue, we found that asking for a donation that was less than their highest previous gift amount increased the total revenue raised from the appeal. This came from a 24% increase in response rate combined with a higher average gift in the treatment group.

The concern with this approach was that we would suppress giving from donors that would have upgraded their gift amount. However, we found that the same number of people increased their giving despite the treatment group seeing a downgraded ask. This means the tactic didn't negatively impact the likelihood of a donor upgrading their highest previous gift.

This tactic needs to be further validated with additional experimentation but is exciting in its potential.

experiments.title: How simplifying the primary header on the Focus site for mobile devices impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by removing additional clutter and creating clarity to the navigation header?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2021 - 09/10/2021
Focus on the Family was running a high urgency giving campaign called SeeLife. During the campaign they featured a donation wikibanner at the top of the site’s primary header and navigation. When looking at the experience on mobile devices, there was a lot of clutter with the addition of this wikibanner to what was already on the site. We hypothesized this was causing friction to the donation pathway. To test into this, we developed two variations. For one of them, we removed the CTA’s in the header that were not needed to make a gift, but kept the “donate” button. For the second version, we removed the CTA’s in the header that were not needed to make a gift, removed the donate button, but added a line of copy above the gift array that said, “please select the amount you would like to give”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren't able to validate the experiment, but we did see get a directional lift with one variation and got even greater clarity with the user experience.
We are learning that donor's are experiencing the most friction with the wikibanner itself. When we added the call to action on top of the gift array, we were able to increase donor conversion (directionally) by 11%! Revenue experienced a 15.4% increase with a 92% level of confidence.
Just adding the donate button back in to the experience had no impact on donor conversion.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Search visitors had a 113.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 12.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 127.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had an 84.4% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How specificity in the value proposition affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity to the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2021 - 08/06/2021
Prison Fellowship International was running a campaign to acquire donors for their Lamplighter program, which told prisoners about Jesus through a Bible study. The page they had developed  had the headline “Bring Light Into the Darkness”. Since the audience they were targeting was primarily Christian, they wondered if they should be more specific and give the donor the opportunity to “Share Jesus with a Prisoner in Darkness”.
They developed this new headline and launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though it did not validate, the new copy produced a 23% decrease in conversion rate. There are a few possible reasons for this:
First, the new copy, while clearer, might be less engaging to the reader and not bring them into the body copy as much. Second, the new value proposition might not align with the rest of the page. Finally, it might come across to the reader as negative to cast the "prisoner" as someone "in darkness".
Ultimately, this experiment was directional and slow to validate, so more radical approaches must be tested to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing the donation page with experiential language for fiscal year end donors will achieve an increased donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2021 - 08/13/2021
During a fiscal year end campaign, we were seeing lower donor conversion compared to the previous year so we decided to develop a treatment to the donation page for the remainder of the campaign to see if we could improve performance. We modified the headline from “Help DTS reach the world with solid biblical training” to “You can help DTS train students to reach the world with the gospel of Jesus Christ!” We also removed body copy that seemed organization-centric that referenced enrollment trends and their accredited degree programs and instead of featuring the high-caliber quality of the training gave examples of vocations that students pursue after their education and training (counseling, pastoring, etc.) to humanize the institution and presented this as an experiential outcome for how they would “share the good news and strengthen believers” which aligns with what the donor wants to accomplish through their gift. We also introduced copy that highlighted the option to give monthly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since the experiment only ran during the final 2 days of the campaign, we would need to run an additional test with similar language to truly test the concept without the existing urgency of the deadline that could be influencing results. The treatment donation page had a 19.1% increase in conversion rate with 93% level of confidence. We want to take this directional lift in gifts on the page and see where else we could apply this learning.
While we did not reach statistical significance to validate the experiment, we did observe some notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment that are outlined below:
Revenue experienced a 47.3% decrease with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New  visitors had a 39.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 23.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had an 88.5% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 76.4% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects email acquisition on a homepage ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition that conveys more urgency and focuses on the revenue at stake for homepage visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2020 - 12/30/2020
In this experiment, we were promoting a new ebook focused on fundraising ideas that can help increase revenue during the last week of the year. We had two main ideas as to how to message the advertisement.
In version A, we used the homepage ad itself to illustrate how fundraisers can use a similar homepage ad to promote their year-end campaign.
In version B, the messaging emphasis focused on the urgency around the biggest giving week of the year being right around the corner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We didn't quite hit our desired 95% level of confidence, but the result was a strong indicator that it was more relevant to emphasize the urgency around the last week of the year – leading to a 50% increase in conversions. As noted below, it was especially effective for website visitors that already had some affinity towards us and our content.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 77.7% increase in all form submissions with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 213.3% increase in all form submissions with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 66.7% increase in all form submissions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language impacts donor conversion on a hero banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on the hero image may increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/27/2021
Care Net gets a healthy conversion rate on the hero image of their website. The call to action is to make a (monthly) gift, though they get a high volume of one time gifts from it as well. To see if we could increase the conversion from the banner, we wanted to test communal language on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we weren't able to validate the test but we did see a 27% directional lift from the communal language. Additionally, this increased overall revenue by 33% at a 86% level of confidence. What is really interesting is that returning visitors had an even greater difference in conversion. The communal language increased donor conversion for returning visitors to the site by 49%! All of these results reinforce the idea that when we talk to the visitor as if they are a friend and even recognize and call-out the fact that we know and appreciate that they are fierce advocates for the cause, we increase their likelihood to give and ultimately convert. The conversion become less about the transactional nature of the gift and more about the impact and the donor.

An additional observation of the impact on another visitor segment in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How trustmarks on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Charity Navigator trust mark for donation page visitors will achieve increase in number of gifts and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2020 - 12/10/2020
In previous experiments, we have found that adding trust marks near the donation button on a donation page can lead to an increase in donations. However, we wanted to take this learning a step further and test how the presence of ANY trust marks and the presence of CERTAIN trust marks would impact revenue.
The control version of the donation page featured two of the organization’s standard trust marks, with the first treatment removing all trust marks and the second treatment containing the standard trust marks and the additional Charity Navigator trust mark.
The Charity Navigator trust mark showed the organization’s score/ranking of 75% with the “Give with Confidence” banner. The placement for all trust marks was directly beneath the donate button at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a valid level of confidence for the experiment, what we observed through this experiment was the treatment with no trust marks and the treatment with the added Charity Navigator trust mark were both seeing an overall increase in conversion rate. However, when we analyze revenue and average gift performance for each version, the first treatment with no charity marks saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control while the second treatment with the additional Charity Navgiator trust mark was approaching a valid lift in revenue (58.3% with a 91% level of confidence). We also noted a few segments that were impacted directly, with an increase in conversion rate for both tablet and email visitors.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 58.3% increase with a 91% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
