Our hypothesis is that when showing the ad service platform (in this case Facebook) that we want to run a campaign, telling them that we’re willing to advertise for 6-months (and subsequently, can spend 6-months worth of budget in total) on this new campaign tells the algorithm that they stand to make more money when showing your ad, compared to other advertisers that might also be bidding to serve ads to that same user.

If you think of digital ad campaigns as an auction (which is what they are) — and you, as a buyer, show up with a big briefcase full of money — the auction house knows you’re a qualified buyer, and will provide you with preferential treatment. Just like with an auction, and as a buyer with a big briefcase full of money, you don’t have to actually spend the money you came into the auction house with. So, if the campaign isn’t performing well, you can always turn off your campaign(s) or ads.

Further experimentation may be required to validate this hypothesis, however, it might be advisable to simply run your campaigns for a longer period of time, with the total budget (under the “lifetime budget” setting) to ensure that you can garner better efficiency, better prioritization, and better return on investment for the same dollar spent by simply appearing bigger than you really are.

experiments.title: How digital brand reinforcement impacts overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing brand ads to the direct mail audience have a positive impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2018 - 03/31/2018
Buckner International has recently made a significant effort to align their messaging across their various communication channels. What this means at a practical level is that the topic discussed in the direct mail letter will align with have several blog posts and what is being discussed on social media. The idea is that the more a constituent hears about a problem, the more likely they will be to response with a gift.
However, not all donors to Buckner follow them on social media or visit their website. In an effort to boost their brand reach, we decided to test showing brand ads that aligned to the current month’s message to those receiving direct mail pieces. We split the direct mail audience in half and showed 50% of their donors their brand ads via Facebook. By splitting their audience in half, we will then be able to analyze the impact of these ads
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach a 95% level of confidence, we did see a directional boost of 23% in donor conversion for those that were shown the brand ads. However, since these brand ads have a cost associated with them and we did not achieve statistically significant results, this is a test we will want to repeat with a new data set to further validate the concept.

experiments.title: How reducing the required steps affects perceived value and  donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the required email acquisition form from the process reduce friction enough to improve the number of new donors acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2015 - 12/02/2015
On Giving Tuesday, the Texas State Historical Association was offering their visitors a free eBook as a way for TSHA to give back to them. Immediately after requesting their free eBook, they were given the opportunity to make a gift. Historically, we had required visitors to fill out an email acquisition form before they would be sent the eBook even if we already had their email address.
We had the hypothesis that if we could remove this step, we would get more visitors to the donation page which would lead to an increased number of donors. So we created a treatment that would send an email with a link to the eBook when the visitor first got to the page and then immediately lead into the opportunity for membership.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned from this experiment that while the email acquisition form did add friction to the process, it also increased the perceived value of the eBook which made the donor conversion easier on the second page.  The treatment that just gave them the eBook resulted in a 41% decreased the number of donors acquired.

experiments.title: How inviting donors to contribute to a matching gift affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift affects conversion versus a traditional matching gift?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2020
Leadership Institute was running a special “pre-Giving Tuesday” campaign as the initial part of their year-end campaign. The team wanted to test whether offering donors the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift would inspire more generosity, as opposed to a traditional matching gift as an incentive (as had traditionally been done). They developed two series of emails and split donors by previous giving amounts to run each through a three-email campaign leading up to Giving Tuesday to determine which approach resulted in more gifts and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: In terms of conversion, there was no statistical difference between the two approaches. Given the same size of housefile recipients, the incentive to "be the match" did not get more people to give. However, a deeper look at the data showed that the "be the match" effort had a large, if not statistically significant effort on average gift size. This approach may have unlocked larger gifts due to the elevated importance it placed on the donor. Since many donors, especially those at LI, are trained to understand that matching gift sizes are usually $10,000+, there may have been a motivation to give more to join a "larger giving group". However, it should be noted that with only 114 gifts between the two campaigns, the sample size is relatively small. This approach (which is somewhat limited in its application abilities) should be tested with a larger sample size and a higher expected number of total gifts.

experiments.title: How emphasizing a recurring gift impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the impact of a recurring increase the overall revenue per visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2017 - 06/06/2017
A past analysis of The Heritage Foundation donor file found that recurring donors provide the highest donor value to the organization and have the best chance of being retained into future years. Based upon this analysis, we decided to launch an experiment to increase the number of recurring givers.
We crafted value proposition copy around the reason to make a recurring gift and included near the recurring gift option on the donation form. We also emphasized making the gift via their Bank Account as these donations tend to have a longer lifetime.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month of collecting data, we did not see any significant impact on the donor’s likelihood to make a recurring gift, select their Bank Account, or increase overall revenue to the organization. This is still a priority for the organization so we will go back to the drawing board to develop new hypotheses to increase someone’s likelihood to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How adding a “Get a Recording” option affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “Get a Recording” option to the registration page lead to greater signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2020 - 02/11/2020
We have many folks who sign up for our webinars and then immediately let us know they can’t actually make it to the live event. We always send out a recording, so there’s not problem. But we wondered if giving them an option on the page to get the recording could increase signups. Our hypothesis was that there could be many people choosing to not register because they can’t make the time work – and there’s no indication on the page that there will be a recording available.
The treatment placed a “Get the Recording” button in the time and date bar. The thought was that this is the key point in which someone would be deciding if they can make it or not. The button opened a lightbox with a dedicated form to sign up for the recording.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the recording option led to an 8.6% decrease in registrations. However, it did not reach a desired level of confidence of 95%. We also looked at webinar attendee rate and playback based on which treatment they signed up from, and the rates are nearly identical. So with the potential for the recording button to decrease registrations – and making no impact on attendance or playback rate – we will not offer the recording option in this format.
It’s possible that the option to get a recording was placed too far away from the form itself. A future test idea would be to make the recording an option within the registration form as that could be the key point in which someone is deciding whether or not they can make the live event.

experiments.title: How a grid layout affects clickthrough on a membership benefits page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a grid layout lead to more clicks into the checkout process?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2019 - 01/26/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if a grid style layout would be effective at showing visitors the various benefits of our 3 membership tiers, and thus lead to more clickthroughs to the checkout process.
The control used a bulleted list to describe the benefits of each tier. The treatment used a large grid with Xs and checkmarks to visually show what was included with each tier.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn’t reach a 95% level of confidence, the experiment had been running for a full month and has been leaning towards validity. The consequences of being wrong in this case a fairly low. But we will conduct follow-up testing to see if we can confidently improve clicks and conversions in the membership sign-up process.
The results indicate that this more visual approach is more effective at demonstrating the member benefits and showing the value of membership. It’s possible that more info is need on the page to clarify what some of the benefits are. And we also need to ensure in future testing that we can track all the way through to a purchase.
It also looks like a new element was added into the control variant during the test. And it’s likely that this skewed the final result.

experiments.title: How a book premium impacted acquisition offer revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a book (gift premium) work to increase either donor conversion rate or revenue (and subsequently ROI) for our acquisition offers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2019 - 01/31/2020
In an attempt to boost ROI from their acquisition efforts in expanding their digital program, The Fund for American Studies (TFAS) was looking for ways to increase their average gift, while ideally not impacting their donor conversion rate. They decided to test a gift premium tied to the $100 gift amount to see if they could improve results substantially and drive ROI to a higher level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 89.7% level of confidence, we were able to achieve a +20.6% increase in revenue by using the book as a gift premium (tied to the $100 gift level) on our acquisition offers.
Although this experiment did not fully validate (at the 95% LoC that we would typically require to declare a winner), because of the preference to increase ROI, TFAS decided to rollout the treatment and take the increased revenue (and ROI).
There are a few more observations made throughout this experiment, notably the following:
We did also observe a -10% donor conversion rate (although that only achieved a 55% LoC, which means it was not validated).
The increase in revenue, albeit with fewer donors for the treatment was driven by the dramatic increase in average gift (+36%).
Specifically, the treatment experience produced nearly 25% of the overall gifts received at the $100+ gift level (to receive the premium), whereas the control experience only achieved 6.4% of the overall gifts received at that same gift level ($100+).
Overall, the overall net incremental cost the organization was an additional $3 to acquire these book premium donors at the $100+ gift level.
For all of these reasons, TFAS decided to take the much higher volume of $100+ donors through their acquisition efforts for the additional $3 net cost to acquire them.
It should be noted that if you are considering using a book premium, that the placement of the book (after the core value proposition) as well as the relevancy of the book to the core value proposition that is chosen is highly recommended.
in this case, a high-profile alumnus wrote the book used in this experiment. We believe that these two factors are critical for acquiring long-term supporters for the organization (as the offer is not the book, but the core value proposition, and that the book sits behind that core offer as an incentive to “give more” to receive it for the donor).
Because of this, future testing with this book serving as the control will be considered, whereas new and more relevant books will be considered as treatments to future acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How a pre-selected value on the gift array impacted donor conversion rate and revenue from acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “most popular” pre-selected value on the gift array impact donor conversion rate or revenue for acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking into ways to increase their donor conversion rate and revenue from the investment they were making in acquiring donors through instant donation forms online. In reviewing the forms, we decided to try adding a “Most Popular” pre-selected option in the gift array to see how it would impact these key metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This treatment improved revenue by 94.4% (with a 92.3% level of confidence). Additionally, the donor conversion rate was showing an increase of 29.5% (although the level of confidence achieved was only 66%). Although these results didn’t officially validate, the lift in revenue is certainly directional and the decision was made to roll this out to gain the apparent benefits of the treatment option.

This increase in revenue was largely driven by an increase in the average gift amount of +55%.

Additionally, the number of $50 or greater sized gifts acquired through the treatment option was doubled in the treatment.

When donors are considering giving a gift during the instant donation page in your acquisition efforts, one of the decisions they have to make is “how much should I give?” This “Most Popular” treatment appears to decrease the friction in the process of making a donation because the donors now have a “starting point” provided to them as to make a decision about “how much” to give.

experiments.title: How placement of an email capture before a quiz improve email registration rates for the Economic Freedom Quiz

rows.name: read the petition now - email capture last
read the petition now - email capture first

experiments.researchQuestion: Does an email registration form at the beginning of the Economic Opportunity Quiz improve email registration rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2019 - 04/06/2019
When we launched the Economic Freedom Quiz, we wanted to improve our email registration rate. When reviewing the user experience, the email capture was originally placed at the end of the quiz, so we wanted to move it to before the quiz started to see if we could improve email registration rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 92.1% level of confidence, we improved the email registration rate by 71.7%.
Our original thought was that the anticipation of finding out your score would lead to a higher rate, which is why it was originally launched at the end of the quiz, however it appears that the quiz takers after clicking would be more willing to give their email address to start the quiz.

experiments.title: How replacing older donation page copy impacted donor conversion rate for a new offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Would reusing the donation page copy previously used to send a series of appeals work behind a new content offer for a related subject?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2019 - 12/13/2019
When launching a new content offer related to the impeachment of the president and how the process works, we decided to launch with to donation page treatments–one that had been used as the donation page for a short series of emails when the news was released, and another that had not yet been tested. This approach was used to mitigate risk with new donation page related to the topic, and since the housefile emails along with the control donation page had produced some nice results, we assumed we would be able to capitalize upon this page’s positioning of the fundraising appeal for new content downloaders.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page (which was previously unseen by the housefile) showed an aggregate increase in donor conversion rate of 25.1%, but did not validate (LoC: 85.5%) because we turned it off early.
Why? Because we actually saw no difference between the treatments for new visitors, but saw a decline in donor conversion rate of 34% (LoC: 92%) for returning visitors to the control page.
Our hypothesis is that donors that had previously seen the control page through the earlier series of appeals felt like they had already given a gift in response to that ask. For this reason, they decided not to give another gift to the page, but for new visitors–the treatment performed slightly better (whereas the control message had no difference).
For that reason, we decided to roll out the treatment copy as the new control, as it showed no negatives for any audience type, which will remove the risks associated with returning visitors feeling like they have already given to “this initiative,” and presenting them with a new ask instead.

experiments.title: How inserting a gift array on a primary donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will inserting a gift array on a primary donation page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2018 - 12/04/2018
Compassion International’s main donation page featured an open field with a $100 suggested gift, and a drop down to choose whether to make the gift one-time or monthly. They were curious to see what happened when the one-time ask was replaced with a range of giving options ($50 / $100 / 250) and when the monthly recurring gift ask was brought out of the dropdown and into the decision flow as a “binary checkbox”. They created a treatment that did both of these and launched and experiment to find a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The gift array, after several months, produced an 18.5% decrease in giving, although some of this may have been due to the placement of the array—instead of being in the same position, it was moved down below the main image. However, this coincided with a 385% lift in recurring donations. Further analysis must be done to determine if the losses from one-time giving are offset by the increases to monthly giving.

experiments.title: How removing the email template impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a plain text email generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/03/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with sending a plain text style email against their standard marketing email template to see if it generated more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In reviewing the two creatives that were involved in this experiment, we initially thought that the marketing email generated more clicks, but after further slicing the web analytics details, we identified that the marketing template had an additional clickable link to an article (which was hyperlinked on the header image) that the plain text version did not include.
Overall clicks were higher on the marketing template, but when you isolate the click traffic on the PRIMARY objective (which is calling the recipient to give a gift), we actually saw that the treatment generated 1,025 clicks against the control’s 526 clicks on the “Give Now” option (which represents a 94% increase in click activity).
Subsequently, the clickthrough traffic on that primary call to action (Give Now) saw a higher conversion rate (16.6%), as well as a 53.1% higher overall revenue than the control.
Further experimentation is required to validate this hypothesis, but initial results look promising.

experiments.title: How more personal phrasing in a reply email impacts opens

rows.name: Can You Please Answer One Question For Me?
Can You Please Let Me Know If You Want to Come?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct subject line increase opens and responses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/06/2019
For an upcoming full-day certification workshop, we sent an email asking people to reply with a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ on whether or not they are interested in attending as a way to a) get commitment but also b) get a sense of numbers to plan the event properly. We wanted to see if being more direct in the subject line by telling them they will be asked to answer a question or being a bit more personal and casual subject line that isn’t as direct about a question would increase opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the more personal, casual approach and not letting them know about the question seems to be a better approach but more testing is needed.

experiments.title: How more personal phrasing in a reply email impacts opens

rows.name: Can You Please Answer One Question For Me?
Can You Please Let Me Know If You Want to Come?

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct subject line increase opens and responses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/06/2019
For an upcoming full-day certification workshop, we sent an email asking people to reply with a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ on whether or not they are interested in attending as a way to a) get commitment but also b) get a sense of numbers to plan the event properly. We wanted to see if being more direct in the subject line by telling them they will be asked to answer a question or being a bit more personal and casual subject line that isn’t as direct about a question would increase opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically valid, the more personal, casual approach and not letting them know about the question seems to be a better approach but more testing is needed.

experiments.title: How an animated video ad image increased ad clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using an animated video ad from Facebook increase ad clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2019 - 10/14/2019
As a part of optimizing The Fund for American Studies’ eBook offer, we wanted to increase ad clickthrough rate to improve traffic through the funnel. To experiment with boosting ad clickthrough, we decided to
experiments.keyLearnings:
Directionally, we observed an increase of ad clickthrough rate by +7.7%, however we did not validate the experiment (achieving only an 81% level of confidence).
We turned off this experiment because after running for two weeks, we achieved the (directional) lift in ad clickthrough rate and traffic, however the email registration rate decreased (-28.5%, LoC: 100%), as did the donor conversion rate (-41.6%, LoC: 65.7%).
Our hypothesis is that although the video ad produced more traffic, with the decrease in email signups and donations, it was likely erroneous click activity after watching (or attempting to rewatch) the ad.
This approach has worked well for some offers and causes, and we should consider retesting this tactic at some point down the road.

experiments.title: How moving Lourdes water to a one page donation increased donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to learn if a one step checkout process would help increase donations for Lourdes Water.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2019 - 10/27/2019
We get a lot of traffic to make a donation for Lourdes Water. It’s our most popular item, so we wanted to try to find a way people could make a donation easier.
experiments.keyLearnings: It increased donations and conversion rate, but the revenue stayed about the same. For our longer checkout process we have an area that asks them to make an additional donation. We found that this is why there was less donations, but similar amount in revenue. We are going to move forward using the one step process for the Lourdes Water, but continue to test different ways to increase the average gifts.

experiments.title: How moving Lourdes water to a one page donation increased donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to learn if a one step checkout process would help increase donations for Lourdes Water.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2019 - 10/27/2019
We get a lot of traffic to make a donation for Lourdes Water. It’s our most popular item, so we wanted to try to find a way people could make a donation easier.
experiments.keyLearnings: It increased donations and conversion rate, but the revenue stayed about the same. For our longer checkout process we have an area that asks them to make an additional donation. We found that this is why there was less donations, but similar amount in revenue. We are going to move forward using the one step process for the Lourdes Water, but continue to test different ways to increase the average gifts.

experiments.title: The impact of using Facebook’s video creation tool on an ad campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a 15sec video created with the Facebook video creation tool convert more people into donors on the ad than a photo?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2019 - 08/11/2019
We’ve had a successful campaign advertising a pledge that led to a monthly donation page on Facebook. In a previous experiment, we found that using an image of a newborn baby on this offer significantly increased donor conversion (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2321)   We decided to test that image against a new video using the video creation tool in Facebook. They hypothesis was that a 15sec video would get more people to convert than the image.
experiments.keyLearnings: 218% more people who clicked the video ad became monthly donors than who clicked the photo ad. This was despite the fact that the photo ad had  14% more impressions. We will begin using and testing videos in more of our campaigns to see the impact they have on conversion.

experiments.title: How presenting segmented offers based on a person’s engagement on parenting articles impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will offering people targeted premium offers increase donor conversion on parenting articles?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2019 - 03/26/2019
Focus on the Family has done significant testing within the articles on their website. From testing the kind of offer to the placement of the offers, they have optimized and continue to optimize the conversion opportunities on the articles pages for the significant volume of traffic. Most recently they learned that presenting people a premium/book at the end of the articles, that readers could receive by giving a gift of any amount, had the most significant impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this learning, they hypothesized that presenting people with a targeted offer based on their engagement on the Focus on the Family website might increase donor conversion. Focus identified the top premium offers based on what is most requested and the kind of content that people read and consume from their site and offered premiums that closely aligned with those topics. This approach was tested against offering people one single premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As we’ve continued to monitor and dig into the results of this experiment, we have discovered an interesting finding that is misrepresenting the results and learnings. A significant portion of the traffic on the article pages are either first time visitors to the Focus website, or this is the first article they’ve consumed from Focus. This audience has not been receiving targeted content offers based on cookies. Because of this, 68% of the donations from the treatment results have been receiving the single content offer/premium.
After discovering this and learning what was happening, we have paused the current experiment and relaunching it to target that audience with targeted content offers based on the current article they are reading. This small nuance to the experiment is critical in knowing whether a targeted content offer based on the kind of content people consume helps increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a membership offer on an instant donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a membership donation ask outperform the generic donation traditionally used?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2019 - 02/10/2019
As part of the efforts to validate the potential for a membership program within Alliance Defending Freedom, we identified three of the highest donor-motivated traffic portions of the website. One of these areas was the donation page shown after a visitors signs the “Statement of Belief” found on the blog and on the homepage. For this page, we then created a treatment that introduced the concept of membership and asked for a gift to support the “broad and far-reaching” work being done.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach a statistical level of significance with this experiment. Directionally, the membership concept seemed to produce a 36% decrease in donations directly to the campaign but this is something that needs to be further validated with additional testing.

experiments.title: How making the donor a hero impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “donor as the hero” treatment increase donor acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2019 - 06/30/2019
Buckner International was launching a new prospect acquisition offer that included an instant donation opportunity. The initial version was focused on the impact of the organization and what their gift will accomplish. However, we had run an experiment on a similar offer in the past and found that a donation page that focuses on the potential donor and builds them up to be a hero had stronger conversion. With this in mind, we decided to test this concept on the new donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we discovered that the “Donor as the Hero” treatment showed a directional lift of 66%. Due to high costs of acquisition, we ended up turning off the advertising before this page could validate statistically but the directional lift would align with the results we saw on the 7 Scriptures offer. It is a key messaging tactic to keep in mind for future appeals.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation page increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2019 - 08/15/2019
Based upon the low traffic volume to the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page, we knew that we would need to try something radical in order to validate an experiment. With this in mind, we decided to launch a radical redesign of the page in conjunction with a similar experiment on Buckner’s main donation page. Both experiments tested similar elements:
We modified the form headers to be numbers steps instead of just labels.
We removed the gift array which we’ve seen help with organizations that have a large volume of mid-level and major donors
We increased the perceived security near the credit card form
We reiterated the gift size and impact near the donation button
experiments.keyLearnings:
While it did not statistically validate and it had a small number of conversions, we did see a directional lift with the radical redesigned donation page. What is surprising is that the experiment run on the main donation page that mirrored these changes saw a completely opposite result (41% decrease.)
When we analyzed both experiments, what we found was that the decrease on the main donation page was the result of a decrease in donors less than $100. The SOS donation page did not have this same issue (we actually saw an increase on this page.) The key difference is in the final subheadline on both donation pages. The SOS donation page makes an ask for $25 and the main donation page did not suggest any gift amount.
It is our hypothesis that removing the gift array from the main page increased the decision friction for the small-dollar donors. The SOS donation page did not experience the same impact since it had the $25 in the final call to action.

experiments.title: The impact of making a monthly donor appeal in a homepage hero image (Hint: 1,392% increased revenue)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which would raise more money, a hero image making a one-time donation appeal, or one that would appeal for new monthly donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2019
Every two weeks, we would change out our homepage hero image featuring a donation CTA as well as our Top Ad donation CTA. We would swap these out to match the language of the most current bi-monthly digital appeal. These hero images were almost always for a one-time donation page.  We hypothesized that, instead of making a one-time donation appeal, we should try acquiring new monthly donors through the hero image. We did not set up a strict AB test, but rather replaced the hero and top ad images with a new monthly appeal and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Control had a higher donor conversion rate, but much fewer clicks. The variation attracted far more visitors to click and, since the appeal was for a monthly appeal, rather than a one-time appeal, resulted in significant more revenue. We ran the test for 72 days. Here are the results:
Control: 327 clicks, 13 donations, $981 raised.
Variation: 1057 clicks, 24 donations, $1,220 raised in monthly revenue, $14,640 in yearly revenue.
The variation had a 227% increase in clicks,  84% increase in donations, and a 1,392% increase in revenue! So, while fewer visitors to the donation page were prepared to become monthly donors, the significant increase in clicks and revenue more than made up for the lower conversion rate.

experiments.title: How leading with a question impact email click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does leading an email with a question increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 07/23/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom was sending out one of their weekly cultivation emails as part of a longitudinal experiment to improve email engagement. In the previous week, we had observed a surprising result in an experiment. When starting the email with a personal anecdote, we saw a decreased level of engagement. We had a hypothesis that this may be due to the way the two emails differed in their introductions. The control started with the question to the reader while the treatment started with the story.
Since there were several other factors that could have led to the observed result, we decided to isolate the question factor and run another A/B split. The only difference in the control and treatments email would then be whether they begin with a question or a statement.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach a statistical level of confidence, we were quickly approaching a validation with a 90% level of confidence. This would suggest that leading with a question is a better way to capture visitor attention that the standard introduction. However, we would need to test this further to validate the concept.

experiments.title: How a personalized sticky bar on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the sticky bar on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2019 - 06/08/2019
CaringBridge had run experiments to show that inserting a “sticky bar” at the top of donation pages could increase conversion rate during one of their matching gift appeal. However, they had recently updated their site code to be able to personalize the messages on the sticky bar to match the person’s donation page that the bar was running on. They wanted to test the impact of this during a match appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The personalized version of the sticky bar increased conversion by 10.1%, though it did not reach full statistical validity because it only ran for two days of the match period. This is a good directional learning, though it is muddied by another result—when this test was re-run later in the second match, it decreased conversion by 9.7%, also not statistically valid.
Ultimately, this might be too small of a change to validate a consistent result. However, the sticky bar has continued to generate increases in conversions across the board, especially on mobile.

experiments.title: How an additional design element affected registrations from a webinar email

rows.name: Plain Text with Webinar Image and Button

experiments.researchQuestion: Will using an additional design element at the bottom of the email lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2019 - 06/14/2019
The majority of our emails are hyper-personal and text only. But recently, we’ve seen digest-style emails perform better using some basic design elements including simple images and HTML buttons. We believe the reason for this is that our emails can be very heavy. And if you’re not reading every line, a design element can quickly show what the offer is and provide a clear call-to-action.
In this case, we wondered if a similar approach could help improve registration rates in our webinar invitation emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was just shy of reaching the desired 95% confidence level. The additional design element led to 20.7% more webinar registrations than the control. This seems to validate the hypothesis that these design element help give people a better understanding of the offer and call to action by adding visual clarity around the title, date, time, and content.
However, it is possible that the novelty of this design itself is leading to an increase. We will need to test again to make sure this increase is an ongoing trend and not a one-off result.

experiments.title: How simplifying the donation page increased the donor conversion rate on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Would simplifying the messaging on the donation page increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 06/25/2019
The Heritage Foundation was running an acquisition campaign to provide prospective donors with a free guide to the religious freedom debate. While scaling up spending to increase top line results, we needed to experiment with a way to increase the donor conversion rate.
The messaging on the control donation page seemed to take too long to get to the point. With an audience that is likely eager to get to their inbox to download their free guide, we felt that we needed to simplify the donation page and get right to the point more quickly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment only achieved an 86.6% level of confidence, the results were directional enough for us to agree to roll out the 31.8% increase in donor conversion rate that we had achieved over the last 14-days.
When it comes to an instant donation page behind an acquisition offer, it’s critically important to keep the donation page messaging as simple and straight forward as possible.
This will mitigate friction and increase the likelihood that an instant donation will be collected.

experiments.title: How including a small, tangible goal impacted overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a small, tangible goal help motivate donors to make a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2019 - 06/02/2019
Buckner International was running an appeal around providing scholarships to foster children in order to go to camp. In the past, we had observed increased results when we used small, tangible goals in our appeal but it had never been tested directly. We decided to run a head-to-head test for this campaign to understand the impact of using these small goals with the Buckner donor file.
For this campaign, we split the full house file in half and send three total emails to each segment. The control segment would not have a goal and the treatment segment would have a goal of sending 10 children to camp. We would then combine the giving across all three messages to validate the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 90% level of confidence, we observed a 120% increase in donor conversion by using a small, tangible goal. We generally shoot for a 95% level of confidence but, considering the difference in overall gifts (and revenue), we believe it is safe to say that the small goal had a positive impact on donor conversion.
In the past, when we have used large goals in the hundreds of thousands of dollars, they have not been very motivational without being tied to a match. Knowing that we can use a smaller goal to motivate donors will be helpful in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a more emotional message did not make a difference in acquiring new emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Would focusing on what you get from the book increase the email registration rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2019 - 05/28/2019
When launching the new Heritage Foundation eBook offer, we wanted to test a message that was more focused on communicating to the end-user, instead of focusing on the positioning of the contents of the eBook itself to see which treatment would generate more email addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we did not see a noticeable difference only achieving a -2.5% decrease in email registration rate, with an 86.2% level of confidence.
It would appear that at least with this offer, it will not matter if we position the contents of the eBook itself or what you will get from the content as a reader as it pertains to the email registration rate.

experiments.title: How email design affects click-through in a digest or newsletter email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will additional design elements in a digest email lead to greater click-through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2019 - 03/18/2019
Email appeal experiments had given us a predisposition to think that email with button, images, and minor design elements are ineffective. But we had really only discovered that to be true in the specific context of a donation appeal.
In this experiment, we wondered if basic design elements could actually lead to greater click-throughs. The control was a plain-text style email in the format we had tested into, and the treatment added section images and clickable html buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this didn’t quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%, it was very close to validating a 32% increase in clicks. With this type of a result, we’re inclined to test it again to get greater confidence in the learning. There’s the possibility of a novelty effect – it improved clicks simply because it is different, and not necessarily because it is better long term.

experiments.title: How the Freedom Quiz button text on the button promotion impacted petition click rates

rows.name: yes, i’ll sign the petition now
read the petition now - email capture last

experiments.researchQuestion: Which button text at the end of the quiz will generate the highest click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2019 - 04/05/2019
Americans for Prosperity launched a new quiz, which led to a petition signature page. At the end of the quiz, we wanted to test which button text would generate the most signatures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 88.5% level of confidence, the treatment text on the button increased petition button click rate by 77.9% over the control.
With our goal being to get as many people from the quiz to the petition (and subsequently the donation page), our learning here is that we should err on the side of “soft asks” on button text. The control message forces the quiz-taker to decide whether or not they want to sign the petition before they’ve ever seen it.
Because the email capture rate is so low across both segments, we wanted to end this experiment quickly to launch an email capture at the front of the quiz experience (instead of at the end of the quiz) to increase overall email capture rates.

experiments.title: How a Daily Signal core fundraising message impacted donor conversion rate for the Dear Reader ads
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a Daily Signal support call to action acquire more donors (or generate more revenue) than the existing message to activate a membership?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2019 - 03/31/2019
In an attempt to move away from the existing control message (focusing upon “partnering with President Trump” by “activating your Heritage membership”), we wanted to experiment with a more TDS-focused message about why The Daily Signal exists, and how we can only continue with the support of readers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 84.6 level of confidence, we substantiated a -29.5% decrease in donor conversion rate. We also saw a -14.8% decrease in revenue (70.2% level of confidence) alongside these results.
We did, however, see a +20% increase in “Dear Reader” ad clicks (with a 95% level of confidence).
We decided to stop this treatment due to the compounding impact of decreased revenue and donor conversion rates.
When digging into the data, we found some interesting pieces of information as it pertains to the type of visitor (new visitor vs. returning visitor).
Here are some observations for new visitors and comparative impact for the treatments within this experiment:
The control was 138% better at converting new visitors.
The control generated a 119% higher revenue/visitor value from new visitors.
The treatment was 36% more effective at converting returning visitors (topping a 31% donor conversion rate).
The treatment generated a 22% higher average gift from returning visitors.
The treatment generated a 67% higher revenue/visitor value from returning visitors.
It would appear that the control message is most effective at converting first time visitors, while the treatment message was most effective at converting returning visitors.
Our recommendation would be to look to roll both treatments out based upon the number of times a visitor has arrived at a page. Specifically, if the the reader has already converted through a “Dear Reader” ad in the past, it might be advisable to immediately swap the ads they see to a message more similar to the treatment.

experiments.title: How targeted digital advertising affected direct mail results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing ads to a targeted group of direct mail recipients result in increased revenue from the treatment group?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2017 - 11/25/2017
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation wanted to test whether showing Facebook video ads to house file direct mail recipients, before and during the response period caused a lift in any key metric: conversion, donation size or total revenue. This test was based on the successful test executed by Hillsdale College.
The Taxpayers Federation created a control audience that was excluded from seeing any video ads, and a treatment audience that would see a rotation of three video ads (but no donation-centric or petition ads) for a one week period before the mail piece hit mailboxes and for three weeks after, the prime time in which a donor would respond. They chose videos that had been popular in the past, but were still relevant. They optimized the ads for reach—aiming to show ads to as large a percentage of the target group as possible. The goal of this test was to lift direct mail revenue—not to add an additional segment of online revenue or capture data through petitions.
Ads were turned off once each video had an average frequency (number of times shown to each person reached) greater than 4.1. However, some videos were displayed as many as 4.5 times.
They spent just shy of $700 (CDN) to show ads to the treatment audience and waited to see the results come in.
experiments.keyLearnings: It appears that with a 25.4% increase in dollars per visitor, at a 92.6% confidence level, that this was a success and should be expanded and replicated in the future. Overall, a $690 investment in Facebook ads, resulted in a nearly $10,000 increase in overall revenue.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad image can affect conversion for a free online course offer [Housefile]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad image that is more closely aligned with the title of the course increase clarity and conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2017 - 10/23/2017
Prior to launching this experiment, we had leaned that it was more effective to use bullet points to clearly convey key objectives of the course to our house file audience.
We hypothesized that we could further increase clarity by visually reinforcing the title of the course “Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors.” So we took our original ad image and tweaked to show the Facebook logo pointing towards a dollar icon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment did not reach full validity, it strongly pointed towards the control as being more effective at converting course registrants. The test was turned off due to the significant increase in cost-per-registration that the treatment ad was creating.
It’s possible that the additions to the original image did not communicate the intended message as clearly as we had thought it might. It’s also possible that our original ad already garnered more attention in the newsfeed as a result of likes and shares from the time it had been running before this experiment.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Blackbaud]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting Blackbaud followers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach full validity as it was turned off due to increase cost-per-registration. The result was leaning towards the bulleted copy as the winner, which is the only result in this series of experiments that runs counter to our hypothesis that bullets keep a potential new subscriber from understanding the full weight of the value proposition.
Although, since this experiment did not reach full validity, I’m more inclined to trust the results of the valid tests in this series.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Web Traffic]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our website traffic, excluding the house file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach validity. It was pointing towards the control as being the winner, further validating the hypothesis that bulleted does not help a potential new subscriber to understand the full value proposition. Whereas, with a contact on our house file, they have a pre-established trust and are simply looking to see if this offer is relevant to them.
This experiment was ended before full validation due to the escalating cost-per-registration.

experiments.title: How a video ad affects the cost of traffic acquisition through Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a video ad drive more traffic to the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2017 - 03/07/2017
NextAfter runs a series of Facebook ads that promotes their research to a house file look-alike audience. The intent is to get people to self identify that they are interested in nonprofit content, before they would be targeted with an acquisition ad.
We thought that using a video advertisement could capture the attention of more people and generate more clicks. So we developed 3 ads.
The control was an illustration like what we have used in the past. Treatment 1 was used a still image of the experiment break-down video. And Treatment 2 was a 15 second video teasing the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The still image of the video started to approach validity with a 36.3% increase in clicks, but the video itself made virtually no difference in click rate what-so-ever.
The most significant metric in this test to us was the Cost-Per-Click (CPC). Regardless of what the click rate is, we want to get the most traffic for the lowest spend possible.
Oddly enough, treatment 1 had a 40% higher CPC than the control even though it had a higher click rate. Treatment 2, the video ad, had a 65% higher CPC than the control even though the click rate was essentially the same.
This test shows us that it is more cost effective for us to use the illustrated ads than it is to use this type of video to drive new traffic to our website.

experiments.title: How removing details in an email affects traffic to the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the date and time from the email drive more traffic to the registration page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2017 - 02/15/2017
When promoting a webinar, we typically try to include the date and time in the emails that we send in order to provide as much clarity as possible to our recipients. But we wondered if putting the date and time in the email was actually disqualifying some people right away because they already knew they would be busy. We thought these elements were creating unnecessary friction at this stage in the registration process.
Our hypothesis was that by removing the date and time from the email, we could drive more click-throughs to the registration page. And once someone was on the landing page, we displayed an exit-intent pop-up offering to send a recording even if they couldn’t make the time work with their schedule.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a lift of 15.3% in click-through rate. Unfortunately, our experiment did not quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%. It came just short at 92.4%.
After analyzing conversion rates, we saw that there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment when it came to how many people actually registered.

experiments.title: How reducing navigation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing donation page navigation increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2016 - 09/08/2016
Americans for Prosperity Foundation was preparing to launch a website redesign. In the few weeks they had before launch, wanted to run a quick donation page experiment to see if they could lift conversion.
The current donation page had a large navigation menu at the top that gave the visitor plenty of opportunities to leave the page before making a gift. They knew that removing these would prevent “unsupervised thinking” and they hypothesized that it would increase conversion.
They used Adobe Target to create a treatment with this navigation removed, and launched an A/B test to measure the winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with no navigation produced a 195.1% increase in conversion, although the results came just short of statistical validity. They ended the test before arriving at validity to launch the new site, although the test will be repeated with the new design.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects the conversion rate on an email acquisition page

rows.name: Learn to Answer Your Donor's Number One Question

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition that focuses on the donor affect the conversion rate of the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2016 - 09/06/2016
With the release of the Why Should I Give to You? study of nonprofit value proposition, we decided we needed to drink our own medicine and put the value proposition of the study to the test.
The control version of the landing page to download the study focused on communicating to the visitor that this research study will help them craft an effective value proposition for their organization.
We hypothesized that the control assumed that visitors to the landing already knew the value of crafting an effective value proposition. So a treatment was created that focused on a more universal goal for the type of traffic visiting the page: “Answer your donor’s number one question: Why should I give to you?”
We split the traffic 50/50, and put them to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a decrease in conversions by 10.5%.
This shows us the the type of traffic being driven to this landing page is more motivated to download the eBook when the copy focused on helping them construct an effective value proposition.
Follow up tests from this will focus on clarifying and reinforcing the value proposition of the control.

experiments.title: How the use of video in a Facebook Ad affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook Ad variation will get more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2016 - 08/12/2016
At NextAfter, we’re trying to distribute our research blog to the wider nonprofit audience in an effort to educate the marketplace about the effect that optimization can have on fundraising.
As part of this effort, we were running a series of Facebook Ads to our housefile lookalike audience. The initial click rates were lower than desired, so we wanted to test a new treatment.
Our hypothesis was that using a video advertisement could generate a higher click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video Facebook Ad received a 71.5% lift in clicks. The reasons for this increase could be:
The movement and action of the video was more effective at catching the eye of Facebook users scrolling through their newsfeed, causing them to pay more attention to the content of the ad. Or…
The video demonstrated and reinforced the value proposition of the blog in a way that helped increase clarity and appeal.
We will continue to test video in Facebook Ads to try and determine exactly what affect it has on Facebook Ad audiences.

experiments.title: How call to action and value proposition affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the headline and intro copy strengthen the value proposition and increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2016 - 06/16/2016
We were hosting a live broadcast to unpack the first article of the Fundraiser’s Creed: People Give to People. We had set up a landing page to allow people to register for the broadcast. Upon peer review, we thought that a few small tweaks could increase the force of the value proposition on the page.
The first hypothesis had to do with the text above the headline. On the control, this copy said “Upcoming Live Internet Broadcast”. This was correct, but it gave the user no motivation or orientation in regards to what we wanted them to do. For the treatment, we changed this copy to “Register now for the live broadcast”.
The second hypothesis centered around the first paragraph. It was intended to address the reader and frame the problem, but we hypothesized that it might bury the actual value proposition for the broadcast, without adding much value. In the treatment, this paragraph was removed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 24% lift in email acquisition. This shows the importance that each piece of copy has — copy can either orient and direct the visitor, or distract them and weaken the value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding a brand name in the sender line affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a brand name to the sender line increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2016 - 03/07/2016
NextAfter sent an email promoting their new eBook “Turning Likes Into Donors” to a rented email list of nonprofit development professionals. The email was sent from Tim Kachuriak, and meant to look like a personal email. However, we wondered — if one of the recipients of the email wasn’t familiar with Tim, would they be likely to open and click the email? We developed a test that added “NextAfter” to the sender line to give the recipient better clarity in regards to the sender and launched an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added brand name reduced clickthrough rate by 20.7%. This shows that the presence of a brand name actually decreases the likelihood that the user will engage. This may be because the brand is as unknown as the name. This experiment also may suggest that people are more willing to engage with a name they don’t know, but not if it’s evident that they are being marketed to.

experiments.title: How the reasons NOT to give affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: By giving reasons NOT to support DTS on Giving Tuesday, will we be able to drive incremental traffic to the donation page and which will lead to an increased number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2014 - 12/04/2014
Giving Tuesday has become a powerful fundraising response to the consumerism of Black Friday and Cyber Monday. But with so many nonprofits are soliciting gifts on the same day, it becomes more difficult to stand out in the crowd. DTS tested a contrarian messaging approach that would both intrigue their supporters and increase traffic to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test treatment was able to increase the click through rate by 528.3% but was not able to convert enough of those visitors to donors to be a statistically valid experiment.  With that said, the results would certainly indicate that this tactic may work in the future as long as we then focus our efforts on optimizing the landing page for the varying motivations of the visitors.

This same tactic of driving an increased number of visitors to the page will be employed in the future but more effort needs to be placed on developing a page that fully addresses the reason for the visit. Our hypothesis is that we did not spend enough time explaining the reasons NOT to give before leading into reasons TO give.

experiments.title: How active language in the call to action increases donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does active language on a donate button increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2017 - 06/14/2017
Live Action wanted to increase donations on their main donation page. They tested the copy used on the “Donate” button in the navigation of liveaction.org. After finding that the copy “Defend the Preborn” had a 60% increase in clicks over the original “Donate” copy and the other treatment, “Support Live Action”, they tested which copy converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: The control was “Defend the Preborn” and treatment #1 was “Support Live Action”. “Support Live Action” had a 46.5% increase in donations over “Defend the Preborn.” So while “Defend the Preborn” was able to get more people to the donation page initially, more people gave a donation using the text “Support Live Action”. What this tells us is that even though people were more motivated to click on “Defend the Preborn”, the lack of clarity in that copy to where they would be taken resulted in less people converting to what the primary objective was on the page – to give a gift. By using more active language with greater clarity, we were able to increase traffic to the donation page and increase donations and revenue.

experiments.title: How increasing the copy on the page affects acquisition rate

rows.name: Highlights the Content of the eBook

experiments.researchQuestion: Adding an in-depth explanation the contents of the eBook will allow the offer to appeal to more visitors and will, in turn, increase the conversion rate of the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2014 - 11/20/2014
Texas State Historical Association was offering one of their more highly valued publications, the Texas Almanac, as a free digital download. All visitors had to do to was enter their email address to receive the eBook.  With the level of traffic their website receives on a daily basis, we were acquiring large volumes of visitors to the page.
Previous tests had proven that a landing page with less copy did not convert as well as the current control.  We theorized that if we could add additional content to the page to further sell the contents and the value of the eBook, we could increase the number of sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We knew that adding the additional content to the page would increase the friction of the page by forcing visitors to scroll further down to find the form.  Our hypothesis was that the additional content would increase the value in the visitors mind which would overcome the additional friction.  This proved to be incorrect.
The additional content actually decreased conversion rate by 6.3%.  While this is not a large drop, it did prove that we needed to find a different method for communicating the value of the Almanac in order to increase the conversion rate of the page.

experiments.title: How a single word can affect click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the word “Free” affect clickthrough?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2013 - 10/28/2013
The TSHA created a new online resource center as a way to acquire email addresses. In order to attract visitors to the page, they placed banners throughout their site. The goal of this test was to identify which message produced the most traffic to the resource center landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a 29% lift in the number of clicks, it appears that the word “free” is indeed a powerful word. The cost in the mind of the visitor that is associated with clicking on the banner is diminished when the perceived value outweighs the cost. By communicating in the ad that access to the resource center is free we were able to reduce the cost burden and thereby increase the number of visitors to the resource center landing page.  While one test is not enough to speak definitively about the subject, the concept of “free” on influencing visitor behavior should not be underestimated.

experiments.title: How adding visual clarity affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which image produces the most emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2015 - 08/13/2015
Previous experiments had proven that one of the best methods of email acquisition for the Texas State Historical Associate was through free content offers. Utilizing their vast library of Texas history information, they started creating eBooks by bundling popular content. Their first eBook, The Battle of the Alamo was getting a great email acquisition rate but we wanted to see if there was still room to improve.
One of the recurring themes in emails to the organization was that they were confused about the offer they were receiving. They believed they were getting a hardbound version of the book instead of the digital format. We hypothesized that by changing out the visual from an actual book to the book on an iPad, we would be able to increase the clarity of the offer. This would, in turn, lead to increased conversion and decreased complaints.
experiments.keyLearnings: The iPad image of the book statistically increased the number of emails acquired by 3.5%. While not a large increase, this bump in conversion showed that the clarity of the offer does impact the results. In this case, however, the image itself was not as critical to the conversion process as other elements of the page which is why the increase was so minimal.

experiments.title: How the right premium affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What will be the impact of offering the Texas Almanac as a premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2015 - 10/26/2015
At the Texas State Historical Association, our previous tests had proven how popular the Texas Almanac was for their audience. In many campaigns, we had started using it as a premium for members who joined at the $50 or more level.
While looking through their website for optimization opportunities, we found that the primary donation page had not been modified for a while. We decided to use this page to create a test that would allow us to isolate the impact the Almanac has on overall donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By offering the Texas Almanac as the primary incentive on the page, we were able to increase the member acquisition by 135%.
Every premium has an associated cost that needs to be accounted for when determining its overall effectiveness.  In this situation, a 135% increase to the number of members acquired with an 11% increase to average gift is more than worth the cost associated with sending out the Almanac to each new member.

experiments.title: How promoting impact over facts affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will explaining why the offer is important (impact) versus describing what the offer is (facts) increase the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2015 - 11/19/2015
The Texas State Historical Association was promoting their latest email acquisition offer which was an eBook featuring an overview of the history of Texas music. The typical approach to promoting these eBooks had been to highlight the details of the offer including the type of content, the media used, and other selling features of the offer.
We had a hypothesis that if we focused less on the facts of the eBook and more on the reason why the content was so important, we could improve the overall email acquisition rate.  We created a new treatment to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: The landing page that focused less on the facts of the book and more on why the book content was so important was able to lift the email acquisition rate by 8.6%. This reiteratives that the “what” behind the offer is less important than the “why.”

experiments.title: How removing an ad’s premium offer affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: What banner will motivate website visitors to become a member during a calendar year end campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2015 - 01/04/2016
The Texas State Historical Association, like many other nonprofits, utilized a banner across their site during their year end campaign to promote an opportunity to give. Historically, all of the banners on TSHA’s sites had been utilized to promote premiums such as eBooks so it made logical sense to promote the premium associated with becoming a member (the Texas Almanac).
However, we realized this premium may not appeal to everyone so we decided to create a treatment that would test out a purely philanthropic ask on the banners.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We discovered that the Almanac premium proved to be a significant motivator for donors.  There was no statistically significant difference in the amount of traffic driven by the ad but those that were driven by the premium offer were 52.6% more likely to become a member.
Another interesting aspect of this experiment revolves around the average gift amount of the two audiences. While not statistically significant, the average gift of the philanthropically driven donors was over double that of the premium driven donors. It is worth revisiting this experiment in future campaigns to see if this trend holds.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of membership levels affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which gift array will result in the greatest number of new members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2015 - 01/04/2016
During their calendar year end campaign, the Texas State Historical Association created a landing page dedicated to acquiring new members. Their membership program consists of 6 different levels ranging from $25 up to $1,000.  After analyzing previous year end campaigns, we realized that the number of new members that select gifts above $250 are few and far between.
Since few people select them, we had the hypothesis that these upper membership levels may actually be decreasing conversion due to the amount of mental friction that they cause.  We decided to test a form that only had four membership levels to see if we could improve the overall conversion of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment revealed that the donation form with four levels of membership actually decreased conversion by 17.1%. Our hypothesis is that these additional membership levels actually provided clarity around the benefits of each level which were mentioned in the text above the form. Knowing which levels offered which benefits allowed the new members to feel safer in their selection of membership.

experiments.title: How increasing the explanation of benefits on a membership form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does clarifying the member benefits and adding the option to defer tangible benefits impact the conversion rate of a donation form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2016 - 03/16/2016
The Texas State Historical Association was in the process of updating their membership levels to reflect new benefits. We also wanted to add an option to opt out of receiving the tangible benefits of membership for those members that did not want them. This was something that could help save costs but did add additional decision friction to the process.
Before this was fully rolled out, we wanted to get an understanding of how this would impact donor conversion. We decided to test the new language and form on a donor acquisition campaign to gauge its impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: While it did not reach statistical validity, this test indicated that adding more description of the benefits near the benefit selection increased conversion rate. We would need to validate these learnings with a larger sample size but it does seem to indicate that this additional language added perceived value to the donors’ minds.

experiments.title: How adding credibility and clarity to the landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page produces the the greatest conversion rate to customers for their conferences?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2014 - 03/27/2014
The Acquire The Fire site is a WordPress multi-page installation that gave the user plenty of options: get resources, find out more about the ministry, see the latest list of tour dates, and access to the store (which sold curriculum, merchandise, and other non-ticket items). Sometimes, access to all of multiple pages is a good thing — it encourages users to spend more time on the site and learn more about the ministry. However, we hypothesized that this could lead to what we call “unsupervised thinking” — by allowing users to wander all over the site, we give them more opportunities to get distracted from the ultimate goal. We needed to be able to guide them down the “path of righteousness” that leads to conversion.
We wanted to create an experiment that helps determine what motivates their customers in order to increase their overall conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After just under a month of testing, this landing page delivered a 35% lift in visitor conversion! Additionally, the name acquisition offers grew Teen Mania’s email list size by 13%. This page was a resounding success, and taught us several things about Teen Mania customers:

1. Credibility matters. There are a lot of youth evangelism events out there, but few of them have the endorsements, coverage, and credibility that Acquire The Fire has. This needs to be up front and repeated throughout the value proposition.

2. Buying tickets is a big decision. Our customers are generally buying tickets for a large group and want to make a good decision with their budget dollars. The more that we can show them what they will experience and reinforce that with quotes and testimonials from people who have experienced an Acquire The Fire event, the more we can reduce anxiety about the event itself.

3. Not all visitors are ready to buy. We know anecdotally that it can take time for youth pastors to get budget approval, parental approval, or youth signups. Because of these delays, not everyone who comes to this site is able to buy tickets. We need ways to engage these visitors and give them something of value in exchange for their email address (and the opportunity to market to them later).

experiments.title: The importance of reinforcing the value proposition for sake of giving, even if we lose more emails.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which petition landing page would acquire more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2016 - 04/20/2016
As part of their moneybomb, SBA List created a petition to help drive more traffic to their donation page. Traditionally, the petition landing page has value proposition copy and the CTA to sign it. They wanted to test a treatment of the petition landing page that didn’t have the additional value proposition language and looked more like a letter to the people they created the petition for. Traffic for both of the petition landing pages was pushed through an email that had value proposition copy and a call to “sign the petition”. They A/B split test the petition landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment ended up getting twice as many emails to it than the control. But once people got to the donation page, they weren’t motivated enough to make a donation. We want more donations, not more emails for this campaign.  Since this test is approaching validity, we can learn from this that the presence of the value proposition  on the control motivated people to not only sign the petition but to give a donation as well and increased donor conversion by 25.1%. We need to reinforce value proposition for the sake of giving, even if we lose more emails.
