experiments.title: How the email acquisition value proposition impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2016 - 08/12/2016
For Susan B. Anthony List, we wanted to see if the existing value proposition around the home page email acquisition form was the most appealing to its audience. From previous experiments with the email file, we had found that the more casual tone was more appealing to their audience and they also like specificity. They wanted more details about the offer before they would take action. We decided to craft a value proposition statement that emphasize these two traits and test it against the existing value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for over two months, we did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence with the test. The change to the design increased the number of factors that we were testing so it is difficult to say that it is the result of the value proposition alone. It is worth noting that with a control conversion rate of 2.4%, this is an already high email acquisition rate for a homepage audience. It may just be that the motivation of the audience was high enough that the value proposition did not have much impact.
In future testing, with a conversion rate this high, it may be worth focusing the tests on lower converting sections of the website so that we can observe a more significant impact.

experiments.title: How the designation of the donation impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition (get more petition signatures vs. work with legislators) will have the greatest impact on donor converison?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2016 - 03/22/2016
Susan B. Anthony List was running a petition against the nomination of a Supreme Court justice. The goal of this petition for the organization was to find email subscribers that aligned around their mission of pro-life legislation. After they signed the petition, new subscribers were shown an instant donation offer. We wanted to test whether people would be more motivated to give to get more signatures for the petition or to advance the core mission of the organization which was to work with legislators on the issue.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were not able to reach statistical validity on the test before the offer was shut off. Spreading the petition seemed to be the more attractive value proposition which is backed up by other experiments but at 85% LoC, we need further testing to definitively be able to state that.

experiments.title: How copy on a donation page affects the force of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: By providing a clear and credible reason to give, we can increase both the average gift and conversion rate of the main donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2014 - 04/30/2014
John Cornyn has served as a U.S. Senator from Texas for more than 14 years and is the current Senate Minority Whip for the 113th Congress. As election season begins, a torrent of traffic comes to JohnCornyn.com. This traffic includes likely voters looking to confirm their affiliation, donors looking to support the Senator, and people seeking information about all of the candidates. The Senator Cornyn’s team wanted to make sure that friction and anxiety were minimized on their main donation page and that credibility and clarity were increased so that the most motivated potential donors would make a gift to support the Cornyn campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment delivered a 258.1% increase in donor conversion, with a statistically valid sample size. Team Cornyn learned a few things about their donor base through this test:
1. Experience matters. Even though the donor might be motivated to get to the donation page, they need to be reminded throughout the process that Senator Cornyn aligns with their core beliefs and is worthy of their donation. Donors to Senator Cornyn are thoughtful — they can’t be expected to just react to a donation opportunity, they must be persuaded along the way.
2. Design matters. Simply adding the copy to the page hurt results. Keeping continuity in the user experience throughout the entire donation process is crucial to a positive lift.

experiments.title: How reducing form functionality affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will lead to the highest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2015 - 08/05/2015
Jews for Jesus had been running a series of optimization experiments on their primary donation form in an effort to improve the overall revenue generated by the page. Our first attempt resulted in no significant improvement when we removed elements of friction from the form and added language to the top of the page highlighting the organization’s unique value proposition.
We decided to attempt another treatment that would modify the value proposition of the page while leaving in place the changes to the form. With this new treatment, we wanted to utilize a personal letter from David Brickner, the organization’s CEO. Analysis of the email program had shown that he was popular with the constituency so we hypothesized the a shorter appeal, signed by him, could lift the average gift on the page enough to impact revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we saw that the new treatment decreased the conversion rate on the page by 13.8%. The language that was used have already proven successful in a previous appeal so we analyzed what else could have been impacting conversion. Upon further reflection, we discovered that, in an effort to reduce the number of decisions made on the form, we may have inadvertently make it more difficult to complete.
One of the features of Jews for Jesus’s donation system is the ability for previous donors to log in to complete their gift without entering any additional information. We removed this feature to make room for quotes from credible sources and to remove the number of decisions a donor has to make. However, with this being the primary location existing donors come to make their gift, this caused additional friction for that key segment. This cause the number of gifts made to the form to decrease.
Future experiments will add this functionality back to the form and we will focus our efforts on purely refining the value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a donation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of value proposition copy on the donation page increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2016 - 05/05/2016
Jews for Jesus sends out a monthly newsletter via email. The newsletter has articles and videos people click-through to read and watch, and they recently added an appeal to the newsletter. In efforts to improve donor conversion, they developed a treatment of the donation page for the newsletter appeal. The donation page that was the control didn’t have any value proposition language on it. For the treatment they added value proposition copy to the page to reinforce the message in the newsletter appeal and used large buttons for the gift array instead of radial buttons. They split traffic and tested the donation pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the addition of value proposition copy on the donation page saw a decrease in donations by 38%. While value proposition usually helps improve conversion, these results suggest that the presence of the value proposition copy may have actually created friction for this highly motivated audience. This audience was already motivated to give once they arrived to the donation page and didn’t need or want additional reasons why they should make a donation.

experiments.title: How two different value propositions for an eAppeal affected donor conversion.

rows.name: It's a challenge to be a Christ-follower

experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2016 - 05/11/2016
Each month, Jews for Jesus sends out an eAppeal. The messaging for these eAppeals comes directly from their mail appeal for the month. When they converted the direct mail letter into an eAppeal this month, there seemed to be two different value propositions in the letter. One value proposition focused on how it is becoming more challenging to be a Christ-follower in America today, and the other focused on a specific outreach event happening in Israel. They decided to test these two different value propositions in the eAppeals and donation pages to see which their audience was most motivated to give to.
Then, they split-tested this with their email file, dividing it in half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this test didn’t validate, initial data tells us that people were more motivated to give to a tangible need and event than the general value proposition of the ministry. In fact, the treatment that was focused on the Israel outreach event saw a 47.3% increase in donations. This was our hypothesis, but because of the relevancy of the control, it was important to test our hypothesis.
We championed the treatment and sent it as the resend to non-opens with a new subject line.

experiments.title: How the landing page layout affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which layout will produce the greatest number of donations for the year end campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2012 - 01/01/2013
As part of their year end fundraising campaign, Media Research Center wanted to find the best way to communicate their unique value proposition that would lead to increased giving. The standard donation page layout had always been a two column approach in order to get the donation form closer to the top of the page. We wanted to see if that was the best approach to communicate the case for support to the potential donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the donation form below the form, we allowed the value proposition communicated within the copy to better “sell” the gift. The single column layout was effectively making an ask before adequately explaining why the gift was needed.  The test did not have a high enough level of confidence to be proven valid so further testing would need to be done to fully validate these results.

experiments.title: How the popularity of 3rd party credibility can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which visual layout or 3rd party sources will produce the greatest conversion rate for the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 07/31/2015
For their primary donation page, the Media Research Center wanted to find what 3rd party sources lend the most credibility to their value proposition. Many conservative figures cite their research in their work but the different individuals have varying levels of popularity.
We created a test of the existing donation page and replaced a one public figure (Laura Ingraham) with another (Rush Limbaugh). The ordering was also changed up to bring the animated element of the page closer to the top. We wanted to see if this new arrangement would improve the conversion of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: By placing a more well-known figure, and his endorsement, closer to the donation form, we were able to reduce the anxiety of the donor enough to improve the conversion rate by 25%.

experiments.title: How the tone of the email can affect the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging tone in the email will produce the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2015 - 08/24/2015
When the Planned Parenthood videos were first starting to be released, the Media Research Center launched a petition with the goal of getting the major media networks to give the story the level of attention it deserved. By the time the 7th video was released, this petition had acquired more than 53,000 signatures. With each subsequent video, we wanted to update these signers on the progress of the petition and give them a way to contribute further with a “Quick Donate” option.
Previous emails had all used a “negative” tone by focusing on the continued lack of media coverage and what was needed to overcome the problem. We had a hypothesis that if we led with a positive tone by acknowledging the signers’ past action and the resulting progress, we could inspire them to take the next step and give a gift.  We decided to split the email file and validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The more negative email was able to produce 11% more opens but both emails had the same number of people click to the landing page to give a gift. However, those clicking from the positive email were not as incentivized to give a gift which led to a 46% decrease in donor conversion. Those visitors that had been inspired by the work left to do (the “negative” tone) and the urgency of the appeal were more highly motivated to give a gift once they made it to the page.
It is critical to remember, in an email appeal, the tone of the entire campaign can be impacted by every step of the process.

experiments.title: How the value communicated in a link can affect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does communicating the value of the gift in the link increase the subsequent donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2015 - 08/31/2015
Media Research Center was running a petition campaign to force the major news networks to spend more time covering the Planned Parenthood undercover videos. We had learned in previous tests that the signers of the petition were highly motivated individuals that did not need a lot of value proposition copy to be convinced to make a gift. As a result, we utilized a “quick donate” strategy in subsequent emails that let the donors select their donation amount in the email and skip directly to the donation page.
While this strategy was proving successful, we wanted to find a way to boost the average gift of the donors since the average gift was below what is normally seen. We had a hypothesis that adding value to the individuals links would inspire larger gifts. As a result, we created a new treatment that applied value proposition copy to the links as a method of communicating greater value to the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this test did not reach the 95% level of confidence necessary to be considered statistically significant, it was quickly approaching a valid result. While additional testing will be necessary to be certain, the increase in donations and revenue would indicate that the value proposition language boosted the overall campaign results.

experiments.title: Ask in Email vs No Ask in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a direct ask in the email result in a lower number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2016 - 02/04/2016
One of the best practices that we had been following was that we should NOT ask for money directly in the e appeals. We wanted to test that best practice with our constituents.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned the opposite to be true.  In fact, we saw a significant lift by asking directly in the email.  We will continue to test this assumption in the next few appeals.

experiments.title: New Donation Form vs Old Donation Form
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shorter donation form give us more donors than our standard long form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2016 - 03/28/2016
We are looking for ways to increase value force, while decreasing cost force on our donation forms. We did this by creating a much simpler form for users based on donation forms we have been seeing. We hoped it would decrease friction and anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this case, we learned that donors were less likely to donate. We will continue to analyze the cost force and see if we can decrease the cost as well as increase the value force to drive donations.
It is possible that we decreased anxiety, but did not do a good enough job increasing value force.
Also, originally this test reached confidence, but after a few more days, there was not a clear confidence for the original form.

experiments.title: Main WEB Donation Form (Short vs Long)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shortened form increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2016 - 03/31/2016
We are looking for ways to optimize our web donation form. This particular form is the source of over 30% of online revenue so it has high impact on donations online.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this case, the shortened form did not increase donations. We learned a few things from this:
Not enough value force
There was too much friction by having to click donate again
We are redesigning another donation form that will focus on adding more value force and decreasing friction.

experiments.title: Web Donation Form (form elements)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will compressing the form elements increase conversion?
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment tells us that our donors prefer form elements to be one per line.

experiments.title: How the way a name acquisition offer is presented impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which acquisition feature would acquire more names?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2017 - 02/19/2017
Live Action developed a petition for people to sign to Defund Planned Parenthood. With the recent political climate and conversation around this topic, they wanted to really push this petition and did so through multiple channels. One of those channels was their website. They had a pop-down feature at the top of their site that asked people if they wanted to sign the petition. They hypothesized that they might be able to get more signatures if they had a pop-up modal on their site. They developed a treatment with this modal and tested it against the pop-down feature.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The pop-up modal increased petition signers by 11%. While the results did not reach the 95% validity that we would like to reach, it did reach 91% which is enough for us to draw insights and push out the treatment as the winning version.
The increase in petition signers and names with the pop-up modal can be attributed to it being more disruptive to the end-user’s experience on the page than the pop-down feature. This disruption puts the offer right in front of the person with no other elements buying for their attention.
Additional testing is recommended for this name acquisition opportunity to see if we can increase the conversion rate even more drastically.

experiments.title: How directly connecting the value proposition to the ask impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a stronger value proposition increase clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2017 - 05/23/2017
As Mother’s Day was approaching, Live Action sent a fundraising email asking people to give a gift in honor of a mom in their life. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase clicks from the email to the donation page by better connecting the Mother’s Day holiday to the value proposition of Live Action. We developed a treatment with this strengthened value proposition and tested it against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t validate, but the data we did collect shows that by strengthening the value proposition of the email, we were able to increase clicks by 22%. Here is a great takeaway from this experiment: When we present an ask, more people are willing to click through (and hopefully give) when we can connect the ask to a strong reason to give. If we don’t do this, the ask sounds and feels transactional. People give not just to give, but to have an even greater impact. A strong value proposition can clearly communicate this and result in more people being motivated to give.

experiments.title: How a direct donation ask vs a premium offer affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a direct donation ask effect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/23/2016 - 03/09/2016
Online for Life created a petition called the End Abortion Declaration. People were pushed to the petition through Facebook advertising and banner ads. After someone would sign the petition, they were presented with a thank you and donation page. In efforts to increase donations, they set up a test. The control was presenting people with the opportunity to get a “Save the Baby Humans” in exchange for a gift to Online for Life. While this had worked well for previous offers, they wanted to test how it would perform against a direct donation ask with value proposition language that supported the End Abortion Declaration. They did an A/B split test to see which donation experience would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The sticker offer had a much higher conversion rate and resulted in the direct donation ask having a decrease in conversion rate by 57.4%.
This tells us that even though the direct donation landing page reinforced the value proposition of the declaration they just signed, they weren’t motivated enough to give to the cause. But when presented with something they could get – the sticker – they converted instantly and gave a donation. The perceived value of receiving the sticker also caused people to give at a much higher rate as represented by the average gift.
The sticker offer was deployed for the remainder of the campaign.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock affects the value proposition and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which countdown clock would increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2015 - 01/20/2016
As part of their year-end campaign, Online for Life wanted to find a way to boost the conversion rate on their donation page.
In a previous test, we had learned that the addition of the “Every 26 seconds” clock on the donation page had increased donor conversion rate. Now, we wanted to test it against a treatment with a timer that counted down to the end of the year.
Our hypothesis was that people would be more motivated to give based on the urgency of the year-end. We ran a split A/B test. The control donation page had the”Every 26 seconds” clock and the treatment was the donation page with the year-end countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The year-end countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask and it also reinforced the value proposition of the donation page. This lead to the increase in conversion rate by 41.6%, but a decrease in average gift by 4.7%. However, overall, the addition of the year-end clock with messaging that supported it resulted in a 35% increase in revenue.
In the future, the countdown clock with financially focused messaging will be deployed towards the end of large year-end campaigns to increase revenue in the final days of giving.

experiments.title: How the layout of a donation form affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Regular buttons will increase the conversion rate given their increased ease of use both for reading and selecting.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2014 - 09/23/2014
The out-of-the-box defaults for Online for Life’s donation system results in giving arrays that consist of radio buttons. Given the amount of text that accompanies each giving option on the form, these radio buttons tend to clutter the form and make it hard to operate on a mobile phone.  We wanted to test a new way of laying out the same information.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new buttons resulted in a 59.9% increase in conversion rate and were also able to increase the average gift by 56.5%. This proved how the new method for displaying the donation options made it easier for donors to understand.  As a result, the gift had more value to the donor resulting in a net increase of 150.2% revenue.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s non-member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for activating non-members for the first time?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind members to activate their membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, the non-member segment that received Facebook ads in the 3rd channel of communication saw an online response rate increase of 20.3%.
Other metrics impacted are as follows:
Average gift: +13.1% increaseTotal Revenue: +36.09% increase
During the campaign, the total cost to run these ads to non-members generated a 2.03 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the "with ads" segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an equal amount to the budget spent in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Where possible, adding a 3rd channel of communication to heed the campaign's call to action for non-members should be considered.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition on a landing page affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a revised value proposition on the landing page affect instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2019 - 03/31/2019
DTS was running a value proposition test on their Hebrews landing page, that focused on how the course would benefit the reader rather than focusing on the teacher of the course or the course content itself. We also adjusted the headline of the landing page. The end donation page and following workflow remained the same in both the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had little to no impact on landing page signups, but something surprising happened in this test. The instant donor conversion rate on the following page from people who signed up on the treatment landing page increased by 35% (not statistically valid, but really close). This could be directly related to the fact that we've just told them the value they're going to get out of this course and the impact it will have on their walk with Christ, rather than focusing our value proposition on course content or course teacher.
Moving forward, further testing is necessary on the value proposition of the landing page and how that flows into the donation page content. Continuing to reiterate the eternal impact of the course is key to higher donor conversion.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the donation page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/16/2019
Back to the Bible sent out an email to introduce the new Bible teacher for their radio broadcast. The email linked to a donation page that continued with the biographical information of the new Bible teacher and had a donation form at the bottom of the page. We hypothesized that if we were linking people to a donation page a stronger connection between the Bible teacher and the donation ask needed to be made in an effort to motivate people to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: Engagement with the email appeal was fairly healthy, but when it came to giving from this, the audience was pretty lethargic. With only a couple of donations coming in from this email appeal, the donation page treatment had no impact on conversion because overall motivation was so low. What we learned from this experiment is that it is a far stretch to go from introducing an audience to someone they never knew in a few short paragraphs to then asking them for a donation. This kind of email would have better been suited as a cultivation piece rather than an appeal.

experiments.title: How optimizing Facebook for purchases impacts donor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will Facebook’s standard “Purchase” event yield better donor acquisition for a petition campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2018 - 12/14/2018
TPPF had been running their Facebook petition campaign for several weeks with the goal of acquiring additional donors for the organization. After nearly a month of activity, email acquisition had been happening at an extremely low cost but we were seeing exceptionally low instant donor conversion. After a discussion with our Facebook rep, we decided to test out Facebook’s native “Purchase” event to optimize our ads. They recommend that we have at least 50 conversions to optimize off of but we wanted to see if it would still be impactful with our low donor acquisition numbers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 5 days, we saw the Purchase event optimization had yielded three donors compared to the Control’s zero donors. This was the result of a 1.0% instant donor conversion rate for petition signers. Knowing the historical donor conversion rate for this campaign (0.1% instant donor conversion rate) this was a clear winner.
In the ideal world, we would have run this experiment for well over a month in order to see at least one purchase come in for the Control to validate the results. However, when our primary metric is to acquire donors and we are seeing significant improvement with the new method of optimization, it did not make sense to continue wasting our advertising budget just to validate an experiment.
It is worth noting that while the instant donor conversion rate jumped to 1% for petition signers, we also saw the email acquisition rate drop by 62% and our cost per email went up 48%. Both of these were successful tradeoffs given that we were finally able to acquire donors.

experiments.title: How a long-form, donor-centered appeal decreased donor conversion rates on the primary donate page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a donor centered message help improve donor conversion rate (or revenue) on the primary donation form for Heritage?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2019 - 03/13/2019
We wanted to test a donor-centered message on the primary donation page, as every treatment historically has been focused on an organizationally centered series of value propositions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 94.7% level of confidence, we validated that the treatment decreased the donor conversion rate by -37.8%.
Although the donor conversion rate decreased in the treatment segment, because the average gift in the treatment jumped pretty substantially (+61.3%) compared to the control, the revenue difference between the segments was only -0.6% (with a 50.86% LoC).
Our hypothesis is that the copy length led to the decrease in donor conversion rate, but we were encouraged by the increase in average gift from the treatment, so we will now develop and launch a short-form version of a donor-centered message to experiment against the short-form control message.

experiments.title: OE Test – July 2017 – John 15:5 Appeal I
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a change in the outer envelope improve donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/15/2019
What Was Tested
We changed the outer envelope from a photograph of a church to a photograph of praying hands resting on a bible. The OE Teaser and all other kit components remained the same.

experiments.keyLearnings: The new OE outperformed the control in average gift and response rate. It is recommended to use the praying hands OE in the future for John 15:5.

experiments.title: RD Size Test – October 2014 – University Appeal II
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the reduced copy positively effect our total income?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2019
What Was Tested
We reduced the size of the copy from 2 pages down to 1/3 a page on a buckslip RD front & back.

experiments.title: OE/Message Test – July 2014 – KYFAH Appeal I
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a new OE and slightly different appeal outperform the control?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2019
What Was Tested
We changed the Outer Envelope from a photo of Obama, the ACLU, and the Bible with a red X through it which said: What’s behind the unprecedented attacks on your religious freedom … and what you can do about it!
To an OE with a photo of a door that says “Official Notice: Keep your faith at home – or else” with a teaser of: A message to Christians from the ACLU and the Obama Administration.
We also varied the text in the appeal slightly to focus more on the ACLU Triple Threat.
OE*

experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a significant decrease in ROI due to a decrease in average gift, response, and an increase in costs.
It is not recommended to use this kit.

experiments.title: How adding a photo to a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a photo to a landing page increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2019 - 03/13/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. After sending the first two emails out to a split landing page test, where they tested the presence of gift handles that explained what each suggested gift amount would be used for, they concluded that adding just one line of copy around the gift handles did not increase likelihood of giving.
The Hillsdale team wondered about including a photo of some of the students who received Constitutions onto the landing page. Rather than telling the donor about what they were doing, they could show them with a photo. Would adding a photo of schoolchildren receiving the Constitution increase donations? They hypothesized that adding the photo would show proof, causing an increase in trust, and would increase emotional appeal. They hypothesized that each of these would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 15% lift from the treatment with the photo. Because this experiment only ran for one day, the experiment results shouldn’t be rolled out to the rest of the donation pages. However, this result was enough for the Hillsdale team to roll out to this landing page for the duration of the campaign.

experiments.title: How emphasizing matching gifts during the serial ask impacted revenue and donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion:
There were actually two tethered research questions associated with this experiment, which were:
Will additional social proof language to reinforce matching your gift to HAFA increase revenue?
Will the higher gift ask amount positively or negatively impact the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2019 - 02/28/2019
During the calendar year end campaign for Heritage, we installed an exit popup which used social proof language along with a symbolic gift ask to people looking to leave the page (on desktop computers only) to try to increase donor conversion rate and revenue increases. We wanted to see if we could increase revenue while maintaining the donor conversion rate when using language to reinforce matching your gift amount to Heritage Action to the same amount the donor just selected to give to the Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the revenue lift did not fully validate we noticed two things, which were:
With 80.8% level of confidence, we saw that the matching gift language increased revenue by 20.8%.
With 100% level of confidence, we also saw that it decreased the instant donation conversion rate by -23.3%.
Although the revenue lift wasn’t validated, it’s enough to be directionally accurate. Our assumption was that this would decrease the donor conversion rate relatively to the lift in revenue, and it appears that our hypothesis was indeed true.
There are two additional noteworthy observations from this analysis, though:
In reviewing the control transactions, the symbolic gift amount of $17.76 represented 87 of the 449 transactions in the “control” segment of the experiment. If those gifts were removed, the control segment was about even with the number of transactions that were in the “treatment” segment (where the treatment would only have seen -3.62% difference).
Finally, when looking at the percentage of gifts that were matched between organizations across the two segments, only 70.8% of the gifts within the “control” segment were matched between the two organizations, but the “treatment” segment (which focused on emphasizing a matching gift between both organizations) boasted a 90.3% matching gift rate.
Relatively speaking, that would be an increase of +27.4% increase in matching gift rates for the treatment segment — which was the driving force between the increase in revenue for that segment.

experiments.title: How visual reinforcement on the donation page increases mobile conversion during a match
experiments.researchQuestion: How will visual reinforcement affect donor conversion on the donation page during a short-term matching gift appeal?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/20/2019
CaringBridge was running a Valentine’s Day fundraising campaign and had a $25,000 matching gift. They knew that most of their donations on these short, high-urgency appeals came through a few donation funnels that landed on the same page. While previous tests had focused on driving traffic, they wanted to see if they could optimize conversion by driving home the match language on the two days that the gift was applied. They created two versions of a sticky top banner for the donation page—one that emphasized the match for “your” gift, and another that emphasized the match for “all donations”. They launched a three-way experiment split between mobile and desktop to see how it affected conversions on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: The personal match language expressed on the sticky bar increased conversion by 16.5%. Though this only reached a 90.1% level of confidence (and is not valid) it was close enough  (especially when combined with the desktop results) to show that this visual reinforcement is a powerful tool to provide extra motivation through the donation process—even if it is only one step.

experiments.title: How the delivery method for a content offer impacts name conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase name conversion by changing the delivery method to be more mobile friendly?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2018 - 01/03/2019
Boys Town offers an eNewsletter on their homepage and with the significant mobile traffic on their homepage we hypothesized that we might be able to increase name conversion/sign-ups by changing the delivery method of the offer to be more mobile friendly and also test adding stronger value proposition copy for the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see directional results with a decrease in names acquired from both treatments. This was a bit surprising to us. With the large mobile audience on Boys Town’s website, a slide up offer is one of the best mechanisms to deliver content to people. Our hypothesis in the results of this experiment is that while the delivery mechanism may have been the most effective one, the offer itself wasn’t compelling or appealing enough for people to actually want it when they saw it.

experiments.title: How social validity impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a quote from a trusted and famed source provide credibility to make others want to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2019 - 02/10/2019
The Leadership Institute has helped many political leaders. To prove their credibility, they wanted to see if including a quote from Vice President Mike Pence would increase conversion on their landing page. They tried a control page without the quote and image of Pence against two treatments: one with the quote and image along the bottom of the copy, and one with the quote and image, right-aligned in the copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the experiment itself did not validate, the combinations of treatments 1 and 2 conclude that the use of a quote from someone favorable and familiar will increase donations. The Leadership Institute should place the validity quote of Vice President Mike Pence on the rest of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How removing fields that aren’t necessary on an acquisition form impacts name conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a simplified form increase name conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2018 - 06/08/2018
The Missionary Oblates offer a free online course called Celebrating Mercy. They hypothesized that simplifying the acquisition form might increase name conversion for the course. The control had additional fields on it that we hypothesized weren’t necessary. To test this, we streamlined the form and removed the address fields (address, city, state, zip code). All other elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: By simplifying the form we increase name conversion by 8%. This experiment didn’t validate, but the results we were seeing were directional with a 87.5% confidence. The address fields aren’t required for a person to take the course. These additional fields were creating friction to the conversion process and by removing them we were able to capture more people and get them enrolled in the course.

experiments.title: How adding privacy language to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding conversational privacy language to an acquisition page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/09/2019 - 01/31/2019
Buckner International wanted to test the effectiveness of adding privacy language below the form on their acquisition page that would increase trustworthiness and decrease friction for users submitting the form, by reiterating what they would receive from Buckner by giving us their information, as well as how their info would be handled. Copy on the page remained the same, as did the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: The privacy language test did not reach statistical validity, but it showed a slight trend towards a decrease in conversion rate. It's possible that the privacy language had the opposite of the intended affect and increased people's wariness to submit the form.More testing in this area will be necessary to validate learnings.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials to an online course acquisition page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding 3rd-party evidentials in the form of testimonials increase course conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2018 - 08/03/2018
DTS was running their Genesis online course, and wanted to test the impact of testimonials from other students who had taken the course as 3rd-party evidentials on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The added testimonials did not have a statistically significant impact on course conversion rate. It is our hypothesis that the value expressed by the quotes was cancelled out by the increase of friction that came from a longer landing page. To validate this, we will want to test shorter quotes or have them inline with the email acquisition form.

experiments.title: How Facebook’s Bid Cap influenced overall results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will setting a bid cap of $50 decrease our costs per donor acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2019 - 01/10/2019
Buckner International had recently experienced two significant lifts to their 7 Scriptures email acquisition offer that had resulted in a boost to instant donor conversion. The resulting boost also reduced the cost per donor. It fluctuated by day but were were seeing some days with a cost per donor acquired of $50 or less. However, it was unpredictable.
In an effort to control the costs, we decided to test out the setting a cap on our bid of $50. This would allow us to potentially limit our costs, assuming the strategy worked.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were unable to validate this experiment because, with the low bid cap of $50, Facebook was not able to show our ads to enough audience to receive a conversion. While we were getting conversions for $50 or less without the cap, limited the spend to this low dollar resulted in Facebook not being able to show the offer enough. Without the audience exposure, we were not able to acquire any donors. While we did save ad spend, we sacrificed results which is not worth the trade off.

experiments.title: How mentioning fundraising efforts in an email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Does telling the reader exactly what they’re about to click on make an impact on clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2017 - 07/22/2017
The Leadership Institute ran a matching grant campaign to raise funds for field representatives to send to college campuses in the fall semester. They wanted to test their email copy to see if going into detail about the grant would inspire more people to give. They tested copy that did not mention the grant against copy that did mention the grant.
experiments.keyLearnings: The email copy that did not mention the matching grant generated a 6.8% click rate, while the email copy that did mention the matching grant only generated a 2.9% click rate. The level of confidence is 87.3%, so the experiment can’t be validated — although it’s likely that with more samples, the test could validate in favor of the copy that did not mention the matching grant.

experiments.title: How a new value proposition focus in a sales email affects mystery donor audit applications

rows.name: What are your 2019 online fundraising plans?
Here’s a great way to increase giving for 2019 and beyond

experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing the value proposition on the lack of time to plan and research lead to greater applications?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2018 - 12/20/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if a different value proposition approach to a mystery donor audit sales email would lead to greater applications. The control we had tested into already focused on how expert opinions are really just that : opinions. If your fundraising isn’t based on research, data, and testing, then you’re just relying on intuition.
The treatment focused on the lack of time to the necessary research to create a plan for 2019. We could step in with this mystery donor audit and do the leg work for you to help you create a strategy for your next year of fundraising. Both the subject line and body copy of the email were included in the treatment variables.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version saw a 70% decrease in clicks through to the landing page. While we didn’t reach full statistical confidence, we were approaching confidence at 89.5%. While we didn’t have enough applications to validate off of conversions (3 for the control and 1 for the treatment), this decrease in clicks is a strong indicator that focusing on time constraints is not the right value proposition focus.

experiments.title: How social pressure in the copy affects change in conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: Does pressuring rather than encouraging someone to improve their knowledge cause a change in conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2018 - 04/25/2018
This experiment was tested on Hillsdale College’s online course page. They wanted to know if copy that pressured the reader to engage would perform better than the more encouraging treatment, which asked the reader to enhance what they already know. The control makes the reader feel like they need to be better or face ridicule, while the treatment encourages the reader. The hypothesis was that the more direct language would pressure the reader to make the conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The copy that applied more pressure to the reader saw a .52% click rate while the more encouraging copy saw a .57% click rate. Since there was no statistically significant difference in results, we can conclude that the more direct copy had no change in conversion results.

experiments.title: How a “how did you hear about us” field affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the presence of a “how did you hear about us” attribution field affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2012 - 12/18/2018
Compassion International had long included a required dropdown field in their checkout process that asked the user what prompted their decision to sponsor a child today. This data was used to help guide their high level budgeting process, and thus had some amount value, but they were curious to see how it affected conversion. They created two test variants—one that made the field optional, and one that eliminated the field entirely. They launched a multivariate test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While none of the treatments gained full statistical validity, the test delivered two important directional learnings. First, there is no increase in conversion by removing the attribution field, which would imply that the data gained is more valuable to have than the revenue gained by removing the field. Second, making the field optional actually decreased conversion (and was approaching validity). One hypothesis as to why is that making the field optional now gave TWO decisions to the user: the first being “should I complete this field?” and the second being “what answer should I give?” This shows how seemingly small tweaks can compound decision friction. The results of this test indicate that this field should stay right where it is, as long as the data is useful to Compassion.

experiments.title: How a different Facebook ad image affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing the format of the webinar lead to an increase in webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2018 - 10/30/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if showing the format of the webinar and putting the production quality on display in the imagery would lead to more webinar registrations. The control used a typical webinar ad design – abstract representation of the topic, with å clear focus on the title and content.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this didn’t quite reach 95% validity, this treatment decreased registrations by 45% across all targeted Facebook audiences. It’s possible that what we thought was an added value (seeing the production quality) was actually a distraction from the actual topic, and the image made it more difficult to quickly see what the content was about as someone scrolled through the Facebook newsfeed.

experiments.title: How the length of copy in an email vs the landing page affects sales lead conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shifting the narrative from the landing page to the email lead to greater sales leads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2018
In this experiment, our goal was to generate sales qualified leads to set up a call related to new mystery donor audit. The control had a brief email explaining the topic, and landing page that went into full detail.
We have seen in the past (particularly with fundraising emails) that emails are often the best place to spend more time explaining a narrative. So for our treatment, we wondered if we could increase conversions by shifting a large portion of the copy from the landing page to the email. The copy is almost identical between the two versions, with a few minor adjustments in the treatment to make the email and landing page flow together naturally.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email and landing page saw a 215% increase in conversion rate at a 93% level of confidence. While we ideally aim for a 95% level of confidence, the impact on sales leads seems apparent and strong enough for us to move forward with treatment decisively.
This further confirms a hypothesis that highly personal emails are more effective at explaining a narrative and story, and that the common “best practice” of writing short email copy to boost clicks is not a reliable way to increase the ultimate goal of conversion.

experiments.title: How adding urgency into Facebook ad copy affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding urgency to an ad through body copy increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2018 - 12/03/2018
Buckner International was offering a free Advent devotional to be delivered each week of December. They wanted to test if adding urgency around the start of the Advent season would increase conversion rate. The “December 2” deadline allow for a clear deadline that would necessitate the need for signing up sooner rather than later. We decided to emphasize that date and include some copy early in the ad to introduce that concept prior to Facebook’s “Read More” link that would hide the bottom portion of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach the desired 95% level of confidence needed to statistically validate the experiment, we did see a directional decrease of 18%. Since we had reached the Dec 2 deadline mentioned in the ad, we had to stop the ad before reaching confidence. Urgency is a factor that has often proved to increase conversion; in this case, however, it is our hypothesis that the added urgency went against the purpose of Advent. An Advent devotional is intended to be used as a way for an individual to slow down during a hectic season.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a free offer-focused value prop convert more donors than a mission-focused value prop?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2018 - 11/05/2018
Hoover Institution has implemented a one-click sign up for Defining Ideas newsletter in their offer chaser emails. Essentially, it takes them right to a donation page instead of requiring them to fill out a landing page before getting to the donation page. They wanted to increase donor conversion on this page, and hypothesized that changing the value proposition on the page to a more resource-focused value prop instead of mission-focused value prop would fulfill this.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn't validate, but saw a directional lift of 120% in donations with the revised value proposition on the page. This version of the value proposition focused on the immediate value of current and future free resources that Hoover would be able to provide because of their gift, rather than focusing on the greater mission of Hoover. This concept should be tested again on future offer donation pages to fully validate the learnings.

experiments.title: How simplifying the imagery on a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a simpler background decrease friction on a landing page and increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2018 - 10/30/2018
Hoover was running their Dueling Populisms content offer, and wanted to test the effectiveness of a simpler landing page on conversion rate. To test this, they removed the background image on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not have any statistically significant impact on conversion, but saw a trend towards a 12% decrease.  More rigorous testing is likely needed to validate learnings on the page.

experiments.title: How communicating a smaller amount to goal impacts click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Which amount would increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2018 - 10/03/2018
During Boys Town September High Urgency campaign focused on kindness/bullying, we communicated to people that we had a goal to distribute 400 Praise Boxes by the end of the campaign. In one of the final emails to people, we gave them an update on the progress of the campaign and how many Praise Boxes we still needed to reach our goal. We hypothesized that a smaller number might increase motivation and get more people to click on the link to help us reach our goal. We tested this against a larger number to the 400 goal.
experiments.keyLearnings: The smaller goal amount increase conversion by 18%. This experiment didn’t validate, but it was directional at 85.9%. The larger amount to goal communicated to people that we really did need their help in these final days. More people were interested in seeing how they could help. Scarcity isn’t always a more motivating factor as we saw with this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding additional giving options copy to the primary donation page impacts conversion

rows.name: Image with Additional Giving Options Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding the additional giving options on the donation page impact donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/04/2018
Boys Town deployed a new primary donation page that when tested against their original donation page, increased donor conversion by 20%. At the initial launch of the new donation page, they had removed the additional giving options. This feature was not part of the new donation page, but was an important element to the Boys Town donor experience. After finding that the new donation page increased donor conversion, they wanted to see what the impact would be if they added the additional giving options back into the donation page. To do this, a line of copy was added just below the hero image. We tested this against the current winning version that only had the hero image.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the experiment didn’t quite validate, we did find that the addition of the copy for other giving options didn’t negatively impact donor conversion. Even better, the additional copy actually increased donor conversion. Knowing this, Boys Town can now keep the additional giving options on the primary donation page without decreasing donor conversion, and at the same time give people who are looking for other ways to give to Boys Town the opportunity to do so.

experiments.title: The impact of adding a second CTA as a P.S. in email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. lead to a higher click-through rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/25/2018
We decided to market our free online course to subscribers who had not yet enrolled. In crafting our emails, we wanted to know if adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. would lead to more people clicking through to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While our results were just below a 95% level of confidence, we saw a clear directional decrease in conversion with the addition of the P.S. Our hypothesis is that the P.S. did not feel natural to the flow and thus made the email seem less personal to the recipients.

experiments.title: How the ask approach in the body copy affects donations in a high-urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a fear of loss motivate more donors than a high-urgency ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2018 - 08/01/2018
Buckner International was nearing the last days of their summer match campaign. Given the tie-in with the direct mail, the actual deadline for the campaign was the next month but we could not wait that long in the online channel before moving on to the next campaign.
Since there was no hard deadline, we wanted to see if the “fear of losing the match” would be as high of a motivator as the urgency with no actual deadline. This approach was tested in the second and third paragraphs of the body copy, and the rest of the email remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although we did receive a 100% increase in gifts with the treatment, this experiment did not reach validity. We should test this approach again in future high-urgency campaigns with a more radical approach to effectively measure whether fear of loss truly motivates donors to give.

experiments.title: How the headline on a popup affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a headline that reinforces the value proposition of the offer convert more people?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2018
Heartlight Ministries was offering a free two-week online course through an exit intent popup on their website. The control popup focused the headline on getting the free course, instead of focusing on the value proposition of the course. The remainder of the copy and design stayed the same, but the headline was tested.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the test was not statistically valid, the value-focused headline treatment did produce a directional 9% lift in conversions. More testing is necessary, but this test suggests that focusing on the free-ness of the offer without explaining the value behind it produces a decrease in conversion, and that clarifying the value is key to them caring about the free course.

experiments.title: How a question in a banner ad affects clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking a question in a banner ad increase clicks to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/12/2018
In this experiment, we were advertising applications for the Nonprofit Innovation & Optimization Awards using a sticky bar on the NIO Summit site. The control used pretty straightforward copy saying you could be recognized for innovative fundraising. The treatment asked a question that we thought might be disarming and more inviting.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment didn’t reach a level of confidence of 95% like we would have liked, but it starts to indicated that the control was the better performer. This could be a result of a loss of clarity in the treatment. By asking a question, the specifics of the awards got moved to smaller text that may have gone unnoticed – leading to less clicks.

experiments.title: How removing a call-to-action link and asking for a reply affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking for a reply lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if we could further optimize our resend email that we use in promoting our monthly webinars. For the control, we used the template we had previously tested into. For the treatment, we wondered if an even more personal call-to-action would increase registrations. So rather than insert a CTA link like a marketing email would, we simply asked if they wanted to get a recording of the webinar – and waited for a reply.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we didn’t reach our desired 95% level of confidence, this experiment certainly was approaching validity with a 24.8% increase in webinar registrations for the treatment. This further confirms our overarching hypothesis that people are more likely to engage and respond to personal email than something they view as marketing.
In order for this method to scale, we’ll have to find a way to more efficiently get people signed up who reply to our emails.

experiments.title: The impact of moving the donation ask earlier in the funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donation ask earlier in the funnel increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2018 - 06/29/2018
The blog for Alliance Defending Freedom get significant traffic on a daily basis. Over the past several months, we had been able to test into a series of new offers that had lifted the email acquisition rate from less than 1% to 4% – 6%. This meant several hundred new emails were coming on to the file on a weekly basis and each of these subscribers were shown a donation page once they submitted their email. This meant a certain percentage of them would convert to donors instantly.
ADF had recently developed a new embeddable donation form that we wanted to test out on the blog. Historical tests had found that, when linking to a donation form, the blog did not serve as a significant source for donations. We wanted to determine if moving the ability to donate directly on to the blog would allow us to reduce the friction associated with the donation funnel and, thereby, increase donor acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Upon first glance, the embedded donation form appeared to have a 60% decrease to overall conversion. However, when digging deeper into the numbers, we discovered that while there was a 60% decrease between the people that saw the treatment compared to the control, the actual conversions that came from the email acquisition offer compared to the donation offer told a different story. If we looked at just those conversions, the direct donation ask had a 264% increase (at 91% level of confidence).
This variance is the result of the high converting traffic that occurs during the fiscal year end period. Essentially, subscribers would read a blog post and they later got an email asking them to make a donation (which was unrelated to their actions on the blog post). However, since they saw the original blog post, their donation was then attributed to the experiment.
What we can take away from this experiment is that we always need to dig deeper into the top level results reported by the various systems we use. We also now know that we need to re-test this concept once the fiscal year has passed.

experiments.title: How recurring gift language impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including value proposition language specific to The Leaders Club on the main donation form increase Leaders Club Acquisition? And how will it impact overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2018 - 06/05/2018
The Heritage Foundation was looking for ways to increase donor acquisition for their recurring giving club, The Leaders Club. As part of a series of experiments, we tested out a few different hypotheses to impact this metric. The first hypothesis we had was that simply explaining the recurring gift option and the potential benefits would increase the perceived value of the offer enough to increase acquisition for the club. We decided to test this hypothesis on the main donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 2 months, we did not reach the desired level of confidence to definitively identify how this experiment impacted both the likelihood to make a gift and the likelihood to make a recurring gift. However, the impact on the likelihood to make a gift was approaching significance (93% LoC) with a 13% decrease by having the Leaders Club language on the page. The impact on the chances to make a recurring gift was a long way from statistical significance (65% LoC).
Given the likelihood that the additional value proposition was hurting conversion and that there was no definitive proof it was helping recurring gift acquisition, we decided to end the experiment and look for alternative options to increase recurring gifts.

experiments.title: How a direct call to action influences clicks on a sticky-bar
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action drive more traffic to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/11/2018
In this experiment, we were launching a t-shirt design contest for the 2018 NIO Summit. We placed a sticky bar on the homepage of www.niosummit.com to let visitors know about the contest. We tested two different calls to actions: Enter Your Design vs Learn More
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Learn More” call-to-action drove 53.4% more traffic to the landing page, although the experiment did not reach the 95% level of confidence we would have liked. Nevertheless, this is a strong indicator that a softer call-to-action for general traffic on the homepage is more effective at increasing clicks.

experiments.title: Do images in an email next to blog links increase engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing images next to blog links in an email increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2018 - 05/01/2018
In the effort to continue to optimize the Weekend Update email, we wanted to see if further reducing the images in an email would impact the open and click rates of the send. Based on prior testing, we knew that reducing the image to text ratio of a message increased open rates significantly.
experiments.keyLearnings: Reducing the images in the weekend update to simply a small signature logo increased clicks by 10.4% and opens by 3.79%. Neither reached 95% significance, but both are close enough for a directional learning.

experiments.title: How a more direct call-to-action affect conversion from a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call-to-action drive higher course registration conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/20/2018
We ran this experiment across several different audience groups in our Facebook advertising of our new email fundraising course. We wondered if a more direct call-to-action could be effective in driving higher conversions. The control used a call-to-action of “Learn More,” while the treatment used a call-to-action of “Active Your Free Online Course.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The more direct call-to-action saw an 84.9% decrease in conversion for this audience group (Network for Good). This runs counter to the result of the same experiment with the Nextafter.com traffic. Together, these are showing that people who have not been exposed to NextAfter need more exposure to the value propositon in order to trust that our offers are worthwhile. Those who have engaged with us in any capacity already have a high level of trust, and simply need to see whether or not the offer is relevant to them.

experiments.title: Will clarity in a Facebook ad headline increase blog traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a clearer Facebook ad headline increase click to the blog post?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/04/2018
In this experiment, we had set up multiple promoted blog posts for the month of March. When setting up a promoted post for a blog comparing direct mail and email fundraising, we wondered if the blog headline was not clear enough to convey the point of the post. So we created a treatment that used clearer language.
experiments.keyLearnings: By changing the word “cannibalizing” to “stealing,” we were able to add additional clarity that led to a 32.4% increase in clicks to the blog post. This demonstrates the need for continual clarity in the way communicate. It’s easy to use jargon or insider phrases that mean something to us, but may not be as clear to the end user.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition and removing an image impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding value proposition and removing an image increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2018 - 04/08/2018
Pregnancy Decision Line gets significant traffic to their “Abortion Costs” webpage. Looking at the original page, we hypothesized that adding more value proposition to the page, and removing the header image might increase conversion. We tested the original page against a treatment that had conversational style copy, a larger phone number at the end of the copy, and no image on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment decreased conversion by 15.6%. Adding conversational value proposition has been known to help reinforce the message and motivate people to take action, but that isn’t what happened on this page. What this tells us is that the people coming to this page are coming to it for answers. Their motivation is high and the additional copy acted more as a point of friction to the decision process than a motivating variable.

experiments.title: How an emotional value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the more emotional value proposition generate more donations than the more tactically-focused value proposition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2018 - 03/28/2018
In a recent experiment, Heritage Action for America had found a new value proposition that had appealed to Heritage Foundation donors in the serial ask found on The Foundation’s donation page. This experiment had nearly doubled the number of donors that were now giving to Heritage Action via the serial ask.
With the success of this experiment, we wanted to see if it would have the same impact on the main Heritage Action donation page. When comparing it to the copy found on that page, the new treatment relied far less on the tactical elements of Heritage Action and focused more on the emotional reasons to give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for nearly two months, we found that the new value proposition was able to increase giving by 69%. Now, it should be noted that there was a relatively low number of visitors and conversions compared; we generally want to see more visitors before turning off a test. However, with a long running test, we have to start being concerned about historical distortion and given the low traffic volume sent to the Heritage Action main donation page, it wasn’t likely that we would see a significant boost in conversion or traffic.
With that said, this value proposition should be further validated with additional experiments in areas that will experience greater volumes of traffic like email campaigns.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition on the acquisition and donation page impacts donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a value proposition specific to the content offer help motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2018
Care Net developed a Lenten Devotional content offer and used Facebook to push people to their site to get the content offer. After people signed up for the content offer, they were then presented with an instant donation ask. This instant donation page used a value proposition that was Care Net focused. We hypothesized that having a donation page specific to the actual content offer might increase donor conversion. We created a treatment where the value proposition was specific to the Lenten Devotional and tested this against the control
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren’t able to validate the experiment, but we did see a significant directional lift with the treatment of 142%! This directional lift tells us that when we align our messaging with the value that people just experienced, we are able to motivate more people to take action and give.

experiments.title: How reducing friction in a shopping cart process affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does reducing friction by removing the child biography page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/18/2018
Compassion International had sought to identify gaps in their customer funnel through a deep-dive data analysis. They learned that the most direct path to sponsorship (with the fewest steps in between) had the highest conversion rate.
In looking at their child sponsorship funnel, they wondered if the “child biography” section, where a user was redirected after clicking the “choose me” button beneath a child was extraneous. If the user was actively selecting “choose me”, did they really want to just add the child directly to their cart, or was the biography an extra, necessary step in the consideration process?
They decided to better understand the consideration process through an A/B test. Half the users would not see the child biography page and would go straight to the cart. The other half would see the biography page as normal. They would look at conversions to understand the importance of the interstitial biography page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the child biography page led to a 26.8% increase in conversion, and also generated a lot of confusion among users who wanted more information on their child. Not only did removing this page affect conversion, it removed the user’s ability to return to that page, which created a negative user experience loop.
The decrease in conversion (though it didn’t achieve 95% significance due to stopping the test) and the feedback from users shows that while a direct path to checkout is ideal, the child biography page is an integral part of the consideration and conversion process.
This illustrates the principle that not all friction is bad friction. Sometimes, an extra step is helpful—and even essential—to the donation process.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition on a donation page can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would acquire more donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2017 - 06/20/2017
The treatment value proposition that was focused on the spiritual impact of a person’s gift increased overall revenue by 50%! There were not only more people who were motivated to give, but these people were motivated to give more. People were willing to invest in a kingdom-focused mission more generously.
While people cared about the event being successful and effective, they responded more when they knew their investment would have a larger impact – an eternal one.
This experiment didn’t validate, but it was leaning towards being valid and was rolled out for the rest of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Harvest Ministries had a high-urgency campaign leading up to their large-scale evangelism event, Harvest America. On the donation page for the campaign, the value proposition and call-to-action was centered on making the event a success. It had more of a logistics angle to it. Because the event was an evangelism outreach, we hypothesized that people might be more motivated to give if the value proposition was focused on the spiritual impact. We developed a treatment and split the traffic coming to the donation page.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition in the body copy and CTA affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2016 - 08/26/2016
For Harvest Ministries mid-month appeal they wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on the landing page. The control and the treatment had the same value proposition, but the treatment had subtle additions to the copy of the headline, body and CTA to stregthen the value proposition of the page. They ran a split test to see which landing page would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment landing page had an increase in donations by 22.8%. The reinforcement of the value proposition throughout the treatment landing page strengthened the message and ask, resulting in more people giving a donation.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer in an appeal affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the Tell Someone course offer receive more donations than the Tell Someone book offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2016 - 08/22/2016
Normally, for Harvest’s mid-month appeals, they offer a resource (normally a book or DVD) to people if they give a gift to the ministry.
For Harvest’s August mid-month appeal they ran a test to see if they could acquire more donations through offering a book (the Tell Someone book) or through a free online course (the Tell Someone course).
