For the book offer, people were taken directly to a donation page where they could get the book and give their donation. For the course offer, people had to first sign up for the course and then be asked for a donation. Using 40% of the email file, they A/B split tested this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results didn’t validate, the initial data pointed to validity and the treatment (the Tell Someone course) had an increase in opens, clicks, donations (25%) and revenue. Using this data, they then sent the remaining 60% of the email file the Tell Someone course offer.
Even though the test didn’t validate, deciding to send the remaining file the Tell Someone course offer was not only the best decision from what we were seeing with initial donations and revenue, but the long-term benefit and value of these donors is so much greater with the course than it would be with a more transactional offer like the book. The course gives people the opportunity to engage with Harvest on an ongoing basis at a deeper level. And at the same time, it allowed them to instantly support the ministry. By offering people something out of the ordinary that had a higher perceived value, people were much more motivated to give through the course offer than the book.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in email copy affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will copy with a clearer call-to-action increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The control email highly incentivized the click by asking the reader to “find out why” the sender (Greg Laurie) admires Joni, the author of the book. While this might drive a lot of clicks, we’ve seen this approach drive much lower conversion rates, as those who click through are not as motivated to accept the offer.
We devised a treatment that moved some of that copy into the email, incentivizing the click by giving them a timely call-to-action to find out how to get their own copy of the book before Mother’s Day.
This treatment produced a 58.7% decrease in clickthrough rate, but we wanted to measure conversion on the following donation page to see if those that clicked had a higher motivation to complete the intended transaction.
experiments.keyLearnings: The clearer call-to-action in the email did produce a 55.1% lift in donor conversion. However, because the email drove much less traffic, the control won in terms of overall revenue.

experiments.title: How the friction added with website navigation affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding the standard website header and navigation to the landing page ease the concerns of visitors about the cost of the course while not affecting or increasing conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2015 - 03/17/2015
Hillsdale College offers a free, online training series named Constitution 101.  After initially enrolling, participants are sent to a contextualized donation opportunity where we ask for a gift to help support the efforts of Hillsdale.
There had been complaints recently that the contextualized donation page gave the impression that the course was not free.  We wanted to alleviate those concerns while not reducing the revenue or conversion rate of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of navigation decreased conversion rate by 17%. Whenever we offer donors the opportunity to explore more areas of the website, we are increasing the likelihood that they will.  While we were trying to decrease anxiety, we inadvertently increased the friction associated with making the gift.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct copy approach that increases clarity improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2015 - 12/02/2015
Harvest Ministries was planning to send fundraising emails on Giving Tuesday. They had crafted an email to the housefile that utilized Greg Laurie’s traditional storytelling style, but wanted to see if a more direct approach that educated their donors about Giving Tuesday would increase results. They tested a new subject line, but also new email copy that took a more direct approach to see if increased clarity improved donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This showed that our donors respond financially when we are straightforward and direct. This 60% lift in revenue was a product of a subject line test that slightly increased open rate and a copy test that increased both clickthrough rate and donor motivation, as evidenced by this experiment.

experiments.title: How matching the visual congruence of the initial ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying and visually matching the original add improve email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2016 - 02/28/2016
As part of the launch and promotion of Hillsdale College’s latest free online course that studied the life and work of CS Lewis, we created an email capture page based upon what we had learned from prior experiments. This page utilized a design and copy similar to past online courses.
However, after reviewing the entire process, we realized that the default email capture form may cause a bit of disorientation for visitors coming from the ad. The ad (shown below) had a completely different look and feel than the landing page.
We had the hypothesis that if we can improve the visual congruence of the page and align the headline better to the call-to-action of the ad, we can increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not a exponential lift, we did find that by visually aligning the design with the original ad we were able to increase email capture rate by 3.9%. This is a concept that will be valuable to utilize in future email acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity and specificity of the value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/24/2015 - 12/04/2015
Hillsdale College launched a popular new course studying the life of Christian apologist C.S. Lewis. Donor conversion was low in comparison to other courses, so they investigated the copy on the donation page. The value proposition presented described Hillsdale’s online courses in general. They knew that this course attracted a lot of people who might not be familiar with the Hillsdale brand so they drafted a second copy treatment to explain why they teach the course, and how it is free. Then they launched an A/B test to see which copy treatment performed better.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that specificity does increase the force of the value proposition, which in turn increased donor conversion rate. This may not be the case with all of Hillsdale’s courses, but when attracting a new audience, we may need to be more educational in why the courses are presented, and how they are funded.

experiments.title: How adding credibility and clarity to the landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page produces the the greatest conversion rate to customers for their conferences?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2014 - 03/27/2014
The Acquire The Fire site is a WordPress multi-page installation that gave the user plenty of options: get resources, find out more about the ministry, see the latest list of tour dates, and access to the store (which sold curriculum, merchandise, and other non-ticket items). Sometimes, access to all of multiple pages is a good thing — it encourages users to spend more time on the site and learn more about the ministry. However, we hypothesized that this could lead to what we call “unsupervised thinking” — by allowing users to wander all over the site, we give them more opportunities to get distracted from the ultimate goal. We needed to be able to guide them down the “path of righteousness” that leads to conversion.
We wanted to create an experiment that helps determine what motivates their customers in order to increase their overall conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After just under a month of testing, this landing page delivered a 35% lift in visitor conversion! Additionally, the name acquisition offers grew Teen Mania’s email list size by 13%. This page was a resounding success, and taught us several things about Teen Mania customers:
1. Credibility matters. There are a lot of youth evangelism events out there, but few of them have the endorsements, coverage, and credibility that Acquire The Fire has. This needs to be up front and repeated throughout the value proposition.
2. Buying tickets is a big decision. Our customers are generally buying tickets for a large group and want to make a good decision with their budget dollars. The more that we can show them what they will experience and reinforce that with quotes and testimonials from people who have experienced an Acquire The Fire event, the more we can reduce anxiety about the event itself.
3. Not all visitors are ready to buy. We know anecdotally that it can take time for youth pastors to get budget approval, parental approval, or youth signups. Because of these delays, not everyone who comes to this site is able to buy tickets. We need ways to engage these visitors and give them something of value in exchange for their email address (and the opportunity to market to them later).

experiments.title: How the power of match language affects conversion

rows.name: "Double My Impact" Call to Action Copy
Action Oriented Call to Action Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that because of the urgent message and the clear impact of the campaign, more donors would respond to a call-to-action that showed the impact of their money.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2013 - 06/06/2013
Americans For Prosperity ran a one-day fundraising campaign that had a matching gift it — a generous donor had offered to double every gift. These are common in fundraising, and are usually a boost to any campaign — but we wondered how much the match actually incentivized the gift. Would more people give if they knew their gift was being matched, or would more people give to support the outcome they wanted?
We devised a test to see which treatment would convert more visitors to donors: match language or impact language?
experiments.keyLearnings: The new call-to-action decreased donor conversion 26.6%, showing that “doubling the gift” is a powerful motivational incentive. This led to a focused effort to secure more matching gifts for online campaigns and continuously promoting the match throughout the fundraising initiative.

experiments.title: How reduced email-specific landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the length of the value proposition on a email-specific landing page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2017 - 08/16/2017
Hillsdale College discovered that creating channel-specific landing pages allowed them to launch tests and document test results with even greater accuracy. By splitting up the incoming traffic based on channel, they hoped they could match the experience closer to the motivation of the user. They hypothesized that individuals who received and read through an email offer about their online course, “Theology 101” may have a high enough motivation that they didn’t need to read a long value proposition on the landing page they clicked to visit. They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale found that the treatment with the reduced value proposition actually decreased email acquisition by 26.2%. This would indicate that the highly motivated user still needs a little nudging even after clicking through the email offer.
The results of this test almost reached validity. However, since they did not officially reach the 95% confidence threshold, we cannot say with certainty that this reduction in negatively impacted conversion.
Despite falling short of validity, this test does provide a helpful learning. It’s clear that the reduction in copy was nearing significance. For that reason, we have launched additional tests similar to this one on a variety of channel-specific landing pages to track how copy length impacts email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How leveraging the power of the unknown affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does it improve conversion to suggest that the visitor has “never heard of” Imprimis?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2015 - 02/17/2015
Hillsdale College launched a Facebook campaign to grow the subscriber list for Imprimis, their renowned speech digest newsletter. Salon Magazine called Imprimis “the most influential conservative publication you’ve never heard of”, which Hillsdale wanted to use in the copy. Within that quote, there are two claims of value: that Imprimis is “the most influential conservative publication” and that the user has “never heard of” it. We decided to test the first claim independently and then measure the impact of adding the second claim to it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Leveraging the power of the unknown had a significant impact on landing page conversion. Overall conversion rate still leaves something to be desired (to be improved through further testing), but we know that adding an element of perceived mystery to the value proposition increases conversion.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”.
A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue -- but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate.
Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner. The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment's gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the "Other amount" option.  This is an example of "unsupervised thinking" which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale's next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How a shortcut to the acquisition form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a shortcut to the bottom of the email acquisition page increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 10/26/2017
The conversion rate for Econ 101 had historically remained around 25% for visitors to the page. This indicated that the visitors had a high motivation. We had the hypothesis that if we could make it easier to convert, we could acquire more email addresses. On mobile especially, the length of the page was a point of friction. We decided to add in navigation bar that would allow the visitor to jump to the bottom of the page to see if eliminating friction this way would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this situation, the addition of a “smooth scrolling” option that jumped visitors to the bottom of the page, decreased email acquisition by 8.3%. While this did not reach a level of statistical significance, it did not seem like it would be a positive impact in either case so we decided to move on to the next experiment. There are multiple reasons why this treatment may not have worked:
The jump to the bottom skipped past the value proposition copy which decreased their likelihood to convert.
The length of the page was not a significant point of friction to start with which meant this change did not fix an actual existing problem.
The thick underline created a visual divider between the headline and copy that interrupted the visual flow down the page.
Visitors did not understand that the arrow was an actual shortcut on the page.
Regardless of the reason, this test would indicate that the “shortcut” to the bottom does not increase the chances of conversion.

experiments.title: How a related background image impacts email acquisition rate on a course signup page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the presence of a related background image increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2017 - 10/26/2017
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to optimize their online course signup page for ‘Great Books 102.’ They wondered: Will the presence of a related background image increase email acquisition rate? They launched a treatment page that contained the same image used in their Facebook promotion and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the presence of a related background image did not increase email acquisition rate on their ‘Great Books 102’ course signup page. Even though the imagery selected for the background does match the image used in the Facebook advertisement, it’s clear that visitors were not significantly affected by the addition of the background image. Therefore, it did not reduce anxiety like it was intended to do.
Further tests are being conducted to determine if any kind of background image is effective in reducing anxiety.

experiments.title: How increasing the visibility of the donation button impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will making the donation button easier to find increase the overall number of gifts received?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2017 - 07/21/2017
This test, after running for nearly two months, we did not reach a 95% level of statistical validity. While the big red button seemed to increase giving, there was no significant change to the number of people that were clicking on the button. With this being the case, it does not seem reasonable to say that the bigger button motivated more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings: Based upon donor feedback, The Heritage Foundation wanted to find a way to make the donation button on the Heritage.org website easier to find. In doing this, we wanted to measure what kind of impact it would have on donor giving.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted the Membership campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Which treatment will result in the greatest lift to donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2017 - 06/30/2017
Neither of the treatments reached statistical validity. After further analysis, a big takeaway from this experiment can be found in the experiment setup itself. With a radical redesign, the goal is to test a variety of significant factors in order to achieve a bigger lift. However, when we do that, we are unable to identify the specific element that caused the lift.
With this test, we tested multiple factors but they were relatively minor to the conversion process. By testing the headline, background, and a button, there was no cohesive hypothesis that could be isolated and learned from in the future.
For the future, we need to focus on isolating individual factors on a page or making significant modifications to the elements of a landing page that we know influence conversion (copy, donation form, etc).
experiments.keyLearnings: During the Heritage membership campaign, we wanted to test out a radical version of the existing Join Heritage donation page. The new version utilized the size of the Heritage member base as a way to increase credibility of the headline. It also removed the majority of the design elements to try to make the content better flow together. A third version was prepared that also added a “Donate” button to the top that would allow people to jump straight to the form.

experiments.title: How targeting an appeal to high “D” DISC profiles impact response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will targeting a high “D” style email and landing page at the high “D” constituents increase overall response rate
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 07/28/2017
For the first email in the 2017 Heritage Board Challenge, we wanted to use the DISC profiles that we were able to append to subscriber and donor records. We created the first email and landing page for the campaign without worrying about the DISC profile of the writing. Once we had the approved copy, we then rewrote the same appeal in the style of the segment that the copy did not address. In this case, it was a high “D”.
We then split the constituents based upon their primary DISC profile and their donor status. We also made sure the split was evenly divided along highest previous giving levels so that one would not be “better” donors than the other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The high “D” style email and landing page decreased the overall response rate across all profiles by 21% at a 87% level of confidence. When we focused in on just the high “D” segment, we saw a more pronounced decrease. While it did not reach a statistical level of confidence, it would indicate that this particular appeal was not able to better speak to high “D” profiles.
While this test was not successful, it is just the first step in validating the approach. There could be many reasons why the appeal did not work (such as selecting the wrong aspect of the appeal, i.e. the Board match). We will want to continue to validate this approach to reach a high level of confidence on its impact.
As a note, the control was an “Sc” email and an “Ic” landing page. The treatment was a “Ds” email and a “Di” landing page.

experiments.title: How addressing High-I subscriber in their style of communication impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will speaking to subscribers in their preferred DISC style increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2017 - 08/11/2017
During The Heritage Foundation’s 2017 Board Match campaign, we decided to test the style of communication to see if we could better align the messaging style with the subscribers’ known DISC scores. Using the data that we appended from the CrystalKnows website, we segmented the subscriber list by both donor status and primary disc style. We then took the Control version of the email and landing page (written in the I style) and recreated it in the style of a high C.
The goal of this experiment was to see if we could increase the giving of a high C subscriber. However, this write up is based upon the trends we observed when analyzing the high I subscribers of this same test.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had no significant impact on the high “C” subscriber base which was the target of the experiment. However, after reviewing the data, we discovered that the high “C” landing page and email adversely impacted the giving of the high “I” subscriber base. While we did not hit the 95% level of confidence we strive for, it was quickly approaching statistical validity.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party evidential quotes impact conversion on the primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd-party evidential quotes improve donor conversion on the primary donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 01/03/2018
In experiments that were run several years back, we found that added 3rd-party quotes from well-known figures would boost conversion rate in fundraising campaigns. However, over the years, this is something that had fallen off of the primary donation page. With Heritage making the news headlines and receiving accolades from significant figures, we decided to re-test this strategy.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the two treatments. As a result, we have shut off the experiment and continue forward with the control version.

experiments.title: How the specifics of a plan influenced conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding specifics about a strategic plan increase likelihood to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2017 - 11/25/2017
During Heritage’s Annual Member Survey, after a subscriber had filled out their survey, we presented them with a donation page. On this page, when making the solicitation, we were talking about the national budget blueprint that Heritage had created. At the end of the page, the call-to-action was to get the plan in as many lawmaker’s hands as possible.
However, we wondered if adding more specifics about the plan would help sell the importance of it. We created at treatment that did just that.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not reach a statistical level of validity to make any claims about whether the increased details helped or hurt the results. It is something that would need to be tested more in depth at a later date.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned acquisition page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does simplifying the process of submitting a story increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2017 - 10/31/2017
National Breast Cancer Foundation was running their BCAM campaign and wanted to test a redesign of their Snapshots of Hope acquisition page, which asked for stories from survivors or family members of people with breast cancer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The redesigned page produced a 22% decrease in conversion rate. It’s possible that the removal of the 3-step process produced anxiety in the mind of the submitter or confusion on the process and requirements.

experiments.title: How adding images to a donation page can impact conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding images to the donation page impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/08/2017
On the primary donation page for NBCF’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase donations by adding images to the page. The control donation page just had copy and the treatment they developed had three images added to the top page. They split the traffic coming to the page and tested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the images on the donation page had a 30% increase in donations. This test didn’t reach validation, but the initial data shows that the addition of images to a donation page motivates more people to give.
For an organization like NBCF, images can be very powerful. The added images helped to reinforce the value proposition message and visually showed people what and who their donation would be going towards.

experiments.title: How the copy tone of an ad impacts conversion.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2017 - 04/18/2017
The Missionary Oblates offered a special Easter Novena to people through paid Facebook ads. They wanted to test the motivation of the end-user by testing the tone of the copy and how they presented the offer. The control ad presented the offer early on in the copy and had a more uplifting tone with an invitation to get the offer. The treatment talked about the importance of Easter and encouraged people to get the Novena.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test didn’t quite validate and there was only a slight difference between the control and the treatment. The initial data showed the ad with a more uplifting tone that presented the offer earlier in the copy had a 8% increase in acquisition. We recommend that a more radical test be done using some of these same concepts from the winning variant in order to validate the experiment in the future.

experiments.title: How presenting a relevant premium offer on a page with high volumes of traffic can impact donor conversion.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: How would adding a donation appeal impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 09/05/2017
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate get significant traffic to their Daily Prayers webpage. People are coming to this webpage from a daily email. Knowing the significant amount of traffic the page gets, we hypothesized we might be able to convert some of these web visitors into donors. We tested adding a donation opportunity on the page with a relevant premium offer. We split the traffic and tested the two variables.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the results weren’t statistically valid (83.5%), we were able to increase donor conversion by 23.2%. What this shows us is that we can convert high volumes highly engaged traffic into donors when we present them with a donation opportunity with a relevant premium offer.

experiments.title: How year-end specific copy affects main donation page conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does year-end specific copy increase donations on a main donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2017 - 01/02/2018
The Missionary Oblates were running a test on their main donation page for year-end. They tested year-end specific copy against their year-round main donation page. They also removed an image and made the headline bigger.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment did not validate, we saw a trend upwards with the year-end specific treatment, with a 12% increase in donations and a significantly higher average gift with the treatment copy. This is likely due to the urgency in the copy along with an automatically more urgent page due to the timing of the test (highest fundraising time of the year).

experiments.title: How the total number of questions in a quiz affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shorter quiz increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2017 - 12/08/2017
This year, Illinois Policy Institute has exposed a serious problem in the state of Illinois. It’s been more than 15 years since the Illinois General Assembly has passed a balanced budget, and lawmakers have turned to tax hikes in order to ease the deficit. However, this is not a long-term solution and it’s caused illinoisans to pay some of the highest taxes in the nation. Illinois Policy developed a plan to balance the budget and ease taxes, all at once. In order to get the word out, they developed a 9-question quiz followed by a tax petition that would build awareness among Illinoisans and encourage them to take action to advance this plan. Before launching, Illinois Policy Institute wondered: Will a shorter, 6-question quiz increase petition signatures?
They created a treatment version and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Illinois Policy Institute found that the shorter quiz did not produce a valid increase or decrease in email acquisition. While these results aren’t statistically significant, there is an important takeaway. We’ve seen a correlation in other testing that shows the longer time commitment an individual makes in consuming an offer, the higher their instant and long-term donor conversion rate is. Since their is no significant difference between email acquisition rate in this test, it’s possible that we can increase donor conversion by continuing to offer the longer quiz (i.e. the one that requires more of a time commitment). The next step for this test sequence is to test an even longer version of the quiz to see if we can impact donor conversion without hurting email acquisition.

experiments.title: How reducing specificity affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler headline clarify the value proposition and increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2015 - 11/24/2015
The Hoover Institution launched a campaign to grow the subscriber base of the Hoover Daily Report, their flagship policy newsletter. They drafted landing page copy that highlighted the authority of their research fellows (who provide content for the Daily Report), but wanted to test a much simpler value proposition inspired by another email newsletter. They hypothesized that this new simple headline would increase email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though it was only a slight lift, there was enough difference to achieve statistical validity. The simpler headline produced a 6% lift in email conversion — which tells us that we don’t always have to be selling. Many times, our prospects can be attracted to subscribe by clarity rather than perceived authority.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn't significantly impact conversion - positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.

experiments.title: How removing elements of friction and strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2017 - 09/26/2017
The primary donation page for FamilyLife used a large image at the top of it with their traditional value proposition copy on it. They recently developed their value proposition and wanted to test a radical redesign of their primary donation page that tested this copy and removed other elements of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign increased donor conversion by 14.3%. By removing elements of friction on the page and strengthening their value proposition, we were able to not only increase the number of people giving a gift, but we were able to also motivate them to give more generously resulting in a 94.4% increase (77% valid) in revenue as well because the average gift increased.

experiments.title: How adding scripture reinforces value proposition in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding scripture reinforce the value proposition to the donor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2017 - 08/16/2017
FamilyLife was running a fiscal year end high urgency campaign and wanted to test the impact of adding a scripture reference into the email on donations.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to an email affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the addition of a third-party testimonial increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2017 - 08/27/2017
FamilyLife was running a high urgency moneybomb campaign. They wanted to test whether adding a testimonial to an email would increase people’s motivation to give and their belief in the value proposition of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The testimonial increased conversion by 167%. This email only reached 91% validation, but it was clear to readers that the quote from an outside source was genuine and motivated more people to give by supporting the value proposition.

experiments.title: How removing a banner in an email appeal impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the banner from the email copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2017 - 12/20/2017
For a FamilyLife email appeal during their year-end giving campaign, they inserted a banner in the body of the copy that served as a call-to-action. This banner then would take people to the donation page. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase donor conversion if we removed the banner and used text. We split the email file and tested this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing the banner from the email, we decreased clicks by 46%. While this metric is important, we ultimately care about donor conversion. Even though there was a 46% decrease in clicks from the email that didn’t have a banner we were able to increase donor conversion by 90%! The donor conversion metric didn’t quite validate (89% valid), but it is leaning towards validity and is at a point that we feel confident enough to draw learnings from.
This tells us that even though we got more people to click through from the control email with the banner, those people were not as highly motivated donors as the people who clicked the text and raw link. The visual piece in the email created an easy click-through point for people, but those people weren’t ready to donate once they got to the donation page. The banner was also a visual piece of friction that possibly prevented people from reading the whole value proposition. By removing the banner, more people read the copy and we were able to fully communicate the value proposition and motivate people to give once they got to the call-to-action at the bottom of the email.
This test also shows why it is important to not base our testing decisions off of up-stream metrics when we can. If we would have done that, we would have championed the version that increased clicks but decreased donations.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a homepage banner affects donation conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a value prop-focused banner increase donations more than a match-focused banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2017 - 12/13/2017
FamilyLife had a high-urgency banner focused on their 2 million dollar matching gift for their year-end campaign. They wanted to test whether a banner that focused more on the value a gift provided would increase donations more than a match-focused banner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not validate, but the treatment banner produced a 21% decrease in donations. Because there were multiple factors tested in the banner, it’s hard to say exactly which one produced a decrease, but the removal of matching gift language and a less urgent color on the banner likely contributed to the decrease, because the matching gift language proved to be a motivating factor for FL donors in other testing.

experiments.title: How match language on a popup affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does match language on a popup increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2017 - 12/19/2017
FamilyLife was running their year-end campaign and wanted to test match language on their website pop-up versus no match language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not validate, but saw a trend towards a 19% increase in gifts with match language on the popup. People were more likely to give when they know that their gift will be matched and make a greater impact.

experiments.title: How suggested gift amounts affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does suggesting gift amounts based upon previous giving increase the revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2015 - 06/22/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to find a way to lower the number of decisions donors had to make when giving their gift. The idea was the suggest a gift amount based upon their previous giving history with the hypothesis that this would lower the friction of the giving form.
To test this hypothesis, we set up an experiment to split traffic between their standard blank gift amount field and a form that would populate three different buttons based upon their last gift amount (this was passed in via the querystring).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By suggesting a gift amount, the overall donation page conversion dropped by 54.9%. This was a surprising result given the success of this type of experiment with other research partners.
This outcome taught us something significant about DTS donors. The average gift for their online donors is significantly higher than the most other nonprofits.  As a result, the suggested gift amounts were far larger than would normally be seen on a donation page.  It is our hypothesis that these larger gift options scared away potential donors to the campaign by presuming too much.  We get better results when we let the donor determine their own level of generousity.

experiments.title: How the location of a success indicator affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design treatment will produce the highest conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2015 - 07/01/2015
The donation page is the most critical step in online fundraising. Any improvement in the conversion rate on the page, even minor, will lead to increased revenue. During Dallas Theological Seminary’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to see if the placement of the campaign progress meter would affect the conversion rate on the form.
Our goal was to gain insight into the donor’s thought sequence but determining where an element that shows process towards a goal should be placed, right at the top of the form or after the impact of a donation has been stated?
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was able to statistically validate but given the short timeframe and the relatively low sample size, we would want to run this experiment again to be certain of these results.  The increased motivation of donors towards the end of a fiscal year end campaign may have impacted the overall performance of the test.

experiments.title: How message tone of an email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does messaging tone affect donation response rate in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2015 - 01/22/2015
As  part of their new monthly email appeal strategy, Hillsdale College wanted to learn which messaging tone produced the best response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment utilized two distinct emails and two distinct landing pages. In the experiment, the softer tone email generated 257% increase in click through rate.  This increase in traffic illustrates a concept that we have observed in other experiments.  By reducing the "sharpness" of the ask in the email, we can increase the volume of traffic to the landing page, however the visitors will be less motivated causing the on-page conversion rate to be lower than the Control.   However, if the increase in traffic is significant enough, the net result will be more revenue.
The softer tone treatment delivered a response rate 85% higher than the original control with a 40% higher average gift.
This experiment is a great example of the importance of analyzing the entire conversion pathway, from email delivery to successful gift, when determining a winner.

experiments.title: How the right value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 - 08/05/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to increase donor conversion rate on their main donation page. Two previous tests had failed to do so by adding value proposition language and changing the open gift field to pre-selected buttons.
The question was raised — what if the first value proposition test had the right language, but the wrong presentation? DTS crafted a new version of the value proposition that started with a fundamental truth that the audience could agree with: “The world needs Biblical truth now more than ever.” This copy also included a quote from Chuck Swindoll that was inline with the text, as opposed to offset in the previous test.
Finally, DTS added a subheadline and strengthened and bolded the call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new landing page produced a 186.5% increase in donor conversion. This told us that presenting the right value proposition in the right way has a massive effect on donor conversion. The results of this test inspired many more tests across different donation pages and acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How reducing copy for highly motivated donors affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing donation page copy increase conversion for highly motivated donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2015 - 08/07/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary sent a survey to their donors and prospects to better understand what they valued most about the organization. The survey was immediately followed by a donation ask. DTS hypothesized that after completing the survey, donors wouldn’t need as much copy to be convinced to make a gift. So they developed a treatment landing page that removed much of the copy — which to a highly motivated donor, could pose as friction.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page with reduced copy increased conversion by 134.3%. This means that the survey did a sufficient job of motivating the donors — all DTS had to do was ask.

experiments.title: How the wrong value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will collect the highest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2015 - 06/22/2015
The primary donation form for Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) had remained consistent for the last several years. It was a well-designed form but it made the assumption that those donors that make it to the page are already convinced to give their gift. This was evident from the lack of any copy or significant calls to action at the top of the form.
In an effort to increase the perceived value of the donation to potential donors, we decided to create a new treatment. This new page would utilize the same donation form but would add copy to the top that discussed one of the unique value propositions of DTS. It also included quotes from well known figures in the Christian community supporting the work of DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donation form treatment resulted in a 28.1% decrease in donor conversion. This would indicate that the value proposition communicated on the page did not appeal to the potential donors. Additionally, by adding the wrong copy and pushing the donation form further down on the page, we introduced friction into the donation process. These two factors combined to reduce the donors and revenue to the page.

experiments.title: How value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing perception of cost and work increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2016 - 02/09/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had a great response to their first free online course on the Gospel of John. But objectively looking at the headline 0f their signup page a few weeks later, they wondered if the value proposition could be improved — specifically, by using something other than the word “study”.
Other experiments had shown that offering the benefits of the offer often outperform words like “study” that make the offer seen like work. So they launched two headline tests to see if they could affect conversion. Both headlines sought to show the real opportunity of the course — to “step inside the classroom”, but with different desired effects. The first headline offered the opportunity to “see the gospel of John like never before” and the other to “grow your understanding of John’s gospel”.
They launched an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, neither variation outperformed the control. This tells us something very interesting — “study” might actually be a positive factor of the value proposition. It suggests that prospects for this course might be looking to “study” and really spend time with the course.
It may also suggest that the value proposition to “step inside the classroom” is unclear or undesirable. This concept lends itself to further testing.

experiments.title: How registration form length affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the perceived length of the form increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2016 - 06/27/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had just released their third free online course on Genesis. Although they had made plenty of changes to the landing page copy, they hadn’t tried to optimize the form, which asked for both online and offline information, totaling 11 fields. We hypothesized that reducing the perceived length of the form (by nesting fields together) would increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The optimized form produced a 4.1% increase in signups. This slight increase suggested that the form didn’t have as much impact on the page as originally thought, and prompted a series of new tests to increase the force of the value proposition in the copy.

experiments.title: How additional premium-focused value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of premium focused value proposition copy increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2016 - 09/19/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary had found a popular offer in their free Gospel of John course. After the user signed up, they were given the opportunity to make a gift.In exchange for a gift of $100 or more they were offered a commentary as the course companion book for the Gospel of John course. DTS decided to try to see if they could increase conversion rate by integrating review content for the commentary into the donation page. They pulled some reviews from a popular book review site onto the page, showed five stars, and gave a positive review for the commentary, hoping that this would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page produced a 47% decrease in conversion, although these results were not statistically valid due to low traffic to the page. However, the treatment also produced an increase in average gift, which may suggested this motivated a certain segment of people who enrolled in the course to increase their average gift to receive the commentary. However, this additional value proposition appeared to distract from the main value proposition, which converted more registered users into donors. Wally learning to not produce a positive impact on this donation page further testing for how the book is offered throughout the course might produce a lift.

experiments.title: How refreshing imagery affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an image more closely tied to the mission increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2015 - 11/12/2015
CaringBridge wanted to increase conversion on their tribute donation pages by adding imagery that resonated with their mission of connecting people during life’s most difficult times. They tested an image of clasped hands to see if it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This was a fascinating test, as a simple image change produced a significant result. This test inspired a new test to see if simply changing the image randomly (which can produce novelty bias) can produce a similar (or greater) lift.

experiments.title: How personalization in donation page headline copy affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a personalized, donor-centric headline outperform a clear, direct headline?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 04/11/2016
Previous CaringBridge tests had shown that in the tribute funnel (where visitors give in honor of their friend of loved one), personalization improved the likelihood of donor conversion. However, previous experiments had also shown that a clear, direct headline (in this case “Give to CaringBridge”) resulted in the highest donor conversion.
The team wanted to test whether a personalized, donor-centric call to action, though longer and less direct, would improve the clarity of the value proposition, present a stronger incentive, and result in higher donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment showed that visitors’ likelihood to convert increased significantly when they were reminded of the impact of their gift. With CaringBridge copy, there has always been a balance between clarity and personalization. Through this tribute funnel, where the gift is closely tied to a personal relationship, personalization wins.

experiments.title: How organization-centric language affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing organizational-centric language increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 05/05/2016
CaringBridge had performed several successful experiments to personalize the copy on their “tribute” donation pages. But user feedback had shown one interesting organizational fact: most people didn’t know CaringBridge was a nonprofit organization. This had produced confusion before, so they developed a single sentence of copy to add to the page in pursuit of clarity:
“Nearly 90 percent of our funding comes from people like you who have experienced the power of CaringBridge firsthand.”
This copy was never tested, and we developed a hypothesis that it might be what we call “organizational-centric” language. This type of language is not inherently bad, but it might clash with the tribute page, which is highly personal.
We developed a treatment that eliminated this sentence and set out to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (without the sentence) increased conversion by 6.6% — not an overwhelming amount, but enough to show that taking the donor’s attention away from the person impacted by the gift reduced the likelihood that they would give.
This doesn’t solve the marketing problem that many people don’t know CaringBridge is a nonprofit, but it tells us that the donation page is not the place to communicate that.

experiments.title: How a direct ask in an exit intent affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct ask increase traffic or donor conversion rate for an exit intent banner?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2017 - 06/01/2017
Toward the end of a matching gift campaign, Buckner International wanted to test out an exit intent on their main website promoting the challenge. Since this was the first time this approach had been used, we wanted to find out which approach (a “learn more” or a direct ask) would have the greatest impact on a visitor’s likelihood to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the direct ask approach reduced to the number of visitors to the campaign page by 50%. While it did not reach the 95% level of confidence we would strive for, it was quickly approaching validity before the campaign ended. However, the goal was not necessarily to get clicks, it was to get donations.
When we looked at the number of actual donations, there was not a statistically significant difference between the two treatments. We were able to drive more traffic to the page but this did not result in more total donations. For future tests such as this, we will need to go with a more radically different treatments to achieve validity.

experiments.title: How personalized value proposition affects offer conversion rate

rows.name: Pray God's Word for your Child

experiments.researchQuestion: Are people more motivated to download by a claim of value that benefits them personally?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2017 - 06/30/2017
Buckner International released a new content offer named 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in Your Life. They wanted to test if people were more likely to download the offer if the headline on the landing page promised something specifically for them, or if they were more likely to download if it would benefit their child.
The control copy led with, “Pray God’s Word for Your Child” and the treatment copy led with, “Get this 7-day devotional to grow in your faith and pray God’s Word for your child”. The treatment copy also included an intro body copy for parents whose bedtime prayers had “gotten stale.” Other copy and elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment did not reach statistical validity, it was quickly approaching the 95% level of confidence that we strive for. As a result, we can draw some insights from this test.
After analyzing the two different versions, it is our hypothesis that the treatment diluted the primary product-level value proposition- praying for your kids- by with an additional offer – growing in your faith. The first headline is high in clarity, while the second headline and additional intro copy likely reduced this clarity.

experiments.title: How the use of a video in a Facebook ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a video ad increase conversion rate over an image ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2017 - 08/07/2017
Buckner International was promoting a new guide on how to talk to your kids about poverty. The goal of the campaign was to serve as an email acquisition mechanism to grow their house file. They had created a promotional video to be used in advertising but we wanted to test this new medium against the standard control which was an image of the offer.
They wanted to see whether including the video instead of an image would increase people’s motivation to download the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The video ad saw an increased conversion rate of nearly 60%. We did not yet reach statistical validity but at a 94% level of confidence, it was quickly approaching this. It is our hypothesis that this is likely because the video lined up more with the ad content and the storyline, creating an emotional connection with the topic that the offer was covering. This increased people’s motivation to download the offer.

experiments.title: How a more personal copy treatment affects conversion rate on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a more personal copy treatment affect conversion rate on an acquisition offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2017 - 08/17/2017
The treatment copy produced an over 50% increase in conversion rate. This is likely due to the more personal copy that resonated with the Facebook ad’s personal tone, creating a more cohesive pathway for a more naturally emotionally-charged offer on the affects of poverty on our children’s worldviews.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International was running a name acquisition campaign tied to a free ebook offer. They wanted to test if more personal, emotionally-charged body copy would affect conversion rate on the offer landing page. We also removed a scripture reference and a bolded portion of copy from the body. All other elements remained the same.

experiments.title: How selling the value proposition in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding additional copy around the value proposition of Heritage will increase the visitors to the page and the overall number of donors once they get there.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2014 - 01/01/2015
The end of the year deadline serves as a way to increase the urgency around the year end campaign. As the clock ticks closer to midnight on the 31st, there is increased urgency to give.  With that increased urgency, we wondered if it was still necessary to sell the value of Heritage to the reader in the email or would a shorter email get them there faster. So we decided to test that.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the test did not validate, it would indicate that the additional value proposition is not necessary so close to the deadline.

experiments.title: How asking for additional information on an acquisition page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does asking for additional information on an acquisition page decrease conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2017 - 10/16/2017
Buckner International wanted to retrieve zip code information from new people coming onto the email file via their content offer, 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life. They wanted to see if adding a required zip code field would significantly decrease conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate- which in this case, was what they wanted. The less validation of the experiment, the better- since it did not have a statistically significant decrease, they could continue to ask for zip codes and get more data with little to no effect on conversion.

experiments.title: How a direct ask via voicemail impacts donor giving behaviors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a direct ask in a voicemail increase the the donor’s likelihood to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 12/05/2017
For Giving Tuesday, Buckner International wanted to test out a new channel of communication to see if we could boost donor giving. We utilized a service named SlyBroadcast that allows us to make straight to voicemail phone calls. It is a way of making a phone call to thousands of participants that would immediately show up as a missed phone call on their phone. While it is a recorded message, it would appear to be a genuine voicemail given the nature of the technology.
We had previously used SlyBroadcast for cultivation and stewardship messages to donors. This time around, we wanted to make a direct ask and request that donors go online or call back to make their Giving Tuesday gift.
We focused our audience on those that had both a phone number and email address on file. We then split this audience in half by their highest previous contribution levels to create equal segments so that we could create a treatment and control group that would not be skewed by past giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically significant, we did see a 28% decrease in the likelihood of someone that could have received the phone call to make a gift (the call had a ~60% deliverability rate so not everyone in the treatment group would have actually received the call). Given the low level of confidence, we would not go so far as to say that the phone call necessarily hurt the chances of a gift but it definitely did not increase the likelihood. Based upon these results, we will be reserving SlyBroadcast calls for cultivation and stewardship only.

experiments.title: How the style of popup influenced year end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: What style of popup will have the biggest influence on year end giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2017 - 01/01/2018
During the last few days of December, Buckner International was running a popup across their website. Historically, we had always used an exit intent so that they would only see the ad when they were finished reading the content. However, given the importance of year end, we decided to test a standard popup that would appear when a visitor first arrived at the site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither of the treatments statistically validated for donation or clicks to the website. However, we did see a surprising trend. The exit intent got more clicks to the donation page than the popup did but the popup received more donations over time. This may be related to a greater number of people seeing the popup which then influenced their decision to give when they received an email later that day. This is something that will definitely need further testing downstream to validate.
For posterity-sake, here are the results of the number of clicks on the popup.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action affected a social media audience’s conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition language to the calls-to-action at the bottom of the page increase the likelihood of a visitor giving their email or making a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/02/2015
The Alliance Defending Freedom blog receives high volumes of traffic each day, largely from social media. We wanted to find ways to motivate this audience to take one of two actions: either subscribe to the newsletter or make a donation.
In a previous experiment, we discovered that just placing the calls-to-action onto the blog posts wasn’t enough to convince people to take a specific action. For our next iteration of this test, we decided to try testing out adding some value proposition language around the desired actions. We also softened the buttons so that they would communicate value to the visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: This new treatment was able to succeed where the last experiment failed. We were able to acquire email addresses by implementing a name acquisition device on the page with accompanying value proposition language. That said, a 0.04% email conversion rate is certainly not breaking any records but it gives us a new starting point. This will allow us to continue to iterate and improve the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How reducing the value proposition copy affects the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will result in the best email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/07/2015 - 11/15/2015
A previous experiment for Alliance Defending Freedom had found that adding a value proposition, or a reason to give their email, on the homepage was able to increase the number of emails acquired by 44%. With the launch of the new website, we wanted to see if would could decrease the amount of copy required to communicate the same value proposition.
Our hypothesis was that by reducing the copy on the page, we could reduce the amount of mental friction associated with reading it. If it had the same level of value, this reduction in friction would be able to further lift conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the shorter value proposition actually reduced the conversion rate by 29.5%. It is our hypothesis that while the treatment was visually shorter, by removing the bullet points, we made it difficult to quickly scan. There was also added value communicated in the headline of the Control treatment with the perceived exclusivity of “Be the first to know”. This tells us that, at least with email acquisition, the added value that comes from the longer value proposition and the additional exclusivity translates into increased conversion.

experiments.title: How a product-level acquisition offer affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will result in the highest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2016 - 02/15/2016
In an effort to convert their existing blog traffic, Alliance Defending Freedom has been experimenting with different email acquisition methods and offers. The existing offer promotes a variety of email publications the visitor will receive and unites them under the ADF’s primary value proposition of defending religious freedom.
We had the hypothesis that if we focused the offer around something more tangible, a digital subscription to Faith and Justice Magazine, that the offer would be more appealing to visitors. This same incentive is offered with the current control but it does not describe the details of the magazine or offer a visual.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Faith and Justice offer resulted in a 37% decrease in emails acquired. This would lead us to believe the individual offer of Faith and Justice, while more tangible, was not as attractive of the ultimate goal of the audience which was learning more about their religious freedoms.  The more general offer did a better job of communicating this value proposition.

experiments.title: How clarifying the call-to-action for mobile visitors affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Does including the call-to-action on the homepage banner for mobile devices increase the number of people that explore the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2016 - 04/20/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom has a unique homepage banner that allows for multiple stories, cases, and appeals to be featured. The banner shows the featured image and includes the headline on top of it. When a user hovers over the image, more details of the article appears including the category and a call to action such as “Learn More” or “Double Your Impact.”
While discussing ways to increase the engagement of the visitors to the site, it was realized that mobile users would never see the call-to-action. It is only visible on hover which is a function not available on phones and tablets.  We decided to run a test targeting mobile devices to see if moving the call-to-action below the headline on the image would encourage more users to explore the website.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the call-to-action on top of the banner; we were able to increase the number of people exploring the website by 11.5%.  Our hypothesis is that without the call-to-action, the visitors were unclear on what they should do next. It made it more obvious that the banners should be clicked on and it clarified what their next steps would be on the subsequent page.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition language impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition copy will be most appealing (and subsequently have the highest conversion rate) with visitors to the blog?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2016 - 05/02/2016
In an effort to capture more data from visitors to their blog, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to test out a modified version of the existing email offer. Through a series of experiments, we had narrowed in on the active email offer which is a general email opt in that offers news and updates for religious freedom cases. We felt that we could increase the relevance of this offer by highlighting that ADF was truly the “boots on the ground” in these situations and could offer first-hand perspectives, not just an update.
experiments.keyLearnings: The updated value proposition copy led to a 37% increase to the number of emails acquired on the blog. In addition to the increased value that the concept of “first-hand perspectives” brought to the offer, we implemented an element of urgency to the headline. By changing “Get updates on your religious freedom” to “Stay informed on changes to your religious freedom.” we introduced the idea that the “changes” may have (and often do have) a direct impact on their day to day life. This made the email offer more important and personally relevant to the visitors.

experiments.title: How the tone of the call-to-action impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which headline will have the greatest email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2016 - 06/06/2016
Alliance Defending Freedom was launching a campaign in light of the recent controversy caused by the North Carolina bathroom bill. As part of this campaign, the landing page contained a statement of belief that encouraged visitors to submit their name and email.
The initial call-to-action that was found right above the form was “Help Keep Women and Children Safe.” We had the hypothesis that this headline may be unintentionally causing friction in the visitors’ minds. With the way it is worded, it infers that the signer will have to do work by “helping”. The statement below it also did not address how adding their signature would impact women and children.
We decided to run a test with a headline that would more closely align with the copy found below the headline.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new headline led to a 15.6% increase to the number of names and emails acquired. By removing the first word, “Help,” from the headline, we took the emphasis off of what the signer had to do and more closely aligned the headline to the statement of belief that followed it.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action will drive more traffic which will, in turn, garner more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2014 - 12/19/2014
In the second email of Heritage’s year end campaign, they wanted to find our which messaging stance appeals most to donors and drives the most donations.  They tried three variations: a direct ask, soft call-to-action around the organization, and a soft call-to-action focused on the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The emails with softer calls-to-action were able to increase the number of clicks more than 91%, however, the traffic was not as motivated to give when compared to those who were asked to make a gift within the email.   As a result, the email making a direct ask was able to convert 50% more donors than the other treatments.

experiments.title: How the page layout impacts visitor engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a sidebar impact visitor engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2017 - 02/21/2017
With the ADF blog, we wanted to find new ways to increase overall visitor engagement. Engagement is defined as anything from donations and email acquisition to visitors exploring the site. One way that the blog increased visitors exploring the site was by listing recent articles at the bottom of each post. We had the hypothesis that this may not be seen by a significant volume of visitors if they didn’t finish each post. To overcome this, we decided to test out “sticky” sidebar that would remain on the right side of the page for desktop visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 3 weeks, the sidebar did not produce any significant impact to either emails acquired or visitors exploring the website. The trend seemed to indicate that it may be negatively impacting these metrics so we decided to shut off this experiment in leu of others that we felt may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How a Rotating Exit Intent Offer Impacts Desktop Email Acquisition Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing people a different offer on each visit increase the chances of email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2017 - 06/30/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom knows that their blog attracts significant amounts of organic traffic. Keeping these visitors engaged is a big opportunity and major step forward in their donor journey. After running a series of tests, they found that using an exit intent pop-up as a final attempt to engage users before leaving a blog page increased email acquisition rate. Though they have seen an increase in engagement and conversion rates since adopting these pop-ups, they wondered if users were weary of seeing the same exit-intent messaging on every visit. To test their hypothesis, they decided to introduce a series of four rotating exit-intent offers. This would provide a new offer on the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th visit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom noticed that the series of rotating offers appeared to decrease email acquisition rates over the course of one month. However, this test was not statistically valid. Their findings must, consequently, remain anecdotal. This test may suggest that four different offers reduced the familiarity, and therefore the clarity, of the exit intent’s true value proposition–to keep exiting visitor up-to-date on news regarding religious liberty. Further tests will be conducted to discover the answer to this question.

experiments.title: How supporting copy length on a Facebook ad affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the length of the supporting ad copy increase clickthrough rate on a facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 12/12/2017
Oral arguments for Jack Phillips–a client of Alliance Defending Freedom–began on December 5th. As the news covered this national story, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to launch a Facebook ad campaign urging people to sign a letter of support for Jack. Since they knew some people were unfamiliar with Jack, they wondered: Will increasing the length of supporting ad copy on a facebook ad increase clickthrough rate? To test this, they created a longer treatment and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom that there was no statistical difference in email acquisition between the control and treatment ad. As we look at this test, it’s important to consider the timing of this campaign. Due to the relevant and captivating nature of this material, this campaign experienced a well above-average clickthrough and email acquisition rate. It’s likely that this played a factor in the A/B test (i.e. people were already pretty motivated to clickthrough, so there was less room for incremental optimization).

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which of the three value propositions, backed by evidentials, will increase both average gift and conversion rate the greatest amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2014 - 07/20/2014
